Prenatal development unfolds in three distinct periods — each with its own tempo, milestones, and vulnerabilities.

Three periods of prenatal development

The germinal period — weeks 1–2

Conception → implantation

Begins at fertilization — sperm meets egg in the fallopian tube, forming a single-celled zygote. The zygote travels toward the uterus, dividing rapidly. By the end of week 2 it has implanted into the uterine wall, where the placenta and umbilical cord begin to form. Many zygotes fail to implant successfully — a normal part of reproduction.

The embryonic period — weeks 3–8

Most vulnerable to teratogens

The defining feature: every major body structure begins to form. The neural tube (precursor to brain and spinal cord) closes by week 4. Heart begins beating around week 4. Limb buds, eyes, and internal organs all develop during this period. By the end of week 8 the embryo has recognizable human features but is still smaller than a grape (~1 inch long).

Why this period matters most

Because organs are forming, not just growing, the embryonic period is when teratogens cause the most catastrophic damage. A disruption during organogenesis can prevent a structure from ever forming. Once an organ is built (entering the fetal period), the same exposure usually causes growth problems or functional deficits — serious but less structurally devastating.

A pregnant person is exposed to the same teratogen at week 5 and at week 25. Why is the week 5 exposure typically more damaging?
Week 5 is within the embryonic period, when organs are first forming. Disruption here can prevent a structure from developing at all — producing major birth defects. By week 25 (fetal period), most organs are already built, so exposure usually causes growth restriction or functional deficits rather than catastrophic malformation. Timing matters as much as dose.

The fetal period — week 9 to birth

Growth & refinement

The longest period — about 30 weeks. Body systems mature, the brain undergoes explosive growth, and the fetus increases roughly 500-fold in weight. Major milestones: movement by week 16, fetus can hear by week 20, lungs become viable by ~24 weeks (the threshold of medical viability), and rapid weight gain in the third trimester prepares the newborn for life outside the uterus.

Spotlight on Research

Fetal activity and children's behavioral inhibition

DiPietro, J. A., and colleagues (2018)

What was the aim?

To examine the continuity question — one of human development's foundational issues — by testing whether fetal behaviors observed in the womb predict children's behavior years later. Specifically, do fetal movements relate to childhood behavioral inhibition (children who tend to be shy and fearful)?

How did they measure?

Sample: 385 women and their children. Prenatal measure: each mother was tested at 24, 32, and 36 weeks gestational age — lying quietly for 50 minutes while ultrasound recorded fetal movement. Childhood measure: when children were ~10 years old, mothers completed questionnaires assessing shyness and fearfulness (e.g., "My child feels shy when meeting new people"; "My child avoids going down high slides at a playground").

What was the design?

A longitudinal study linking prenatal measurements to childhood outcomes. Participants gave informed consent; the ultrasound procedures involved no known risks.

What were the results?

At 24 and 36 weeks, no differences in fetal movement across later behavioral-inhibition groups. At 32 weeks gestational age, however, fetuses who would later be classified as shier and more fearful children showed the least fetal activity, while fetuses who would later be classified as less shy and fearful were also the least active — with the most active fetuses in the middle (average) group.

What did the investigators conclude?

The findings support continuity from prenatal life forward: in DiPietro and colleagues' words, "over fairly protracted differences in time and place, core features of individual differences are preserved" (DiPietro et al., 2018, p. 868). Fetal behavior at one specific developmental window (~32 weeks) appears to relate to behavioral disposition in childhood.

What would strengthen the conclusions?

Estimates of children's inhibition relied solely on maternal reports; converging evidence from teachers, peer observations, and structured behavioral tasks would strengthen the case. The design is correlational, so it can't establish causation — fetal activity may share genetic or biological causes with later temperament rather than directly causing it. The window-specificity (32 weeks but not 24 or 36) suggests a particular developmental period matters, but the mechanism isn't yet clear. Following the same children into adolescence and adulthood would also clarify whether the continuity holds across longer timescales.

Why this matters clinically

It reinforces that temperament has prenatal roots, not just postnatal experience. Behavioral inhibition is a documented risk factor for childhood anxiety disorders, so early identification may enable early intervention. Understanding that some "shy and fearful" patterns are temperamental from the start can also reduce parental anxiety and counter the assumption that they reflect a parenting failure.

A person is described as having the genotype for an autosomal dominant condition (say, Huntington's disease) but currently has no symptoms because they are still young. How do you explain this in terms of genotype vs. phenotype, and what would "penetrance" mean in this context?
Genotype = the underlying genetic makeup at a particular locus. Phenotype = the observable traits/characteristics produced when the genotype interacts with the environment and development. Someone can carry the genotype for a condition without (yet) showing the phenotype if the condition is age-dependent — as with Huntington's disease, which typically begins to express symptoms in mid-adulthood despite the genotype being present from conception. Penetrance = the proportion of individuals with a particular genotype who actually express the corresponding phenotype. Huntington's has very high penetrance (nearly everyone with the gene eventually develops symptoms); other genetic conditions have lower penetrance, where some genotype-carriers never express the phenotype. The genotype-phenotype distinction is central to genetic counseling because someone can be a carrier and not currently affected, yet still pass on the genotype.
A baby is born at 25 weeks' gestation. What is the "age of viability", what survival challenges does this baby face beyond their gestational age, and what does the textbook say about the perceptual capacities a fetus has by this point?
Age of viability is roughly 22 to 28 weeks after conception — when most bodily systems function well enough that a fetus born at this time has a chance to survive (typically around 7 months after conception). Babies born this early face: (1) respiratory difficulty — lungs are not yet mature; (2) temperature regulation problems — they lack the insulating fat layer that develops in the 8th month after conception; (3) feeding, infection, and brain-development risks. With modern neonatal intensive care, survival is possible but not guaranteed. By this point, the fetus has already developed considerable perceptual capacity: response to touch at 14 weeks, light at 26 weeks, sound at 29 weeks, and by 34 weeks can distinguish different speech sounds including different vowels and languages.
Researchers found that infants whose mothers drank carrot juice frequently during the last month of pregnancy showed preferences for carrot-flavored cereal at 5–6 months of age. What does this demonstrate, and what's its developmental significance?
This demonstrates fetal learning — the fetus can encode and later recognize distinct stimuli (in this case, taste-flavor compounds transmitted through amniotic fluid). The same principle holds for distinct sounds, like the mother's voice: newborns prefer their mother's voice over an unfamiliar female voice, indicating prenatal auditory learning. Developmental significance: these capacities are adaptive. They help newborns recognize the primary caregiver (mother's voice and smell), facilitate early bonding, and lay groundwork for language learning by exposing the fetus to the rhythms and sounds of their native language. By 34 weeks the fetus can distinguish different speech sounds and different languages — the brain is already preparing for the linguistic environment it will be born into.

Test Yourself · Section 2.2

  1. The period of the zygote ends approximately when?
  2. During which of the three prenatal periods are most body structures and internal organs created?
  3. What is the "age of viability" and at approximately what gestational age does it occur?
  4. Name three sensory or perceptual capacities the fetus has developed by 34 weeks gestational age.
  5. If a woman discovers she is 7 weeks pregnant, which prenatal period is the embryo in, and why is this a particularly vulnerable window?

Diverse Perspectives — Who should have access to ART?

Assisted reproductive technology (ART) — the landscape

More than 40 years ago, Louise Brown made history as the world's first test-tube baby — conceived in a petri dish rather than in a uterus. Today, ART is used more than 306,000 times annually in the US, producing more than 80,000 babies per year (CDC, 2020a).

Common methods include:

  • In vitro fertilization (IVF) — egg and sperm mixed in a petri dish; several fertilized eggs placed in the uterus with the hope of implantation
  • GIFT / ZIFT — sperm or fertilized eggs injected directly into fallopian tubes
  • ICSI (intracytoplasmic sperm injection) — a single sperm injected directly into an egg; for severe male-factor infertility
  • Egg or sperm donation — using gametes from a donor
  • Gestational surrogacy — another person carries the pregnancy

Children conceived through ART show normal social and emotional development (Imrie & Golombok, 2020). The technology has changed what counts as a family.

The risks and inequities

Medical risks

Multiple eggs are often transferred to increase success odds, raising the risk of twins, triplets, or higher-order multiples (Ramalingam et al., 2016). Multiples have higher rates of preterm birth, low birth weight, and birth defects (Luke et al., 2016).

Cost barriers

Each IVF cycle costs about $12,000, with total costs sometimes exceeding $100,000. Most US states don't require insurance coverage for ART. The result: motherhood through ART is largely limited to affluent women — disproportionately white, by demographic patterns.

Age and "older" parenthood

Most women using ART are in their 30s; about 5% are 45 or older (CDC, 2020a). Critics call this unfair to children whose parents may be elderly during their adolescence. Defenders point out modern healthy women at 50 can expect ~35 more years and some research suggests older parents make better parents (less anxious, more financially stable, more patient).

The bioethics questions the textbook poses

(1) Should ART be available to all women regardless of income, ethnicity, or age — or remain a privilege of affluent younger women? (2) Are the risks (multiples, birth defects) always justified? When might they not be? (3) Are there circumstances in which adoption should be strongly recommended over ART?

This is the kind of Diverse Perspectives question the textbook trains you to ask. Reproductive technology raises questions about medical access (a treatable condition limited by ability to pay), distributive justice (who benefits, who's left out), and cultural assumptions (whose parenthood do we consider legitimate). For nursing practice: support families navigating these decisions without moralizing; advocate for equitable access.

A 38-year-old patient who has been trying to conceive for 3 years asks her nurse about IVF. She mentions she has insurance through her employer but is worried about costs. What does the nurse need to know about ART access, costs, and equity considerations to counsel her well?
ART access varies dramatically by geography, employer, and ability to pay — making this a major equity issue in reproductive medicine. What ART includes: in vitro fertilization (IVF, most common), intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), gamete intrafallopian transfer (GIFT), zygote intrafallopian transfer (ZIFT), egg/sperm donation, gestational surrogacy. Costs (US): (1) Each IVF cycle ~$12,000-$15,000 in 2024; total often exceeds $30,000-$100,000 across multiple cycles, medications, embryo storage, additional procedures. (2) Insurance coverage varies enormously by state: as of 2024, ~20 states require some fertility insurance coverage, with substantial variation in scope; most US workers have no coverage. (3) Some employers (especially large tech companies, universities) offer fertility benefits as recruiting tools. (4) Out-of-pocket costs concentrate ART access among affluent, primarily white, college-educated couples. Equity dimensions: (1) Income: fertility care often unavailable to lower-income families despite infertility affecting all groups. (2) Race/ethnicity: Black women have higher rates of infertility but lower utilization of ART, reflecting both access barriers and historical medical mistrust. (3) Sexual orientation: LGBTQ+ families face additional access barriers — some states/clinics restrict to married couples, insurance often excludes "voluntary" use. (4) Age: success rates decline with age; some clinics decline patients over 45; egg quality drops more steeply than uterine receptivity. (5) Geographic: rural areas have fewer clinics; urban access is much better. Counseling principles: (1) Provide accurate information about success rates by age and method (e.g., IVF with own eggs at 38 has ~30% live-birth rate per cycle; at 42 it's ~10%). (2) Acknowledge financial realities — discuss insurance verification, financing options, fertility grants, clinical trials. (3) Discuss alternatives openly — adoption, gestational surrogacy, donor gametes, accepting no biological children. (4) Address emotional aspects — infertility produces grief, isolation, relationship strain; psychological support is part of fertility care. (5) Discuss multifetal pregnancy reduction if multiples occur. (6) Don't moralize — patient autonomy in reproductive decisions is foundational. (7) Connect to mental health support; infertility is associated with depression and anxiety. For systemic advocacy: nursing organizations support fertility care as medical care (rather than "elective") and advocate for insurance coverage.

2.3 · Influences on Prenatal Development

The uterus is a protected environment, but it isn't sealed off from the world. What the pregnant person consumes, contracts, and experiences crosses to the fetus.

Influences on prenatal development

Teratogens — agents that harm

A teratogen is any agent that causes abnormal prenatal development. The effect depends on three factors: timing (which period), dose (how much), and the individual fetus's genetic susceptibility.

Drugs & substances

Alcohol — leading cause of preventable intellectual disability; produces fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASD). Tobacco — restricts oxygen, raises stillbirth and low birth weight. Cocaine, opioids — withdrawal in newborn, developmental risks.

Infections

Rubella (German measles) — vision and hearing loss, heart defects. Zika — microcephaly. Syphilis, HIV, cytomegalovirus — each carries specific risks. Vaccination prevents most of these.

Environmental hazards

Lead, mercury — neurodevelopmental damage. Radiation — high doses cause major malformations; routine medical imaging is far below danger thresholds. Air pollution — links to low birth weight and developmental issues.

Maternal factors

Nutrition — folic acid deficiency raises neural tube defect risk. Chronic stress — preterm birth and lower birth weight. Age — risks rise both very young (under 17) and over 35.

A pregnant patient asks if it's safe to have one glass of wine occasionally during pregnancy. What's the evidence-based answer?
There is no established safe amount of prenatal alcohol consumption. Alcohol crosses the placenta freely and the developing brain is sensitive to it throughout pregnancy. Even low doses have been linked to subtle FASD features. Public health agencies (CDC, ACOG, AAP) all recommend complete abstinence from alcohol during pregnancy.
A pediatric nurse evaluates a 4-year-old with developmental delays, ADHD-like symptoms, growth restriction, and characteristic facial features (smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, small palpebral fissures). What is the most likely diagnosis, what's the cause, and what is the priority clinical approach?
The presentation is classic for fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS), the most severe form on the FASD spectrum. The "sentinel facial features" (smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, small palpebral fissures) combined with growth deficiency and CNS involvement (intellectual disability, ADHD-like symptoms, learning difficulties) is the diagnostic triad. Cause: prenatal alcohol exposure — there is no safe established level. Priority clinical approach: (1) Early intervention services (special ed, speech/OT/PT as needed). (2) IEP for school. (3) Treat co-occurring conditions (ADHD often co-occurs and responds to standard treatment). (4) Structured environment, consistent routines, lower stimulation when overwhelmed. (5) Family support and education — parents need to understand that behavioral challenges reflect neurological damage, not willful misbehavior. (6) Long-term follow-up — FASD increases risk of secondary disabilities (school failure, mental health issues, substance use, criminal justice involvement) without support. Critical for nursing: FASD is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability in the US, and is dramatically underdiagnosed — many cases never get the label and lose access to services.

Prenatal testing

Several tools let prospective parents and clinicians assess fetal development. Ultrasound uses sound waves to image the fetus — routine, low-risk. Amniocentesis samples amniotic fluid to detect chromosomal disorders (~16 weeks). Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) tests placental tissue earlier (~10 weeks) but carries slightly higher miscarriage risk. Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) uses fetal DNA in the maternal bloodstream — increasingly common as a first screen.

2.4 · Labor and Delivery

Birth is the final transition of the prenatal period. Labor proceeds through three stages and produces a newborn whose status is rapidly assessed and addressed.

Labor, delivery & newborn

Three stages of labor

Stage 1 — Dilation

The longest stage (often 12–24 hours for a first birth). Cervix dilates from 0 to 10 cm. Contractions become stronger, longer, and closer together. Ends when the cervix is fully dilated.

Stage 2 — Delivery

Pushing through to birth — typically 30 min to 2 hours. The baby moves through the birth canal and emerges. Ends with the baby's birth.

Stage 3 — Afterbirth

Delivery of the placenta — usually within 15–30 minutes after birth. Uterus continues to contract to control bleeding.

Assessing the newborn — APGAR

At 1 & 5 minutes

Developed by Dr. Virginia Apgar in 1952. Five criteria, each scored 0, 1, or 2 (maximum total = 10). Assessed at 1 minute and again at 5 minutes after birth.

A — Appearance

Skin color. 0 = pale/blue all over; 2 = pink all over.

P — Pulse

Heart rate. 0 = absent; 1 = under 100 bpm; 2 = 100+ bpm.

G — Grimace

Reflex response to suction or stimulation. 0 = no response; 2 = vigorous cry.

A — Activity

Muscle tone. 0 = limp; 2 = active movement.

R — Respiration

Breathing. 0 = absent; 1 = weak/irregular; 2 = strong cry.

Interpreting

7–10 = good condition. 4–6 = needs intervention. 0–3 = serious — emergency resuscitation needed.

A newborn at 1 minute: pink body but blue extremities, HR 110, grimaces and cries when suctioned, has good muscle tone, strong cry. What's the APGAR score?
9 out of 10. Appearance = 1 (blue extremities), Pulse = 2 (over 100), Grimace = 2 (strong reflex response), Activity = 2 (good muscle tone), Respiration = 2 (strong cry). Total = 1+2+2+2+2 = 9. A score of 7–10 indicates good condition; this newborn is doing well.

Approaches to childbirth

The shift from medicalized to prepared childbirth

For much of the 20th century, hospital labor and delivery involved extensive medical procedures often left women fearful, tense, and in more pain. In the mid-20th century, two European physicians — Grantly Dick-Read (1959) and Fernand Lamaze (1958) — argued for a more natural and prepared approach, framing labor and delivery as life events to be celebrated rather than medical procedures to be endured.

Today, many varieties of "prepared childbirth" share three fundamental elements:

  • Education — going to classes to learn what's happening during pregnancy, labor, and delivery. Reduces fear by replacing the unknown with concrete understanding.
  • Natural pain management — emphasis on unmedicated methods (deep breathing, relaxation, visualization, positioning, massage, hydrotherapy) over medical interventions. The most common medical intervention in the US, epidural analgesia, reduces pain but can cause headaches and decreased blood pressure (ACOG, 2011b).
  • Support person — co-parent, relative, close friend, or a trained doula who provides emotional support, advocates for the laboring person's wishes, and helps with pain management techniques. Hodnett et al. (2012): continuous labor support shortens labor, reduces medication use, and increases satisfaction.

Setting choices and providers

Hospital birth (US default)

Virtually all US births occur in hospitals (~98%). Advantages: immediate access to emergency interventions (C-section, NICU). Disadvantages: more interventions, less personal control, restricted positions and movement, IV restrictions, time pressure.

Birth center

Free-standing or hospital-affiliated facilities staffed by midwives. Aim to combine homelike setting with medical safety. Best for low-risk pregnancies with hospital backup nearby.

Home birth

About 1% of US births (MacDorman & Declercq, 2019). Higher in some other developed countries. ACOG considers home birth safe ONLY when: (1) healthy pregnancy; (2) problem-free labor expected; (3) trained healthcare professional attends; (4) comprehensive medical care nearby if needed. Some medical professionals remain skeptical (Declercq, 2012); proponents cite better relaxation, greater control.

Midwife-led care

In most countries, midwives deliver most low-risk births. Different from US obstetrician-led model. Evidence shows midwife-led care has equivalent safety for low-risk pregnancies with less intervention. CNMs (certified nurse-midwives) and CMs (certified midwives) have advanced training.

Doula support

Doulas are NOT medical professionals — they provide non-medical labor support. Continuous doula support is associated with shorter labor, less medication use, fewer C-sections, fewer instrumental deliveries, and higher satisfaction (Hodnett et al., 2012; Cochrane review). Doulas can be a key equity intervention — improving outcomes for Black birthing parents in particular.

Pain management options

Non-medical: position changes, walking, hydrotherapy (showers, tubs), breathing techniques, massage, counter-pressure, TENS. Medical: nitrous oxide ("laughing gas"), IV opioids, epidural analgesia, combined spinal-epidural, general anesthesia for emergency C-section.

Adjusting to parenthood

Physical and psychological transition

After a healthy birth, parents experience excitement, pride, and joy — and major adjustments. Physically, the new mother's body undergoes substantial changes: breasts begin producing milk; the uterus contracts back to pre-pregnancy size over 5-6 weeks; estrogen levels drop sharply; healing from delivery (vaginal tears or C-section incision); sleep deprivation; possible breastfeeding challenges.

Psychologically, parents must reorganize routines around the baby's sleep-wake cycle. Fathers (or non-birthing partners) sometimes feel left out when birthing parents devote most attention to the baby. Relationship dynamics shift; previous patterns disrupt; expectations may not match reality.

Baby blues vs. postpartum depression

Baby blues: roughly half of all new mothers experience irritability, resentment, and crying spells in the first 1-2 weeks postpartum. Reflects both the stress of caring for a newborn and the dramatic physiological changes as the body returns to non-pregnant state (American Academy of Pediatrics, 2018). Self-resolving; doesn't require specific treatment beyond support, rest, and validation.

Postpartum depression (PPD): 10-15% of new mothers experience irritability lasting months, accompanied by feelings of low self-worth, disturbed sleep, poor appetite, and apathy.

Biological contributors

Hormonal changes after birth place some women at risk (O'Hara & McCabe, 2013). Genetic predisposition. Sleep deprivation amplifies risk.

Experiential contributors

Single parenthood, depression history before pregnancy, other life stresses (loss, moving, illness), unplanned pregnancy, lack of other adult support (e.g., absent partner) all raise risk (Edwards et al., 2012; Hutchens & Kearney, 2020).

Impact on baby

Untreated PPD affects the baby. Mothers who are lethargic and emotionless don't touch, cuddle, or talk to babies enough. They're less effective at feeding and sleep routines (Field, 2010). Children of mothers with persistent untreated PPD have more behavioral problems later, including antisocial behavior (Barker et al., 2012).

Protective: breastfeeding

Mothers who breastfeed are less likely to become depressed, possibly because breastfeeding releases hormones (oxytocin, prolactin) that act as natural antidepressants (Gagliardi, 2005). When PPD develops in breastfeeding mothers, treatment compatible with breastfeeding is available.

Clinical urgency: PPD is treatable but often missed. Screening (e.g., Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale) at well-baby visits, postpartum appointments, and primary care visits identifies cases that would otherwise go unrecognized. Untreated PPD has long-term consequences for both parent and child.

Linking Research to Life — help for depressed mothers

Evidence-based PPD treatment

Researchers have developed evidence-based interventions for mothers experiencing depression (Goodman & Garber, 2017). Effective programs share two key features:

1. Direct depression treatment (CBT framework)

Mothers learn to: (a) identify negative thoughts, feelings, behaviors; (b) realize these are often distorted; (c) restructure thinking to be more accurate and positive.

Example: a mother whose infant won't stop crying thinks "I can't get my baby to stop. I'm a terrible mom." Through therapy: she learns this thought is distorted — she's actually an excellent mom in many ways, and the baby is probably crying because he's exhausted from missing a nap.

2. Positive parenting skills

Depression leads many mothers to parent ineffectively — either coercive/hostile or withdrawn/ignoring. In treatment, mothers learn: being affectionate, having reasonable expectations, encouraging desired behaviors. This breaks the depression-poor-parenting-distressed-child cycle.

Outcomes

When mothers participate: they become less depressed, parenting improves, children have fewer behavioral problems. Programs are cost-effective and relatively easy to implement (Goodman & Garber, 2017).

Treatment barriers

Many women who'd benefit don't receive treatment. Three main barriers: (1) Awareness — they don't know help is available or don't recognize PPD as treatable medical condition. (2) Affordability — therapy costs, lack of insurance coverage, lost work time. (3) Stigma — mental health treatment carries shame in many communities; mothers fear judgment, custody concerns, or being labeled "bad mom."

For nursing practice: (1) Screen for PPD at every postpartum and well-baby visit; (2) Normalize the discussion — frame screening as routine, not as accusation; (3) Educate about PPD as biology + experience, not personal failure; (4) Address concrete barriers proactively — provide local resources, sliding-scale programs, telehealth options; (5) Engage partners and family in supporting treatment; (6) Follow up to ensure treatment uptake; (7) Coordinate with mental health providers for medication when therapy alone insufficient (zuranolone for PPD, SSRIs compatible with breastfeeding); (8) Address suicidal ideation immediately — PPD with suicidality is a psychiatric emergency.

A new mother at her 6-week postpartum visit tells the nurse she's been crying often, can't shake feelings that she's a "terrible mom", isn't bonding with her baby, and is having trouble sleeping even when the baby is asleep. The patient asks if this is "just baby blues". How should the nurse respond, and what's the evidence-based next step?
This is NOT baby blues — these symptoms warrant immediate PPD screening and likely intervention. Distinguishing features: (1) Timing: baby blues resolve within 2 weeks; this is 6 weeks post-delivery. (2) Severity: baby blues involves transient tearfulness and mood lability; PPD involves persistent low mood, self-worth issues, sleep/appetite changes, and bonding difficulties. (3) Bonding interference: "not bonding with my baby" is a particular red flag — baby blues doesn't typically include bonding interference. (4) Sleep disturbance beyond baby's needs: not sleeping when baby is asleep suggests insomnia, a depression symptom. Action steps: (1) Validate: "What you're describing sounds harder than baby blues. This is a real medical condition that affects 10-15% of new mothers, and it's treatable. You're not failing — your body and brain are reacting to enormous physiological and life changes." (2) Screen with Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS): 10-item validated tool; score ≥10 suggests possible PPD; ≥13 suggests probable PPD. Item #10 specifically asks about suicidal thoughts — critical to assess. (3) Assess suicidal ideation explicitly: ask directly. PPD with suicidality is a psychiatric emergency. (4) Assess thoughts of harming the baby: rare but critical to rule out postpartum psychosis (1-2 per 1,000 births; psychiatric emergency requiring hospitalization). (5) Provide same-day referral: mental health provider, primary care, OB. Don't say "follow up at next visit" if symptoms are significant. (6) Treatment options: Therapy first-line for mild-moderate (CBT, IPT — Goodman & Garber framework). Medication for moderate-severe: SSRIs (sertraline first-line — well-studied in breastfeeding); newer option zuranolone (Zurzuvae) FDA-approved for PPD in 2023, 14-day oral course showing rapid response. Combined treatment for severe cases. (7) Address concrete barriers: explain insurance coverage, locate sliding-scale therapy, telehealth options, Postpartum Support International (PSI) free helpline 1-800-944-4773. (8) Engage support system: partner, family, friends. Practical help with infant care while mother heals. (9) Education: PPD is biology + experience, not personal failure. Hormones, sleep deprivation, life upheaval. Many famous women have had PPD; treatment works. (10) Follow-up: within 1-2 weeks to ensure she's connecting with treatment, not just given a referral. For breastfeeding patients: SSRIs, especially sertraline, are compatible with breastfeeding. Zuranolone requires temporary cessation. Don't pressure stopping breastfeeding for medication. Outcomes when treated: most women recover; treatment improves maternal mood, parenting, and child outcomes; untreated PPD has documented long-term effects on the child.

Birth complications & outcomes

Birth complications

Preterm birth (before 37 weeks) and low birth weight (under 2,500 g) are the biggest risk factors for newborn complications. Very low birth weight (under 1,500 g) is associated with serious long-term issues including increased risk of developmental disability. Modern NICU care has dramatically improved outcomes — viability now begins around 24 weeks. Cesarean delivery (C-section) accounts for about 30% of US births, used when vaginal delivery would endanger parent or baby.

Low birth weight categories & infant mortality

clinical detail

Low birth weight is stratified into three categories, each with progressively worse outcomes:

  • Low birth weight (LBW): under 2,500 g (5 lb 8 oz). Increased risk for short-term complications (feeding, temperature regulation, jaundice) and small but real risks for developmental delays.
  • Very low birth weight (VLBW): under 1,500 g (3 lb 5 oz). Substantial NICU stays typical; risk of respiratory distress, intraventricular hemorrhage, retinopathy of prematurity, long-term developmental issues.
  • Extremely low birth weight (ELBW): under 1,000 g (2 lb 3 oz). Very high risk of serious complications; survival possible from ~22–24 weeks with intensive NICU support, but neurodevelopmental disability rates are elevated.

US infant mortality disparities: The overall US infant mortality rate (~5.4 per 1,000 live births) hides dramatic racial inequities. Non-Hispanic Black infants die at more than twice the rate of non-Hispanic White infants (~10.4 vs ~4.5 per 1,000). These disparities reflect not biology but the cumulative effects of structural racism on healthcare access, maternal health, prenatal care quality, chronic stress, and social determinants. They persist after controlling for income and education — meaning a college-educated Black mother has worse outcomes than a White mother who didn't finish high school. For nursing: this is one of the most concrete examples of how the biopsychosocial framework matters in real practice — the social and cultural forces (not just biology) produce dramatically different outcomes.

A NICU nurse is reporting on three newborns: A weighs 2,200 g, B weighs 1,200 g, and C weighs 850 g. Classify each and rank the urgency of risk profile.
A (2,200 g) = Low birth weight (LBW): under 2,500 g but over 1,500 g. Generally good outcomes with standard support; monitor for jaundice, feeding, temperature regulation. B (1,200 g) = Very low birth weight (VLBW): under 1,500 g but over 1,000 g. Substantial NICU course; respiratory support often needed, IVH risk in first 72 hours, watch for retinopathy. C (850 g) = Extremely low birth weight (ELBW): under 1,000 g. Highest risk profile — full intensive care, surfactant, ventilation typically, very high rates of long-term neurodevelopmental issues (~25–50% depending on gestational age and complications). Urgency ranking: C > B > A. Key clinical point: weight and gestational age track together but aren't identical — a small-for-gestational-age full-term infant has a different risk profile than a same-weight extremely preterm infant.
A nursing student reads that US infant mortality for non-Hispanic Black infants is more than 2× the rate for non-Hispanic White infants. The disparity persists after controlling for income and education. What does this evidence indicate about the cause, and what are the implications for nursing care?
The cause is not biological/genetic — it's structural. The fact that the disparity persists after controlling for income and education tells us that race itself functions as a marker for differential treatment and exposure, not for biological difference. Specific mechanisms: differential prenatal care quality and access, provider bias in pain management and concern-taking, chronic stress from racism producing accelerated biological aging ("weathering" hypothesis), differential environmental exposures, differential intrapartum care. Implications for nursing: (1) Take maternal complaints seriously equally across all groups — research shows providers under-respond to Black women's reports of pain and distress. (2) Screen for and address social determinants of health. (3) Recognize implicit bias as part of professional practice — examine your own. (4) Advocate for systemic change in care delivery. (5) The biopsychosocial framework matters here — sociocultural forces produce real biological outcomes (infant deaths) through specific mechanisms. This is the core of health equity in modern nursing practice.

2.5 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter — anchor your understanding by knowing who proposed what and what evidence supports it.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Gregor Mendel — Established the basic principles of inheritance through pea plant experiments. Foundation for understanding dominant/recessive alleles, segregation, and independent assortment.
  • Virginia Apgar — Developed the APGAR newborn assessment in 1952. Five criteria (Appearance, Pulse, Grimace, Activity, Respiration) scored 0–2 each at 1 and 5 minutes. Still the universal first newborn assessment.
  • Sandra Scarr & Kathleen McCartney — Theory of niche-picking: as children develop, they increasingly select environments that fit their genetic predispositions, amplifying gene-environment correlations.
  • Robert Plomin — Foundational behavioral geneticist; demonstrated heritability of many psychological traits using twin and adoption studies. Coined the term "nonshared environmental influences."
  • Kenneth Lyons Jones & David Smith — First clinically described fetal alcohol syndrome (1973). Established alcohol as the most preventable cause of intellectual disability.

Specific studies cited

  • Behavioral genetics twin studies (Plomin and colleagues): Estimate heritability of intelligence, personality, mental illness. Showed that identical twins reared apart are more similar than fraternal twins reared together for many traits.
  • Fetal alcohol research (Jones & Smith, 1973; subsequent prospective cohorts): Established the spectrum of FASD — fetal alcohol syndrome, partial FAS, alcohol-related neurodevelopmental disorder.
  • Zika virus prospective cohorts (Brazil, 2015–2017): Documented microcephaly and other severe neurodevelopmental outcomes in babies of infected mothers, especially first-trimester exposure.
  • CDC PRAMS (Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System): Long-running US surveillance of maternal behaviors and outcomes. Source of much epidemiological data on prenatal risk factors.
  • Tom Boyce & Bruce Ellis — "Orchid & dandelion" hypothesis: Some children are highly responsive to environmental conditions (orchids); others thrive across a range (dandelions). Gene-environment differential susceptibility.

2.6 · Key Terms & Definitions

The 57 vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter. Definitions are precise enough for testing.

Glossary by category

Genetics & inheritance (21 terms)

  • Chromosomes — Threadlike structures in the nucleus containing genetic material. Humans have 23 pairs (46 total).
  • Autosomes — The first 22 pairs of chromosomes; the same in females and males.
  • Sex chromosomes — The 23rd pair; XX for biological female, XY for biological male.
  • Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) — The double-helix molecule that makes up chromosomes and carries genetic information.
  • Gene — A segment of DNA that codes for a specific protein or trait.
  • Genotype — A person's complete inherited genetic makeup.
  • Phenotype — The observable traits resulting from genotype × environment interaction.
  • Alleles — Alternative forms of the same gene (e.g., for eye color).
  • Homozygous — Having two identical alleles for a trait.
  • Heterozygous — Having two different alleles for a trait.
  • Dominant — An allele whose trait is expressed even when paired with a different allele.
  • Recessive — An allele whose trait is expressed only when paired with another recessive allele.
  • Incomplete dominance — Both alleles partially express, producing an intermediate phenotype (sickle-cell trait is the classic example).
  • Sickle-cell trait — The heterozygous form of the sickle-cell gene; gives some malaria protection without full disease severity.
  • Phenylketonuria (PKU) — Autosomal recessive disorder where the body can't metabolize phenylalanine; managed with diet.
  • Behavioral genetics — The study of genetic contributions to behavior, primarily through twin and adoption studies.
  • Polygenic inheritance — A trait influenced by many genes acting together (intelligence, height, personality).
  • Monozygotic twins — Identical twins from one zygote that split; ~100% shared DNA.
  • Dizygotic twins — Fraternal twins from two separate eggs and sperm; ~50% shared DNA (like ordinary siblings).
  • Niche-picking — As children develop, they actively select environments that match their genetic predispositions, strengthening gene-environment correlation.
  • Nonshared environmental influences — Environmental factors that differ between siblings raised in the same family (different friends, teachers, experiences); often more important than shared environment in shaping individual differences.

Prenatal development (20 terms)

  • Prenatal development — Development from conception to birth (~38 weeks).
  • Zygote — The fertilized egg; the single cell formed when sperm meets egg.
  • In vitro fertilization — Eggs fertilized in the laboratory then implanted into the uterus.
  • Implantation — When the developing zygote burrows into the uterine wall (around day 6–10).
  • Germ disc — A small cluster of cells in the implanted zygote that will develop into the baby.
  • Placenta — The structure that connects the developing baby to the uterine wall; provides nutrients and oxygen, removes waste.
  • Embryo — The developing baby from week 3 through week 8; the period of major organ system formation.
  • Ectoderm — Outer layer of the embryo; develops into hair, outer skin, and nervous system.
  • Mesoderm — Middle layer of the embryo; develops into muscles, bones, and circulatory system.
  • Endoderm — Inner layer of the embryo; develops into digestive and respiratory systems.
  • Amnion — The fluid-filled sac that surrounds and protects the developing baby.
  • Amniotic fluid — Fluid inside the amnion; cushions the baby and provides constant temperature.
  • Umbilical cord — Connects the developing baby to the placenta.
  • Cephalocaudal principle — Development proceeds from head downward (head develops before legs).
  • Proximodistal principle — Development proceeds from the center outward (arms before fingers).
  • Period of the fetus — Week 9 through birth; the longest prenatal period; size and weight gains dominate.
  • Cerebral cortex — The wrinkled outer layer of the brain responsible for higher mental processes; develops dramatically during the fetal period.
  • Vernix — The greasy white substance covering the fetus that protects skin during prenatal development.
  • Age of viability — The point (about 22–24 weeks) when a fetus can potentially survive outside the womb with intensive medical support.
  • Spina bifida — A neural tube defect where the spinal column doesn't close completely; prevention linked to maternal folic acid.

Influences & prenatal testing (8 terms)

  • Stress — Significant prolonged stress in pregnancy can affect fetal development through cortisol and immune changes.
  • Teratogen — Any agent (drug, infection, environmental hazard) that causes abnormal prenatal development.
  • Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD) — The range of effects caused by prenatal alcohol exposure, including FAS (full syndrome with facial features + growth + CNS), partial FAS, alcohol-related neurodevelopmental disorder. Leading preventable cause of intellectual disability.
  • Ultrasound — Uses sound waves to image the fetus; routine, low-risk; assesses growth, position, anatomy.
  • Amniocentesis — Sampling amniotic fluid (~16 weeks) to test for chromosomal disorders.
  • Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) — Sampling placental tissue (~10 weeks); earlier than amnio but slightly higher miscarriage risk.
  • Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) — Tests fetal DNA fragments in maternal blood; very high accuracy for major chromosomal trisomies; increasingly first-line.
  • Fetal therapy — Medical or surgical interventions performed on the fetus before birth (transfusions, certain surgeries).

Labor, delivery & outcomes (8 terms)

  • Crowning — The moment when the baby's head appears at the vaginal opening during the second stage of labor.
  • Hypoxia — Lack of adequate oxygen, particularly to the fetal brain during difficult labor; can cause neurological injury.
  • Cesarean section (C-section) — Surgical delivery through an incision in the abdomen and uterus; ~30% of US births.
  • Preterm (premature) — Born before 37 weeks gestation.
  • Low birth weight (LBW) — Less than 2,500 g (5 lb 8 oz).
  • Very low birth weight (VLBW) — Less than 1,500 g (3 lb 5 oz).
  • Extremely low birth weight (ELBW) — Less than 1,000 g (2 lb 3 oz).
  • Infant mortality — Death within the first year of life; expressed per 1,000 live births. US rate varies dramatically by race, ~2× higher for non-Hispanic Black infants vs non-Hispanic White infants.

Clinical & supplementary terms (16 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • Nondisjunction — Failure of a chromosome pair to separate properly during meiosis (egg or sperm formation), resulting in a gamete with one extra or one missing chromosome. The main mechanism behind Down syndrome (extra chromosome 21) and other trisomies. Risk rises substantially with advanced maternal age because egg cells are present from before birth and accumulate damage over time.
  • Carrier (genetic) — Person who has one copy of a recessive disease-causing allele but doesn't express the disease phenotype themselves. Two carriers can produce an affected child if both pass on the recessive allele. Common in conditions like cystic fibrosis, sickle cell disease, Tay-Sachs.
  • NIPT (non-invasive prenatal testing) — Screening test analyzing fragments of fetal DNA in maternal blood; detects chromosomal abnormalities (Down syndrome, trisomy 18/13) with high sensitivity. Available from ~10 weeks gestation. Non-invasive (no miscarriage risk, unlike amniocentesis or CVS) but is a screening test, not diagnostic — positive results require confirmation.
  • Health equity — The principle that every person should have a fair and just opportunity to attain their full health potential. Distinguished from health equality (same resources to all) — equity requires addressing historical and structural barriers.
  • FASD (fetal alcohol spectrum disorders) — Umbrella term for the range of conditions caused by prenatal alcohol exposure. Includes fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS, the most severe form), partial FAS, alcohol-related neurodevelopmental disorder, and alcohol-related birth defects. There is no known safe amount of alcohol in pregnancy.
  • Assisted reproductive technology (ART) — Medical interventions to achieve pregnancy when natural conception isn't possible. Includes IVF, ICSI, GIFT, ZIFT, gamete donation, gestational surrogacy. Used in 306,000+ US cycles annually, producing ~80,000 babies/year (CDC, 2020a). Access varies dramatically by income, insurance, geography — a major equity issue.
  • In vitro fertilization (IVF) — Most common form of ART. Eggs retrieved from ovaries, fertilized with sperm in a petri dish, embryos transferred to uterus. Average cycle cost ~$12,000-$15,000 in 2024; success rates ~30% at age 38, declining with age. Louise Brown (1978) was the first successfully born via IVF.
  • Intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) — A single sperm injected directly into an egg using a microscopic pipette. Used when male-factor infertility makes natural fertilization impossible. Performed as part of IVF cycle.
  • Doula — Trained non-medical labor support person who provides emotional support, comfort measures, advocacy, and continuous presence during labor. Distinct from doctors and midwives (who provide medical care). Continuous doula support shortens labor, reduces medication use, decreases C-section rates, and increases satisfaction (Hodnett et al., 2012). Birth doulas, postpartum doulas, and bereavement doulas exist.
  • Midwife — Healthcare professional trained to provide primary care for low-risk pregnancies and births. Certified Nurse-Midwife (CNM): an RN with graduate-level midwifery training. Certified Midwife (CM): a non-nurse with graduate midwifery training. Certified Professional Midwife (CPM): trained through apprenticeship and direct-entry programs. In most countries, midwives attend most low-risk births.
  • Prepared childbirth — Educational approach to labor and delivery emphasizing knowledge (classes about pregnancy and labor), natural pain management (breathing, relaxation, visualization, positioning), and support person involvement. Originated with Grantly Dick-Read (1959) and Fernand Lamaze (1958) as alternatives to medicalized birth.
  • Epidural analgesia — Common medical pain management in US births. Local anesthetic injected into epidural space below spinal cord. Effective for pain but can cause headaches, decreased blood pressure, and sometimes slower second-stage labor (ACOG, 2011b).
  • Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) — Validated 10-item self-report screening tool for postpartum depression. Score ≥10 suggests possible PPD; ≥13 suggests probable PPD. Item #10 screens for suicidal ideation. Recommended by ACOG for routine postpartum screening.
  • Zuranolone (Zurzuvae) — FDA-approved in 2023 as the first oral medication specifically indicated for postpartum depression. 14-day course; works rapidly (within days) compared to weeks for SSRIs. Requires temporary breastfeeding cessation.
  • Postpartum Support International (PSI) — Largest US organization supporting maternal mental health. Provides free helpline (1-800-944-4773), trained provider directory, online support groups, and family education. Critical resource for nurses to know.
  • Behavioral inhibition — Temperamental tendency toward shyness, withdrawal, and fearfulness in response to novel situations. Originally identified by Jerome Kagan. Documented to have prenatal correlates (DiPietro et al., 2018 — fetuses with less movement at 32 weeks were shier/more fearful at age 10). Modest but real predictor of childhood anxiety disorder risk.

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — a case that illustrates how heredity and environment interact through the lifespan.

Ben & Matt Kail — niche-picking analysis

The case: Ben & Matt Kail pick their niches

Ben and Matt are brothers born 25 months apart. Same parents, same home, same neighborhood, similar schools. Yet from an early age they showed strikingly different preferences. Ben was the more outgoing, exploratory brother — he enjoyed widening the range of his environments, joining clubs, trying new sports, traveling. Matt was the more focused, internally-oriented brother — he gravitated toward depth over breadth, picking a few interests and pursuing them intensely.

As they grew, those differences amplified. Ben chose schools and experiences with diverse possibilities; Matt chose ones where he could concentrate. Neither path was scripted by their parents.

How do you explain two brothers, raised in the same household, producing such different developmental paths? Use the biopsychosocial framework, and pay particular attention to niche-picking.

Worked analysis

Biological forces

The brothers share roughly 50% of their DNA (like any siblings), but the half they don't share matters. Inherited differences in temperament, attention, and reactivity are visible from infancy — well before parenting could explain them.

Psychological forces

Different temperaments → different preferences → different choices. As each brother developed self-awareness, he formed a self-concept that fit his lived experience, which then shaped what he sought out next.

Sociocultural forces

Same family, but each brother's microsystem became different over time. They picked different friends, different clubs, different teachers. The shared sociocultural baseline diverged because each was selecting from it differently.

Life-cycle forces

Being born 25 months apart isn't a small thing. Ben was the older sibling navigating school first; Matt watched and chose differently — sometimes because of, sometimes in contrast to, Ben.

The key concept: niche-picking

Heredity doesn't just give you traits — it influences what environments you seek out. Ben's outgoing temperament led him to pick high-variety niches; Matt's depth-oriented temperament led him to pick focused ones. Each niche then reinforced the temperament. This is why genetically similar people raised in the same household can end up so different — they didn't just inherit different traits, they curated different developmental environments using those traits.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 2 core content.

Q1

Two parents are both carriers (Aa) for an autosomal recessive disorder. What is the probability that any single child of theirs will be affected by the disorder?

  1. A. 0%
  2. B. 25%
  3. C. 50%
  4. D. 75%
Show rationale

A. Both parents carry the recessive allele, so the child can inherit it.

B. CORRECT. Punnett square for Aa × Aa: AA, Aa, aA, aa. Only the aa combination expresses the recessive phenotype = 1 out of 4 = 25%.

C. 50% is the probability of being a carrier (Aa), not of being affected.

D. 75% would be affected only if dominant inheritance — and even then, this answer mixes up the math.

Q2

During which prenatal period are environmental teratogens most likely to cause major structural birth defects?

  1. A. Germinal period (weeks 1–2)
  2. B. Embryonic period (weeks 3–8)
  3. C. Fetal period (weeks 9 to birth)
  4. D. Postnatal period (after birth)
Show rationale

A. During the germinal period, exposure more often causes implantation failure (and pregnancy loss) than structural defects — the embryo hasn't begun organogenesis.

B. CORRECT. The embryonic period is when all major organ systems are forming. Disruption during organogenesis can prevent a structure from ever developing — producing the most catastrophic, structurally devastating defects.

C. Fetal-period exposure typically affects growth and function (smaller, slower, learning problems) rather than causing major missing or malformed structures.

D. Postnatal exposures are not "prenatal teratogens" by definition.

Q3

A newborn at 1 minute has the following: limp muscle tone, heart rate 80, no grimace response to suctioning, blue color all over, weak irregular breathing. What is the APGAR score and what does it indicate?

  1. A. 1 — emergency resuscitation needed
  2. B. 2 — emergency resuscitation needed
  3. C. 4 — needs intervention
  4. D. 7 — good condition
Show rationale

A. Limp = 0 (Activity), HR < 100 but present = 1 (Pulse), no grimace = 0, blue all over = 0 (Appearance), weak irregular = 1 (Respiration). Total = 0+1+0+0+1 = 2, not 1.

B. CORRECT. Activity 0 + Pulse 1 + Grimace 0 + Appearance 0 + Respiration 1 = 2. A score of 0–3 indicates severe distress requiring immediate emergency resuscitation.

C. A score of 4 (4–6 range = "needs intervention") is more concerning than "needs intervention" implies, but this newborn scores lower than that.

D. A score of 7–10 indicates good condition. This newborn shows signs of severe distress.

Q4

A researcher finds that identical (MZ) twins are much more similar in introversion-extraversion than fraternal (DZ) twins. What does this finding most directly support?

  1. A. Introversion-extraversion is entirely environmentally determined
  2. B. Heredity contributes substantially to introversion-extraversion
  3. C. Introversion is the dominant trait and extraversion is recessive
  4. D. Twin studies are unreliable for personality research
Show rationale

A. If it were entirely environmental, MZ and DZ twins (both raised in similar home environments) would show similar similarity. The MZ-DZ difference points the other way.

B. CORRECT. MZ twins share 100% of DNA; DZ twins share ~50%. Greater MZ similarity for a trait indicates that shared genes contribute to the trait — that is, the trait is heritable. This is the core logic of twin studies.

C. Personality dimensions like introversion-extraversion are polygenic continuous traits — not single-gene dominant/recessive patterns.

D. Twin studies have limitations, but this finding actually demonstrates their value — they let researchers separate genetic from environmental contributions.

Q5

Which of the following best illustrates Scarr & McCartney's concept of niche-picking?

  1. A. A parent enrolls their musically-inclined child in piano lessons.
  2. B. A naturally athletic teenager seeks out friends who play sports, joins multiple teams, and eventually pursues a college scholarship in athletics.
  3. C. A child inherits brown eyes from a brown-eyed parent.
  4. D. Twin siblings score similarly on an IQ test.
Show rationale

A. This is parent-driven environmental selection, not the child actively choosing their own environment.

B. CORRECT. The teen is actively seeking environments that match their genetically influenced traits (athleticism), and those chosen environments amplify the original tendency. This is niche-picking — heredity shapes development partly by shaping what environments you choose.

C. This is just direct genetic transmission of a phenotype — no environmental selection involved.

D. Similarity between twins reflects shared genes and/or shared environment, but says nothing about the active selection of environments.

Q6

The DiPietro et al. (2018) study found a relationship between fetal movement at 32 weeks gestational age and later childhood behavioral inhibition. What does this finding most directly support in developmental theory?

  1. A. Discontinuity between prenatal and postnatal behavior
  2. B. Continuity from prenatal life forward — core features of individual differences are preserved across substantial time and developmental change
  3. C. Environmental factors are more important than biological ones
  4. D. Behavioral inhibition is caused by fetal movement
Show rationale

A. The findings show continuity, not discontinuity. They suggest preserved individual differences across prenatal and childhood periods.

B. CORRECT. The study addresses one of human development's foundational questions: continuity vs. discontinuity. Finding that fetal activity at 32 weeks predicts later behavioral inhibition supports continuity — "core features of individual differences are preserved" (DiPietro et al.) across substantial time and developmental change. The result is meaningful precisely because it shows an early biological signal predicting later behavior — supporting the idea that some temperamental dimensions have prenatal roots.

C. The study doesn't address the relative weight of biology vs. environment; it shows a biological precursor predicting later behavior.

D. Correlation in a longitudinal study does not establish causation. Many third variables could explain the link.

Q7

A baby is born at 25 weeks gestational age. Compared to a full-term newborn, what specific challenges does this baby face beyond gestational immaturity, and what is the developmental term for this threshold?

  1. A. Age of viability (~22–28 weeks); challenges include immature lungs (respiratory distress), inability to regulate temperature (lacks insulating fat that develops in the 8th month), and elevated risk of infection, feeding problems, and developmental issues
  2. B. Age of conception; no special challenges
  3. C. Embryonic period; the baby has not yet developed organs
  4. D. Postnatal period; only nutritional support is needed
Show rationale

A. CORRECT. The age of viability (~22–28 weeks after conception, typically described as around 7 months) is when most body systems are developed enough to support life outside the uterus, given modern NICU support. Babies born at this threshold face respiratory distress (lungs aren't yet mature), temperature dysregulation (the insulating fat layer develops in the 8th month after conception), feeding difficulties, infection risk, and elevated risk of long-term developmental issues. Modern neonatal intensive care has substantially improved outcomes, but they're still meaningfully worse than for term babies.

B. Not a real term for this developmental milestone.

C. The embryonic period ends around 8 weeks after conception; by 25 weeks the baby is in the fetal period and has all major organs (though immature).

D. Substantial medical intervention beyond nutrition is required.

Q8

Pregnant women who drank carrot juice frequently in the final month of pregnancy had infants who later (at 5–6 months) preferred carrot-flavored cereal compared to infants whose mothers did not drink carrot juice. What does this finding most directly demonstrate about prenatal development?

  1. A. The fetus has no perceptual capacity in late pregnancy
  2. B. The fetus can learn — including taste preferences transmitted via amniotic fluid — and these prenatal experiences can shape postnatal preferences; this kind of learning is adaptive and forms part of how newborns recognize their environment and begin to learn language
  3. C. Newborns cannot taste
  4. D. The carrot effect is purely genetic
Show rationale

A. Opposite of what the evidence shows — the fetus has substantial perceptual capacity by late pregnancy.

B. CORRECT. The finding demonstrates prenatal learning — the fetus encodes specific stimuli (here, flavor compounds transmitted through amniotic fluid) and shows preferences for those stimuli postnatally. The same principle applies to auditory stimuli (newborns prefer their mother's voice over strangers' voices). This learning is adaptive — it helps newborns recognize the primary caregiver and prepares them for the linguistic environment they'll be born into. By 34 weeks the fetus can distinguish different speech sounds and even different languages.

C. Newborns clearly can taste; this is well-established.

D. The systematic effect of maternal diet on infant taste preference rules out a purely genetic explanation — the carrot juice mothers and control mothers wouldn't differ genetically by group assignment.

Q9

A 35-year-old patient at 11 weeks gestation is interested in early genetic screening for trisomies. She wants the earliest option with the lowest miscarriage risk. Which prenatal testing modality best fits her stated priorities?

  1. A. Amniocentesis at 16 weeks
  2. B. Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) — uses fetal DNA fragments in maternal blood; can be done from ~10 weeks; very high accuracy for major chromosomal trisomies (Down, trisomy 18, trisomy 13); no miscarriage risk because it's a blood draw from the mother
  3. C. Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) at 10 weeks
  4. D. Ultrasound only
Show rationale

A. Amniocentesis is highly accurate but later (~16 weeks) and carries small miscarriage risk (~0.1–0.3%).

B. CORRECT. NIPT matches both her priorities. It analyzes cell-free fetal DNA fragments present in maternal blood — available from ~10 weeks gestation, very high sensitivity and specificity for major trisomies (Down, 18, 13), and zero procedural miscarriage risk because it's just a maternal blood draw. Note: NIPT is a screening test — positive results require confirmation by amnio or CVS before action. NIPT is increasingly first-line, especially for advanced maternal age.

C. CVS provides a diagnostic answer earlier than amnio (~10 weeks) but carries slightly higher miscarriage risk than amnio. Doesn't match her low-risk priority.

D. Ultrasound alone doesn't directly detect chromosomal trisomies — it can show physical markers (nuchal translucency) suggestive of risk, but it's not a screening test for chromosomal disorders.

Q10

A pediatric nurse is performing a developmental assessment on a 6-year-old who has facial features suggesting fetal alcohol syndrome (smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, small palpebral fissures), growth restriction, and intellectual disability. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical understanding?

  1. A. The condition can be reversed with educational intervention alone
  2. B. The presentation is consistent with fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS), part of the FASD spectrum; there is no safe established level of prenatal alcohol exposure; the condition is permanent but supportive intervention (early intervention services, structured environment, IEP, behavioral support) improves functional outcomes
  3. C. The features will spontaneously resolve by puberty
  4. D. FASD is rare and unlikely without obvious maternal alcoholism
Show rationale

A. FASD includes permanent brain damage. Educational intervention helps, but cannot reverse the underlying neurodevelopmental impact.

B. CORRECT. The triad of facial features + growth restriction + CNS involvement defines full FAS. Important points: (1) No established safe level — CDC/ACOG/AAP all recommend complete abstinence. (2) Permanent: prenatal alcohol exposure causes structural and functional CNS damage. (3) Treatable but not curable: early intervention services (special education, behavioral support, structured environment, IEP) substantially improve outcomes. (4) FASD is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability in the US. (5) Often co-occurring: ADHD, anxiety, learning disabilities — diagnose and treat all.

C. Facial features may become less prominent but the underlying neurodevelopmental damage is permanent.

D. FASD is one of the most common preventable forms of developmental disability; many cases occur in mothers who drank moderately, not just those with alcoholism.

Q11

A NICU nurse admits a newborn at 28 weeks gestation weighing 950 g. Which classification applies, and what are the most appropriate priority clinical concerns?

  1. A. Low birth weight (LBW); monitor for jaundice
  2. B. Extremely low birth weight (ELBW, under 1,000 g) and very preterm; priority concerns include respiratory distress syndrome (surfactant deficiency), intraventricular hemorrhage, retinopathy of prematurity, temperature regulation, feeding difficulties (likely needs NG/OG feeds initially), and high risk of long-term neurodevelopmental disability requiring early intervention follow-up
  3. C. Very low birth weight only, no extra concerns beyond standard NICU monitoring
  4. D. Term, normal weight
Show rationale

A. LBW is under 2,500 g — this newborn is much smaller. Misses both severity and the specific risks.

B. CORRECT. At 950 g, this is extremely low birth weight (ELBW). Combined with 28 weeks gestation, the priority concerns are: respiratory (RDS due to surfactant deficiency, often requiring surfactant administration and ventilation), neurological (intraventricular hemorrhage especially in first 72 hours), visual (retinopathy of prematurity), thermal (poor thermoregulation, isolette/warmer essential), feeding (immature gut, NEC risk, typically TPN initially then advance), infection (high risk, sepsis workup if any signs), and long-term (high rate of neurodevelopmental disability; ensure early intervention referral at discharge).

C. Below 1,000 g is specifically ELBW (more severe than VLBW) and carries different risks profile.

D. Term is 37+ weeks; normal weight is 2,500–4,000 g. This newborn is far outside both.

Q12

A nursing student notices that infant mortality among non-Hispanic Black infants in the US is more than twice the rate of non-Hispanic White infants. The student asks what explains this. Which framing best reflects the evidence and the biopsychosocial framework?

  1. A. It's primarily a genetic difference between racial groups
  2. B. The disparity reflects cumulative effects of structural racism on healthcare access, prenatal care quality, chronic stress, and social determinants — not biology; the disparity persists even after controlling for income and education, indicating that race itself functions as a marker of differential treatment and exposure
  3. C. It can be fully explained by income differences
  4. D. It's a statistical artifact with no real-world consequences
Show rationale

A. No. Race is not a meaningful biological category for these outcomes; the disparity is driven by social and structural forces, not biology.

B. CORRECT. This is one of the clearest examples of sociocultural forces shaping biological outcomes. Evidence: (1) The disparity persists AFTER controlling for income and education — a college-educated Black mother has worse infant outcomes than a White mother who didn't finish high school. (2) Specific mechanisms include differential prenatal care access and quality, provider bias in pain management and concern-taking, chronic stress from racism (weathering hypothesis — accelerated biological aging), differential exposure to environmental hazards, and differential treatment in maternity care. (3) Implications for nursing practice: address bias in care delivery, advocate for systemic change, ensure equitable care quality, take maternal complaints seriously across all groups, screen for and address social determinants. This is core to the modern definition of health equity.

C. Income matters, but the disparity persists across all income levels — so income alone is not sufficient explanation.

D. The disparity is well-documented (CDC data over decades) and has very real consequences — thousands of preventable infant deaths annually.

Unit 1 · Early Development · Chapter 3

Tools for Exploring the World: Physical, Perceptual, & Motor Development

TL;DR · One-glance summary

In the first 2 years, the infant transforms from a reflex-driven newborn into a walking, perceiving, self-aware toddler. Growth follows cephalocaudal (head-down) and proximodistal (center-out) principles. Motor development moves through a regular sequence — though the timing varies widely. The senses are nearly all functional at birth (only vision needs more time). Around 18–24 months, children recognize themselves in mirrors — the first clear sign of self-awareness.

The newborn

  • Reflexes — rooting, sucking, Moro, Babinski
  • Behavioral states — alert, fussy, sleep (REM-heavy)
  • SIDS — back-sleeping, no soft bedding, no tobacco
  • Temperament — easy / difficult / slow-to-warm-up

Physical & motor

  • Cephalocaudal — head-to-toe
  • Proximodistal — center-outward
  • Locomotor sequence ~universal; timing varies
  • Gross motor (large muscles) before fine

Perception

  • Hearing & taste functional at birth
  • Vision develops fastest in first 6 months
  • Depth perception by 6 mo (visual cliff)
  • Habituation reveals what infants notice

Self-awareness

  • Mirror / rouge test ~18–24 mo
  • Self-recognition → "I" / "me" language
  • Theory of mind emerges ~age 4
  • False-belief task = the classic marker

3.1 · The Newborn

Newborns arrive with a set of pre-wired reflexes, predictable behavioral states, and a unique temperament. What looks like passive helplessness is actually a highly organized system for survival and learning.

The newborn — reflexes, states, temperament

Reflexes — pre-wired behavior

Reflexes are unlearned, involuntary responses to specific stimuli. They serve immediate survival functions (feeding, protection) and also lay the foundation for later voluntary motor behaviors. Most fade within the first 6 months as cortical control develops.

Rooting

Stroke the cheek → infant turns head toward the stroke. Function: helps locate the nipple for feeding.

Sucking

Anything touching the lips triggers rhythmic sucking. Function: feeding.

Moro (startle)

Sudden loud sound or loss of support → arms fling out then curl back. Function: probably protective; absence suggests neurological problem.

Babinski

Stroke the sole of the foot → toes fan out and curl. Function: diagnostic indicator only; persists past ~1 year suggests neural problem.

Stepping

Held upright with feet touching surface → makes stepping movements. Disappears around 2 months, reappears as voluntary walking.

Grasping (palmar)

Stroke palm → fingers curl into tight grip. Strong enough to support body weight briefly. Disappears ~3–4 mo.

Why are newborn reflexes more than just curiosities — what's their developmental role?
Two roles: (1) immediate survival (rooting and sucking enable feeding; Moro is protective); and (2) foundations for later voluntary behavior — the stepping reflex maps onto walking, the grasping reflex onto reaching. Absence of expected reflexes is also a neurological diagnostic: a missing Moro at birth signals brain or spinal cord problems.

Assessing the newborn — Apgar & NBAS

Apgar score

A quick assessment of the newborn's status right after birth (Apgar, 1953), focusing on the body systems needed to sustain life. Five vital signs — heart rate, breathing, muscle tone, reflexes (e.g., coughing), and skin tone — each scored 0, 1, or 2 (2 is optimal). The five scores are summed: 7+ = good physical condition; 4–6 = needs special attention and care; 3 or less = life-threatening, requiring emergency care.

NBAS (Neonatal Behavioral Assessment Scale)

A comprehensive newborn evaluation (Brazelton & Nugent, 1995), administered when a fuller picture is needed. 28 behavioral items (e.g., response to light, sound, and touch) plus 18 reflex items, used to evaluate four systems: autonomic (breathing, temperature regulation), motor (controlling movement and activity level), state (maintaining a state, e.g., staying alert or asleep), and social (interacting with people). Built on the view that newborns are remarkably competent — examiners actively work to bring out the baby's best performance, assisting if an infant doesn't at first succeed.

Why the NBAS matters beyond the bedside

It's used both clinically (evaluating individual babies) and in research. As a dependent variable, infants whose mothers were depressed during pregnancy score lower (Suri et al., 2014). As a predictor, higher NBAS scores have been linked to children being more empathic and less accident-prone at age 10 (Feldman, 2015).

Compare the Apgar and the NBAS as measures of a newborn's well-being. When is each used?
The Apgar is a fast, vital-signs screen done right at birth (heart rate, breathing, muscle tone, reflexes, skin tone; scored 0–2 each) to flag a baby who needs immediate or emergency care. The NBAS is a far more comprehensive behavioral assessment (28 behavioral + 18 reflex items across autonomic, motor, state, and social systems) used when a fuller evaluation is warranted, and it doubles as a research tool that can predict later development. In short: Apgar = quick survival screen at birth; NBAS = in-depth behavioral profile.

Behavioral states & sleep

Newborns cycle through predictable states: alert inactivity, waking activity, crying, and several sleep stages. Newborns sleep ~16 hours/day with much of that in REM — likely supporting brain development. Sleep gradually consolidates into longer night blocks across the first year.

SIDS — Sudden Infant Death Syndrome

Prevention works

SIDS = sudden, unexplained death of an apparently healthy infant under 1 year, typically during sleep. Peak incidence: 2–4 months. Cause unclear; likely a confluence of vulnerability + sleep environment + age-related triggers. Rates have dropped dramatically since the 1990s thanks to evidence-based prevention.

The "Back to Sleep" intervention

Public health campaign launched in 1994. Recommendations: place infants on their back to sleep, on a firm flat surface, with no soft bedding, pillows, or stuffed animals; keep the baby in the parents' room (not bed) for the first 6 months; avoid tobacco exposure prenatally and postnatally; breastfeed if possible. SIDS rates fell by ~50% within a decade of widespread adoption.

A new parent asks where their 2-month-old should sleep. What are the four evidence-based recommendations to minimize SIDS risk?
(1) On the back, never on the stomach or side. (2) On a firm flat surface (crib or bassinet) — no soft mattresses, couches, or adult beds. (3) Nothing else in the crib — no pillows, blankets, bumpers, or stuffed animals. (4) Room-sharing without bed-sharing for the first 6 months. Bonus: avoid tobacco exposure and breastfeed if possible.

Temperament — early personality

Temperament is the biologically-rooted style of emotional reactivity, attention, and activity that emerges in infancy. Thomas & Chess's classic three categories:

Easy (~40%)

Regular sleep / feeding rhythms, generally positive mood, adapts well to change, approaches novelty.

Difficult (~10%)

Irregular rhythms, intense negative reactions, slow to adapt, withdraws from novelty. Higher risk for later adjustment issues, especially without responsive caregiving.

Slow-to-warm-up (~15%)

Mild reactions, low activity, withdraws from novelty initially but adapts gradually with repeated exposure.

The remaining ~35% don't fit neatly into a category. Temperament shows moderate stability into adulthood, especially the dimension of negative reactivity — but environment substantially shapes outcomes.

A pediatric nurse is counseling parents of a "difficult" temperament infant who are exhausted and starting to feel like "bad parents." Using Thomas & Chess's framework, what concept best guides counseling?
The key concept is goodness of fit — Thomas & Chess's principle that what matters most is not the temperament itself, but the match between child temperament and parenting style/environmental demands. Difficult temperament (intense reactions, irregular biological rhythms, slow adaptation, negative mood, withdrawal from novelty) is not the parents' fault and is largely biological. Counseling approach: (1) Normalize: ~10% of infants have this pattern. (2) Adapt to the child's tempo rather than fighting it — predictable routines, gradual transitions, anticipating reactions, more soothing. (3) Don't blame yourself — children with difficult temperament can have outstanding outcomes with the right environment. (4) Provide concrete strategies: longer warm-up periods, gentle introductions to new situations, consistent caregivers, environment matched to sensitivity. (5) The same temperament can produce a thriving adult OR a struggling one depending on fit — parents are not destined to have a difficult child. The clinical reframe: this isn't "bad parenting" — it's higher demand parenting requiring different strategies.

3.2 · Physical Development

The body and brain grow on predictable schedules and according to predictable spatial principles.

Physical & brain development

Growth principles

Two directional rules

Two principles describe the direction of physical and motor development:

Cephalocaudal

Head-to-toe. Head controls develop before trunk controls; trunk before legs. Infants can hold up their head before sitting; sit before walking.

Proximodistal

Center-outward. Trunk and shoulders before arms; arms before hands; hands before fingers. The pincer grip (thumb + forefinger) comes last.

A 4-month-old can roll over and hold their head up steadily but can't sit unsupported. A 6-month-old can sit and reach precisely with the whole hand, but can't pinch a Cheerio with thumb and forefinger. Which two principles are these examples of?
