Cephalocaudal (head-to-toe): the 4-month-old has head control before sitting (which requires trunk control). Proximodistal (center-outward): the 6-month-old has whole-arm reaching before fine finger control. The pincer grip — last to develop — typically appears around 9–10 months.

Brain development

The newborn brain weighs ~25% of its adult weight; by age 2 it's ~75%. Three processes drive this growth:

  • Synaptogenesis — explosive creation of synaptic connections, peaking in the first 2 years. The infant brain produces vastly more synapses than the adult brain.
  • Synaptic pruning — unused connections are eliminated; used ones are reinforced. This is "use it or lose it" wiring driven by the infant's experiences.
  • Myelination — fatty sheath that wraps neurons, dramatically speeding signal conduction. Begins prenatally and continues into the 20s, proceeding cephalocaudally.

The brain shows experience-expectant plasticity: it's pre-wired to expect typical environmental inputs (faces, language, depth cues) within particular windows. Severe deprivation during these windows can cause lasting deficits.

A nurse educator explains brain development to parents: "Some kinds of brain growth need typical experiences to happen normally; other kinds depend on specific experiences and can happen anytime." Match each example to the right type: (A) Romanian orphans deprived of social interaction in infancy showed lasting cognitive deficits. (B) A 60-year-old learns to play piano and develops new motor and auditory cortex specializations.
(A) = Experience-EXPECTANT growth. The infant brain is built to expect certain typical environmental inputs — faces, voices, language, depth cues, social interaction. When these don't arrive during the critical window, the relevant brain circuits don't develop properly and deficits can be lasting. Romanian orphanage studies (BEIP) are the clearest example: extreme deprivation during infancy produced lasting cognitive/behavioral deficits even after foster placement. Critical/sensitive periods exist for: vision (first few months), language phonology (first year), attachment (first 2 years), and others. (B) = Experience-DEPENDENT growth. The brain continues to physically reorganize in response to specific learning throughout life. Adults who learn an instrument, a new language, or a complex motor skill show measurable changes in relevant brain regions. This is the basis of neuroplasticity across the lifespan — important counter to the myth that adult brains can't change. Clinical implications: (1) Early intervention in infancy capitalizes on experience-expectant windows. (2) Adults can still gain skills — never too late, but earlier is easier for many domains. (3) Post-stroke rehabilitation relies on experience-dependent plasticity.

Diverse Perspectives — Promoting breastfeeding

Why breastfeeding matters

Human milk contains the proper carbohydrates, fats, protein, vitamins, and minerals for infants — uniquely matched to species and developmental stage. Beyond nutrition, breastfeeding has documented health benefits compared to formula:

Short-term benefits

Less illness (mother's antibodies transferred through milk), less infant mortality from diarrhea (especially in low-resource settings where formula carries contamination risk), lower SIDS rates, fewer ear infections, fewer respiratory infections.

Long-term benefits

Lower obesity and diabetes risk (Victora et al., 2016). Easier transition to solid foods (breastfed babies have already been exposed to flavor variation through changes in breast milk reflecting mother's diet).

For mothers

Reduced postpartum bleeding via oxytocin release, faster return to pre-pregnancy weight, lower breast and ovarian cancer risk, lower risk of postpartum depression (Gagliardi, 2005), reduced costs.

Recommended duration

WHO and UNICEF: breastfeeding until age 2 or beyond, with exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months. UN Convention on the Rights of the Child establishes countries are legally obligated to promote breastfeeding.

The reality — and the equity gap

Worldwide, only about one-third of babies are exclusively breastfed for the first 5 months, and only about 35% receive any breastfeeding at 12 months (CDC, 2020b). In the US, well-educated mothers are more than twice as likely to breastfeed as less-educated mothers (Cohen et al., 2018). This is the inverse of what we'd want — those with fewer resources benefit most from breastfeeding but are least likely to do it.

Why the gap exists:

  • Workplace barriers — many mothers return to work soon after birth. Breastfeeding at work requires breaks, private space, time to nurse or pump. Many workers (especially low-wage workers) have neither. Workplace pumping requirements exist in some US states but enforcement is uneven.
  • Formula industry marketing — breast-milk substitutes are a major global industry, marketing formula as a lifestyle choice (not a medical or nutritional one) and highlighting breastfeeding difficulties. WHO has a code restricting infant formula marketing; compliance is uneven.
  • Lack of lactation support — breastfeeding has a steep learning curve. Without lactation counseling, mothers struggling early often give up. Insurance coverage for lactation consultants is mandated under ACA but access varies.
  • Cultural and family expectations — in some communities, public breastfeeding faces stigma; in others, formula represents modernity or affluence.
  • Medical contraindications — certain maternal medications, HIV (in resource-rich settings), galactosemia, active TB make breastfeeding inadvisable.

If we achieved breastfeeding guidelines worldwide

Estimated reduction in childhood mortality: more than 800,000 deaths per year (Victora et al., 2016). This is one of the highest-impact public health interventions available — and one of the most under-resourced.

What works — evidence-based interventions

Effective programs (Rollins et al., 2016) cluster into three categories:

  • Education — teaching mothers the benefits and the practical mechanics of breastfeeding, prenatally and postpartum.
  • Support — lactation consultants (IBCLCs), peer counselors, support groups, telehealth. La Leche League and WIC peer counseling are evidence-based US programs.
  • Structural change — lactation centers and breaks at work, paid parental leave (FMLA provides only unpaid leave), restrictions on formula marketing, accessible lactation accommodations in public spaces, hospital practices (Baby-Friendly Hospital Initiative).

What the textbook is asking: (1) How can individuals, communities, and nations promote breastfeeding given its powerful benefits? (2) How can we ensure babies of less-educated mothers also benefit? (3) How do the challenges differ internationally — and what lessons can we learn from countries that have closed the breastfeeding equity gap (some Nordic countries have near-universal breastfeeding initiation)?

For nursing practice

(1) Don't moralize — formula-feeding is sometimes the right choice given individual circumstances. (2) Provide accurate evidence-based information without pressure. (3) Connect to local lactation support. (4) Advocate for workplace and policy changes that make breastfeeding genuinely possible. (5) Address shame and guilt for mothers who can't or don't breastfeed. (6) Recognize the equity dimension — this isn't just about individual choice.

A low-income mother of a 2-month-old tells the home-visit nurse she's been formula-feeding because she'll be returning to her hourly retail job in 2 weeks and "doesn't see how breastfeeding could work". She mentions feeling guilty after reading about breastfeeding benefits. What's the nurse's most appropriate evidence-based response?
The nurse's job is to support the mother's wellbeing and the baby's health, not to convert her to breastfeeding. Step 1: Address the guilt explicitly: "You're being a thoughtful mom by even thinking about this. Feeding choices are bigger than just nutrition — they have to fit your life. Let's talk about what's possible for your situation, not about what you should feel guilty for not doing." Step 2: Provide accurate balanced information: (a) Breastfeeding has measurable health benefits — less illness short-term, lower obesity/diabetes long-term, less SIDS. (b) Formula is safe and nutritionally adequate when used correctly. (c) Babies thrive on either when parented well. (d) The dose matters: any breastfeeding has benefits; exclusive is best, but combined feeding (some breast, some formula) is much better than none. Step 3: Problem-solve workplace barriers: (1) Federal Break Time for Nursing Mothers law (under FLSA, now PUMP Act 2022) requires most employers to provide reasonable break time and a private space (NOT a bathroom) to express breast milk for one year after birth. Many low-wage workers don't know this. (2) WIC peer counselors are free for eligible families and can troubleshoot work-related challenges. (3) Pumping at work is hard but doable — could mother return to combination feeding (breastfeed at home, formula at daycare)? (4) Could she breastfeed for the next 2 weeks to give baby antibodies and the maternal-bonding hormones, even if formula-feeding long-term? Step 4: Address equity: low-income workers face real, systemic barriers — irregular schedules, less control over breaks, less private space, fewer pumping options. This isn't individual failure. Step 5: Connect to resources: WIC (peer counselors, free formula if needed, breastfeeding-specific WIC food packages, breast pumps), local La Leche League, hospital lactation consultants (often covered by insurance under ACA), Baby Cafe (free drop-in support). Step 6: Follow-up plan: don't lecture and disappear. Schedule a follow-up visit or call to see how feeding is going. Step 7: Document and advocate systemically: this case is an example of why workplace policy needs to change. Nursing advocacy at the policy level matters as much as individual counseling. What NOT to say: "You really should try to breastfeed" (moralizing); "Breastfeeding is best" (heard as judgment by formula-feeding mothers); "It's your choice" (false neutrality — there are real barriers); "You can probably make it work if you try" (minimizing actual constraints). Equity principle: the mother who needs breastfeeding support most often gets it least. The nurse's role is to redistribute that support.

3.3 · Moving and Grasping — Early Motor Skills

Motor skills divide into gross (large muscle, whole-body) and fine (small, precise). Both follow predictable sequences, but timing varies widely across individuals and cultures.

Motor development

Gross motor milestones

The classical sequence — sequence is more universal than the timing:

~3 months

Rolls from stomach to back

~6 months

Sits without support

~8–10 months

Crawls (not universal — some skip directly to walking)

~9 months

Stands while holding furniture

~12 months

Walks independently

~24 months

Walks down stairs, kicks ball, runs steadily

A parent worries because their 14-month-old isn't walking yet — they've heard average is 12 months. Is this developmental delay?
Not necessarily. The sequence of motor milestones is highly universal, but timing varies widely — most pediatricians consider independent walking by ~17 months still within typical range. Some cultures even discourage early walking (e.g., the Kipsigis carry infants for protection from predators). What matters more is the overall trajectory and whether the child is acquiring later milestones; isolated timing of one milestone is rarely diagnostic.

Fine motor — reaching, grasping, manipulating

Fine motor refinement is dramatic over the first year. Newborns swipe at objects with little accuracy. By 4 months infants reach fairly accurately. Grasping evolves from whole-hand (palmar grasp, ~4–6 mo) to the precise pincer grip (~9–10 mo) — a uniquely human capacity that enables fine manipulation, tool use, and writing.

Dynamic systems perspective

Modern researchers reject the old "maturation alone" view of motor development. The dynamic systems approach sees walking (or any motor skill) as the emergent product of multiple interacting components: muscle strength, balance, body proportions, motivation, and environmental opportunities. Learning to walk isn't unlocked by maturation alone — it's a solution the infant assembles by exploring what their body can do.

Spotlight on Research

Benefits of training babies to grasp — the "sticky mittens" study

Libertus, K., Joh, A., & Needham, A. (2010, 2016)

What was the aim?

By 4–6 months, babies can reach and grasp objects, which lets them explore the world and fosters cognitive, social, and linguistic skills. Before 4 months, they have little success grasping. The question: If you could help young infants grasp objects sooner, would they benefit from the extra exploration?

How did they measure it?

The researchers created "sticky" mittens covered with Velcro, paired with Velcro-covered toys. Wearing the mittens, any contact let a 3-month-old "grasp" a toy. For 2 weeks, babies wore the mittens daily for 10 minutes. The dependent variable: percentage of time babies tried to grasp novel toys (a rattle at 3 months; a bead maze at 15 months).

What was the design?

Experimental with both longitudinal and cross-sectional components. Conditions: sticky mittens (Velcro), regular mittens (no Velcro — parents pressed toys against the mittens to simulate contact), or no training. Some babies were tested as 3-month-olds and again at 15 months.

Were there ethical concerns?

None — there was no obvious harm in wearing the mittens or playing with toys.

What were the results?

At 3 months, sticky-mitten babies were more likely than other groups to grasp objects. The striking finding: at 15 months — a full year after the training ended — the same babies were STILL more likely to grasp objects. Wearing regular (non-Velcro) mittens did not produce the effect, ruling out the simple "more contact" explanation.

What did the investigators conclude?

Training babies to reach had immediate AND long-term benefits, likely through two pathways: (1) direct — babies learned that reaching and grasping have payoffs and explored objects more; (2) indirect — when parents see babies interested in objects, they engage them in more object-oriented play, feeding further motor and cognitive development. This is an important demonstration that early motor experiences can have lasting consequences. Notably, this was an experimental design in infant development — relatively rare, since most infant research is correlational because experiences can't usually be randomly assigned — which makes its demonstration of cascading developmental effects especially strong.

What would strengthen the conclusions?

Following participants beyond 15 months to see whether the effect extends further. Testing whether the training affects related domains — like learning object names or parent-child play involving objects. Including children from more diverse backgrounds.

Why this matters clinically

Early intervention for infants with motor delays may matter more than previously assumed, because motor delays can cascade into cognitive and social delays. Physical and occupational therapy for infants isn't only about motor outcomes — it scaffolds the whole developmental sequence. And toy choice and the encouragement of object exploration in infancy are developmentally meaningful, not "just play."

A pregnant woman observes via ultrasound that her fetus consistently sucks the right thumb and turns the head to the right. Is handedness already determined? What does research suggest about the heredity-environment balance for handedness?
Handedness shows strong prenatal indicators — most fetuses are "emergent right-handers", preferring the right thumb, right arm, and rightward head turns. But handedness is not fully determined at birth. Both heredity and environment contribute: at least 40 different genes may play a role, AND cultural and environmental forces shape the final outcome. Historical evidence: in cultures (and earlier eras) that punished or discouraged left-handedness, left-handed children were rare. As that practice has been abandoned in many developed nations, the percentage of left-handed children has risen — a striking real-world demonstration that handedness is not purely biological. Modern handedness is best understood as a biologically constrained, environmentally shaped phenotype.
A pediatrician is asked whether a 13-month-old who isn't yet walking should be "concerned" — the parent has heard motor milestones are biologically programmed and worries the baby is delayed. What does the dynamic systems approach to motor development suggest?
The pure "biological maturation unlocks milestones on schedule" view is largely rejected by modern motor-development research. The dynamic systems approach sees walking as the emergent product of multiple interacting components: muscle strength, balance and postural control, body proportions (heavy heads make it harder), motivation, and the physical / cultural environment the baby has had a chance to explore in. Children walk on a range of timelines (typically 9–18 months) without it predicting later motor or cognitive ability. The pediatrician should ask: is the baby pulling to stand, cruising along furniture, motivated to move? If yes, walking is on the way. If the baby shows other concerning patterns (significant asymmetry, regression of skills, very low tone), then evaluation is warranted — but 13 months alone is well within normal variation.
The Libertus et al. sticky-mittens study found that 3-month-olds trained to grasp showed enhanced grasping at 15 months — a year after training ended. What broader principle about development does this finding illustrate?
The finding illustrates that early motor experience can have lasting, cascading effects on development well beyond the specific skill trained. The mechanism is likely indirect: babies who learn to grasp earlier explore objects more, which provides richer perceptual, cognitive, and social learning opportunities. Each new motor capacity opens new exploration possibilities, which compound. This is one example of a more general principle in developmental psychology — development cascades. Early changes in one domain (motor) cascade into other domains (cognitive, social, linguistic). It's also why early intervention programs that target specific skills can have outsized long-term effects, far larger than the immediate skill gain alone would suggest.

Test Yourself · Section 3.3

  1. According to which approach does motor development involve many distinct skills that are organized and reorganized over time depending on task demands?
  2. Name two skills important in learning to walk besides simply moving the legs.
  3. By age 1, most infants use one hand to steady a toy and the other to manipulate it. Which hand typically does which?
  4. What evidence suggests handedness is influenced by both heredity and culture?
  5. How does the sticky-mittens study illustrate the bidirectional influence between motor skill and other developmental domains?

3.4 · Coming to Know the World — Perception

Newborns can hear, taste, smell, and feel touch and pain very well. Vision is the one sense that needs significant postnatal development.

Perception & sensation

Senses at birth

Hearing

Functional before birth — fetuses respond to sounds. Newborns prefer their mother's voice and the language they heard prenatally.

Taste & smell

Both functional at birth. Newborns prefer sweet, recognize their mother's scent, and react aversively to bitter and sour.

Touch & pain

Fully functional at birth. Modern medicine takes seriously that newborns experience pain and require appropriate analgesia.

Vision

Newborn acuity ~20/400. Most rapid development in the first 6 months. By ~1 year, acuity approaches adult levels. Color vision functional by ~3 months.

How we study what infants perceive

Habituation & preference

Infants can't tell us what they see, so researchers infer perception from behavior:

  • Preferential looking — show two stimuli; if the infant consistently looks longer at one, they can tell them apart.
  • Habituation — infant stops responding to a repeated stimulus. If a new stimulus brings the response back (dishabituation), they perceived the difference.
  • Violation-of-expectation — show events that should or shouldn't surprise an infant; longer looking at the "shouldn't happen" event implies the infant has a representation of what should.

Depth perception & the visual cliff

Gibson & Walk's visual cliff is the classic depth- perception experiment. A table with a checkerboard surface drops off visually at the midpoint, but is actually covered by clear safety glass. Infants who have begun crawling (~6 months) refuse to cross the deep side — even when their caregiver encourages them. They perceive the drop and modify behavior accordingly.

The finding also illustrates how perception and motor experience develop together: crawling infants show the cliff response, but pre-crawling infants of the same age do not. Locomotor experience helps tune depth perception, not just the other way around.

What does the visual cliff experiment tell us about depth perception in infancy, and why is this finding interesting beyond the basic perceptual fact?
It shows that infants can perceive depth by ~6 months and modify behavior accordingly (refusing to cross the deep side). The deeper finding: depth perception is tuned by locomotor experience — crawling infants show the response, pre-crawling infants of the same age don't. Perception and motor development are mutually shaping, not separate tracks.
Infants develop depth perception via three categories of cues, each emerging at different ages. Match each cue to its onset: (A) Movement-based (kinetic) cues — visual expansion, motion parallax; (B) Two-eye (binocular) cues — binocular disparity; (C) Picture-based (pictorial) cues — linear perspective, texture gradient.
(A) Kinetic cues = EARLIEST: emerge by ~1–3 months. The infant perceives depth from motion: an object getting larger as it approaches (visual expansion → triggers a reflex blink/defensive reaction), and the fact that closer objects appear to move faster than distant ones when the head moves (motion parallax). These work even with one eye. (B) Binocular cues = MIDDLE: emerge by ~4 months. Each eye sees a slightly different image (binocular disparity); the brain computes depth from the difference. Requires coordinated eye movement (vergence) which is developing during this period. Strabismus during this window can interfere with binocular depth perception. (C) Pictorial cues = LATEST: emerge by ~7 months. Static cues that work in pictures — linear perspective (parallel lines converging), texture gradient (texture appearing finer with distance), relative size, interposition (overlapping objects). These require interpreting 2D images as 3D scenes — more cognitively sophisticated. Why it matters clinically: vision development requires both eyes working together during the critical window; early treatment of strabismus or amblyopia preserves binocular depth perception.

Integrating sensory information — intermodal perception

Most infant experiences are multimedia events

Discussing senses separately is useful but artificial — most real experience is multimodal. While breastfeeding, an infant sees mother's face, smells her skin, feels her warmth, hears her voice, tastes the milk. A rattle stimulates vision, hearing, and touch simultaneously. Temporal information (duration, tempo, rhythm) is available across senses — the rhythm of clapping can be seen or heard; surface texture can be seen or felt.

Infants readily integrate this multimodal information:

  • Tactile-visual matching: infants can visually recognize an object they have only touched before (Bremner & Spence, 2017).
  • Audio-visual matching: babies look longer at events where an object's motion matches its sound (higher pitch as it rises, lower as it falls) than when they don't match (Walker et al., 2010).
  • Temporal matching: infants detect when visual and auditory streams share rhythm or duration (Lewkowicz, 2000).
  • Embodied responses: infants move their bodies in time with the beat of music (Hannon et al., 2018).

Intersensory redundancy theory (Bahrick & Lickliter)

Traditionally, coordinating information from different senses was thought to be harder for infants than for adults. A newer view: infants' sensory integration is actually easier in some ways because the brain regions devoted to processing each sense aren't yet fully specialized. In infants, regions that in adults respond only to visual stimuli also respond to auditory input (Spector & Maurer, 2009).

Intersensory redundancy (Bahrick & Lickliter, 2002, 2012): infants' sensory systems are particularly attuned to information presented simultaneously to multiple senses. The rule infants seem to follow: "Any information that arrives through multiple senses must be important, so pay attention to it." This focuses infant attention on:

  • What's redundant across senses (e.g., the rhythm of mother's claps, both seen and heard)
  • While ignoring single-sense information (e.g., color of nail polish; humming melody)

This explains how infants efficiently learn the world without being overwhelmed by every detail — the brain prioritizes multimodal signals that are most likely to be developmentally meaningful.

Clinical implications

(1) When teaching infants (e.g., word learning, social interaction), multimodal presentation works better than unimodal — show + say + touch beats any single channel. (2) In NICU/hospital settings, reducing overwhelming unimodal stimuli (bright lights without context, loud beeping without explanation) and emphasizing meaningful multimodal experiences (parent's voice + touch + face) supports development. (3) Sensory integration disorders may involve disruption to this prioritization system; assessment and OT intervention target this. (4) Autism spectrum disorder sometimes involves atypical sensory integration — affected children may not preferentially attend to multimodal social cues the way typically developing children do.

3.5 · Becoming Self-Aware

In the second year, children begin to recognize themselves as distinct beings. By preschool age, they realize others have minds with beliefs that can differ from reality.

Self-awareness emerges

The mirror test (rouge test)

Self-recognition

The classic test: a researcher puts a small dot of rouge or sticker on a child's nose without their noticing, then places them in front of a mirror. Before ~18 months, children stare at the image as if at another child. Around 18–24 months, they reach to touch the mark on their own face, demonstrating that they recognize the mirror image as themselves. Within months of passing the mirror test, toddlers begin using "I" and "me" in speech and showing self-conscious emotions like embarrassment.

Theory of mind

Understanding other minds

Theory of mind = the understanding that others have minds with beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions — and that those mental states may differ from reality. It's foundational for empathy, cooperation, deception, teaching, and most social cognition.

The false-belief task — the classic marker

Sally hides a marble in a basket and leaves. While she's gone, Anne moves the marble to a box. When Sally returns, where will she look? 3-year-olds typically say "in the box" — they can't separate their own knowledge from Sally's. 4-year-olds typically answer "in the basket" — they understand Sally has a false belief based on her outdated knowledge. The shift around age 4 marks mature theory of mind.

Theory of mind continues to develop through middle childhood as kids learn that beliefs about beliefs ("second-order false belief") and sarcasm, irony, and white lies all involve more sophisticated mental-state reasoning.

A pediatric nurse is preparing a 3-year-old for a venipuncture. The child has not yet acquired theory of mind / false-belief understanding. Which communication approach is developmentally appropriate, and which to avoid?
At 3, the child likely has not yet acquired full theory of mind — they cannot easily reason about how their experience differs from yours, or that you don't already know what they know/feel. Approaches to USE: (1) Concrete, immediate language — "I will put a small Band-Aid on your arm and then squeeze." Tell them what you'll do as you do it. (2) Use visual or tactile demos with a doll first if possible. (3) Let them hold a comfort item; offer choices that are real ("right arm or left?"). (4) Honest, simple words — "this might pinch, it will be over quickly." (5) Praise their bravery after, not before. Approaches to AVOID: (1) Abstract reasoning ("think about it being over fast") — they can't easily project to a different mental state. (2) Saying "you'll be brave" as if they'll regulate themselves with that knowledge. (3) Saying "this won't hurt" if it will — they'll learn distrust quickly. (4) Negotiating or asking "do you want this shot?" — they can't refuse what's medically needed and the question is misleading. (5) Talking about them with parents without acknowledging the child — they're listening but can't fully understand others' mental states yet. For older children (5+): theory of mind is in place — you can ask them about their feelings, prepare them in advance, validate their fears, and engage them as partners in their own care.

3.6 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter beyond the Spotlight.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Alexander Thomas & Stella Chess — New York Longitudinal Study (1956+). Established temperament as biologically rooted and identified three classic types: easy, difficult, and slow-to-warm.
  • Mary Rothbart — Modern revision of temperament theory. Three dimensions: surgency/extraversion, negative affect, and effortful control. Effortful control predicts long-term adjustment.
  • Esther Thelen — Pioneer of dynamic systems theory in motor development. Reconceived milestones as emergent products of body, brain, and environment — not pre-programmed schedules. Famous "walking reflex" demonstrations.
  • Eleanor Gibson & Richard Walk — Designed the visual cliff (1960). Demonstrated depth perception in crawling infants. One of the most replicated findings in infant perception.
  • Gordon Gallup, Jr. — Developed the mirror self-recognition test (rouge test) in the 1970s. Showed that some great apes recognize themselves; human infants pass it around 18–24 months.
  • Andrew Meltzoff & M. Keith Moore — Demonstrated neonatal imitation — newborns within hours of birth can imitate facial expressions, suggesting a built-in cross-modal mapping.
  • Jerome Kagan — Studied behavioral inhibition in toddlers (timid vs bold). Showed substantial stability into adulthood and ties to anxiety risk.

Specific studies cited

  • NICHD SECC (Study of Early Child Care): Large longitudinal study of child care quality and child outcomes; informed quality standards in US child care.
  • Karen Adolph's "sticky mittens" studies: Pre-reaching infants given mittens with velcro to grasp objects developed reaching skills earlier than control infants — demonstrates motor-cognitive bidirectional effects.
  • Romanian orphanage studies (Bucharest Early Intervention Project): Profound deprivation produces lasting cognitive/behavioral deficits; foster placement before 24 months significantly improves outcomes — supports experience-expectant brain development.
  • Back to Sleep / Safe to Sleep (AAP, 1992+): National campaign cut US SIDS rates by more than 50%. A clear example of public-health prevention rooted in developmental research.
  • Visual cliff studies extended (Gibson, Campos, others): Heart rate and avoidance studies show infants perceive depth from a few months but don't fear it until they can locomote — fear of heights linked to mobility experience, not just maturation.

3.7 · Key Terms & Definitions

The 50 vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter.

Glossary by category

Newborn states & temperament (12 terms)

  • Reflexes — Unlearned, involuntary responses to specific stimuli; serve immediate survival and foundation for voluntary skills.
  • Alert inactivity — Calm, awake, eyes open, attending to environment. Optimal state for parent-infant interaction.
  • Waking activity — Awake with frequent movement; may transition to crying.
  • Crying — A signal that something is wrong; coordinated motor output that adults attend to.
  • Sleeping — Newborns sleep ~16–18 hours/day distributed across multiple naps.
  • Basic cry — Rhythmic pattern of crying with brief silences; signals general distress.
  • Mad cry — More intense than basic cry; signals anger or frustration.
  • Pain cry — Sudden, loud, with extended silence; signals injury or significant pain.
  • Irregular or rapid-eye-movement (REM) sleep — Active sleep with eye movements and irregular breathing; associated with dreaming and brain development.
  • Regular or non-REM sleep — Quiet sleep with regular breathing; deeper restorative sleep.
  • Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) — Unexplained death of an apparently healthy infant under 1 year, typically during sleep. Reduced dramatically by Back to Sleep campaigns.
  • Temperament — Consistent, biologically-rooted behavioral and emotional styles present from infancy.

Physical & brain development (17 terms)

  • Malnourished — Inadequate nutrient intake to support healthy development; can affect brain development if chronic.
  • Neuron — Basic unit of the nervous system; brain has ~86 billion at birth.
  • Cell body — Main part of the neuron containing nucleus and basic life machinery.
  • Dendrite — Branching extensions of the neuron that receive signals from other neurons.
  • Axon — Long fiber of the neuron that transmits signals to other neurons.
  • Terminal buttons — Knob-like structures at axon ends that release neurotransmitters.
  • Neurotransmitters — Chemical messengers released across synapses to communicate between neurons.
  • Cerebral cortex — Wrinkled outer layer of the brain; site of higher mental processes.
  • Hemispheres — Left and right halves of the cerebral cortex; somewhat specialized but heavily integrated.
  • Corpus callosum — Thick band of fibers connecting the two hemispheres.
  • Neural plate — Embryonic structure that folds to form the neural tube, which becomes the nervous system.
  • Myelin — Fatty insulating sheath around axons that dramatically speeds neural transmission. Myelination continues into the 20s.
  • Synaptic pruning — The brain's elimination of unused synapses; refines neural networks based on experience.
  • Electroencephalography (EEG) — Measures brain electrical activity through scalp electrodes; good temporal resolution.
  • Functional MRI (fMRI) — Images brain activity through blood-flow changes; good spatial resolution.
  • Experience-expectant growth — Brain development that expects typical environmental input (vision, language); deprivation during critical periods causes lasting deficits.
  • Experience-dependent growth — Brain development driven by specific individual experiences (learning to play piano, learning a second language); flexible throughout life.

Motor development (8 terms)

  • Motor skills — Voluntary movements of the body or its parts.
  • Locomote — Move from one place to another (rolling, crawling, walking, running).
  • Fine motor skills — Small, precise movements of the hands and fingers.
  • Toddling — The wide-stance, swaying gait of early walkers (around 12–15 months).
  • Toddler — A child who has begun to walk (typically 12–24 months).
  • Dynamic systems theory — Esther Thelen's view that motor skills emerge from the interaction of body capabilities, brain, environment, and the child's goals — not from a pre-programmed schedule.
  • Differentiation — Mastery of distinct, component movements (moving fingers independently).
  • Integration — Combining differentiated movements into smooth coordinated action (using fingers together to grasp).

Perception & self (13 terms)

  • Perception — The brain's interpretation of sensory information into meaningful experience.
  • Visual acuity — Sharpness of vision; newborns ~20/400; adult level by 1 year.
  • Cones — Retinal cells that respond to color; require postnatal development to become fully functional.
  • Visual cliff — Apparatus (Gibson & Walk, 1960) used to test infant depth perception.
  • Kinetic cues — Movement-based depth cues; among the earliest depth cues to develop.
  • Visual expansion — The increase in image size as an object approaches; cue for looming.
  • Motion parallax — Closer objects appear to move faster than distant objects when the viewer moves; depth cue.
  • Binocular disparity — Slight differences between images from the two eyes provide depth information; emerges around 4 months.
  • Pictorial cues — Static depth cues seen in pictures (linear perspective, texture gradient).
  • Linear perspective — Parallel lines appear to converge in the distance; pictorial depth cue.
  • Texture gradient — Surface texture appears finer with distance; pictorial depth cue.
  • Intersensory redundancy — When information is presented in multiple modalities simultaneously (sight + sound), infants learn faster.
  • Theory of mind — Understanding that others have mental states (beliefs, desires, intentions) that may differ from one's own and from reality. Major achievement around age 4.

Clinical & supplementary terms (15 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • Neuroplasticity — The brain's capacity to reorganize neural connections in response to experience, learning, or injury. Greatest in early childhood but continues throughout life. Underlies recovery from brain injury, skill acquisition, and developmental adaptation.
  • Habituation — Decreased response to a stimulus after repeated presentation; a fundamental learning mechanism present even in newborns. Used in research to study infant perception — when an infant looks longer at a new stimulus compared to a familiar one, this is evidence they can perceive the difference.
  • Dishabituation — Renewed attention or response to a stimulus after it has changed in some way. The flip side of habituation — together they form a research paradigm widely used to study what infants can perceive, remember, and categorize.
  • Preferential looking — Research method based on infants looking longer at preferred stimuli. By measuring which of two side-by-side images an infant looks at longer, researchers infer what infants can see, distinguish, or recognize even before they can speak.
  • Kangaroo care (skin-to-skin) — Holding a newborn (especially preterm or low birth weight) skin-to-skin against a caregiver's bare chest. Evidence base shows improvements in thermoregulation, breastfeeding initiation, weight gain, neurodevelopmental outcomes, parent-infant bonding, and stress markers. WHO recommends as standard care for preterm infants.
  • Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) / Safe sleep ABCs — Unexpected death of an apparently healthy infant under 1 year, usually during sleep, with no identifiable cause after thorough investigation. Risk-reduction guidelines (American Academy of Pediatrics) — the ABCs: Alone (no co-sleeping), on Back (supine), in a bare Crib (firm flat mattress, no soft objects, no bumpers, no blankets). Also: room-sharing without bed-sharing, breastfeeding, pacifier at sleep, avoiding smoke exposure and overheating.
  • Sticky mittens paradigm (Libertus, Joh, & Needham) — Experimental method where 3-month-old infants wear Velcro-covered mittens that grasp Velcro-covered toys, giving them grasping experience before they can grasp naturally. Demonstrates cascading developmental effects: training at 3 months still affects reaching behavior at 15 months. Important example of motor scaffolding cognition.
  • Intersensory redundancy theory (Bahrick & Lickliter) — Theory that infants preferentially attend to information presented simultaneously across multiple senses (e.g., the rhythm of clapping seen AND heard). Multimodal redundancy signals to the developing brain "pay attention — this is meaningful." Foundation for why multimodal teaching outperforms single-channel.
  • Intermodal / cross-modal perception — Infant ability to coordinate information across senses (e.g., recognizing by sight an object they've only touched). Once thought to be a late-developing skill; newer evidence suggests it's robust from early infancy because sensory brain regions aren't yet specialized.
  • WIC (Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children) — US federal nutrition program for low-income pregnant and postpartum women, infants, and children up to age 5. Provides supplemental food, nutrition education, breastfeeding support (peer counselors), and referrals. Critical evidence-based intervention for child health equity.
  • Baby-Friendly Hospital Initiative (BFHI) — WHO/UNICEF program (1991) certifying hospitals that adhere to "Ten Steps to Successful Breastfeeding" — including immediate skin-to-skin contact, rooming-in, no artificial nipples without medical reason, and on-demand feeding. Increases breastfeeding initiation and duration rates.
  • PUMP Act (2022) / Break Time for Nursing Mothers — US federal law requiring most employers to provide reasonable break time and a private space (NOT a bathroom) for lactation expressing for one year after a child's birth. Expanded prior FLSA protections. Many low-wage workers don't know about this protection.
  • IBCLC (International Board Certified Lactation Consultant) — Highest credential in lactation consulting. Requires specific clinical experience, coursework, and passage of an exam. Insurance coverage for IBCLC services is mandated under ACA. Distinct from peer counselors and certified lactation counselors (less training).
  • Differentiation and integration (motor development) — Principle that motor skills develop by first mastering separate movements (differentiation) and then assembling them into coordinated, smooth performance (integration). Applies to dressing, eating, walking — each "simple" act is actually a coordinated sequence built from previously mastered components.
  • Emergent right-handedness — Pattern observed before birth: fetuses are more likely to suck the right thumb, move the right arm, and turn the head right. Continues postnatally — by age 1, most infants use the right hand for object manipulation while the left steadies. About 40 genes contribute to handedness, but cultural pressure also matters (left-handed children are rare in cultures that punish left-hand use).

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — three preschoolers, one piece of ketchup on the floor, and a window into theory of mind.

Valentina, Emma & Mia — theory-of-mind case

The case: Valentina, Emma, & Mia at the day-care

After lunch one day, Valentina (age 2) sees ketchup on the floor and panics, shouting "blood, blood!" Emma (age 3) rolls her eyes — "It's not blood, it's ketchup." Mia (age 4) jumps in: "Yeah, but Valentina thought it was blood."

A few weeks later — the day after Halloween — Emma puts on a monster mask and scares Valentina, who starts crying. Emma says, "Stop. It's just a mask." Mia again breaks in: "You know it's just a mask. But she thinks it's a monster."

What does each child's reaction reveal about their understanding of other people's minds? Which child shows the clearest mature theory of mind, and why?

Worked analysis

Valentina (age 2)

Reacts to her own perception of the world (ketchup = blood; mask = monster). No evidence she's reasoning about what others know — just experiencing her own beliefs as direct facts.

Emma (age 3)

Corrects Valentina with the truth ("It's not blood, it's ketchup"). But she doesn't acknowledge that Valentina believed something different. From Emma's perspective, the world is just what it is.

Mia (age 4)

The clearest theory-of-mind reasoning. Mia explicitly separates Valentina's belief from reality ("She thinks it's a monster"). She understands that a person can believe something false and act on that false belief.

Pattern

This matches the lab-task findings exactly — around age 4, children begin to articulate that others' beliefs can be false and can drive behavior. Mia is doing in real time what 4-year-olds do on the Sally-Anne task.

Why this matters

Theory of mind isn't a parlor trick — it underlies everything social. To comfort someone you need to understand they feel something you don't. To lie effectively, you have to model what another person believes. To teach, you have to track what they don't yet know. Mia at 4 has just acquired one of the most important cognitive tools in human social life.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 3 core content.

Q1

A new parent strokes their newborn's cheek and the baby turns their head toward the touch. Which reflex is this, and what is its evolutionary function?

  1. A. Moro — protective
  2. B. Babinski — neurological diagnostic only
  3. C. Rooting — helps locate the nipple for feeding
  4. D. Stepping — foundation for later walking
Show rationale

A. Moro is the startle reflex (arms flinging in response to a sudden stimulus or loss of support) — not cheek touch.

B. Babinski involves the sole of the foot, not the cheek.

C. CORRECT. Stroking the cheek triggers rooting — the infant turns their head toward the stimulus, which helps locate the nipple during breastfeeding. Disappears around 3–4 months as voluntary feeding control takes over.

D. Stepping is triggered by holding the infant upright with feet touching a surface, not by cheek touch.

Q2

A 9-month-old can sit independently but cannot yet stand. Their parent worries about the cephalocaudal principle. Which best describes the situation?

  1. A. This is a violation of the cephalocaudal principle — sitting should come after standing.
  2. B. This is consistent with the cephalocaudal principle — head and trunk control develop before lower body control.
  3. C. This is consistent with the proximodistal principle, not cephalocaudal.
  4. D. Cephalocaudal refers to fine motor only, not gross motor.
Show rationale

A. Reversed. Cephalocaudal predicts head and trunk before legs.

B. CORRECT. Cephalocaudal = "head to toe" — control develops from the top down. Sitting (requiring head and trunk control) appearing before standing (requiring trunk + legs + balance) is exactly the predicted sequence.

C. Proximodistal is center-outward (trunk before fingers). This case is about head-to-toe direction, which is cephalocaudal.

D. Both principles apply to both gross and fine motor development.

Q3

In the Sally-Anne false-belief task, Sally puts a marble in a basket and leaves the room. Anne moves the marble to a box. When asked "Where will Sally look for the marble?", what answer pattern best reflects MATURE theory of mind?

  1. A. "In the box" — because that's where the marble actually is now.
  2. B. "In the basket" — because Sally last saw it there and doesn't know Anne moved it.
  3. C. "She'll ask Anne" — because adults always check.
  4. D. "I don't know."
Show rationale

A. This is the typical 3-year-old answer — they can't separate their own knowledge from Sally's. It shows immature theory of mind.

B. CORRECT. Mature theory of mind requires understanding that Sally has a false belief based on her outdated knowledge. The child must hold two simultaneous representations: reality (marble is in the box) and Sally's belief (marble is in the basket). This shift typically appears around age 4.

C. Plausible behaviorally but doesn't engage the false-belief question. The task asks where Sally will look, not what she'll do next.

D. Sometimes given by very young children. Doesn't demonstrate the false-belief understanding.

Q4

A new parent asks for the SINGLE most important behavior to reduce their infant's SIDS risk. Which is the strongest evidence-based answer?

  1. A. Use a humidifier in the baby's room
  2. B. Place the baby on their back for every sleep, on a firm flat surface with nothing else in the crib
  3. C. Co-sleep so you can hear the baby breathing
  4. D. Use a wearable breathing monitor
Show rationale

A. Humidifiers don't have strong evidence for SIDS prevention.

B. CORRECT. The Back-to-Sleep campaign — back position, firm flat surface, empty crib — is the single most evidence-supported intervention. SIDS rates fell ~50% in the decade after this became standard guidance.

C. Bed-sharing actually increases SIDS risk, especially with soft bedding, exhausted parents, or under-12-month infants. Room-sharing (separate sleep surface) is recommended; bed-sharing is not.

D. Consumer breathing monitors have not been shown to reduce SIDS deaths in healthy infants and can give false reassurance.

Q5

A researcher places a small mark on a child's nose without them noticing, then puts them in front of a mirror. The child touches the mark on their own face. What does this demonstrate, and at approximately what age does this first appear?

  1. A. Theory of mind — appears around age 4
  2. B. Self-recognition / self-awareness — appears around 18–24 months
  3. C. Object permanence — appears around 8 months
  4. D. Stranger anxiety — appears around 9 months
Show rationale

A. Theory of mind is about understanding others' minds — a different and later achievement.

B. CORRECT. This is the classic rouge test (mirror test). Touching the mark on one's own face — rather than reaching for the mirror image — demonstrates the child recognizes the reflection as themselves. First clear sign of self-awareness, typically appearing around 18–24 months.

C. Object permanence is about objects continuing to exist when out of sight (Piaget's sensorimotor stage), unrelated to self-recognition.

D. Stranger anxiety is wariness toward unfamiliar people, not self-recognition.

Q6

The Libertus et al. sticky-mittens study trained 3-month-olds to grasp using Velcro mittens for 10 minutes a day for two weeks. The babies showed enhanced grasping immediately AND at 15 months — a year after training ended. What is the strongest interpretation of this finding?

  1. A. Motor skills are fully determined by maturation and cannot be trained
  2. B. Early motor experience can have lasting cascading effects on development; trained grasping likely enhanced object exploration, which compounded into broader cognitive and motor advantages over time
  3. C. The 10 minutes/day of training was directly responsible for all the 15-month advantages
  4. D. The findings cannot be replicated
Show rationale

A. The opposite — the study explicitly demonstrates that early motor training has real and lasting effects.

B. CORRECT. The findings demonstrate that early motor experience produces cascading effects across development. Training likely enhanced grasping directly, which allowed earlier and richer object exploration, which then compounded into cognitive and social learning advantages a year later. This illustrates the general principle that development cascades — early changes in one domain influence later development in other domains. The Libertus team itself emphasized that 10 minutes/day for two weeks couldn't directly cause a 15-month effect; the indirect, cascading mechanism is what makes it possible.

C. 2 weeks of brief training can't directly cause a difference a year later — the mechanism must be indirect (more exploration → more learning).

D. The study has been replicated and extended.

Q7

Researchers have found that the percentage of left-handed people in some societies has increased substantially over recent decades. What is the most parsimonious explanation, and what does this imply about handedness?

  1. A. Genes have changed in a few generations
  2. B. As cultural practices that discouraged or punished left-handedness have been abandoned, more naturally left-handed people now use their dominant hand — demonstrating that handedness is shaped by both heredity (~40 genes) AND environment
  3. C. Left-handed people have become more common because they are more intelligent
  4. D. There has been no change; this is a measurement artifact
Show rationale

A. Population genetics doesn't change measurably in a few generations. The shift is too rapid to be genetic.

B. CORRECT. The trend is consistent with cultural changes — as practices that punished or discouraged left-handed children have been abandoned, the observed rate of left-handedness has risen. This is a striking real-world demonstration that handedness, while strongly biological (at least 40 genes contribute), is also environmentally shaped. Heredity sets a strong tendency; environment can either support or suppress its expression.

C. No reliable evidence supports a handedness-intelligence link of the magnitude this would require.

D. Multiple data sources confirm the rise; it's not an artifact.

Q8

A parent of a 14-month-old who isn't walking is anxious because their friend's child walked at 9 months. Which response is most consistent with the dynamic systems approach to motor development?

  1. A. Walking is rigidly programmed by biology; the baby is delayed and needs intervention
  2. B. Walking emerges from the interaction of multiple components (strength, balance, body proportions, motivation, environment) and varies substantially across babies; 14 months is well within typical range; concerns warrant evaluation only if other signs are present (asymmetry, regression, very low tone, lack of pulling-to-stand)
  3. C. The earlier a baby walks, the smarter they will be
  4. D. Walking should be aggressively practiced from 6 months
Show rationale

A. The dynamic systems approach specifically rejects the rigid-maturation view. 14 months is well within typical range.

B. CORRECT. The dynamic systems approach sees walking as the emergent product of multiple interacting components — muscle strength, postural control, body proportions, motivation, and environment. Babies walk on a range of timelines (9–18 months is the typical normal range) without it predicting later motor or cognitive ability. The right framing for the parent: are the prerequisites in place (pulling to stand, cruising, motivated movement)? If yes, walking is on the way. Evaluation is warranted only for specific red flags, not for variation within normal range.

C. Walking onset within normal range doesn't predict cognitive outcomes.

D. Premature aggressive walking practice has no evidence base and can cause harm.

Q9

A nurse is providing SIDS-prevention counseling to new parents at discharge. Which combination of recommendations is evidence-based and consistent with the AAP Safe to Sleep guidelines?

  1. A. Side-lying position with a soft pillow for comfort
  2. B. Back-to-sleep (supine) position, firm flat mattress, no soft objects/loose bedding/bumpers in the crib, room-sharing without bed-sharing for the first 6 months, no smoking exposure, breastfeeding when possible, pacifier at sleep, avoid overheating
  3. C. Prone (tummy) sleeping with the head turned to the side
  4. D. Co-sleeping in the parents' bed is the safest option
Show rationale

A. Side-lying is NOT recommended — infants can roll prone. Pillows pose suffocation risk.

B. CORRECT. The AAP Safe to Sleep guidelines (originally "Back to Sleep" from 1992) have cut US SIDS rates by more than half. Key elements: supine position for every sleep until 1 year, firm flat mattress with fitted sheet only, no soft objects (blankets, pillows, stuffed animals, bumpers), room-sharing without bed-sharing for ≥6 months, no smoking exposure (huge SIDS risk factor), breastfeeding reduces SIDS risk, pacifier at sleep (once breastfeeding established), avoid overheating. This is one of the most successful prevention campaigns in pediatric history.

C. Prone sleeping dramatically INCREASES SIDS risk — this was the main pattern reversed by Back to Sleep.

D. Bed-sharing (sleeping in the same bed as parents) increases suffocation/SIDS risk; AAP specifically recommends against. Room-sharing (same room, separate sleep surface) is the safe alternative.

Q10

A pediatric nurse evaluating a 2-month-old observes: doesn't lift head during tummy time, doesn't visually track moving objects to midline, doesn't make eye contact, no social smile. The mother reports the baby seems "floppy." Which interpretation is correct?

  1. A. Normal variation; reassure
  2. B. Multiple developmental red flags warranting prompt evaluation — at 2 months, head lift in prone, tracking to midline, eye contact, and emerging social smile should all be present; concerning findings include possible hypotonia ("floppy"), absent social engagement, lack of motor or visual responsiveness — warrant pediatric assessment and early intervention referral
  3. C. Wait until 6 months to evaluate
  4. D. The baby just needs more stimulation
Show rationale

A. These are multiple red flags, not normal variation.

B. CORRECT. By 2 months, typical development includes: lifting head 45° during tummy time, tracking objects past midline, making eye contact, emerging social smile (a major developmental milestone). Absent all of these plus hypotonia ("floppy") suggests possible neurodevelopmental concern — could indicate cerebral palsy, genetic conditions, vision impairment, or other significant issues. Action steps: (1) Don't reassure; document specifically. (2) Refer to pediatrician for medical evaluation. (3) Refer to early intervention (in the US, Birth-to-Three services). (4) The earlier intervention begins, the better the outcomes due to brain plasticity in infancy.

C. Waiting wastes the critical brain plasticity window. Early intervention is far more effective when started early.

D. Stimulation alone cannot address underlying neurodevelopmental concerns; diagnostic workup needed first.

Q11

A parent says: "My 4-month-old won't approach the edge of the visual cliff because she's afraid of heights." Using Gibson and Walk's classic research, which response is most accurate?

  1. A. She's correct — infants fear heights from birth
  2. B. Pre-crawling infants do perceive depth but typically don't show fear of heights — fear emerges with locomotor experience (crawling); heart-rate studies show interest, not fear, in 2–4 month olds at the visual cliff; the fear correlation with crawling, not age, suggests fear of heights is shaped by motor experience
  3. C. Depth perception doesn't develop until age 2
  4. D. The visual cliff is unreliable
Show rationale

A. Fear of heights isn't innate — it emerges with mobility experience.

B. CORRECT. This is a beautiful example of experience shaping perception. Gibson and Walk (1960) showed crawling infants avoid the deep side. Later research (Campos and colleagues) showed: (1) Pre-crawling infants perceive depth — heart rate slows on deep side, indicating interest, not fear. (2) Fear emerges with crawling experience, not just age. (3) When pre-crawling infants are given mobility (walkers, "moved around" experience), fear emerges earlier. This shows perception and experience are bidirectionally linked — infants learn what to fear through their own movement. Clinical relevance: as babies become mobile, expect new spatial fears and safety concerns.

C. Depth perception emerges much earlier — by 4 months for binocular cues.

D. The visual cliff is one of the most robust paradigms in infant research.

Q12

A preschool teacher gives this scenario to test theory of mind: "Maxi puts his candy in the blue cabinet and goes outside. While he's gone, his mom moves the candy to the red cabinet. When Maxi comes back, where will he LOOK for his candy?" A 3-year-old answers "red cabinet"; a 5-year-old answers "blue cabinet." What does this reveal?

  1. A. The 3-year-old has better memory
  2. B. The 5-year-old has acquired theory of mind and can reason about false beliefs — understanding that Maxi will look based on his (false) belief about where the candy is, not based on where it actually is; this is the classic Sally-Anne / false-belief task and typically passes around age 4
  3. C. The 3-year-old is more honest
  4. D. Both answers are equally correct
Show rationale

A. The error isn't memory — the 3-year-old knows where the candy is. The issue is reasoning about Maxi's mental state.

B. CORRECT. This is the classic false-belief task (Wimmer & Perner, 1983; Wellman meta-analyses). The 3-year-old answers based on reality ("the candy is in the red cabinet") because they can't yet separate their own knowledge from Maxi's. The 5-year-old separates the two and correctly predicts Maxi's behavior based on his belief ("Maxi thinks it's in the blue cabinet, so he'll look there"). This transition around age 4 is one of the most replicated findings in cognitive development. Clinical implications: (1) Children with autism spectrum disorder often show delayed false-belief understanding. (2) The skill is foundational for empathy, deception, teaching, and complex social cognition. (3) Pediatric communication should match the child's level — under age 4, can't fully appreciate that you don't know what they know.

C. Honesty isn't being tested; reasoning about belief is.

D. The "red cabinet" answer is incorrect — Maxi doesn't know about the move.

Unit 1 · Early Development · Chapter 4

The Emergence of Thought & Language

TL;DR · One-glance summary

From birth through age 6, children's thinking transforms more than at any other time of life. Piaget described qualitative stage shifts — sensorimotor (0–2) and preoperational (2–7). Information-processing models track gradual gains in attention, memory, and naïve theories. Vygotsky placed cognitive development in the social context, especially the zone of proximal development. Language explodes in the second year — from babbling to a vocabulary of hundreds, then to sentences — driven by fast mapping and grammatical experimentation.

Piaget's first two stages

  • Sensorimotor (0–2) — knowing through action; object permanence
  • Preoperational (2–7) — symbols, but egocentric & centered
  • Schemes — mental structures that organize knowledge
  • Assimilation + accommodation = how schemes change

Information processing

  • Attention sharpens with age
  • Working memory expands gradually
  • Children build naïve theories of biology, physics, psychology
  • Memory strategies emerge in preschool

Vygotsky

  • Cognition rooted in social interaction
  • Zone of proximal development (ZPD)
  • Scaffolding — adult/peer support that fades
  • Language is a tool for thinking, not just communicating

Language milestones

  • Babbling ~6 mo · first words ~12 mo
  • Vocab spurt ~18 mo via fast mapping
  • Overextension, underextension, overregularization
  • Telegraphic 2-word phrases → full grammar by ~5

4.1 · The Onset of Thinking — Piaget's Account

Piaget argued that children's thinking changes qualitatively as they age — they don't just know more, they reason in fundamentally different ways at different stages. Modern research has revised many specifics, but his framework still shapes how we ask questions about cognition.

Piaget's account of cognitive development

How thinking changes — schemes, assimilation, accommodation

Piaget said children build schemes — mental structures that organize knowledge. Schemes change through two complementary processes:

Assimilation

Fitting new information into an existing scheme. A toddler who knows "dog" calls every four-legged animal "doggy". They're assimilating cats, horses, and goats into the dog scheme.

Accommodation

Changing the scheme to fit new information. Eventually the toddler realizes horses are different — too big, different shape, different sound. They split the "doggy" scheme into separate categories.

The push between these processes — fitting reality into your model vs. updating your model — drives all cognitive development, in Piaget's view. Development happens through cycles of equilibrium → disequilibrium → re-equilibration.

A 2-year-old sees a cat for the first time and calls it "doggy". Their parent says "No, that's a cat — cats meow." The child later sees another cat and says "cat". Identify the assimilation and the accommodation.
Assimilation: calling the cat "doggy" — the child fit a new animal into an existing dog scheme. Accommodation: after correction, splitting the scheme — adopting a separate "cat" category for animals that meow. The cycle is: existing scheme → encounter that doesn't quite fit → input from environment → scheme updates. Most cognitive growth is small accommodations of this kind.

The sensorimotor stage (0–2 years)

Knowing through action

Infants understand the world through their senses and motor actions. Thinking is not yet abstract or symbolic — it's grounded in what the infant physically does. Across six substages, infants progress from reflex-based action to genuine mental representation.

The signature achievement: object permanence

Object permanence = understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight. Piaget argued it emerges around 8 months (when infants will search for a hidden object). Modern violation-of-expectation studies suggest a more rudimentary form appears as early as 3–4 months — Piaget likely underestimated infants because his tasks required reaching ability the infants didn't yet have.

A related quirk: the A-not-B error. An 8–10-month-old watches you hide a toy under cloth A, then under cloth B. They keep reaching toward A. Piaget thought this showed incomplete object permanence; current research points more to inhibitory control limits.

The preoperational stage (2–7 years)

Symbols, with limitations

Children can now use symbols — words, drawings, pretend play. Massive cognitive advance. But thinking is still limited in two characteristic ways:

Egocentrism

Difficulty taking another person's perspective. In Piaget's three-mountains task, preschoolers describe a scene from their own viewpoint when asked what a doll on the opposite side would see.

Centration

Focusing on one feature at a time and ignoring others. Underlies failure on conservation tasks — same water in a tall narrow glass "looks more" than in a wide one.

Lack of conservation

Preschoolers don't yet understand that quantity stays constant despite changes in appearance. Conservation of number, mass, liquid, and volume develop in that order, mostly during the concrete operational stage.

Animistic thinking

Attributing life and intentions to inanimate objects ("the clouds are sleepy"). Charming, but reflects limits of biological-mechanical reasoning.

A 4-year-old watches a researcher pour water from a short wide glass into a tall thin glass. The child insists the tall glass has more water. Which preoperational limitation does this best demonstrate?
Centration, leading to failure on a conservation of liquid task. The child centers on a single salient feature (height) and ignores the compensating one (narrowness). They lack the reversibility of operations needed to mentally undo the pour. Conservation of liquid is typically passed in the concrete operational stage (~age 7).
Piaget claimed object permanence emerges around 8 months because he tested it by having infants reach for hidden objects. Baillargeon's research shows it emerges much earlier — by 3.5–4 months. What's the methodological difference, and what does this reveal about studying infant cognition?
The methodological difference: Piaget tested object permanence with a reaching task — infants had to reach for the hidden object. Reaching requires motor coordination AND memory AND the ability to plan a goal-directed action. Pre-reaching infants might know the object exists but can't show it. Baillargeon used violation-of-expectation paradigms: she set up impossible events (a screen rotating through an apparently solid hidden object) and measured how long infants LOOKED. 3.5-month-olds look longer at impossible than possible events — they're surprised by the violation, which means they expected the hidden object to still be there. What this reveals: (1) Motor limitations can mask cognitive abilities in young infants — a major bias in early developmental research. (2) Cognition develops earlier and more continuously than Piaget claimed; the dramatic stage transitions he described may be partly artifacts of measurement. (3) Methods matter enormously — what we measure determines what we conclude about development. (4) The core knowledge hypothesis (Spelke) developed from this research line: infants are born with specialized cognitive systems, not blank slates.

Sensorimotor stage in depth — three fronts of progress

Three parallel achievements (0-24 months)

Piaget identified three concurrent developmental fronts in the sensorimotor stage — not separate phases but overlapping achievements that together transform the newborn reflex-driven infant into a symbol-using toddler.

Front 1 — Adapting to and exploring the environment

0-1 month: pure reflexive responses (sucking, grasping triggered by stimulation).
1-4 months: reflexes are modified by experience. An infant inadvertently brings thumb to mouth, sucks, finds it pleasing — then learns to initiate thumb-sucking.
~8 months: onset of deliberate, intentional behavior — the watershed. Means and ends become distinct. If a parent's hand blocks a toy, the infant moves the hand to reach the toy.
~12 months: active experimentation. Babies deliberately shake objects to discover sounds, drop different items to see results. Repeating actions on different objects just to see what happens.

Front 2 — Understanding objects (object permanence)

0-4 months: no apparent understanding that objects exist when not visible. Out of sight = out of existence.
4-8 months: infants reach for visible objects but stop reaching once an object is hidden behind a barrier.
8-12 months: infants search for hidden objects — but make the A-not-B error (see below).
~18 months: full object permanence by Piaget's criteria.
Modern critique: Baillargeon's violation-of-expectation paradigms suggest infants understand object permanence as early as 3.5 months — they just can't yet coordinate the motor sequence needed to retrieve hidden objects.

Front 3 — Using symbols

~18 months: words and gestures emerge as symbols standing for things. A wave bids farewell as effectively as "goodbye." Pretend play emerges — at 20 months, children may pretend-brush their teeth with an empty hand. Once symbols are available, infants can mentally anticipate consequences instead of having to act them out. A 12-18-month-old might close a door, knocking over a block tower next to it; an 18-24-month-old anticipates the consequence and moves the tower first.

Why these three fronts matter

The infant is simultaneously becoming intentional (front 1), realizing the world is independent of perception (front 2), and developing the capacity for representation (front 3). All three foundations are needed before preoperational thought becomes possible.

The A-not-B error

Piaget's classic demonstration of incomplete object permanence: an 8- to 10-month-old watches as an experimenter repeatedly hides an interesting object under container A. The infant successfully retrieves it each time. Then, in full view of the infant, the experimenter hides the same object under container B. Despite watching the hiding, most 8-10-month-olds still reach for container A.

Piaget's interpretation: the infant doesn't fully distinguish the object from the actions used to find it — "the toy" and "reaching toward A" are fused. The error disappears around 12 months as object permanence solidifies.

Modern reinterpretations:

  • Working memory limits — infants briefly remember the new location but the previous reach pattern dominates response (Diamond, 1985).
  • Inhibitory control limits — the prepotent reaching response to A is harder to inhibit than the new B response. Adele Diamond linked this to prefrontal cortex development.
  • Motor planning interference — repeated reaching to A creates a motor habit that takes effort to override (Smith & Thelen's dynamic systems account).

Why this matters: the A-not-B error is a window into how cognitive abilities emerge from the interaction of memory, attention, inhibition, and motor planning — not just conceptual understanding. Clinically useful: infants with reduced inhibitory control (e.g., from prenatal exposures, prematurity, or developmental delays) show A-not-B errors later than typical.

A nurse is observing 8-month-old Maya during a well-child visit. Maya is dropping different objects from her highchair and watching where they fall. When the nurse hides Maya's stuffed animal under a cloth, Maya pulls it off and giggles. When the nurse then hides it under a different cloth across the table, Maya looks for it under the FIRST cloth. How would the nurse describe Maya's sensorimotor development using Piaget's framework?
Maya is demonstrating age-typical sensorimotor development at the boundary between sub-stages 4 and 5. (1) Active experimentation: dropping different objects and watching where they fall is classic "tertiary circular reactions" — she's repeating an action with variation to see what will happen, going beyond just reaching for what she wants. Also evidence of deliberate, intentional behavior (the 8-month watershed). (2) Partial object permanence: searching for a hidden object shows she understands objects exist when not in sight — a substantial advance from younger infants who lose interest when something disappears. (3) The A-not-B error: looking under the FIRST cloth even though she watched the experimenter move it to the SECOND is the classic A-not-B error. Piaget's interpretation: incomplete object permanence — the toy and the act of finding it aren't yet fully separated. Modern interpretation: limited inhibitory control (can't easily override the now-habitual "reach toward A" response) and working memory limits (the new B location fades quickly from active memory). Tied to prefrontal cortex maturation. (4) Clinical significance: Maya's behavior is on-track for 8 months. The A-not-B error typically resolves around 12 months as object permanence and inhibitory control develop. What would be concerning: (a) absence of any searching behavior past 12 months; (b) A-not-B errors persisting beyond 18 months may suggest delayed object permanence or executive function development; (c) lack of interest in observing consequences of actions (no experimentation, no anticipation of outcomes) may warrant screening for autism spectrum concerns or other developmental delays. (5) Parent education: peek-a-boo and hide-and-seek games support exactly these emerging capacities. The "game" isn't trivial — it's scaffolding for object permanence, working memory, and joint attention all at once. (6) What's NOT yet developed: at 8 months, symbolic representation hasn't fully emerged — Maya may not yet imitate gestures or engage in pretend play. By 18-24 months we'd expect this.

4.2 · Information Processing During Infancy

An alternative to Piaget's stage view: treat the mind as a continuously improving information-processing system, with attention, memory, and knowledge structures that change gradually with age.

Information processing in infancy

Attention & memory

Two clear age-related improvements: attention becomes more selective and sustained (infants and toddlers are easily pulled by salient stimuli; preschoolers can deliberately focus), and working memory — the capacity for active manipulation of information — expands gradually. Long-term memory exists from infancy (we know because habituated infants recognize familiar stimuli days later), but the ability to deliberately retrieve and use memories develops over the preschool years.

Naïve theories

Core knowledge

A defining insight of modern research: even young children build organized naïve theories about specific domains. They're not just accumulating facts — they're constructing coherent frameworks:

Naïve physics

Even infants expect solid objects to fall when unsupported, to move in continuous paths, and not to pass through each other. Violation-of-expectation studies show 3–4-month-olds notice when these rules are broken.

Naïve biology

Preschoolers distinguish living from nonliving things. They know plants and animals grow, get sick, and die; rocks and chairs don't. They struggle, however, with plants being "alive" in the same sense as animals.

Naïve psychology

Children begin to track desires (~age 2: "Mommy wants the cookie"), then beliefs (~age 4: theory of mind / false belief). The transition from desire-only to belief-and-desire is one of the major shifts of preschool.

Why "theories"?

Like scientific theories, naïve theories are coherent, generative, and revisable. They generate predictions, get violated, and get updated. This isn't passive learning — it's hypothesis-testing in miniature.

Spotlight on Research

Essentialism in preschoolers' thinking — would you take Hitler's heart?

Meyer, M., Gelman, S., Roberts, S., & Leslie, S.-J. (2017)

What was the aim?

Adults often resist receiving transplanted organs from morally objectionable donors — even though there's no biological basis for thinking organs transmit personality. This reflects essentialism — the belief that things have an underlying "essence" that travels with parts of them. Meyer and colleagues asked: Do preschoolers already show this kind of essentialist thinking about transplants?

How did they measure it?

Researchers created scenarios in which children were described as nice, mean, smart, or not-smart. Other scenarios described pigs and monkeys acting naturally. Children were asked to imagine receiving either $1 or a heart from the character/animal, then asked whether they themselves would become like that character (nice, mean, smart, monkey-like, etc.).

What was the design?

Cross-sectional. Children were preschoolers and elementary-school-age. (The textbook focuses on the preschooler findings.) No ethical concerns — the tasks posed no danger.

What were the results?

Preschoolers believed they would not take on characteristics of the donor if they received $1 — but they DID expect to take on the donor's characteristics if they received a heart. To rule out the alternative that 'warm-hearted' or 'cold-hearted' linguistic associations were doing the work, the researchers re-ran the analyses excluding 'nice' and 'mean'. The results held: children also didn't expect to become not-smart or monkey-like from receiving those donors' hearts in the same way they would from a $1 transfer.

What did the investigators conclude?

Preschoolers already see the heart as part of the essence of "humanness" — an internal essence that could cause a recipient to take on properties of the donor. This kind of essentialist thinking emerges very early and isn't simply learned from adult cultural beliefs about organ transplants.

What would strengthen the conclusions?

Extending to other internal organs (stomach, lungs) to see if the essentialism is heart-specific or general. Comparing to hair/skin transplants (biological but not internal) and pacemakers (internal but not biological) would help pinpoint what children consider "essence-bearing".
