They begin to identify with adults and explore the opportunities their culture offers — play has purpose as children try on adult roles (parent, teacher, athlete, writer). They explore the environment, ask endless questions, imagine possibilities. Initiative is moderated by guilt as children realize their initiative may overstep into others' territory. The virtue is PURPOSE — the courage to envision and pursue valued goals. Unresolved: chronic guilt, fear of standing out, inhibited ambition.

Why "balance" not "win"

Each crisis is a balance, not a pass/fail. Pure trust without mistrust = naïve, dangerous. Pure autonomy without doubt = reckless. Pure initiative without guilt = lacking conscience. The virtues emerge from the productive tension between the two poles — not from one pole defeating the other. This is why Erikson's stages have lasting value clinically: when adults struggle, the question is often "which pole is overweighted?"

What determines quality of attachment

Decades of attachment research point to one main predictor of secure attachment: predictable, responsive parenting (De Wolff & van IJzendoorn, 1997; Boldt et al., 2016; Zeegers et al., 2017). When a mother responds promptly to her baby's crying and provides reassurance, the baby learns that social interactions are predictable and satisfying. This produces secure attachment.

The mechanism is the internal working model — the infant's developing mental model of how responsive and dependable caregivers (and by extension others) are. When parents are dependable and caring, the working model says "people meet my needs." When parents respond slowly, intermittently, or angrily, the working model says "social relationships are inconsistent and frustrating."

The internal working model is the bridge from early attachment to adult relationships — friendships, romantic partnerships, parenting one's own children. The pattern set in infancy is influential but not deterministic — attachment style can shift with corrective experiences (good therapy, sustained loving relationships, intervention programs).

Linking Research to Life — attachment and child care

The big question parents ask

When a parent returns to work and places their infant in daycare, the natural question is: "Will this hurt my baby's attachment to me?" This question was the focus of decades of research — and the answer is more nuanced than either alarmist or reassuring camps suggested.

What the research shows (NICHD Study of Early Child Care and Youth Development)

The most rigorous evidence comes from the NICHD Study of Early Child Care and Youth Development, a longitudinal study following over 1,300 children from birth through adolescence across 10 US sites. Key findings:

Quality of parenting still matters most

The strongest predictor of secure attachment was maternal sensitivity at home — NOT whether the child was in childcare. High-quality parenting + childcare → secure attachment. Insensitive parenting at home was the risk factor regardless of childcare.

Quality of childcare matters too

High-quality childcare is associated with better language development, school readiness, and cognitive outcomes. Low-quality care can interact with insensitive parenting to compound risk. Quality indicators: small group sizes, low staff turnover, trained caregivers, responsive interactions, age-appropriate activities.

Quantity has modest effects

Children spending more total hours in childcare (over 30+ hours/week) showed slightly more behavior problems at school entry, but the effect was small. The effect attenuated by adolescence and was much smaller than the effect of parenting quality.

Type of care

Center-based care (vs. home care or relative care) had small associations with both more academic skills AND more behavior problems. No type of care is uniformly best — the right choice depends on the child, the family, and the specific provider.

The bottom line: returning to work and using childcare does NOT harm attachment when parenting at home is sensitive and the childcare is decent quality. This is the message most working parents need to hear.

For nursing practice — counseling working parents

  • Validate the worry: the fear that work might hurt the baby is universal and well-meaning. Don't dismiss it.
  • Provide accurate information: the research is reassuring. Sensitive parenting at home matters far more than whether mom (or dad) is in the workplace.
  • Help evaluate childcare quality: small group sizes (esp. for infants — ideal <4 infants per caregiver), low staff turnover, warm and responsive caregivers, predictable routines, parental access. Visit and observe before enrolling.
  • Protect the home dyad: when both parents work, dedicated parent-infant time (breakfast, bath time, evening play, weekends) is what fuels attachment. Quality time, not quantity per se.
  • Address postpartum mental health: PPD and anxiety interfere with sensitive parenting more than work does. Treat the parent.
  • Recognize systemic issues: the US has weak parental leave compared to peer nations. Many families return to work earlier than they'd prefer because they have to, not because they want to. Nursing advocacy at policy level matters.
A new mother tells the pediatric nurse at the 4-month well-baby visit that she's returning to work in 2 weeks, and her friend has told her this will "damage her baby's attachment". She's anxious and considering quitting her job. What does the nurse need to know to counsel her with evidence-based reassurance and practical guidance?
The friend's claim is not supported by the evidence. The NICHD Study of Early Child Care and Youth Development — the largest, most rigorous study of this question — followed over 1,300 children from birth through adolescence. Key findings to share: (1) Maternal sensitivity at home was the strongest predictor of secure attachment, NOT whether the child was in daycare. High-quality parenting + childcare → secure attachment. (2) Quality of childcare matters too — high quality is associated with better outcomes; low quality combined with insensitive parenting can compound risk. (3) Quantity of care has only modest effects — children in 30+ hours/week showed slightly more behavior problems at school entry, but the effect was small and attenuated by adolescence, far smaller than parenting effects. (4) No type of care is uniformly best — depends on the child, family, provider. What to say to the mother: "Returning to work doesn't damage attachment when your time WITH your baby is responsive and warm, and your childcare is decent quality. Your friend is wrong about this — decades of research show that what matters most is the quality of your interactions with your baby, not how many hours you're together. Many securely-attached babies have working parents." Practical childcare quality counseling: (1) Small group sizes: for infants, ideal is 3:1 or 4:1 child-to-caregiver ratio. (2) Low staff turnover: ask the center how long current caregivers have worked there. Frequent turnover disrupts caregiver-infant relationships. (3) Warm, responsive caregivers: visit and observe. Do caregivers respond to babies' cues? Make eye contact? Talk to them? Hold them when distressed? (4) Predictable routines: babies thrive on consistency. (5) Parental access: can mom drop in unannounced? Visit during breastfeeding hours? Open-door policies matter. (6) Trained caregivers: CDA, AA, or BA in early childhood education is ideal. (7) Reasonable child-staff ratios: NAEYC/state licensing standards as floor. Protecting the home dyad: dedicated time matters more than total hours. Breakfast, bath, bedtime routines, weekends, and uninterrupted attention when home. Put the phone away. Read together. Make eye contact during feeds. Watch for postpartum mental health: anxiety like this (anticipatory anxiety about return to work) can mask or accompany PPD. Screen with EPDS. Address postpartum anxiety/depression — these interfere with sensitive parenting more than work does. Address structural realities: this mother has 4 months postpartum, which is more than many US workers get. The US is one of few wealthy nations without paid parental leave. Many working parents feel pressure they shouldn't have to face. Validate that the policy environment is challenging, not just her individual choices. The nurse's bottom line: support this mother in returning to work without guilt. The evidence base is clear and reassuring. Help her evaluate childcare quality. Watch for her mental health. The relationship will be fine if she remains sensitive and responsive in the time she's home.

5.2 · Emerging Emotions

Emotion development follows a clear sequence — basic emotions first, then more complex self-aware emotions, then increasing skill at regulating and recognizing them in others.

Emotional development

Basic emotions appear first

A small set of emotions appears in the first year, observable from facial expressions and matched across cultures. Joy / pleasure shows in the social smile around 2 months. Anger emerges around 4–6 months — toddlers become predictably angry when goals are frustrated. Fear appears in the second half of the first year as stranger wariness (~7–8 months) and separation anxiety (~9 months). Basic sadness and disgust round out the early set.

Self-conscious emotions — require self-awareness

~18 months and beyond

A second class of emotions requires the child to have a concept of self and some sense of standards. They emerge only after self-recognition (~18 months) and develop through preschool. These are sometimes called secondary or moral emotions:

Embarrassment

Awareness of being observed/evaluated by others. Appears soon after self-recognition (~18–24 mo). Doesn't require knowing right from wrong — just that others can see you.

Pride

Feeling good about an accomplishment one credits to oneself. Requires evaluating self against a standard.

Guilt

Feeling bad about a specific behavior — typically wanting to repair it. Emerges through preschool as conscience develops. Distinct from shame in being focused on the act, not the self.

Shame

Feeling bad about the self as exposed. More globally negative than guilt; predicts withdrawal rather than reparation. Associated with harsher socialization styles.

Social referencing — borrowing the caregiver's appraisal

By ~12 months, infants in ambiguous situations look at their caregiver's face and use that emotional signal to guide their own behavior. If mom looks calm at the visual cliff, the infant crosses. If mom looks fearful, the infant stays. This social referencing is the root of how children learn to feel about things they've never encountered before, and how emotions get transmitted across generations.

A 12-month-old at the playground sees a dog approach for the first time. They glance back at their parent. The parent smiles warmly. The infant then reaches toward the dog. Which developmental process is this, and why is it important?
Social referencing. In an ambiguous or novel situation, the infant uses the caregiver's emotional reaction to guide their own response. This is important because it's the main mechanism by which children acquire emotions toward things they've never directly experienced — fears, comforts, attitudes about people and objects. It's also why parental anxiety transmits so efficiently to young children, and why caregivers can powerfully shape what children learn to find safe or scary.
A pediatric nurse approaches a 9-month-old for vitals. The infant immediately begins crying, clings to the parent, and turns away from the nurse. The parent feels embarrassed. What's happening developmentally, and how should the nurse adjust her approach?
This is stranger wariness (sometimes called stranger anxiety) — a normal developmental phenomenon that emerges around 7–8 months, peaks around 12 months, and gradually declines. It signals that the infant has formed selective attachment to familiar caregivers and now distinguishes them from strangers. It's a marker of healthy attachment development, NOT difficult behavior. Nursing approach adjustments: (1) Don't take it personally or push through — increasing your warmth or speed will likely increase distress. (2) Approach slowly and from the side, not directly facing the infant. (3) Let the parent be the closest person to the infant; have the parent hold the baby for the exam when possible. (4) Talk softly to the parent first, letting the infant observe positive interactions. (5) Use a gentle warm-up — let the infant see and even touch your stethoscope before using it. (6) Keep equipment out of sight until needed. (7) Distract during procedures with toys or bubbles. (8) Reassure the parent: "this is exactly what we'd expect at this age — it means he's developed a strong bond with you." Same principle applies to separation anxiety peak around 12–15 months — keep parent present during procedures.

Emotion regulation

Newborns rely entirely on caregivers to regulate their emotional states. Through the first 2 years, infants develop simple self-soothing strategies (looking away, thumb-sucking, seeking the caregiver). Through preschool, more flexible regulation appears — distraction, talking to oneself, reappraising a situation. Effective emotion regulation in early childhood is a strong predictor of later social and academic success.

5.3 · Interacting with Others

The toddler and preschool years are when children move from parallel play alongside peers to genuine cooperative interaction — with the prosocial, aggressive, and friendship-building patterns that come with it.

Social interaction & behavior

Prosocial behavior

Prosocial behavior = voluntary behavior intended to benefit another: helping, sharing, comforting, cooperating. Surprisingly early: infants as young as 14–18 months spontaneously help an adult who can't reach a fallen object. Sharing develops more slowly — toddlers will share when prompted but rarely volunteer until age 3–4.

The most prosocial children typically have parents who model prosocial behavior, reason with them about others' feelings, and respond warmly. Material rewards for helping can actually reduce later voluntary prosocial behavior (the over-justification effect).

reduce later voluntary prosocial behavior.">
A 4-year-old shares half their snack with a crying friend with no expectation of getting anything in return. What developmental capacities does this require, and what parenting practices most reliably foster this?
It requires empathy (recognizing the friend's distress), a basic theory of mind (understanding the friend is hungry), and self-regulation (overriding the desire to keep the food). It's fostered by parents who (1) model prosocial behavior themselves, (2) use inductive discipline — explaining the impact of behavior on others ("how do you think Aiden felt?"), and (3) provide warm responsive caregiving. Material rewards generally reduce later voluntary prosocial behavior.
A pediatric nurse observes a 4-year-old at a play therapy session. The child engages exclusively in solitary play and shows no interest when other children try to engage with her. Same pattern across observations. Why is this concerning, and what should the nurse consider?
By age 4, children typically progress through Parten's play stages: solitary → onlooker → parallel → associative → cooperative. Most 4-year-olds engage in associative and cooperative play with peers. Exclusive solitary play with consistent lack of interest in peer interaction across observations is a red flag warranting evaluation. Considerations: (1) Autism spectrum disorder — limited peer interest, restricted shared engagement, difficulty with reciprocal play are core features; M-CHAT-R/F screening was likely done at 18 and 24 months but missed cases happen, and current concerns warrant evaluation. (2) Social anxiety / selective mutism — child wants to interact but is too anxious; usually shows different pattern with familiar people. (3) Receptive language disorder — child can't follow peer interaction. (4) Severe deprivation / institutional history — restricted social opportunities. (5) Temperament alone — slow-to-warm children can take longer but eventually engage; persistent non-engagement is concerning. Action steps: (1) Comprehensive developmental screening. (2) Referral to developmental pediatrician or pediatric psychology. (3) Audiology check. (4) Speech-language evaluation. (5) Don't wait — early intervention windows are critical for social development.

Play develops in stages (Parten)

Solitary (0–2 yr)

Plays alone, doesn't engage with others' play.

Parallel (~2 yr)

Plays alongside other children with similar materials, but not interacting with them.

Associative (~3 yr)

Interacts during play (sharing toys, brief exchanges) but no organized goal.

Cooperative (~4+ yr)

Coordinated play around a shared goal — pretend scenarios, rule-based games, joint construction.

Pretend / make-believe play emerges around 18 months and becomes more complex through preschool. It supports language, theory of mind, self-regulation, and creativity.

Aggression

Physical aggression peaks around age 2, then declines as language and self-regulation develop. Through preschool and school years, verbal aggression and relational aggression (excluding, spreading rumors) increase. Children with persistently high aggression past the typical 2-year peak are at greater risk for later conduct problems; the strongest interventions involve teaching parents to use authoritative discipline.

5.4 · Gender Roles & Gender Identity

Children learn culturally specific information about gender very early — by age 2 they show sex-typed preferences, and by age 3 they can label their own gender. The deeper understanding of what gender is develops more slowly.

Gender development

Three components of gender development

Gender identity

A person's internal sense of being male, female, both, or neither. Most children can label their own gender by age 2–3. Distinguishing identity (internal) from role (cultural prescription) and orientation (attraction) is important — they're separable.

Gender stability

Understanding that gender stays the same over time ("I was a girl when I was a baby and I'll be a woman"). Develops by ~age 4.

Gender constancy

Understanding that gender doesn't change with superficial appearance — a girl is still a girl even if she cuts her hair short and wears boys' clothes. Comes by ~age 6, often considered the mature understanding.

Gender schema theory

Once children form a basic gender category, they build gender schemas — organized mental structures with information about what males and females typically do. These schemas then guide attention, interpretation, and memory. Children pay more attention to gender- consistent information, remember it better, and use it to predict what's appropriate for themselves.

The schemas are heavily shaped by parents, peers, media, and language. Some are flexible ("girls can do anything boys do"); others rigid ("doctors are men"). Rigid schemas predict more sex-typed behavior and reduced flexibility in interest exploration.

A 4-year-old says "only women can be nurses" and refuses to believe the new male nurse at their pediatrician's office is a nurse, insisting he must be a doctor. Which concept best explains this, and what's likely to change with age?
The child is operating from a rigid gender schema — an organized mental framework about what males and females typically do — and using it to override direct evidence (the man is clearly working as a nurse). With more experience, schemas typically become more flexible. By concrete operational stage (~7), children better separate biological gender (constant) from cultural roles (variable). Exposure to counter-stereotypical examples is one of the most effective ways to loosen rigid schemas.

Sex-typed preferences emerge early

By age 2 many children show preferences for toys, activities, and playmates traditionally associated with their gender. Same-sex playgroups intensify through preschool. The causes are a complex blend of biological factors (prenatal hormones influence some preferences) and social ones (modeling, reinforcement, schemas). Importantly, considerable variability exists within and across cultures, and many children show interests that don't map onto traditional sex-typed patterns.

Spotlight on Research

Reasoning about gender-related properties — children's essentialism about gender

Taylor, M., Rhodes, M., & Gelman, S. (2009)

What was the aim?

Do children believe that physical and behavioral properties of boys and girls are inherent and stable? For example, do they think a girl necessarily grows up to have breasts (physical) — and that she necessarily likes to play with dolls (behavioral)? Or do children distinguish between biology-bound traits and culturally-flexible ones?

How did they measure it?

Participants heard about a baby girl who, immediately after birth, went to live on an island with only her uncle and other men — no contact with women. They then saw a photo of her grown up and were asked about her physical properties ("Will she grow up to be a mommy or a daddy?") and behavioral properties ("Will she like to play with a tea set or with trucks?"). A parallel boy-on-female-island scenario was also used.

Who participated & what was the design?

68 5-year-olds, 64 10-year-olds, and 32 college students. Half girls at each age. The design was experimental (physical vs behavioral domain comparison) and cross-sectional (three age groups). No ethical concerns — children enjoyed hearing stories about island babies.

What were the results?

For physical properties: participants of all ages agreed the baby would acquire the biological-sex-typical features regardless of the rearing environment (the girl would still grow up with female anatomy). For behavioral properties, however, there was a steady downward developmental trend: 5-year-olds thought boys and girls would necessarily engage in gender-stereotypic behaviors, while adults believed a boy raised by women would behave in feminine-stereotypic ways and a girl raised by men would behave in masculine-stereotypic ways.

What did the investigators conclude?

In Taylor, Rhodes, and Gelman's words: "young children treat the concepts of 'boy' and 'girl' as equivalent to species, in the extent to which features are inborn, inflexible, and intrinsically linked to category membership… [By] adulthood, participants viewed both male and female behavior as more open to the environment and flexible." Children develop gender essentialism early and only gradually come to see behavior as environmentally shaped. (A secondary finding: participants at all ages thought girls were more open to masculine influence than boys were to feminine influence.)

What would strengthen the conclusions?

The sample was mostly European American, from Midwest US university towns. Replication with children from different cultures and backgrounds would address generalizability. Extending the property list to psychological properties (personality traits) would clarify whether children treat those more like physical or behavioral characteristics.

How does this vary, and why does it matter?

The differentiation between physical and behavioral properties strengthens around age 9, mapping onto the general decline of essentialist thinking (see Meyer et al., 2017, Ch 4). The shift isn't universal in timing: children in environments with rigid gender roles hold essentialist beliefs longer, while children in egalitarian environments shift earlier, and educational interventions can accelerate it. Clinical/educational implications: (1) young children's rigid stereotypes reflect a cognitive limitation, not personal conviction, so modeling and counter-stereotypical exposure work better than direct argument ("girls can be doctors too!") which may not penetrate the essentialist worldview yet; (2) the ~6–9 window is a productive time for diversity education; (3) gender-affirming care for gender-diverse children must navigate this developmental cognition while recognizing that gender identity is distinct from beliefs about what gender determines; (4) health-related stereotypes ("boys don't cry," "girls are bad at math") follow similar patterns and can be addressed.

A 5-year-old is asked: "If a baby girl was raised on an island with only men, what would she be like when she grew up?" She insists: "She'd still be a girl, and she'd still like dolls and dresses." What does this illustrate about young children's understanding of gender, and how does it differ from adults?
The 5-year-old is showing gender essentialism — treating gender categories as if features are inborn, inflexible, and intrinsically linked to category membership (Taylor, Rhodes, & Gelman, 2009). She doesn't distinguish between physical properties (which are biologically tied to sex) and behavioral properties (which are environmentally shaped). Both feel equally fixed to her. The developmental shift: by age 10, children show more flexibility in behavioral properties; by adulthood, people see behavior as substantially shaped by environment while recognizing physical sex characteristics as biological. Young children essentially see "boy" and "girl" the way they see "cat" and "dog" — as natural species categories with inherent essences. This is why gender stereotypes can be so rigid in early childhood and why direct counter-stereotypical exposure is one of the more effective interventions.
An 8-month-old, a 2-year-old, and a 4-year-old are all upset because their cookie was taken away. Describe how each is likely to regulate their distress and what skills the older children have that the younger one doesn't.
8-month-old: relies almost entirely on the caregiver for regulation. May cry inconsolably, seek physical comfort, or use simple self-soothing (thumb-sucking, looking away). The infant has very little independent emotion regulation capacity. 2-year-old: has begun developing simple self-regulation strategies — may try a different activity, look for a substitute, briefly protest then move on. Still strongly relies on caregivers but is beginning to use distraction and self-soothing intentionally. 4-year-old: has substantially more flexible regulation. Can use distraction deliberately, talk to themselves about their feelings, reappraise the situation ("Mom said maybe after dinner — I'll get one then"), seek out an adult to discuss the feeling, or simply tolerate the disappointment. Why this matters: early emotion regulation is one of the stronger predictors of later social competence, academic success, and mental health. Skills develop with both maturation AND repeated experience of being co-regulated by caregivers — which is why warm responsive caregiving in early years matters so much.
In the Strange Situation, an infant freezes when the parent returns, then approaches, then turns away, then approaches again — showing contradictory and confused behavior. Which attachment category does this fit, why is it concerning, and what is it most often associated with?
This is disorganized attachment — characterized by contradictory, confused, or "frozen" behavior at reunion that doesn't fit any single coherent strategy (avoidant, resistant, or secure). It accounts for roughly 15% of infants in low-risk samples but is dramatically over-represented in maltreatment populations. Most often associated with: caregivers who are themselves frightened or frightening (often due to unresolved trauma, severe mental illness, or being themselves abusive). The infant has no coherent strategy because the caregiver who should be the source of comfort is also the source of fear. Why it's concerning: disorganized attachment in infancy is one of the strongest predictors of later psychopathology — dissociative disorders, conduct problems, and risk for borderline personality features. Clinically: disorganized attachment in a clinical assessment warrants careful examination of the caregiving environment, including possible maltreatment, and connection to interventions like the Circle of Security or attachment-based parenting programs that have evidence in this population.

Test Yourself · Section 5.4

  1. By approximately what age can most children correctly label their own gender (gender identity)?
  2. By approximately what age do most children achieve gender constancy (understanding that gender doesn't change with appearance)?
  3. What is a gender schema, and how does it influence what children pay attention to and remember?
  4. Based on Taylor et al. (2009), do young children treat physical and behavioral gender properties the same way? When does this view start to change?
  5. Name one factor that influences how rigid or flexible a child's gender schemas become.

Evolving gender roles

Gender roles change with the times

Gender roles are not etched in stone — they change with the times. In the US, the range of acceptable roles for girls/boys and women/men has never been wider than today (Liben, 2016). Some fathers stay home as primary caregivers; some mothers work full-time as sole family support. Same-sex parents, transgender parents, and non-binary parents are increasingly visible. What's the impact of these changes on children's gender development?

Family Lifestyles Project (Weisner et al., 1994)

This long-running research followed families where adults were committed to rearing their children without traditional gender stereotypes — sharing household, financial, and childcare tasks equally. Results revealed which aspects of gender development parents can influence and which are more resistant.

More easily changed (occupational stereotypes)

Children raised in egalitarian homes had fewer stereotypes about occupations. They agreed that girls can be president of the United States and drive trucks; that boys can be nurses and secretaries. Future role possibilities are remarkably open to parental influence.

More easily changed (object use)

Children also had fewer stereotypes about object use — they claimed boys and girls are equally likely to use an iron, a shovel, a hammer and nails, a needle and thread. Tool use is malleable by exposure and modeling.

Less easily changed (friendships)

Even children raised egalitarian-minded parents still tended to have same-sex friends. Peer self-segregation by gender is a robust phenomenon difficult for parents to override.

Less easily changed (play preferences)

The boys still enjoyed physical play; the girls still enjoyed drawing and reading. Some sex-typed activity preferences persist despite egalitarian parenting — likely reflecting some combination of biological tendencies, peer culture, and broader cultural messages parents can't fully shield against.

The interpretation: gender roles are highly responsive to culture and socialization in some domains (career, capability beliefs, role flexibility) and less malleable in others (peer affiliation patterns, some play preferences). For most of human history, social structures organized labor by gender — these patterns leave a long developmental shadow that cultural change shifts gradually, not instantly (Eagly & Wood, 2013).

What this means for practice: parents who want to raise children with flexible gender views will succeed most clearly in beliefs about capabilities and future possibilities (career, education, leadership). They'll succeed less easily in peer-based domains (friendships, play partners). Both kinds of change are meaningful — the former enables life options, the latter changes only with broad cultural shift.

Diverse Perspectives — single-sex schools or coeducational?

The contested terrain

In the US today, more than 1,500 schools segregate boys and girls — sometimes in separate classes within a school, sometimes in entirely separate schools. The question of whether this benefits children is genuinely contested, with strong views on both sides drawing on different evidence.

Arguments for single-sex education

  • Different learning styles — proponents argue boys and girls learn differently and single-sex education lets teachers cater to each. (Cool colors and high activity for boys; warm colors and less activity for girls is one model — though scientifically contested.)
  • Less harassment — girls interested in science are often teased by male classmates; same-sex environments may reduce this.
  • Teacher attention — in coed classes teachers devote more attention to boys; single-sex classes may redirect attention to girls.

Arguments against single-sex education

  • Boys and girls don't fundamentally learn differently — the brain-difference argument is overstated; individual variation within genders far exceeds average between-gender differences.
  • Segregation analogy — gender segregation may be no more justified than racial or socioeconomic segregation; coeducational environments help children become more tolerant and cooperative across differences.
  • Stereotypes are reinforced — in all-boy classes, boys' views of girls are based on stereotypes (not real interaction); same for all-girl classes (Pahlke & Hyde, 2016).
  • Binary framework — single-sex schooling reinforces an outmoded binary view of gender, where male/female are seen as distinct nonoverlapping categories (Hyde et al., 2019). Excludes transgender, non-binary, and gender-fluid students.

What the evidence says

The challenge: students attending single-sex schools typically have higher achievement before they attend those schools and come from more affluent families. So any apparent benefit could be selection bias, not the schools.

Best evidence: Pahlke et al. (2014) meta-analysis of more than 1.5 million students from 21 countries. Findings:

  • Overall: students in single-sex and coeducational schools did NOT differ in achievement, self-concept, or stereotypes.
  • Some specific differences: boys did better in math when attending single-sex SCHOOLS, but not when attending single-sex CLASSES within coed schools.
  • Girls' verbal skills: greater in single-sex CLASSES within coed schools, but not in single-sex schools.

The honest summary: single-sex education may benefit some children, some of the time, for some specific outcomes — but is NOT a panacea that boosts achievement across the board. The variation suggests other factors (teacher quality, curriculum, school resources, family characteristics) likely matter more than same/mixed-sex composition.

What the textbook asks

(1) What earlier research in this chapter supports single-sex schooling? (Hint: developmental peer self-segregation, harassment effects.) What supports coeducational? (Hint: gender stereotype reduction through contact, broader social learning.) (2) How does the Taylor et al. essentialism study inform this debate — does separating children by gender risk amplifying their early-developing gender essentialism? (3) Under what circumstances might single-sex schooling be more effective? (4) How might these findings extend (or not) to non-binary students? (5) Which children might benefit relatively more — students from particular backgrounds, with particular goals, in particular subjects?

5.5 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Erik Erikson — 8 psychosocial stages; first stage (trust vs. mistrust, 0–1 yr) sets foundation for all later stages.
  • John Bowlby — Attachment theory; attachment is a biological-evolutionary system for protection. Concept of internal working model: early attachment shapes a template for later relationships.
  • Mary Ainsworth — Strange Situation paradigm. Identified attachment categories: secure, avoidant, resistant (ambivalent). Mary Main later added disorganized.
  • Harry Harlow — Rhesus monkey contact-comfort studies. Showed attachment isn't just about feeding — infant monkeys preferred a soft cloth "mother" with no food over a wire "mother" providing milk. Refuted behaviorist accounts of attachment.
  • Diana Baumrind — Identified parenting styles based on warmth × control: authoritative (warm + firm), authoritarian (cold + firm), permissive (warm + lax), uninvolved (cold + lax). Authoritative most strongly linked to positive outcomes.
  • Mildred Parten — Classic taxonomy of play: unoccupied → onlooker → solitary → parallel → associative → cooperative. Sequence is still descriptive of preschool play patterns.
  • Carol Dweck — Theory of mindset (fixed vs growth); applied to gender and learning. Children praised for effort rather than ability show more persistence.
  • Sandra Bem — Gender-schema theory; children learn cultural gender schemas, then organize new information around them. Bem also proposed psychological androgyny (people can be high on both masculine and feminine traits).
  • Eleanor Maccoby — Documented gender segregation in childhood play and the "two cultures" of male and female peer groups.

Specific studies cited

  • Ainsworth Strange Situation (1978): 20-minute lab procedure with separations and reunions. Foundation of modern attachment research; the secure base concept is operationalized through reunion behavior.
  • Harlow's contact-comfort studies (1958): Demonstrated infant monkeys prefer soft "mothers" without food over wire "mothers" with food. Established the importance of contact comfort.
  • Romanian orphanage studies (Bucharest Early Intervention Project): Severe early deprivation produces lasting attachment and developmental deficits; high-quality foster care before 24 months improves outcomes substantially.
  • NICHD Study of Early Child Care: Large longitudinal study; quality of care matters more than care setting (home vs day-care); secure attachment is possible across diverse care arrangements when caregiving is responsive.
  • Bandura's Bobo doll experiments (1961, 1963): Children imitate aggression they see modeled, especially when the model is rewarded. Foundational for understanding aggression and observational learning.
  • Twin studies of prosocial behavior: Show moderate heritability for prosocial tendencies, mediated by oxytocin, behavioral control, and emotion regulation.

5.6 · Key Terms & Definitions

The vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter.

Glossary by category

Erikson stages & attachment (11 terms)

  • Hope — Strength acquired by resolving Erikson's stage 1 (trust vs. mistrust, 0–1 yr).
  • Will — Strength acquired by resolving stage 2 (autonomy vs. shame & doubt, 1–3 yr).
  • Purpose — Strength acquired by resolving stage 3 (initiative vs. guilt, 3–5 yr).
  • Evolutionary psychology — The view that behavioral tendencies (including attachment) evolved because they aided survival of ancestors.
  • Attachment — Enduring emotional bond formed between an infant and a primary caregiver.
  • Secure attachment — Infant uses caregiver as a secure base; explores when caregiver is present, distressed at separation, comforted by reunion (~65% of US infants).
  • Avoidant attachment — Infant shows little distress at separation, ignores caregiver at reunion. Often reflects rejecting/unresponsive caregiving (~20%).
  • Resistant (ambivalent) attachment — Infant is intensely upset at separation but mixed/angry at reunion. Often reflects inconsistent caregiving (~10%).
  • Disorganized (disoriented) attachment — Infant shows contradictory behaviors — approaches and withdraws, freezes. Associated with frightening or trauma-related caregiving (~5%).
  • Internal working model — Bowlby's term for the mental representation of self, others, and relationships, formed from early caregiving experience.
  • Basic emotions — A small set of emotions (joy, anger, fear, sadness, disgust) appearing in the first year, expressed similarly across cultures.

Emotions, play, prosocial (10 terms)

  • Social smiles — Smiling directed at another person; emerges ~2 months. Cornerstone of early social bonding.
  • Stranger wariness — Fear or caution toward unfamiliar people; emerges ~7–8 months, peaks around 12 months, declines thereafter.
  • Social referencing — Looking to a caregiver's emotional expression to decide how to feel about an ambiguous situation. Common by 12 months.
  • Parallel play — Children play near each other but not with each other; typical of toddlers (~18 months–3 years).
  • Simple social play — Children engage in similar play activities and exchange social bids; emerges around 18–24 months.
  • Cooperative play — Children play together with shared goals and roles; emerges around 2–3 years.
  • Enabling actions — Statements/behaviors that support the partner's contribution (more characteristic of girls' interaction style in childhood).
  • Constricting actions — Statements/behaviors that limit the partner's contribution (more characteristic of boys' interaction style).
  • Prosocial behavior — Voluntary behavior intended to benefit another (helping, sharing, cooperating, comforting).
  • Empathy — Feeling another's emotion as if it were one's own; develops from rudimentary contagion in infancy to full perspective-taking by middle childhood.

Gender development (6 terms)

  • Social role — Set of cultural expectations about how members of a social category (e.g., a gender, occupation) should behave.
  • Gender stereotypes — Beliefs about characteristics of females and males. Children know these well by age 4.
  • Relational aggression — Harming others through manipulating relationships (exclusion, rumor-spreading); more characteristic of girls' aggression patterns. (Boys show more physical aggression on average.)
  • Gender identity — One's internal sense of being female, male, both, neither, or another gender.
  • Gender-schema theory — Bem's theory that children develop mental schemas for gender, then organize and remember new information based on these schemas.
  • Gender constancy — Understanding that gender is stable across time and situations (a girl will be a woman; she remains a girl even if she dresses differently). Develops ~5–7 years.

Clinical & supplementary terms (17 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • Strange Situation (Ainsworth) — Standardized 20-minute laboratory procedure for assessing infant attachment style, involving structured separations and reunions with caregiver. Yields four classifications: secure, insecure-avoidant, insecure-resistant/ambivalent, and (added later) disorganized.
  • Insecure-avoidant attachment — Infant shows little distress at separation and avoids caregiver on reunion. Often associated with rejecting or emotionally unavailable caregiving. Doesn't mean the child isn't bonded — they have learned to suppress attachment behaviors.
  • Insecure-resistant / ambivalent attachment — Infant is highly distressed at separation but not comforted on reunion (clinging while also resisting). Often associated with inconsistent caregiving — caregiver responds sometimes but not predictably.
  • Disorganized attachment — Infant shows contradictory or disoriented behaviors on reunion (e.g., approaching with averted gaze, freezing). Often associated with frightening or frightened caregiver behavior, sometimes linked to trauma or unresolved loss in the caregiver. Highest risk classification for later psychopathology.
  • Circle of Security — Evidence-based attachment-focused intervention that helps caregivers recognize and respond appropriately to children's needs for exploration ('top of the circle') and comfort ('bottom of the circle'). Developed by Marvin, Cooper, Hoffman, Powell.
  • Co-regulation — Process by which a caregiver helps a child manage emotions and arousal — through soothing tone, physical presence, modeling calm, naming feelings, removing or modifying stressors. Children's capacity for self-regulation develops THROUGH repeated experiences of co-regulation.
  • Separation anxiety (developmental) — Normal distress at separation from primary caregivers, peaking 8-18 months as object permanence develops (child now understands the caregiver still exists when out of sight, but can't yet trust their return). Distinct from clinical separation anxiety disorder.
  • Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) — Neurodevelopmental condition characterized by persistent difficulties in social communication and interaction, and restricted/repetitive behaviors and interests. Wide variation in presentation and support needs. Early identification (often via deficits in joint attention, eye contact, name response) enables evidence-based early intervention.
  • Hope, will, and purpose (Erikson's virtues) — The three psychosocial strengths emerging from successful resolution of Erikson's first three stages. Hope from trust vs. mistrust — openness to new experience with healthy caution. Will from autonomy vs. shame/doubt — confidence in intentional action within limits. Purpose from initiative vs. guilt — courage to envision and pursue valued goals. Each is a balance, not a victory of one pole over another.
  • Internal working model (Bowlby) — Mental representation of how responsive and dependable caregivers (and by extension others) are. Built from repeated experiences in infancy. Becomes the template for friendships and romantic relationships throughout life. Influential but not deterministic — can shift with corrective relationships and therapy.
  • NICHD Study of Early Child Care and Youth Development — Landmark US longitudinal study of over 1,300 children from birth through adolescence, examining how childcare affects development. Main finding: maternal sensitivity at home matters more than whether the child is in childcare. Quality of childcare also matters; quantity has only modest effects.
  • NAEYC quality standards — National Association for the Education of Young Children's quality benchmarks for early childhood programs. Used for accreditation and as research-based quality indicators (small group sizes, qualified staff, low turnover, responsive interactions, developmentally appropriate practice).
  • Family Lifestyles Project (Weisner et al., 1994) — Long-running research following families committed to egalitarian gender roles. Showed parents can influence children's beliefs about occupations and object use but had less success changing children's peer friendships and play preferences. Some aspects of gender development are more malleable than others.
  • Gender essentialism (developmental) — Young children's tendency to treat gender as a single uniform essence that determines everything about a person (physical traits AND behaviors). Differentiates around ages 6-9 as children realize behavioral properties are environmentally shaped while physical properties are biologically determined (Taylor et al., 2009).
  • Pahlke & Hyde meta-analysis (2014) — Meta-analysis of more than 1.5 million students across 21 countries examining single-sex vs coeducational education. Overall: no consistent differences in achievement, self-concept, or stereotypes. Suggests school composition matters less than teacher quality, curriculum, and resources.
  • Gender-affirming care — Healthcare model that supports transgender and gender-diverse individuals in their identified gender. Includes social transition support, mental health care, and (for older adolescents/adults) medical interventions. Evidence base supports better mental health outcomes when affirmation is consistent across family, school, and healthcare settings.
  • Bidirectional influence (parenting) — Recognition that children also shape their parents — temperament, behavior, needs, and responses change how parents parent. Modern attachment research moves beyond unidirectional "parent → child" models to acknowledge mutual influence. Important for not blaming parents for difficult children, and for noticing how interventions changing the child can also help the parent.

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — a 4-year-old grabs her friend's crayons. Her dad uses reasoning to teach prosocial behavior.

Joshua, Lily & the crayons — prosocial behavior case

The case: Joshua, Lily, & the crayons

Joshua's 4-year-old daughter Lily is playing with her friend Aiden. Lily asks Aiden if she can borrow his crayons. Aiden refuses. Lily pushes him aside and grabs the crayons anyway.

Joshua intervenes. Instead of just punishing Lily or making her return the crayons, he asks: "How do you think Aiden felt when you pushed him? Happy or sad?" Lily: "I dunno." Joshua: "I think you know." He continues: "Pushing him hurts. And taking things from someone after they say no — that makes them angry and unhappy. Next time, ask. And if they say no, you have to respect that."

What discipline style is Joshua using? Why is it predicted to be more effective than power-assertive alternatives (yelling, time-out, taking the crayons back)? Connect this to the Big Three drivers of prosocial behavior from the chapter.

Worked analysis

The style: inductive discipline

Joshua is using inductive discipline — explaining why a behavior is problematic in terms of its impact on another person. Fits within the authoritative parenting framework: warm + explanation-driven control.

Why it works

It builds internal motivation — Lily isn't just avoiding punishment, she's learning a moral principle she can apply elsewhere. Power-assertive discipline (force, threats) tends to produce compliance only when the adult is watching.

The Big Three drivers

Prosocial behavior is most reliably fostered by modeling (Joshua respects Lily's no's too), inductive reasoning (this conversation), and warm responsive parenting (he listens; he doesn't shame her). Joshua hits all three.

The empathy hook

"How do you think Aiden felt?" forces Lily to take Aiden's perspective. That perspective-taking is what makes prosocial behavior work — without it, sharing is just a rule. With it, it's an understanding of how to be a good friend.

The lesson the textbook draws

Telling children to be nice doesn't work very well. Showing them how their behavior affects others — repeatedly, calmly, in their own moments — is one of the strongest predictors of which kids grow into prosocial adults. The parent's tone matters as much as the content; warm reasoning beats cold lecturing.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 5 core content.

Q1

In the Strange Situation, a 13-month-old plays calmly while their parent is present, shows little distress when the parent leaves, and actively turns away when the parent returns. Which attachment classification fits, and what caregiving pattern is most often associated with it?

  1. A. Secure — sensitive responsive caregiving
  2. B. Avoidant — emotionally distant or rejecting caregiving
  3. C. Resistant — inconsistent caregiving
  4. D. Disorganized — frightening or maltreating caregiving
Show rationale

A. Secure infants are visibly distressed at separation and seek contact at reunion. This child is showing the opposite pattern.

B. CORRECT. Minimal distress at separation plus active avoidance at reunion is the textbook avoidant pattern. Linked to caregivers who are emotionally unavailable or who consistently reject bids for closeness — the infant has learned not to expect comfort.

C. Resistant infants are highly distressed at separation and simultaneously seek and resist comfort at reunion. Different pattern.

D. Disorganized infants show contradictory, confused, or frozen behavior at reunion — not consistent avoidance.

Q2

A parent sets clear rules, explains the reasoning behind them ("we don't hit because it hurts others"), listens to the child's input, and adjusts as the child grows. Which parenting style is this, and what outcome profile does research predict?

  1. A. Authoritarian — obedient but lower self-esteem
  2. B. Permissive — happy but poor self-regulation
  3. C. Authoritative — competent, confident, socially skilled
  4. D. Uninvolved — significant social and academic risk
Show rationale

A. Authoritarian = high control + low warmth; "because I said so" without explanation or input. The parent described listens and explains.

B. Permissive = high warmth + low control; few rules. The parent described has clear rules.

C. CORRECT. Clear rules + reasoning + responsiveness = authoritative parenting. Across cultures and decades, associated with the best outcomes: high competence, good self-regulation, social skills, school success.

D. Uninvolved = low everything. Not what's described.

Q3

At approximately what age do self-conscious emotions like embarrassment, pride, guilt, and shame first appear, and why don't they appear earlier?

  1. A. ~6 months — they appear alongside basic fear and anger
  2. B. ~12 months — they appear alongside social referencing
  3. C. ~18 months — they require a concept of self (the mirror test)
  4. D. ~4 years — they require theory of mind
Show rationale

A. Basic emotions (joy, anger, fear, sadness) appear in the first year. Self-conscious emotions do not.

B. Social referencing develops at ~12 months but it doesn't require self-recognition. Self-conscious emotions do.

C. CORRECT. Self-conscious emotions require the child to have a concept of self — to recognize themselves as an entity that can be observed and evaluated. That capacity appears around 18–24 months (the mirror / rouge test). Embarrassment shows up almost immediately after self-recognition; pride, guilt, and shame develop more gradually as standards are internalized.

D. Theory of mind helps refine self-conscious emotions (especially guilt and shame in social contexts), but the basic emotions themselves appear before mature theory of mind.

Q4

An 18-month-old sees their parent struggling to pick up a dropped pile of papers. Without being asked, the toddler walks over and helps pick them up. What does this most directly demonstrate?

  1. A. Theory of mind — the toddler understands the parent's beliefs
  2. B. Early spontaneous prosocial behavior
  3. C. Gender schema — the toddler is imitating a same-gender adult
  4. D. Conservation of number
Show rationale

A. Theory of mind in its mature form (false belief understanding) doesn't appear until ~age 4. Toddler helping is more basic.

B. CORRECT. One of the more striking findings in developmental psychology — infants as young as 14–18 months spontaneously help an adult who can't reach a fallen object. Doesn't require theory of mind or training. It suggests prosocial impulses are present remarkably early.

C. Possible contributor but doesn't explain the behavior itself, which appears regardless of the adult's gender.

D. Wrong domain — conservation is a Piagetian concept about quantity.

Q5

A 5-year-old says "If a girl cuts her hair short and wears boy's clothes, she becomes a boy." What does this best demonstrate about their understanding of gender, and what milestone are they likely to reach next?

  1. A. They have gender identity but not yet gender constancy; gender constancy typically develops around age 6
  2. B. They have full mature gender understanding
  3. C. They have no concept of gender yet
  4. D. They have theory of mind
Show rationale

A. CORRECT. The child clearly knows what gender is (gender identity) and can label others. But they haven't yet understood that gender doesn't change with superficial appearance — that's gender constancy, typically achieved around age 6. The sequence is: gender identity (~age 2–3) → gender stability across time (~age 4) → gender constancy across appearance (~age 6).

B. Mature understanding includes gender constancy. This child lacks it.

C. They can identify boys and girls and label themselves. They have a concept; it's just incomplete.

D. Theory of mind is about beliefs and minds. This question is about gender understanding, a different developmental track.

Q6

Taylor, Rhodes, and Gelman (2009) found that 5-year-olds expected a baby girl raised entirely on an island of men to grow up with both feminine physical features AND feminine behavioral preferences. By adulthood, participants expected the physical features but thought the behavioral preferences would be shaped by environment. What does this developmental shift reflect?

  1. A. Children become less interested in gender as they age
  2. B. Children initially show strong gender essentialism — treating both physical and behavioral gender properties as inborn and inflexible; as they develop, they distinguish biological features from environmentally-shaped behavior, but the essentialism about behavior persists into elementary school and only fully softens by adulthood
  3. C. Adults have more rigid gender beliefs than children
  4. D. Cross-sectional designs produce unreliable findings
Show rationale

A. The shift isn't about interest level — it's about how children categorize gender properties.

B. CORRECT. Young children show pronounced gender essentialism — treating both physical AND behavioral gender properties as if they were inborn, inflexible, and intrinsically linked to category membership (similar to how they think about animal species). The developmental change isn't in physical-property judgments (those remain biological at all ages) but in behavioral-property judgments: gradually, with age, people come to see behavior as more open to environmental shaping. This has practical implications for gender stereotypes — they're hardest to flexibility-shift in early childhood precisely because children are most essentialist then.

C. Adults are typically LESS rigid about behavioral aspects of gender than young children are.

D. Cross-sectional designs have limitations (cohort effects), but the finding here is consistent with other research and developmental theory.

Q7

In the Strange Situation, a 12-month-old reacts to the parent's return after separation with contradictory behavior: she approaches, then freezes, looks away, approaches again, then turns away in a confused pattern. What attachment classification does this fit, and what's its clinical significance?

  1. A. Secure attachment; no significance
  2. B. Disorganized attachment; clinically significant because it's strongly associated with caregivers who are themselves frightened or frightening (often due to unresolved trauma, severe mental illness, or maltreatment), and it predicts elevated risk for later psychopathology including dissociative disorders and conduct problems
  3. C. Avoidant attachment; no concern
  4. D. Resistant attachment; means the child is hungry
Show rationale

A. Secure attachment shows comfort-seeking at reunion that's effective in reducing distress. Not described here.

B. CORRECT. Disorganized attachment is characterized by contradictory, confused, or "frozen" behavior at reunion that doesn't fit any coherent strategy. It's the most concerning classification: ~15% in low-risk samples but dramatically over-represented in maltreatment populations. The mechanism: the caregiver who should provide comfort is also a source of fear (often due to unresolved trauma or, in worst cases, abuse), leaving the infant with no workable strategy. Disorganized attachment in infancy is among the strongest predictors of later psychopathology. Clinical follow-up should include careful evaluation of the caregiving environment.

C. Avoidant attachment is consistent withdrawal from the parent at reunion, not confused/contradictory behavior.

D. Resistant (ambivalent) attachment shows angry rejection alongside contact-seeking. Not about hunger.

Q8

Compared to an 8-month-old, a 4-year-old upset by a frustrating situation has access to more sophisticated emotion regulation strategies. Which best describes the developmental sequence?

  1. A. Emotion regulation is fully developed at birth
  2. B. 8-month-old: depends on caregivers and simple self-soothing (sucking, looking away); 2-year-old: beginning intentional distraction; 4-year-old: can use deliberate distraction, self-talk about feelings, reappraisal, and seeking out an adult; skills develop with both maturation AND repeated co-regulation experiences with responsive caregivers
  3. C. Emotion regulation is purely a function of personality, not development
  4. D. By age 4, children are emotionally identical to adults
Show rationale

A. Newborns have almost no emotion regulation capacity — they depend entirely on caregivers for soothing.

B. CORRECT. Emotion regulation develops gradually through a combination of brain maturation (especially prefrontal cortex development supporting executive function) and repeated co-regulation experiences with responsive caregivers. The sequence: 8-mo dependence on caregiver + simple self-soothing → 2-yr beginning distraction + still strong caregiver reliance → 4-yr flexible strategies including reappraisal and verbal self-regulation. This developmental sequence is one of the strongest predictors of later social competence, academic success, and mental health — which is why warm responsive early caregiving has such outsized importance.

C. Strong evidence shows experience shapes emotion regulation development.

D. 4-year-olds have made significant progress but still develop emotion regulation through adolescence.

Q9

An L&D nurse is teaching new parents about responsive caregiving and attachment. Which of the following best aligns with Bowlby and Ainsworth's evidence-based recommendations?

  1. A. Don't pick up the baby when crying or you'll spoil them
  2. B. Respond consistently and sensitively to infant cues (feeding, comforting, eye contact, talking) — secure attachment is built through responsive caregiving over the first year; "spoiling" through responsiveness in infancy is a myth; secure attachment predicts better emotional, social, and cognitive outcomes
  3. C. Strictly schedule feedings every 3 hours regardless of cues
  4. D. Let the baby self-soothe for at least 15 minutes before responding
Show rationale

A. Decades of attachment research refute this. Responsive caregiving builds secure attachment, not spoiling.

B. CORRECT. The evidence from Bowlby, Ainsworth, and decades of attachment research is clear: sensitivity and consistency of caregiving in the first year predict secure attachment. Secure attachment in turn predicts better emotion regulation, social competence, academic achievement, and mental health outcomes. The "don't spoil them" idea was a behaviorist holdover that's been overturned. Specific guidance: respond to cues, hold often, make eye contact, talk to the baby, respond to crying within reasonable time. For nursing: parental responsiveness can be coached; some new parents need explicit reassurance that responding is the RIGHT thing to do.

C. Demand feeding (responding to hunger cues) is recommended for breastfed infants in particular; rigid scheduling can suppress feeding cues and reduce milk supply.

D. Extinction crying for newborns is not evidence-based and undermines attachment development. (Note: this is different from later sleep-training around 6+ months, which is a separate debate.)

Q10

A pediatric nurse evaluates a 14-month-old in the Strange Situation. When separated from mother, the child shows distress. When mother returns, the child approaches BUT then arches backward, looks away, and resists being held while still seeking proximity. Which attachment pattern does this exemplify, and what caregiving pattern is most often associated?

  1. A. Secure attachment
  2. B. Resistant (ambivalent) attachment — characterized by clear distress at separation AND difficulty being soothed at reunion, with mixed approach/resistance behaviors; most often associated with inconsistent caregiving (sometimes responsive, sometimes not), making the child uncertain whether their cues will be answered
  3. C. Avoidant attachment
  4. D. Disorganized attachment
Show rationale

A. Secure infants are distressed at separation but easily comforted at reunion. This child resists comfort.

B. CORRECT. Resistant (ambivalent) attachment — about 10% of US infants. Pattern: high distress at separation, mixed approach/resistance at reunion, difficulty being comforted. The child wants the caregiver but can't fully use them as a secure base. Caregiving correlate: inconsistent responsiveness — sometimes warm and available, sometimes distant or intrusive. The child can't predict caregiver response so they maintain anxious vigilance. Long-term implications: increased risk for anxious-preoccupied adult attachment style; clinical interventions (parent-infant therapy, video-feedback parenting interventions) can shift the pattern.

C. Avoidant infants show LITTLE distress at separation and IGNORE the caregiver at reunion — opposite pattern.

D. Disorganized infants show contradictory or disoriented behaviors (freezing, conflicting approach-withdraw); usually associated with frightening caregiving or trauma history.

Q11

A teen mother of a 3-year-old states: "I just demand respect. He does what I say or there are consequences. I don't waste time explaining." Which Baumrind parenting style does this describe, and what outcomes does evidence suggest?

  1. A. Authoritative — best outcomes
  2. B. Authoritarian (high control, low warmth) — children tend to be obedient but show poorer self-esteem, more anxiety/depression, less internalized morality, and worse social skills than children of authoritative parents; effective alternative is authoritative parenting which combines firm expectations WITH warmth, reasoning, and listening
  3. C. Permissive — best outcomes
  4. D. Uninvolved — neutral effects
Show rationale

A. Authoritative combines high control with high warmth and explanation. This description has high control but explicitly low warmth/explanation.

B. CORRECT. Authoritarian parenting = high control + low warmth + minimal explanation. Outcomes: short-term obedience but children show poorer self-esteem, more anxiety/depression, less internalized morality (do right only when watched), worse social skills, and difficulty with autonomous decision-making in adolescence. Authoritative alternative: high control PLUS high warmth, reasoning ("we don't hit because it hurts"), age-appropriate listening, and consistent limits enforced kindly. Evidence is robust across cultures (though with some cultural variation in optimal blend). For nursing/social work: parent coaching to shift from authoritarian to authoritative is one of the strongest interventions for child behavior problems.

C. Permissive (high warmth, low control) produces impulsive, less self-regulated children; not the best outcomes.

D. Uninvolved parenting (low warmth, low control) is associated with the WORST outcomes across multiple domains.

Q12

A school nurse encounters a 9-year-old girl whose mother reports she was "completely socially fine until 7th grade — now girls in her class are leaving her out of group chats and a girl is spreading rumors." The nurse should:

  1. A. Reassure that this is normal social development
  2. B. Recognize this as relational aggression — a form of aggression peaking in early adolescence, more common among girls but harming all genders; effects can be as serious as physical aggression including anxiety, depression, school avoidance; appropriate response includes screening for mental health symptoms, validating the child's experience, contacting school counselor, and engaging family in problem-solving
  3. C. Tell the child to "just ignore it"
  4. D. This isn't a nursing concern
Show rationale

A. While relational aggression IS common, it's not benign — minimizing harms the child.

B. CORRECT. Relational aggression (excluding, rumor-spreading, social manipulation) is real aggression and produces real harm — comparable mental health impact to physical bullying. Patterns: peaks early adolescence, somewhat more common among girls but boys experience it too, can be invisible to teachers/parents because it operates in social channels. Nursing role: (1) Validate the child's experience — they're not exaggerating. (2) Screen for depression, anxiety, school avoidance, sleep changes, self-harm. (3) Coordinate with school counselor and admin — schools have anti-bullying policies that include relational aggression. (4) Educate family that ignoring rarely works; structured intervention does. (5) Connect with mental health resources if symptoms emerge. Online/social media amplification means relational aggression is now 24/7; addressing it requires social-media awareness.

C. Telling children to ignore bullying often makes things worse — the bullies escalate, the child feels unsupported.

D. School nursing absolutely includes mental health and social-emotional health — this is core scope.

Unit 2 · School-Age & Adolescence · Chapter 6

Off to School: Cognitive & Physical Development in Middle Childhood

TL;DR · One-glance summary

From age 6 to ~11, children become genuine logical thinkers about concrete reality, fail formal-operational tasks but pass conservation, and acquire the academic skills (reading, math, writing) that will carry them forward. Intelligence is a contested concept — Sternberg proposes 3 components, Gardner proposes 8+. Individual differences in IQ are substantial and reflect both heredity and environment. ADHD and learning disabilities affect a meaningful minority. Physical development continues steadily; childhood obesity is now a leading public- health concern.

Cognitive change

  • Concrete operational (~7–11): logic about concrete things
  • Pass conservation (number, mass, liquid, volume)
  • Memory strategies (rehearsal, organization) emerge
  • Better metacognition — thinking about thinking

Intelligence theories

  • Psychometric — IQ as one or a few factors
  • Sternberg triarchic — analytic, creative, practical
  • Gardner multiple — 8+ independent intelligences
  • Tests: Stanford-Binet, WISC; mean 100, SD 15

Differences & learning

  • IQ is moderately stable from school age
  • Stereotype threat affects performance
  • Learning disabilities: ~5–15% of children
  • ADHD: inattention & hyperactivity-impulsivity

Physical

  • Steady growth — ~2–3 in/yr, ~5 lb/yr
  • Motor refinement; gender differences emerge
  • Obesity: BMI ≥95th percentile
  • Accidents leading cause of death

6.1 · Cognitive Development

School-age children think more powerfully than preschoolers, but their reasoning still has clear limits. Piaget called this stage concrete operational; information-processing research adds detail about memory, attention, and strategy use.

Cognitive development in middle childhood

Piaget's concrete operational stage

~7 to 11 years

The hallmark: logical reasoning about concrete, observable things. Mental operations can now be reversed, decentered, and applied flexibly — but only to real or imagined-but-concrete situations. The transition out of the preoperational stage shows up most clearly in conservation tasks:

Conservation of number

Two rows of 6 pennies remain equal whether one is spread out or compressed. Mastered ~age 6–7.

Conservation of liquid

Pouring water from a wide glass into a tall one doesn't change the amount. Mastered ~age 7–8.

Conservation of mass

Rolling a ball of clay into a snake doesn't change its mass. Mastered ~age 7–8.

Conservation of volume

Two equal-volume objects displace the same water. Hardest — not until ~age 11.

The child can also seriate (order objects by length or weight), classify hierarchically ("dogs are animals; collies are dogs"), and use class inclusion. What they still can't do reliably is reason about purely abstract or hypothetical possibilities — that comes with formal operational thinking (Ch 8).

A 7-year-old correctly says that rolling a ball of clay into a snake doesn't change its mass, but insists that two equal-volume balls displace different amounts of water depending on shape. What does this pattern reveal about Piaget's stages?
The child has reached concrete operational (passing conservation of mass) but hasn't yet mastered conservation of volume — the last and most difficult conservation, often not achieved until ~age 11. Conservation isn't acquired all at once; it develops in a roughly predictable order: number → mass → liquid → length → weight → volume. This is sometimes called horizontal décalage — the same logical principle is applied at different ages depending on the content domain.

Information processing in middle childhood

Three big gains:

  • Working memory expands — span doubles from preschool to middle childhood.
  • Memory strategies emerge — rehearsal (~age 7), organization (~age 9), elaboration (later). Children must know a strategy and apply it spontaneously.
  • Metacognition — children become able to think about their own thinking, evaluate their understanding, and notice when they don't understand something.

Many "developmental" cognitive differences turn out to be strategy differences. Teach a preschooler to rehearse and their memory improves substantially. Stop cuing them and they tend not to rehearse spontaneously — they have the capacity but not yet the habit of strategic memory.

A school nurse helps a 9-year-old with diabetes learn to track blood sugar levels and recognize symptoms. The child keeps forgetting key information. Using middle-childhood cognitive development, how can the nurse teach more effectively?
At 9, the child has emerging but still-developing memory strategies and metamemory. They can use strategies if prompted but rarely apply them spontaneously. Teaching approach: (1) Explicit strategy teaching — don't assume they'll figure out HOW to remember. Teach specific strategies: "When you check, say the number out loud AND write it down — both ways helps it stick." (2) Use organization — group related information ("three things that mean LOW: shaky, sweaty, hungry"). (3) Use elaboration — link new info to existing knowledge ("low sugar is like running out of fuel — your body needs more to keep going"). (4) Build metamemory — teach the child to notice when they don't know something. "How sure are you? If you're not sure, what should you do?" (5) Concrete tools — visual diary, color-coded charts, smartphone app reminders. (6) Repetition with retrieval practice — quizzing self is more effective than rereading. (7) Involve family — caregivers scaffold these strategies until the child internalizes them. Why this matters: pediatric chronic disease self-management depends on memory and metacognition; teaching strategies explicitly improves adherence and safety.

6.2 · Aptitudes for School

What we call intelligence turns out to be a contested concept. The psychometric tradition treats it as one or a few measurable factors; alternative theories propose richer architectures.

Aptitudes & intelligence testing

Psychometric approach & IQ tests

The first practical intelligence test was developed by Binet in 1905 to identify French schoolchildren who needed extra help. The American version became the Stanford-Binet; the most widely-used modern tests are the Wechsler scales (WISC for children, WAIS for adults).

Modern IQ scores are calibrated to have mean = 100, SD = 15. About 68% of people score 85–115; about 95% score 70–130. Scores below 70 meet criteria (alongside adaptive behavior) for intellectual disability; above 130 is in the gifted range.

Two influential alternatives

Sternberg's triarchic theory

Three relatively independent forms: Analytic (academic problem-solving — what IQ tests measure), Creative (handling novelty), Practical (everyday street smarts). High performance in one doesn't predict high in another.

Gardner's multiple intelligences

At least 8 distinct intelligences with their own developmental trajectories: linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalist. Traditional IQ taps mainly the first two.

Both theories share a central claim: "intelligence" isn't one thing. Critics of multiple-intelligence approaches point out that the proposed intelligences correlate moderately in practice — suggesting some common factor underneath. The debate continues.

Sternberg's and Gardner's theories both push back on traditional IQ. What's the core difference between them?
Sternberg distinguishes three modes of intelligence based on the kind of problem you're solving — analytic (academic), creative (novel), practical (everyday). Gardner distinguishes eight (or more) domains — linguistic, musical, spatial, bodily-kinesthetic, etc. — each with its own neural substrate and developmental trajectory. Sternberg's framework is about types of thinking; Gardner's is about content areas. Both reject IQ-as-single-number, but they slice the pie differently.
A nursing student has a high analytic IQ and excellent academic test scores but struggles in clinical rotations — she misreads patient emotions, doesn't notice when a family member is overwhelmed, and gives technically correct but emotionally tone-deaf information. What's likely going on, and what's the evidence on this?
This pattern suggests high analytic intelligence but lower emotional intelligence (EI) — the ability to recognize, understand, and manage one's own and others' emotions. Components of EI: (1) Self-awareness — recognizing one's own emotions. (2) Self-regulation — managing emotional responses. (3) Empathy — accurately reading others' emotions. (4) Social skills — handling interpersonal situations effectively. Evidence: EI is at least partially distinct from cognitive IQ — high IQ doesn't guarantee high EI. EI predicts workplace performance, leadership, relationship quality, and mental health independently of cognitive ability. For nursing specifically, EI predicts: patient satisfaction, care quality, clinical judgment in interpersonal situations, resilience and burnout resistance. The good news: EI is more trainable than analytic IQ. Specific approaches: (1) Practice noticing and naming emotions (own and others'). (2) Active listening exercises. (3) Reflective practice — after interactions, ask "what was the patient/family feeling? What did I miss?" (4) Feedback from supervisors specifically on emotional reading. (5) Diverse clinical experiences with debrief. (6) Some programs explicitly teach EI as a curriculum component. For this student, structured mentorship with emotional-skill focus would likely substantially improve clinical performance.

Binet and the development of intelligence testing

The origin of intelligence testing — Binet, Simon, and the school crisis

Intelligence testing emerged from a practical school problem, not a theoretical question. In the US, school enrollment nearly doubled between 1890 and 1915 because of immigration and reforms restricting child labor. Suddenly classrooms were full of students who didn't learn as readily as the "select few" who had populated them previously. What to do with these new students was the pressing question of the day.

In 1904, the Minister of Public Instruction in France asked two psychologists, Alfred Binet and Theophile Simon, to devise a way to identify children who would struggle in school without special instruction. Their approach: select simple tasks that French children of different ages should be able to do — naming colors, counting backward, remembering numbers in order — and identify which tasks typical children at each age could solve.

Children's mental age (MA) referred to the difficulty of the problems they could solve correctly. A child who solved problems that the average 7-year-old could solve had an MA of 7, regardless of their actual age (chronological age, CA).
