Binet and Simon used MA to distinguish "bright" from "dull" children — a 6-year-old with an MA of 9 was bright; a 6-year-old with an MA of 4 was dull. They confirmed that "bright" children did better in school than "dull" children. The first objective measure of intelligence was born.

The Stanford-Binet and the IQ formula

Lewis Terman at Stanford University substantially revised Binet and Simon's test, publishing the Stanford-Binet in 1916. Terman introduced the intelligence quotient (IQ) as the ratio of mental age to chronological age multiplied by 100:

IQ = (MA ÷ CA) × 100

At any age, a perfectly average child has IQ = 100 (mental age matches chronological age). A 4-year-old with an MA of 5 has IQ = (5÷4) × 100 = 125 — same IQ as an 8-year-old with an MA of 10.

Roughly two-thirds of children taking a Stanford-Binet test score between IQ 85 and 115. About 95% score between 70 and 130. About 2.5% score above 130 (giftedness criterion). About 2.5% score below 70 (intellectual disability criterion).

Modern IQ tests don't actually use the MA/CA ratio anymore — they use deviation IQ, comparing a child's performance to the average for their age group statistically. IQ = 100 still means average for age; higher and lower scores reflect standard deviations above or below age peers. The original ratio approach is still important historically because it shaped public understanding of intelligence.

Modern intelligence tests — the Wechsler scales

Today, the Wechsler scales are the most widely used IQ tests. Three versions cover different age ranges:

WAIS-IV (ages 16-90)

Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale, 4th edition. Yields a Full Scale IQ plus four index scores: Verbal Comprehension, Perceptual Reasoning, Working Memory, Processing Speed. Roughly 90-minute administration. Used in clinical, educational, occupational, and forensic contexts.

WISC-V (ages 6-16)

Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, 5th edition. Yields Full Scale IQ plus five primary index scores: Verbal Comprehension, Visual Spatial, Fluid Reasoning, Working Memory, Processing Speed. Most commonly used for school-age children, particularly for special education evaluation.

WPPSI-IV (ages 2.5-7)

Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence, 4th edition. For young children. Used for early identification of giftedness, developmental delays, or kindergarten readiness questions.

Why Wechsler over Stanford-Binet

The Wechsler scales separate verbal from non-verbal abilities, which is useful for identifying specific learning patterns. They also yield more clinically useful subscores. Both tests are still in widespread use; choice depends on referral question and context.

Do intelligence tests work?

What IQ scores predict

Tests are useful only if they predict important outcomes. IQ tests do — sometimes strikingly. Robert Brody (1992) called IQ "the most important predictor of an individual's ultimate position within American society."

School outcomes (strongest)

Correlation 0.5-0.7 with school grades, achievement test scores, and number of years of education completed (Brody, 1992; Geary, 2005). This isn't surprising — IQ tests were designed to predict school success.

Occupational success

People with higher IQs are more likely to hold high-paying, high-prestige jobs (Strenze, 2015). Correlations are moderate (0.3-0.5) but consistent across studies and decades.

Health and longevity

People with higher IQs tend to live longer because they engage in healthier behaviors — they're less likely to smoke, more likely to use seat belts, more likely to follow medical advice (Deary et al., 2021). Health behaviors are themselves cognitively demanding.

Social outcomes

Higher-IQ individuals tend to be more liked by peers and have stronger social relationships (Strenze, 2015). The "lonely genius" stereotype is largely a myth.

What IQ tests DON'T measure

IQ tests measure a real and useful construct, but they don't measure everything that matters. Important limits:

  • Creativity — IQ tests use convergent thinking (single right answer). Creativity uses divergent thinking (multiple original responses). These correlate weakly above moderate IQ levels.
  • Practical intelligence — Sternberg's "street smarts," knowing how to navigate real-world situations. IQ tests don't tap this directly.
  • Emotional intelligence — recognizing, understanding, and managing emotions (own and others'). Distinct from cognitive IQ; predicts different outcomes (leadership, relationship quality).
  • Wisdom and judgment — including knowing what one doesn't know, weighing competing values, and applying knowledge across contexts.
  • Persistence, grit, conscientiousness — personality traits that strongly affect achievement independently of ability.
  • Skills not yet learned — IQ measures current cognitive ability, not potential, motivation, or what could be achieved with appropriate instruction.

Test bias and equity concerns: IQ tests have been criticized for reflecting cultural knowledge and language patterns familiar to dominant-culture children. Modern tests have improved at minimizing this but haven't eliminated it. Black, Indigenous, Latinx, and English-language-learner students often score lower — not because of underlying differences in ability but because of differences in experience, opportunity, test familiarity, and stereotype threat. Using IQ tests to make consequential decisions (special education placement, gifted identification) requires careful interpretation in context.

TAB 4: 6.3 Individual Differences -->

6.3 · Individual Differences in Ability

IQ scores reflect both heredity and environment, and they predict school performance but not life success. Stereotype-related effects can substantially distort what tests actually measure.

Individual differences in ability

Heredity and environment

Heritability estimates for IQ in industrialized populations are roughly 50%. That doesn't mean half your IQ is "fixed" — it means that in current environments, about half the population variation in IQ corresponds to genetic differences. Environments matter substantially: children adopted out of severely deprived environments into enriching ones typically gain 10+ IQ points.

The Flynn effect — IQ scores rising about 3 points per decade across the 20th century — shows how environment-sensitive the construct is. Genes don't change in 50 years, so environmental factors (nutrition, schooling, exposure to formal reasoning tasks) must be driving the gains.

Stereotype threat

When members of a stigmatized group are reminded of negative stereotypes about their group's ability, their performance on relevant tests declines — not because the stereotype is true, but because stereotype awareness consumes cognitive resources and triggers anxiety. Steele & Aronson's classic study: simply telling Black and White students that a test "measured intellectual ability" produced a Black-White gap; telling them the same test was "a problem-solving exercise unrelated to ability" eliminated it.

A school administrator argues that observed group differences on IQ tests must reflect underlying ability differences because "the test items are objective". What's the strongest counter from the stereotype threat research?
Test items can be "objective" and the test can still systematically underestimate the ability of stigmatized group members, because performance depends on more than the test items themselves. Stereotype threat shows that simply framing a test as ability-related vs. neutral can erase or create group gaps — the same individuals score differently on the same items based on context. So observed performance gaps reflect both ability AND testing conditions. The Black–White gap in the classic Steele & Aronson study disappeared when the test was framed differently.
James Flynn documented that IQ scores have risen approximately 3 points per decade in the 20th century — meaning the average modern 10-year-old would score above the 95th percentile compared to 10-year-olds from 1900. What does the "Flynn effect" tell us about the nature of IQ?
The Flynn effect has major implications: (1) IQ is highly environmentally responsive. A 30-point gain in a century cannot be explained by genetic change (too fast); it must reflect environmental improvements: nutrition, education (especially in abstract reasoning), smaller family size (more cognitive attention per child), more cognitively demanding work, more exposure to test-like materials (puzzles, screens, abstract problems). (2) IQ tests are renormed periodically to keep the mean at 100; without this, an average modern person would score ~130 by 1900 norms. (3) The gain is largest on abstract/fluid reasoning subtests (Raven's, similarities) — not on vocabulary or general information. Suggests modern minds aren't "smarter" across the board but are more practiced at the kinds of abstract reasoning IQ tests demand. (4) Implications for hereditarian arguments: between-group IQ gaps in a single generation cannot be assumed to be genetic when within-population gains of similar size happen environmentally. (5) Recent slowdown/reversal: the Flynn effect has slowed or reversed in some high-income countries since the 1990s — possible causes include declining academic rigor, attention impacts of screen use, or test ceiling effects. (6) Bottom line: IQ is real but environmentally malleable, especially at the population level.

Intellectual disability & giftedness

Intellectual disability = IQ ≤70 plus significant deficits in adaptive functioning, with onset before 18. Causes vary; many are genetic (Down syndrome, Fragile X), some prenatal (FAS), some perinatal or postnatal. Giftedness (IQ ≥130) historically focused on test scores alone; modern conceptions also emphasize creativity, motivation, and domain-specific talent.

Gifted children — in depth

Modern definitions go beyond IQ

Traditionally, giftedness was defined by IQ alone — a score of 130 or greater. Modern definitions are broader and include exceptional talent across many domains: art, music, creative writing, dance, sports, and so on (Worrell et al., 2019). A child can be exceptionally talented in music without exceptional IQ; a child can have high IQ without exceptional achievement.

Five characteristics of gifted children (Worrell et al., 2019)

1. Substantially above-average ability

In their domain of giftedness, performance significantly exceeds age peers. The threshold for "substantially" varies by domain — for academic giftedness, ~98th percentile or above is typical; for athletic giftedness, even more selective.

2. Passion and drive to master the subject

Gifted children typically love their domain — they don't need to be pushed to practice or learn. Ability without passion rarely produces sustained achievement. This is part of what makes "talented but unmotivated" a clinical pattern needing intervention.

3. Creativity and divergent thinking

Gifted children produce novel thoughts and actions. Divergent thinking: producing many original responses to an open-ended stimulus (the opposite of convergent thinking, which seeks a single right answer). Creativity correlates only modestly with IQ — high IQ doesn't guarantee creativity.

4. Requires nurturing

Without encouragement and support from parents and stimulating mentors, talent withers. Gifted children need challenging curricula, knowledgeable teachers, and like-minded peers. This is why access to gifted programs matters — talent without environment can disappear, especially for children in under-resourced schools.

5. Generally happy and well-adjusted

The stereotype of the troubled, isolated genius is largely false. Gifted children and adults are typically happier and healthier than peers (Worrell et al., 2019). As adults, they report high satisfaction with careers, relationships, and life in general (Lubinski et al., 2006). Exceptions exist — twice-exceptional children (gifted + disability) may struggle more — but the population as a whole does well.

Long-term outcomes

Bernstein et al. (2019): 35-year longitudinal study of gifted teens. As adults, many were leaders in corporate sector, law, science, medicine. Talent identified early — and nurtured — predicts substantial life achievement.

Twice-exceptional (2e) children

Some children are both gifted AND have a disability — autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, learning disability, sensory or physical impairment. The "twice exceptional" or "2e" framework recognizes that these children need both:

  • Gifted programming for their advanced abilities
  • Special education support for their disability

Without both, 2e children often fall through cracks: they may be excluded from gifted programs because of a disability label, or denied special education because they're "too smart to have a problem." The actual experience is one of intense frustration — knowing what you want to do, not being able to do it for reasons you can't control.

Clinical signs of 2e: bright child whose written work is incoherent (giftedness + dysgraphia); brilliant ideas but disorganized execution (giftedness + ADHD); precocious vocabulary but social difficulties (giftedness + ASD); high verbal scores with low performance scores on IQ testing (giftedness + specific learning disability). Identification requires comprehensive evaluation, not single-test screening.

6.4 · Academic Skills

Reading, writing, and math are the foundation school skills. All three develop through identifiable phases, all three are vulnerable to specific learning disabilities, and one common condition — ADHD — cuts across all of them.

Academic skills

Reading

Two strands develop in parallel:

  • Word recognition — going from letters to sounds (decoding) to automatic word recognition. Phonological awareness (the understanding that words are made of sounds) is the key precursor; children who struggle with it are at high risk for later reading problems.
  • Comprehension — extracting meaning from text. Builds on vocabulary, background knowledge, working memory, and strategies (predicting, summarizing, rereading).

Dyslexia = a specific reading disability marked by difficulty with decoding despite typical intelligence and instruction. Affects ~5–10% of children. Phonological-awareness training is the strongest intervention.

A pediatric nurse evaluates a 5-year-old before kindergarten and asks: "What rhymes with cat?" The child looks puzzled. The nurse asks: "Say bat without the b." Child says "bat." Asked to clap the syllables in "butterfly" she taps once. Why are these mini-tasks important and what should be done?
The nurse is screening for phonological awareness — the understanding that spoken words are made of sound units (phonemes, syllables, rhymes). Phonological awareness is the strongest single predictor of early reading success and one of the earliest detectable markers of dyslexia risk. What the child showed: (1) Can't generate rhymes — fundamental phonological skill missing. (2) Can't remove the initial sound — phoneme manipulation deficit. (3) Can't clap syllables — syllable segmentation deficit. These are concerning at age 5; most kindergarteners can do these tasks. Action steps: (1) Refer to speech-language pathology for formal phonological awareness assessment. (2) Hearing screening to rule out hearing loss as cause. (3) Communicate with kindergarten teacher about early literacy support. (4) Don't wait for reading failure in 2nd or 3rd grade — early intervention is dramatically more effective. (5) Evidence-based interventions like Orton-Gillingham, Lindamood-Bell, or structured literacy programs target exactly these skills. Why this matters clinically: pediatric nurses, school nurses, and well-child providers are positioned to catch reading risk YEARS before formal academic failure. A 2-minute screening can change a child's educational trajectory. The "wait and see" approach has been replaced by "response to intervention" models that catch struggling readers early.

Math & writing

Math develops from concrete counting through arithmetic operations to abstract manipulation. Key milestones: counting one-to-one (~age 3), understanding cardinality (the last number counted = the total), arithmetic strategies (counting-on, retrieval), and word problems requiring symbolic translation. Dyscalculia affects ~5% of children and is often distinct from dyslexia.

Writing combines fine motor skills, language, and metacognition. Develops from invented spelling to conventional spelling, and from listing ideas to organizing and revising. Editing skills are the hardest and latest to develop.

Attention-Deficit / Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)

~5–10% of school-age children

ADHD is a persistent pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity that interferes with functioning. DSM-5 criteria require:

  • Multiple symptoms from one or both categories.
  • Symptoms present before age 12.
  • Symptoms present in two or more settings (not just at school OR just at home).
  • Clear evidence the symptoms interfere with social, academic, or occupational functioning.
  • Symptoms not better explained by another disorder.

Causes are largely genetic and neurobiological — differences in dopamine signaling and in attention/executive networks of the brain. Stimulant medication (methylphenidate, amphetamines) plus behavioral therapy is the strongest evidence-based treatment. The disorder often persists into adulthood, though hyperactivity decreases.

A 7-year-old is highly active and easily distracted at school. The teacher suggests an ADHD evaluation. Why is it important to verify the symptoms appear in multiple settings before diagnosing?
ADHD is a persistent, cross-setting pattern of inattention/hyperactivity-impulsivity. Symptoms that show up only at school could reflect academic problems, learning disability, anxiety, family stress, an inappropriate classroom environment, or sleep deprivation — none of which are ADHD. The DSM-5 specifically requires symptoms in two or more settings (typically home AND school) to avoid misdiagnosing situation-specific behavior as a chronic disorder. Misdiagnosis can lead to unnecessary medication and missed treatment of the real underlying cause.

Spotlight on Research

Word structure training enhances later reading comprehension

Lyster, S.-A. H., Lervåg, A., & Hulme, C. (2016)

What was the aim?

Vocabulary size strongly predicts reading comprehension. One effective way to grow vocabulary is teaching children about word structure — prefixes (un-), suffixes (-ed), and compound words. The question: Would training preschoolers on word structure improve their reading comprehension years later?

How did they measure it?

As preschoolers, training-group children learned to recognize prefixes (e.g., unhappy), suffixes (e.g., walked), and compound words (e.g., toothbrush) in 30-minute sessions over 17 weeks. Control children received no training. Seven years later, as sixth graders, all children's reading comprehension was tested using a long four-page text, plus two shorter texts (one with a table, one with a map). Multiple-choice questions assessed comprehension.

Who participated & what was the design?

163 Norwegian preschool children; 93 were retested as sixth graders. The study was experimental (random assignment to training or control) and longitudinal (~7-year follow-up). Parents provided informed consent; no ethical concerns.

What were the results?

On all three reading tasks, sixth graders who had received word-structure training as preschoolers answered more questions correctly than control-group children. The effect was consistent across text formats (prose, text-with-table, text-with-map).

What did the investigators conclude?

In Lyster and colleagues' words: "By giving preschoolers and young schoolchildren knowledge of the structure of words and helping them to integrate meaning with the spoken and written forms of words, we may enhance their oral and written language development." The mechanism: word-structure training enriches vocabulary, which feeds into reading comprehension years later. This is another developmental cascade — a relatively brief early intervention with effects extending into elementary school.

What would strengthen the conclusions?

The study was done in Norwegian, which has consistent letter-sound mapping. English is more variable in pronunciation; replication in English-speaking samples would test whether the effect generalizes (the researchers suggest training might be more complicated but the benefits potentially larger). Direct measurement of how vocabulary changes across time, linked to training and outcomes, would also tighten the causal pathway.

What Do You Think? · Cultural Differences in Achievement

Compared to high-school students worldwide on math, US students consistently score lower than students in many Asian and European countries. The very best US students perform at the level of average students in many Asian nations — a gap evident by age 5 and persisting for at least 25 years of measurement. Part of the explanation is schooling: Taiwanese 5th graders typically spend 8 hours in school + 2–3 hours of homework nightly, while US 5th graders spend 6–7 hours in school + less than 1 hour of homework. Asian parents typically set higher standards, are more involved in their children's learning, and more often focus on mistakes than US parents (who focus on successes). But this comes with cost: Asian children also report less happiness, lower self-worth, and greater pressure to succeed.

  • Could we take the best of both cultural approaches — high standards, active parental involvement, structured time — and combine them with attention to children's emotional wellbeing?
  • What specific parenting practices would such an integrated approach include?
  • Could the same practices apply to other domains (reading, science, music) without the same emotional cost?
  • How much of the gap is parenting vs. schooling vs. broader cultural valuation of education? Are they separable?
Lyster et al. (2016) found that Norwegian preschoolers who received 30-minute sessions over 17 weeks on word structure (prefixes, suffixes, compound words) showed better reading comprehension 7 years later as sixth graders. What developmental principle does this illustrate, and why is the duration of effect striking?
The finding illustrates that brief, targeted early language interventions can produce long-lasting developmental cascades. The pathway: training in word structure → enriched vocabulary → better reading comprehension. The duration is striking because the intervention itself was modest (~8.5 hours total of training over 17 weeks) but the effects persisted across 7 years of subsequent development. The mechanism is almost certainly indirect compounding: better word-recognition skills enable more independent reading early in school, which enriches vocabulary further, which improves reading comprehension further, which gives access to more advanced texts, and so on. Each step builds on the last. This is a classic development cascade — early skill differences compound across time into substantial later differences. It also provides empirical support for the importance of early literacy interventions: small differences in preschool vocabulary and word knowledge can have outsized later consequences.
Across 25+ years of international comparisons, US students consistently underperform peers in many Asian countries on math — a gap visible by age 5. What multi-level factors contribute, and what's the tradeoff in Asian systems that produces these higher math scores?
Schooling factors: longer school day (Taiwanese 5th graders: 8 hours school + 2–3 hours homework vs. US 5th graders: 6–7 hours school + <1 hour homework), more academic time within the school day. Parenting factors: Asian parents typically set higher standards, are more actively involved in homework, extend assignments to teach additional skills, and focus more on errors and improvement rather than successes (which is the more common US pattern). Cultural factors: stronger cultural valuation of academic achievement; recognition of effort over innate ability. The tradeoff: research (Ng et al. 2007; Pomerantz et al. 2008) finds that Asian children — alongside their stronger achievement — report less happiness, lower self-worth, and greater pressure to succeed. The pattern is that Asian systems produce higher math achievement at an emotional cost; US systems prioritize happiness and self-esteem at an academic cost. Neither set of practices is intrinsically superior — they prioritize different outcomes.
A high school study found that telling Black students before a difficult test that "this measures cognitive ability" produced significantly lower scores than telling matched students the same test "measures problem-solving puzzles, not ability". What's the mechanism, and how does this finding generalize?
The mechanism is stereotype threat — the fear of confirming a negative stereotype about one's group can itself impair performance. When the test was framed as measuring cognitive ability, the negative stereotype about Black intellectual ability became cognitively salient, consuming working memory resources that should have been devoted to solving the test. When the test was framed as a neutral puzzle, the threat didn't activate and performance was equivalent to other groups. How it generalizes: stereotype threat has been demonstrated for women in math, older adults on memory tasks, white men on athletic tasks, LGBTQ+ individuals on social tasks, low-SES students academically, and many other groups. The effect is not about ability — it's about the cognitive load of negotiating a stereotype while performing. Interventions: framing tests as practice or skill-building rather than ability measurement; affirming students' broader identities before stressful tests; ensuring diverse representation in testing environments.

Test Yourself · Section 6.4

  1. What three components of word structure did Lyster et al. (2016) teach preschoolers to recognize?
  2. Based on the Lyster study, brief preschool training in word structure had measurable effects on reading comprehension how many years later?
  3. Name two schooling-related factors and two parenting-related factors that contribute to the US-Asia math achievement gap.
  4. What's the documented emotional cost associated with Asian parenting practices that produce higher math achievement?
  5. What is stereotype threat, and what intervention strategies can reduce its impact?

Linking Research to Life — basic research on reading skills to effective interventions

Why oral language is the foundation for reading

Reading isn't a separate skill that appears at school entry — it's built on oral language. Children need well-developed oral language skills to read well: they must be able to express themselves in words and understand others' speech. Without these, much of what they read won't make sense (Droop & Verhoeven, 2003).

Many children — particularly those from less language-rich environments — start kindergarten with weak oral language foundations and predictably struggle with reading. Research has shown that oral language can be taught and that doing so improves reading comprehension years later.

Evidence-based interventions — Nuffield Early Language Intervention

Fricke et al. (2013) evaluated a 30-week program designed for preschoolers and kindergarteners with impaired oral language skills. The program:

  • Started in preschool, continued into Reception (the UK equivalent of kindergarten)
  • Children participated in small-group sessions 3 times per week
  • In Reception, individual sessions were added twice weekly
  • Activities focused on vocabulary, expressive language, and listening skills

Sample activities:

Interactive book reading

Adult reads a picture book with the child. Child is encouraged to talk about what they see. Adult expands and corrects: child says "The fish is red." Adult responds: "Yes, the fish is red with a big green fin" — introducing the new word in context. Cabell et al. (2019); Mol et al. (2009) show this format reliably increases vocabulary.

Listening skills training

Children use sound to identify musical instruments an adult plays behind their back (bell vs. drum). Reminds children that listening well requires being quiet and orienting toward the sound (Department for Education and Skills, 2007).

Vocabulary games and dialogic reading

Structured back-and-forth around target vocabulary words. The goal isn't just exposure but engagement — the child uses the words, not just hears them.

Expressive language scaffolding

Adults explicitly model and prompt complete sentences, accurate grammar, narrative structure. Children practice telling stories, explaining things, describing scenes.

Results (Fricke et al., 2013): compared to controls, intervention children had larger vocabularies, expressed themselves more effectively, and listened more effectively — both immediately after training AND six months later. Their reading comprehension was also better.

Replication (Fricke et al., 2017): most effects replicated when the program was implemented by an independent group, not the original researchers. This is a critical test — many promising interventions fail when scaled up. The Nuffield Early Language Intervention survives this scaling test.

Practical impact: the program is now available to schools as an off-the-shelf, documented intervention for children with impaired oral language skills. Basic research on reading-related skills produced an effective real-world program — exactly what the textbook's "Linking Research to Life" feature is meant to illustrate.

Effective schools, effective teachers

Four characteristics of successful schools

Because US education is run locally, schools differ enormously. Researchers (El Nokali et al., 2010; Good & Brophy, 2008; Preston et al., 2017) have identified four characteristics of schools where students consistently succeed:

1. Clear academic focus

Staff and students alike understand that academic excellence is the primary goal. The school day emphasizes instruction — not nonacademic activities. Students are recognized publicly for academic accomplishments. Teachers engage students through dialogue, feedback, and responsiveness. Climate: academic achievement is celebrated and modeled, not treated as nerdy or optional.

2. Safe, nurturing climate

Students can devote energy to learning instead of worrying about being harmed. They feel that staff genuinely cares about their success. Bullying is addressed effectively; discipline is consistent and fair. Physical safety is established (no fights in halls, no fear of attacks); emotional safety is established (students can ask questions without humiliation).

3. Parental and community involvement

Parents participate through parent-teacher organizations or informally (helping teachers prepare lessons, grade papers). Social agencies provide mentors for at-risk students. Businesses partner with schools for internships or vocational training. Signals to students: a network of adults is committed to their success. Strongest in communities where this is culturally expected; weakest where parents feel unwelcome or work hours prevent involvement.

4. Monitoring progress

Students, teachers, and programs are evaluated regularly using objective measures that reflect academic goals. Without measurement, schools can't tell if they're succeeding. Without honest feedback, problems compound. Best schools welcome accountability; struggling schools sometimes resist or game it.

What master teachers do (Good & Lavigne, 2018)

On a daily basis, individual teachers have the most direct impact on student achievement. In any school, some teachers are highly sought after because their classes succeed — students learn and the classroom climate is positive. What do master teachers do differently?

  • Manage the classroom effectively so most time goes to instruction, not discipline. Classes start on time. Transitions between activities are smooth. Behavioral expectations are clear and consistent.
  • Have high expectations for all students — not just the easy ones. Communicate to every student "I expect you can do this." This effect is well-documented (Rosenthal & Jacobson's classic teacher expectations work).
  • Provide clear, organized instruction with explicit goals. Students know what they're supposed to learn and why.
  • Use varied teaching methods — direct instruction, group work, individual practice, real-world application. Not single-method rote.
  • Give frequent, specific feedback — both about what's correct and what to improve. General praise ("good job!") is less effective than specific feedback ("your introduction sets up the argument clearly; the conclusion could tie back to your opening claim").
  • Adjust instruction to individual needs — recognize when students don't understand and reteach in a different way, rather than just moving on.
  • Build relationships with students — students learn more from teachers they trust and feel cared about by.
  • Engage parents — communicate proactively (not just when there's a problem), invite parents to contribute, treat parents as partners in education.

For nursing & healthcare practice

These principles transfer directly to patient education. (1) Clear focus on what the patient needs to know. (2) Safe and supportive learning climate (don't shame). (3) Engage family. (4) Monitor learning (teach-back: "Can you tell me how you'll take this medication when you get home?"). (5) High expectations matched to actual capacity. (6) Varied methods (verbal, written, demonstration, video). (7) Specific feedback. (8) Build the relationship. The "effective teacher" framework is essentially the "effective health educator" framework.

6.5 · Physical Development

Middle childhood is a period of steady physical growth, with the most important changes accumulating slowly rather than dramatically.

Physical development

Growth & motor development

School-age children gain about 2–3 inches and 5 pounds per year. Bone density increases, muscle strength grows, and motor skills become increasingly refined. Children get better at coordinated activities — riding bikes, throwing and catching, dance, sports. Boys and girls are about equal in physical performance until puberty, when sex differences increase.

Childhood obesity

Major public-health concern

Defined as BMI ≥ 95th percentile for age and sex. About 1 in 5 US children meets this threshold. Causes are layered: genetic susceptibility, diet (especially high added sugar and processed food), reduced physical activity (screen time displacing play), neighborhood environment (food access, walkable streets), and family eating patterns. Childhood obesity raises immediate risks (type 2 diabetes, sleep apnea, joint problems) and predicts adult obesity.

Effective interventions involve the family, not just the child — changing what's in the kitchen, building routines around active play, and addressing emotional eating. Restrictive diets aimed at children alone tend to backfire.

A pediatric nurse evaluates a 10-year-old with BMI at the 99th percentile. What comorbidities should be screened for, and what's the evidence on intervention?
Severe childhood obesity warrants comprehensive workup for comorbidities — many are present in childhood, not just adulthood. Routine screening: (1) Type 2 diabetes / prediabetes — HbA1c or fasting glucose; T2DM in children is rising dramatically. (2) Hypertension — pediatric BP norms apply (>95th percentile for age/sex/height). (3) Dyslipidemia — fasting lipid panel. (4) Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) — most common pediatric liver disease in US; check ALT. (5) Obstructive sleep apnea — screen for snoring, daytime fatigue; consider polysomnography. (6) Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) — in adolescent girls with irregular menses. (7) Orthopedic — Blount disease, slipped capital femoral epiphysis. (8) Psychological — depression, anxiety, weight stigma effects, disordered eating; teasing/bullying. Evidence-based intervention: (1) Multi-disciplinary family-based intervention is most effective. (2) Targets: family meals, food environment changes, physical activity (60 min/day), screen-time limits, sleep hygiene. (3) Address comorbidities and mental health concurrently. (4) Avoid shame-based approaches — they worsen outcomes. (5) Pharmacotherapy and bariatric surgery have evolving roles in severe pediatric obesity. (6) Critical: address social determinants — food insecurity, housing instability, neighborhood food access. Individual willpower interventions without environment change rarely succeed.

Health & accidents

School-age children are typically very healthy — common illnesses are mostly infections that resolve. The leading cause of death in this age group is accidents, especially motor-vehicle injuries. Bicycle helmets, seat belts, booster seats, and supervised water play all substantially reduce risk.

Participating in sports

Benefits of organized youth sports

Children's growing motor coordination, cognitive skills, and capacity for teamwork let them participate in organized sports — baseball, softball, basketball, soccer, swimming, gymnastics, and many others. Benefits go far beyond physical fitness:

Physical benefits

Exercise, improved motor skills, cardiovascular fitness, bone density, weight management. Counters the rise in sedentary screen time.

Self-esteem and initiative

Mastering a skill, contributing to a team, and seeing tangible improvement build self-efficacy. Bedard et al. (2020); Eime et al. (2013) document this consistently.

Social competence

Sports teach how to work effectively in complementary roles — every player has a function. Children learn to negotiate, cooperate, and resolve disagreements. Friendships form around shared activity (Brenner, 2016).

Cognitive engagement

Children apply emerging concrete-operational skills — devising playing strategies, modifying rules, reading game situations, anticipating opponents' moves. Sports are cognitively demanding, not just physically.

Potential hazards

These benefits are real, but not automatic. Several patterns produce negative outcomes:

  • Early sport specialization — focusing on a single sport before early/mid-adolescence leads to overuse injuries, burnout, and limited peer networks (Brenner, 2016; Waldron et al., 2020). The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends DELAYING single-sport specialization until at least age 15-16. Multi-sport participation through middle childhood produces better long-term athletic outcomes AND fewer injuries.
  • Overuse injuries — increasingly common in youth sports. Pitcher's elbow, Sever's disease (heel pain), Osgood-Schlatter disease (knee pain), stress fractures. These were rare in children 30 years ago and are now epidemic in single-sport-specialized youth.
  • Concussion risk — contact sports (football, hockey, soccer, lacrosse, rugby) carry concussion risk. Cumulative concussions are associated with chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) later in life. Heads Up Concussion protocols, mandatory removal-from-play, and graduated return-to-play are now standard.
  • ACL injuries — particularly common in female athletes (soccer, basketball, gymnastics). Prevention programs (FIFA 11+, PEP) reduce risk by ~50% but uptake is uneven.
  • Antisocial behavior — adolescents' sports participation has sometimes been linked to delinquent behavior, especially when sports culture is hyper-competitive and combined with peer-group risk taking (Gardner et al., 2009). Outcomes are positive when sports participation is COMBINED with activities involving adults (school, religious, youth groups) — Linver et al., 2009; Zarrett et al., 2009.
  • Pressure and burnout — children pushed too hard by parents or coaches lose intrinsic motivation. Many quit organized sport in early adolescence after years of participation.

The role of coaches and parents

Whether sports help or harm depends substantially on the adults involved.

Positive patterns: when coaches encourage players, emphasize skill development, and model good sportsmanship — children enjoy playing, improve their skills, and develop self-esteem. When parents support but don't pressure, children thrive.

Negative patterns: when coaches emphasize winning over skill development and criticize or punish players for bad plays, children lose interest and stop playing (Curran et al., 2015). When adolescents experience sports as stressful and lack supportive relationships with teammates and coaches, they often burn out — losing interest and quitting (Lu et al., 2016; Pacewicz et al., 2019).

Parents pushing too hard is a recognized clinical pattern — projecting unfulfilled ambitions onto children, criticizing performance, comparing children to others, attending every game with anxious attention to outcomes. The child's experience: every game becomes a test. Long-term outcomes include anxiety, eating disorders, identity foreclosure on athletic identity, and depression when athletic success doesn't materialize.

For pediatric nursing practice

(1) Ask about sports participation in well-child visits — what sports, how many hours, how many days off per week. (2) Screen for overuse injury symptoms in heavily-specialized children. (3) Educate families on AAP recommendations: at least one day off from organized sport per week, at least 2-3 months off from a specific sport each year, delay single-sport specialization. (4) Discuss concussion safety — symptoms, when to remove from play, return-to-play protocols. (5) For girls in soccer/basketball/gymnastics — recommend ACL prevention training. (6) Watch for burnout patterns — child who used to love a sport now dreads it. (7) Recognize parent-pressure patterns and address them sensitively. (8) Promote sports as enjoyable activity, not just achievement vehicle. (9) Validate non-elite participation — most benefits come from any participation, not from competitive success. (10) Connect families to community resources (YMCA, recreation leagues) for children who'd benefit from sport but can't afford competitive teams.

An 11-year-old soccer player presents to the pediatric clinic with persistent knee pain. Her mother mentions she plays soccer year-round on two teams (club and select), trains 4-5 days per week, and "never gets a break from her sport because she's so committed". What does the nurse need to know about pediatric sports medicine, overuse injury, and how to counsel this family?
This child is showing classic early-specialization overuse injury risk profile. Likely diagnoses to consider: (1) Osgood-Schlatter disease — knee pain at the tibial tubercle (just below the kneecap) from traction injury during growth; very common in growing athletes; self-limited but painful, can persist for months to years. (2) Sinding-Larsen-Johansson syndrome — pain at the lower pole of the patella, similar mechanism. (3) Patellofemoral pain syndrome — anterior knee pain from muscle imbalance, common in athletic adolescent girls. (4) ACL injury risk elevated in female soccer players. (5) Stress fracture — rare but serious; consider if pain is severe, localized, or worsening. The systemic problem — early specialization: (a) AAP and American Medical Society for Sports Medicine recommend AGAINST single-sport specialization before age 15-16. (b) Year-round single-sport play is the strongest risk factor for overuse injury in pediatric athletes. (c) "Two teams in the same sport" increases volume beyond what AAP recommends. (d) AAP guidelines: at least one day OFF organized sport per week; at least 2-3 months OFF a specific sport each year. This child meets NONE of these criteria. Counseling the family: (1) Validate commitment: "Your daughter is clearly dedicated and you've supported her well. Now we need to protect that long-term." (2) Educate on the paradox: counterintuitively, athletes who specialize early are MORE likely to drop out and LESS likely to play at elite levels long-term than multi-sport athletes who specialize later. The "more is better" assumption is wrong. (3) Frame as sustainability: "For her to play soccer at her best for years to come — and possibly at higher levels — we need to do this differently NOW." (4) Specific recommendations: (a) take a minimum 2-3 month break from soccer each year (cross-train with other activities); (b) at least 1 full day off per week; (c) drop one of the two soccer teams — playing on multiple same-sport teams is the highest-risk pattern; (d) consider adding a different sport (swimming, basketball, track) for cross-training; (e) ensure adequate sleep (9-11 hours for age), nutrition, hydration. (5) ACL prevention: enroll in a structured neuromuscular training program (FIFA 11+, PEP program) — reduces ACL injuries ~50% in female soccer players. Many clubs now require this. (6) Pain management: ice, NSAIDs short-term, physical therapy if indicated. NOT continuing to play through significant pain. (7) Warning signs for parents: pain that wakes child at night; localized swelling; pain that doesn't improve with rest; significant gait change. Watch for parental over-involvement: assess gently whether mother's framing ("never gets a break because she's so committed") reflects child's actual choice or parental pressure. Ask the child alone — "Do YOU still love soccer? Are there other sports you'd like to try?" Burnout and parental pressure are huge issues in elite-track youth athletics. (8) Mental health screen: anxiety and disordered eating are elevated in highly-specialized female athletes. (9) Concussion history: ask about prior concussions; soccer carries heading risk. (10) Refer to sports medicine if pain doesn't resolve with conservative management, or for formal injury prevention/return-to-play guidance. The big-picture conversation: "The research shows that athletes who specialize early are more likely to get injured, burn out, and quit. Athletes who play multiple sports through middle school and specialize in high school actually go further. We want your daughter to love soccer for a long time — that means doing it differently right now."

6.6 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Jean Piaget — Concrete operational stage (~7–11 years); children can reason logically about concrete, observable phenomena but struggle with abstract or hypothetical.
  • Alfred Binet — Created the first practical intelligence test (1905) to identify French schoolchildren needing extra help. Concept of mental age.
  • David Wechsler — Developed the Wechsler scales (WISC for children, WAIS for adults). Multidimensional assessment beyond a single IQ score.
  • Charles Spearman — Proposed general intelligence (g): a single underlying factor that contributes to performance on diverse cognitive tasks.
  • Howard Gardner — Theory of multiple intelligences: 8+ distinct intelligences (linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalist). Popular in education despite mixed empirical support.
  • Robert Sternberg — Triarchic theory: analytic, creative, and practical intelligence are distinct. Later expanded to include wisdom-based intelligence.
  • Claude Steele & Joshua Aronson — Discovered stereotype threat: anxiety about confirming a negative stereotype about one's group impairs performance on relevant tasks. Replicated across race, gender, age, and other identity dimensions.
  • James Flynn — Documented the Flynn effect: IQ scores have risen ~3 points per decade in the 20th century, reflecting environmental improvements (nutrition, education, abstract reasoning practice).

Specific studies cited

  • Stereotype threat studies (Steele & Aronson, 1995+): Black students who were told a test measured intellectual ability performed worse than Black students given the same test framed as a "puzzle." White students showed no effect. Demonstrated that test performance is influenced by social-evaluative context.
  • Twin and adoption studies of IQ heritability: Identical twins reared apart correlate ~0.74 in IQ; fraternal twins reared together ~0.60; adopted siblings ~0.32. Estimates heritability ~50% in industrialized populations.
  • Lyster et al. (2016): Preschool training in phonological awareness, morphological awareness, and orthographic awareness showed measurable effects on reading comprehension years later. Demonstrates the power of early targeted literacy intervention.
  • Reading interventions for dyslexia: Structured, systematic phonics-based instruction with intensive practice is the most evidence-based approach. Reading wars have largely settled around this consensus.
  • Cross-cultural math achievement studies: US students score lower than students from East Asian countries on standardized math tests; differences attributed to instructional time, problem structure, parental involvement, and cultural attitudes (effort vs ability framing).

6.7 · Key Terms & Definitions

The 22 vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter.

Glossary by category

Piaget & information processing (8 terms)

  • Mental operations — Cognitive actions that can be performed mentally; mastery of these marks the concrete operational stage.
  • Deductive reasoning — Drawing conclusions that must follow from given premises (e.g., "all dogs bark; this is a dog; therefore this dog barks"). Begins to develop in concrete operational stage; matures in formal operational.
  • Working memory — Mental workspace for actively manipulating information; capacity grows from infancy through adolescence.
  • Long-term memory — Stable storage of information; effectively unlimited capacity; access depends on retrieval cues.
  • Organization — A memory strategy: structuring information into meaningful categories aids recall.
  • Elaboration — A memory strategy: connecting new information to existing knowledge (analogies, examples, vivid imagery).
  • Metamemory — Knowledge about how memory works (e.g., "this is hard to remember, I should write it down"); develops during middle childhood.
  • Metacognitive knowledge — General awareness of one's own thinking processes; foundation of effective learning.

Intelligence & aptitude (10 terms)

  • Cognitive self-regulation — Adjusting one's cognition based on goals and progress (planning, monitoring, evaluating); develops through middle childhood.
  • Emotional intelligence — Ability to recognize, understand, and manage one's own and others' emotions. Predicts social and workplace outcomes independently of cognitive IQ.
  • Analytic ability — Sternberg's first triarchic dimension: school-style analytical reasoning, problem-solving with single right answers.
  • Creative ability — Sternberg's second dimension: generating novel, useful ideas; divergent thinking.
  • Practical ability — Sternberg's third dimension: applying knowledge to real-world problems ("street smarts"). Sometimes diverges substantially from analytic IQ.
  • Wisdom — Sternberg's later addition: applying analytic, creative, and practical intelligence toward the common good with awareness of values.
  • Mental age (MA) — Binet's measure: the chronological age at which a child's performance is typical. (Largely replaced by deviation scores.)
  • Intelligence quotient (IQ) — Originally MA/CA × 100; now a normed deviation score with mean 100, SD 15.
  • Culture-fair intelligence tests — Tests designed to minimize cultural content bias (using shapes/patterns rather than vocabulary). Imperfect — no test is truly culture-free.
  • Stereotype threat — Anxiety about confirming a negative stereotype about one's group impairs performance on tasks relevant to that stereotype. A major contributor to test-score group differences.

Academic skills & abilities (9 terms)

  • Divergent thinking — Generating many possible ideas/solutions; component of creativity.
  • Intellectual disability — Significantly subaverage intellectual functioning (IQ ≤70) combined with adaptive behavior limitations, with onset before 18. Categories: mild, moderate, severe, profound.
  • Learning disability — Specific deficit in academic skills (reading, writing, math) despite normal intelligence and adequate instruction. Examples: dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia.
  • Word recognition — Decoding letters into sounds and recognizing whole words; one of two main strands of reading.
  • Comprehension — Extracting meaning from text; the other main strand of reading.
  • Phonological awareness — Awareness that spoken words are made of sound units (phonemes); strongest predictor of early reading success.
  • Knowledge-telling strategy — Young writers' approach: write down whatever you know about the topic. Linear and unsophisticated.
  • Knowledge-transforming strategy — Mature writers' approach: organize information around purpose, audience, and argument; revise; build meaning rather than just transmit.
  • Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) — Persistent inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity that interferes with functioning. Affects ~5% of school-age children. Treated with behavioral approaches and/or stimulant medication.

Clinical & supplementary terms (15 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • Horizontal décalage — Piaget's term for the observation that children acquire similar concrete-operational concepts at different ages across different content domains (e.g., conservation of number masters around age 6, conservation of weight around 8, conservation of volume around 11). Demonstrates that cognitive 'stages' are not as uniform as Piaget initially proposed.
  • ADHD subtypes (DSM-5) — ADHD presents in three subtypes: predominantly inattentive (formerly 'ADD'), predominantly hyperactive-impulsive, and combined. Subtype can shift across development — many children present as hyperactive in early grades but inattentive by adolescence.
  • Metacognition — Thinking about thinking — awareness of one's own cognitive processes, including knowing what one knows, monitoring comprehension, planning strategies, evaluating outcomes. Develops dramatically in middle childhood and predicts academic success.
  • Intelligence (Gardner's multiple intelligences) — Howard Gardner's proposal that intelligence is not unitary but multiple — linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalist, existential. Widely popular in education though contested as a scientific theory.
  • Intelligence (Sternberg's triarchic theory) — Robert Sternberg's three-part model: analytical intelligence (academic-style problem-solving), creative intelligence (generating novel ideas), practical intelligence (street smarts, applying knowledge in real contexts). Together: 'successful intelligence'.
  • Mental age (MA) and chronological age (CA) — Binet's original framework: MA is the age level whose typical performance the child matches; CA is the child's actual age. Foundation for the original IQ formula (IQ = MA/CA × 100). Modern IQ tests no longer use this ratio but the concept shaped public understanding.
  • Stanford-Binet — Terman's 1916 adaptation of Binet-Simon for American children. Introduced the IQ formula. Currently in its 5th edition; still used clinically though less common than the Wechsler scales.
  • Wechsler scales (WAIS-IV, WISC-V, WPPSI-IV) — The most widely used modern intelligence tests. Yield a Full Scale IQ plus separate index scores (Verbal Comprehension, Perceptual/Visual Spatial Reasoning, Working Memory, Processing Speed). Three age-banded versions: WAIS for adults, WISC for school-age children, WPPSI for preschoolers.
  • Deviation IQ — Modern statistical method for calculating IQ — comparing a person's performance to the average for their age group rather than computing MA/CA ratios. IQ = 100 is age-average; standard deviations above and below produce higher and lower scores. Replaced the ratio IQ in modern testing.
  • Divergent thinking — Thinking that produces many original, unusual responses to an open-ended prompt (the opposite of convergent thinking, which seeks one correct answer). Foundation of creativity. Measured by tests like the Torrance Tests of Creative Thinking. Correlates only modestly with IQ above moderate ability levels.
  • Twice-exceptional (2e) — Children who are both gifted AND have a disability (ADHD, autism, learning disability, sensory or physical impairment). Need both gifted programming and special education support. Often missed by systems that recognize only one of their two patterns. Frustration is intense — they know what they want to do but can't always execute.
  • Nuffield Early Language Intervention — Evidence-based oral language intervention for children with impaired language skills. 30-week program based on Fricke et al. (2013) research — interactive book reading, listening skills training, vocabulary games. Replicated in independent implementation (Fricke et al., 2017). Demonstrably improves vocabulary, expressive language, listening, AND later reading comprehension.
  • Osgood-Schlatter disease — Common cause of knee pain in growing pediatric athletes (especially during growth spurt, ages 10-15). Traction apophysitis at the tibial tubercle from repetitive quadriceps pull. Self-limited but painful; can persist months to years. Often associated with single-sport specialization and overuse.
  • Early sport specialization — Focusing on a single sport before early-to-mid adolescence. Associated with elevated overuse injury, burnout, mental health symptoms, and (paradoxically) lower long-term athletic achievement. AAP recommends delaying single-sport specialization until at least age 15-16; multi-sport participation through middle school produces better outcomes.
  • ACL injury prevention programs (FIFA 11+, PEP) — Structured neuromuscular training protocols (jumping, landing, balance, strength) that reduce anterior cruciate ligament injuries by ~50% in female soccer/basketball players. Underused in US youth sports despite strong evidence. Many clubs now require participation.

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — the author's own teenage paper-route case. A 15-year-old uses combinatorial reasoning to win a newspaper contest.

Robert Kail at the races — combinatorial reasoning case

The case: Robert Kail goes to the races

In the spring of 1965, the Indianapolis Star announced a contest for its newspaper carriers: whoever could generate the most words from the letters of SAFE RACE would win two tickets to the Indy 500. The 15-year-old Robert Kail (the textbook's first author) wanted to win.

He developed a systematic procedure: write down every 1-letter combination of the available letters, then every 2-letter combination, then every 3-letter, working all the way up. Then look each one up in a dictionary to check if it was a real word. Weeks later he had generated 126 valid words. He won the contest.

What kind of reasoning is this — and why is it interesting for a chapter on middle childhood (a stage younger than Robert at the time)?

Worked analysis

What kind of reasoning?

Combinatorial reasoning — systematically generating all possible combinations and testing each one. A hallmark of formal operational thinking in Piaget's framework. The kind of move that a 15-year-old can do but that a 10-year-old typically can't.

What concrete-operational kids would do

Try to think of words from the letters one at a time — "what words can I make?" They'd come up with the obvious ones (SAFE, FACE, ACE, CARE, RACE) but would miss the long tail of less obvious combinations because they aren't generating systematically.

Why this is in ch 6

The case highlights the limits of concrete operational thinking by contrast. School-age kids can use logic on concrete content but typically don't yet plan systematic exhaustive procedures. The transition to formal operations is what makes Robert's approach possible.

Connection to intelligence

This is also a nice illustration of Sternberg's analytic intelligence applied to a real-world problem — and arguably some practical intelligence (recognizing that systematic beats inspired). IQ tests don't usually ask about contest strategies, but this is exactly the kind of thinking they're trying to measure.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 6 core content.

Q1

A child watches you pour the same amount of juice from a short wide glass into a tall narrow glass. They confidently say "they have the same amount because nothing was added or removed". Which Piagetian stage and operation is this child most likely demonstrating?

  1. A. Sensorimotor; object permanence
  2. B. Preoperational; centration
  3. C. Concrete operational; conservation of liquid
  4. D. Formal operational; combinatorial reasoning
Show rationale

A. Sensorimotor is 0–2 years. Not relevant here.

B. Preoperational children FAIL this task — they say the tall glass has more, centering on height.

C. CORRECT. Passing conservation of liquid (and giving a logical justification — "nothing added or removed") is the textbook marker of concrete operational thinking. The child has reversibility and decentration.

D. Formal operations is about hypothetical / abstract reasoning. This task is concrete.

Q2

A child scores 115 on a standard IQ test. Roughly what percentile does this correspond to, and what's the practical interpretation?

  1. A. ~5th percentile — below average
  2. B. ~50th percentile — average
  3. C. ~84th percentile — above average
  4. D. ~99th percentile — exceptional / gifted
Show rationale

A. 5th percentile is around IQ 75.

B. 50th percentile is the mean — IQ 100.

C. CORRECT. IQ tests have mean 100 and SD 15. A score of 115 is 1 SD above the mean = about the 84th percentile = above average but well within the typical range (~68% of people score 85–115).

D. 99th percentile corresponds to IQ ~135 — the gifted range.

Q3

A student is brilliant at coming up with novel solutions to ill-defined problems and creating unusual products, but struggles with standardized academic tasks. According to Sternberg's triarchic theory, which intelligence is this student likely strongest in?

  1. A. Analytic — academic problem-solving
  2. B. Creative — handling novelty
  3. C. Practical — everyday "street smarts"
  4. D. All three equally
Show rationale

A. Analytic intelligence is what standardized tests typically measure. The student struggles with those — suggesting analytic is NOT their strongest.

B. CORRECT. Generating novel solutions to ill-defined problems is the defining feature of Sternberg's creative intelligence — the ability to handle situations that aren't routine. Importantly, this can dissociate from analytic — students can be high in creative and lower in analytic, or vice versa.

C. Practical intelligence is about everyday street smarts (navigating social situations, common-sense problem solving). Different focus.

D. The whole point of the triarchic theory is that these dissociate.

Q4

A 7-year-old is highly active and easily distracted ONLY in classroom settings — at home, on the playground, and at extracurriculars, they are calm and focused. Should this child receive an ADHD diagnosis?

  1. A. Yes — even one setting is enough for diagnosis
  2. B. No — ADHD requires symptoms in two or more settings; this child's pattern suggests a school-specific cause
  3. C. Yes — but only stimulant medication, not behavioral therapy
  4. D. Yes, but only if their parents request it
Show rationale

A. Single-setting symptoms specifically don't meet DSM-5 criteria for ADHD.

B. CORRECT. ADHD is a persistent, cross-setting pattern. A child only showing symptoms in one setting likely has a situation-specific issue (learning disability, anxiety, mismatch with teaching style, classroom environment, etc.). Misdiagnosing situational behavior as ADHD risks unnecessary medication AND missing the actual problem. Proper evaluation looks at home AND school AND other settings.

C. Even if the child had ADHD, behavioral therapy plus medication is the evidence-based combination — not medication alone.

D. Diagnosis requires meeting the criteria, regardless of parent request.

Q5

A researcher gives the same math test to two groups of female students. Group A is told "this test typically shows no gender differences." Group B is told "this test typically shows men do better than women." Group A performs significantly better than Group B. What does this best demonstrate?

  1. A. Group A students were actually more skilled at math
  2. B. Gender differences in math ability are biological
  3. C. Stereotype threat — awareness of negative stereotypes about one's group reduces performance
  4. D. The Flynn effect
Show rationale

A. Random assignment to the two groups would equalize underlying skill. The difference must come from the instructions.

B. If the differences were biological, the instructions wouldn't change performance. The fact that they do is direct evidence against the biological-only account.

C. CORRECT. This is the textbook stereotype-threat result. Simply making the stereotype salient (or not) is enough to shift performance — the same individuals do worse when reminded of a negative stereotype about their group. The mechanism is cognitive: anxiety + monitoring + working-memory load all consume resources that would otherwise go to the test.

D. The Flynn effect is the rise in IQ scores across decades, unrelated to within-session instruction effects.

Q6

Lyster, Lervåg, and Hulme (2016) trained Norwegian preschoolers for 30 minutes weekly over 17 weeks on word structure (prefixes, suffixes, compound words). What did they find at 7-year follow-up, and what's the strongest interpretation?

  1. A. No lasting effect was found
  2. B. As sixth graders, the training group scored significantly better on reading comprehension than controls across multiple text types; this illustrates a developmental cascade — brief targeted early language training enriches vocabulary, which compounds across 7 years into substantial reading-comprehension differences
  3. C. The training only worked while it was happening
  4. D. The training helped only Norwegian-speaking children specifically
Show rationale

A. Effects were robust and persistent — significant differences on all three reading-comprehension tasks at 7-year follow-up.

B. CORRECT. The 7-year persistence of effects from ~8.5 hours of total training demonstrates a powerful developmental cascade. The mechanism is compounding: better word recognition → more independent reading → richer vocabulary → better comprehension → access to more advanced texts → further learning. Small early differences compound across years. This is one reason early literacy interventions can have outsized later effects compared to what their immediate impact would suggest.

C. The opposite — the long-term persistence is one of the study's most important findings.

D. Norwegian's consistent letter-sound mapping might mean the effects could be even larger in English (which has more variable pronunciation). Replication is needed but the result isn't language-bound.

Q7

A US parent reads that Taiwanese 5th graders spend 8 hours in school + 2–3 hours homework nightly and outperform US peers on math. They want to apply this model directly to their child. What's the most balanced response?

  1. A. Yes, copy the model exactly — academic achievement is the most important outcome
  2. B. Higher academic standards and more involved parenting do produce achievement gains, but research finds Asian children also report less happiness, lower self-worth, and greater pressure to succeed; a balanced approach would integrate higher academic expectations with attention to emotional wellbeing rather than copying either system wholesale
  3. C. The differences are entirely genetic — copying parenting won't help
  4. D. There is no real difference; the data are biased
Show rationale

A. Ignores the documented emotional costs that come alongside the achievement gains.

B. CORRECT. Research consistently finds that Asian parenting and schooling practices produce higher math achievement at an emotional cost — children report less happiness, lower self-worth, and more pressure. US practices prioritize happiness and self-esteem at an academic cost. Neither set of practices is universally superior — they prioritize different outcomes. A balanced approach: set higher academic standards and stay actively involved (the achievement-promoting elements), while protecting attention to wellbeing, autonomy, and intrinsic motivation. This is more nuanced than the wholesale-copy approach.

C. The fact that scores differ across cultures within the same broad ethnic groups (e.g., comparing US and Asian-American performance, or comparing different Asian countries) argues strongly against a purely genetic explanation.

D. The gap is consistent across many measures over 25+ years and several methodologies — not a measurement artifact.

Q8

A teacher wants to reduce stereotype threat in a diverse classroom before an important math test. Which approach best reflects the evidence?

  1. A. Tell students explicitly that "girls usually do worse on math" so they prepare for it
  2. B. Frame the test as practice or skill-building rather than ability measurement; affirm students' broader identities and competencies; ensure diverse representation in classroom materials and examples — these reduce the activation and impact of stereotype threat without ignoring real performance differences
  3. C. Eliminate all tests
  4. D. Stereotype threat doesn't exist; it's all in the students' minds
Show rationale

A. This activates stereotype threat, which research consistently shows worsens performance for the targeted group.

B. CORRECT. Evidence-based interventions for stereotype threat: (1) reframe the assessment away from ability measurement; (2) self-affirmation exercises (briefly writing about values or strengths before the test) reduce threat impact; (3) diverse representation in materials and examples; (4) identity-safe environments where students don't feel their group identity puts them on display. These approaches don't ignore real performance differences — they reduce the artificial performance reduction caused by threat itself.

C. Tests serve real functions in measuring learning. The goal is to remove artificial performance suppression, not to eliminate assessment.

D. Stereotype threat is one of the more robustly demonstrated effects in psychology — including with physiological correlates (heart rate, cortisol) that prove the effect isn't purely subjective.

Q9

A school nurse is approached by a teacher who suspects an 8-year-old has dyslexia. The child reads slowly, has trouble matching letters to sounds, and reverses some letters. Which is the most appropriate response?

  1. A. Reassure that letter reversals are normal until middle school
  2. B. Recommend formal evaluation — dyslexia involves persistent difficulties with accurate word recognition, decoding, and spelling despite adequate instruction and intelligence; assessment by school psychology team is appropriate; evidence-based intervention is structured systematic phonics-based instruction (Orton-Gillingham approach); early intervention substantially improves outcomes; ensure vision and hearing screening also done
  3. C. Wait until age 10 before evaluation
  4. D. Suggest the family try harder at home
Show rationale

A. Some letter reversals are common in early reading but persistent decoding difficulties at age 8 warrant evaluation.

B. CORRECT. Dyslexia = persistent difficulty with accurate word recognition and decoding despite adequate instruction and intelligence. Key features: trouble matching letters to sounds, slow reading, spelling difficulties, possible reversals (less diagnostic than once thought). Action steps: (1) Formal evaluation by school psychology team — IDEA requires it when LD is suspected. (2) Vision and hearing screening first — rule out sensory cause. (3) If diagnosed, IEP with evidence-based intervention: structured, systematic, phonics-based instruction (Orton-Gillingham approach is the gold standard). (4) Don't wait — earlier intervention has the strongest evidence base. (5) Address the emotional side: children with reading difficulties often develop anxiety and shame around reading; protecting self-esteem matters as much as academic skill.

C. Waiting wastes critical intervention years; outcomes are worse with delayed intervention.

D. Dyslexia is a neurodevelopmental condition, not a motivation issue.

Q10

A 9-year-old patient is being evaluated for possible ADHD. Parents report: difficulty focusing on homework, easily distracted, but no hyperactivity, fidgeting fine in school. The teacher confirms inattention. Which presentation is most likely, and what should the workup include?

  1. A. ADHD doesn't exist in girls/quiet children
  2. B. ADHD predominantly inattentive presentation (formerly "ADD") — quieter, more frequently missed in girls; workup should include comprehensive history from multiple settings (school, home), DSM-5 criteria checklist, screen for differential diagnoses (anxiety, learning disability, sleep disorder, hearing/vision issues), and consider rating scales (Vanderbilt, Conners); evidence-based treatment combines behavioral approaches and/or stimulant medication
  3. C. The teacher is mistaken
  4. D. Just try diet changes
Show rationale

A. ADHD occurs across genders; the inattentive subtype is just more easily missed because it doesn't disrupt the classroom.

B. CORRECT. DSM-5 recognizes three ADHD presentations: predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive-impulsive, and combined. The inattentive presentation is often missed especially in girls and quiet children — they don't disrupt class so they're overlooked, sometimes diagnosed only in middle/high school when academic demands exceed coping. Workup essentials: (1) DSM-5 criteria require symptoms in 2+ settings, present before age 12, causing impairment. (2) Rating scales (Vanderbilt, Conners) from parent AND teacher. (3) Rule out anxiety, depression, learning disability, sleep apnea, hearing/vision problems — these can mimic or co-occur. (4) Audiology/vision screening. (5) Consider executive function testing. Evidence-based treatment: behavioral approaches (parent training, classroom modifications, organizational support) plus stimulant medication has the strongest evidence base. Combined treatment > either alone for moderate-severe ADHD.

C. Teacher reports from a multi-hour daily setting are valuable diagnostic data, not noise.

D. Diet changes have limited evidence; not first-line. Children deserve appropriate diagnostic workup and evidence-based treatment.

Q11

A school nurse is teaching healthy eating to children in a low-income school with high obesity rates. Which intervention approach has the strongest evidence base?

  1. A. Tell parents to make their kids exercise more
  2. B. Multi-level intervention targeting environment, family, and child: school food environment (healthier cafeteria options, water-not-soda), physical activity opportunities, family education without shame or fat-stigma, screening for medical comorbidities (insulin resistance, sleep apnea, fatty liver), addressing food insecurity, and partnering with families on culturally appropriate strategies; individual willpower interventions alone don't work
  3. C. Public weight-shaming of overweight students
  4. D. Strict calorie counting starting at age 6
Show rationale

A. Individual "willpower" interventions have weak evidence; child obesity is driven by complex environmental factors.

B. CORRECT. Childhood obesity (~20% of US children) is a public health issue driven by multiple interacting factors. Evidence-based intervention is multi-level: environmental (school food, food deserts, walkable neighborhoods, screen-time defaults), family (cooking skills, family meals, modeling, avoiding shame-based approaches that worsen disordered eating), individual (medical workup for comorbidities, addressing food insecurity, building physical activity), and cultural (working WITH community values, not against). Critical to avoid: stigma — research shows weight stigma INCREASES eating disorders and worsens metabolic health. Nursing role: screening, family education, identifying comorbidities (T2DM, sleep apnea, fatty liver, joint problems), addressing social determinants (food insecurity is a major driver), connecting families to nutrition resources, respectful family-centered communication.

C. Shame and stigma worsen outcomes — both psychological and physical. Never appropriate.

D. Strict calorie restriction in children risks growth disruption and disordered eating; not recommended.

Q12

A pediatric nurse explains to parents why their 10-year-old, who has an IQ of 120, may still struggle with abstract reasoning in math word problems involving variables (e.g., "if Alex has 3 more apples than twice the number Bob has..."). Which is the most accurate developmental explanation?

  1. A. The child must have a learning disability despite high IQ
  2. B. At 10, the child is in Piaget's concrete operational stage (~7–11 years) — reasoning is logical when applied to concrete, observable content but struggles with abstract, hypothetical, or symbolic content like algebraic variables; this is developmentally typical, not a deficit; formal operational reasoning (abstract, hypothetical-deductive) emerges around age 11–12, more reliably by mid-adolescence
  3. C. The child needs more practice
  4. D. The IQ score must be wrong
Show rationale

A. Difficulty with abstract symbolic reasoning at age 10 is typical, not a learning disability.

B. CORRECT. At age 10, the child is in Piaget's concrete operational stage. Hallmarks: logical reasoning, mental operations, conservation, classification — but applied to concrete, observable content. Abstract symbolic reasoning (algebraic variables, hypothetical scenarios, "if X were the case, then Y") is the next stage's work — formal operational thinking, emerging around age 11–12 and developing through adolescence. High IQ doesn't accelerate stage transitions; it just predicts faster progression within stages. Educational implications: (1) Teach math with concrete representations (manipulatives, drawings, story contexts) at this age. (2) Variable-heavy algebra is developmentally premature for most 10-year-olds. (3) Use bridge approaches (pre-algebra with concrete examples). (4) Reassure parents that abstract math fluency comes with development; pushing too hard too early can produce math anxiety without speeding up the cognitive transition.

C. Practice alone doesn't accelerate the cognitive transition to formal operational thinking.

D. The IQ score isn't wrong — it just measures different abilities than abstract symbolic reasoning.

Unit 2 · School-Age & Adolescence · Chapter 7

Expanding Social Horizons: Socioemotional Development in Middle Childhood

TL;DR · One-glance summary

School-age children's social worlds expand outward. Family stays central, but peers rise to roughly equal importance, with friendships, status, and bullying becoming defining experiences.
