Children develop more nuanced self-esteem across multiple domains and get sharper at understanding others — moving from concrete descriptions ("she's a good reader") to psychological ones ("she can be sarcastic"). Electronic media is now a major developmental context; parental and structural choices about it matter a lot.

Family

  • Parents shift toward co-regulation — guidance from distance
  • Authoritative parenting still predicts best outcomes
  • Divorce: kids recover, but transitions are stressful
  • Siblings: rivalry & support, both real

Peers & friendships

  • Friendships deepen from "play partners" to confidants
  • 5 peer statuses: popular, rejected, controversial, neglected, average
  • Rejected children are at highest risk for later problems
  • Bullying: intentional, repeated, power imbalance

Self & others

  • Self-esteem becomes domain-specific (academic, social, athletic, appearance)
  • Social comparisons become more frequent and consequential
  • Descriptions of others shift: concrete → psychological
  • Prejudice and stereotypes are learned early, modifiable

Media

  • Children: TV & games dominate; social media less
  • Content + context + amount all matter
  • Aggressive content → modest aggression effects (Bandura's legacy)
  • Prosocial content → measurable prosocial gains

7.1 · Family Relationships

The family remains the most influential context in middle childhood, but the child–parent relationship is renegotiated as the child gains independence and spends more hours with peers and teachers.

Family relationships in middle childhood

Co-regulation — parenting at greater distance

In infancy and early childhood, parents largely manage children's behavior directly. In middle childhood, parents shift toward co-regulation — setting expectations and providing structure, but expecting the child to handle moment-to-moment decisions. The relationship becomes more verbal, more negotiated, and more reasoned. Authoritative parenting continues to predict the best outcomes; the warmth + reasoned structure combination scales naturally into co-regulation.

Divorce & family transitions

Divorce is hard for children — but the long-term outcomes are more nuanced than the cultural narrative suggests. Most children of divorce show short-term distress (academic dips, behavioral problems, emotional difficulty) but recover within ~2 years. Long-term outcomes depend heavily on what happens after: continued parental conflict, economic instability, and multiple transitions are worse than the divorce itself.

What predicts better child outcomes after divorce

Low parental conflict (before, during, and after), continued involvement of both parents, stable routines and economic situation, and parents who are themselves coping well. Conflict, not the divorce, predicts most of the harm — children in high-conflict intact families often fare worse than children whose divorced parents co-parent calmly.

A 9-year-old's parents are considering divorce. Their main concern is the long-term effect on their child. Based on the research, what predicts whether the child will be substantially harmed?
Not the divorce itself, but parental conflict, post-divorce stability, and continued involvement of both parents. Children in high-conflict intact families often fare worse than children in low-conflict divorced families. Most children show short-term distress but recover within ~2 years. Worst predictors: continued conflict, economic instability, multiple transitions (parents repartnering, repeat moves, repeat divorces), and parents who are themselves struggling without support.
A pediatric nurse counsels parents who say their 10-year-old "won't listen anymore" and they're tempted to "crack down harder." Why does authoritarian escalation typically backfire in middle childhood, and what should the nurse recommend?
Middle childhood is when children begin testing parental authority while still needing it — escalating authoritarian control typically produces resentment, sneaking, and damaged relationship without resolving underlying issues. Why authoritarian escalation fails: (1) Children at this age value autonomy and fairness; harsh control without reasoning feels arbitrary. (2) Discipline that works for 4-year-olds (time-outs, simple consequences) doesn't fit 10-year-olds, who need increasing autonomy with scaffolding. (3) Coercive cycles (Patterson) escalate: parent demands → child resists → parent escalates → child resists more. (4) Damages the relationship needed for adolescent-era influence. What works — co-regulation with authoritative warmth: (1) Maintain firm expectations AND explain reasoning. (2) Listen first — is the child arguing because they don't understand, because they have a legitimate point, or because they're tired/hungry/stressed? (3) Pick battles — major safety/values issues get firm enforcement; minor preferences get flexibility. (4) Use natural consequences when possible. (5) Stay calm — yelling models emotional dysregulation. (6) Repair after conflict — name what went wrong, acknowledge your share, reconnect. (7) For chronic conflict: parent management training programs (e.g., Triple P, Incredible Years) have strong evidence base. This is the foundation for adolescence — children whose parents shift to co-regulation in middle childhood navigate adolescence more smoothly than those whose parents either escalated control or abandoned it.

Siblings

Sibling relationships are typically a child's longest-running relationship. They combine genuine warmth (shared history, mutual support) with genuine conflict (competing for parental attention, different temperaments). Sibling rivalry is intensified by parental favoritism — even small perceived unfairness produces lasting friction. Older siblings often play teaching and modeling roles; younger siblings tend to learn faster on some social-cognitive tasks (like theory of mind).

Diverse Perspectives — families with same-sex partners as parents

The landscape

Many US children have a gay or lesbian parent. In most cases, children were born in a prior heterosexual marriage that ended in divorce when one parent came out. Less common but increasingly visible: children born to single lesbians or to same-sex couples through artificial insemination, adoption, or surrogacy (Tasker & Lavender-Stott, 2020).

Gay and lesbian couples are more similar to heterosexual couples as parents than they are different — though lesbian and gay couples more often share child-rearing tasks evenly than heterosexual couples (Farr & Patterson, 2013).

What the research consistently shows

Process > structure

When research directly compares the impact of family structure (heterosexual vs. same-sex parents) against family process variables (parental stress, parental warmth, parental discipline), process predicts child outcomes; structure does not (Farr et al., 2010; Golombok et al., 2014). Translation: how parents parent matters; the genders of the parents don't.

Gender development unaffected

Preschool boys and girls of same-sex parents identify with their own sex normally and acquire typical gender-based preferences, interests, activities, and friends — at the same rate as children of heterosexual parents. Children do not "catch" their parents' sexual orientation.

Adolescent outcomes

As adolescents, most children of lesbian mothers are heterosexual, though daughters may be somewhat more open to exploring same-sex relationships than peers (Gartrell et al., 2011). The increase reflects greater openness, not fundamentally different orientation rates.

Other outcomes equivalent

Self-concept, social skills, peer relations, moral reasoning, behavior problems, intelligence — children of lesbian mothers resemble children of heterosexual parents (Farr et al., 2020). Warm, caring parenting matters; parent sexual orientation doesn't.

Some children report stigma

Some children of lesbian mothers report being bullied because their mother is lesbian. They also report benefits from their parents' identity — being more accepting and understanding of others (Imrie & Golombok, 2020). Both are real.

Big picture

Research on same-sex parenting (along with findings on blended families) challenges the conventional wisdom that the traditional two-parent mother-father family necessarily provides the best circumstances. Multiple committed adults matter — that's clear from divorce research. Who the adults are seems to matter less than what they do. A question worth sitting with: given that children in same-sex households may have less direct in-home exposure to one sex, how do they still acquire knowledge of other-sex gender roles? Research suggests peers, extended family, media, and the wider culture supply ample exposure, so gender learning is not confined to the home.

For nursing practice

  • Use inclusive language: "your parents" or "your family" rather than "your mom and dad". Update intake forms to allow "parent 1 / parent 2" or open-ended family structure description.
  • Recognize legal complexity: in some jurisdictions, non-biological/non-legal same-sex parents have less authority for medical decisions. Ask about legal parent status and second-parent adoption to ensure the right adult can consent.
  • Don't pathologize: research is clear that same-sex parenting doesn't harm children. Concerns about a child should focus on the child's actual presentation, not the family structure.
  • Address bullying risks: screen for school-based bullying related to family structure; connect to LGBTQ+ youth support resources if needed (GLSEN, PFLAG, Trevor Project, Family Equality).
  • Support family advocacy: same-sex parent families may face systemic challenges (schools, healthcare, custody) that benefit from validated resources and connections.

Child maltreatment — types, causes, signs

Four types of maltreatment

Maltreatment comes in many forms; the four major categories overlap and often co-occur:

Neglect (~75% of cases)

The MOST COMMON form of maltreatment. Failure to provide adequate food, clothing, shelter, medical care, supervision, or education (USDHHS, 2016). May be due to poverty, parental impairment, or willful denial. Often invisible — no bruises, just chronic understimulation, undernutrition, or unmet medical needs.

Physical abuse

Assault leading to injuries — bruises, fractures, burns, traumatic head injury. May be discrete events or chronic patterns. Pattern of inconsistent explanations, injuries inconsistent with developmental stage (a non-mobile baby with bruises), or pattern injuries (belt marks, cigarette burns, bite marks) are red flags.

Sexual abuse

Fondling, intercourse, exposure to sexual content, or other sexual behaviors imposed on a child. May be intra-familial (most common) or extra-familial. Disclosure is often delayed; symptoms include behavioral regression, sexualized behavior inappropriate for age, withdrawal, sleep disturbance, somatic complaints.

Psychological / emotional abuse

Ridicule, rejection, humiliation, terrorizing, isolating. Often co-occurs with other types. Hardest to identify and prosecute but among the most damaging long-term. Signs include extreme low self-esteem, anxiety symptoms, suicidality, attachment disturbance, and (paradoxically) over-compliance with the abusing parent.

Who maltreats — and why

Most abusing parents do NOT have a specific mental or psychological disorder. Risk emerges from combinations of factors at three levels (Cicchetti & Toth, 2006):

Cultural / social conditions

(1) Cultural acceptance of physical punishment — in countries where spanking is illegal (Austria, Croatia, Germany, Israel, Sweden, others), abuse rates are lower. US cultural acceptance of physical punishment increases the path from discipline to abuse. (2) Poverty — maltreatment is more common in families living in poverty, partly because daily stress is higher and parents have less cognitive bandwidth to reason instead of punish. COVID-19 saw increases in maltreatment when parents lost jobs (Lawson et al., 2020). (3) Social isolation — when families lack relatives, neighbors, or community connection, both children lose protective adults and parents lose support.

Parent characteristics

(1) Own history of maltreatment — about ONE-THIRD of abused children become abusing parents. Most do not. But having been abused doubles or triples risk; it normalizes abuse as parenting. (2) Ineffective parenting skills — abusing parents often have limited repertoires; they don't know reasoning, time-out, or natural-consequence strategies. (3) Mental health challenges — depression, substance use, intimate partner violence in the home all elevate risk.

Child characteristics

(1) Younger age — younger children are more vulnerable physically AND less able to seek help or report. (2) Stepchildren — children are more often abused by step-parents than biological parents. (3) Difficult temperament — children with high irritability, attention problems, or special needs are at elevated risk because they tax parents more. NONE of this BLAMES the child — child characteristics interact with parental capacity, not cause abuse.

The accumulation principle

No single risk factor causes abuse. Risk emerges from multiple factors accumulating: a parent with their own abuse history, in poverty, socially isolated, with a temperamentally difficult child, under acute life stress — risk is high. Removing ANY of these factors substantially reduces risk. This is why intervention works.

Consequences for maltreated children

Maltreatment has effects across virtually every developmental domain:

  • Physical — direct injuries; chronic stress effects on growth, immune function, brain development
  • Cognitive — lower school achievement, language delays, attention difficulties, executive function deficits
  • Social — disorganized attachment, peer rejection, difficulty trusting, aggression
  • Emotional — depression, anxiety, PTSD, emotion regulation problems
  • Adult outcomes — Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) research documents long-term effects on physical health (heart disease, diabetes), mental health, substance use, and life expectancy

Effects are not deterministic — many maltreated children recover with intervention, supportive relationships, and time. Resilience factors include at least one stable caring adult, internal cognitive resources, and meaningful community connection.

Linking Research to Life — preventing child maltreatment

A multi-pronged approach

Because maltreatment results from accumulated risk factors, prevention requires addressing several at once. No single intervention works for all families. The major evidence-based strategies:

Evidence-based prevention programs

Nurse-Family Partnership (NFP)

Evidence-based home visiting program. RNs visit low-income first-time mothers from pregnancy through child's 2nd birthday. Documented reductions in maltreatment, ED visits, child injuries; improvements in maternal employment, education, and child development. Olds et al. (1997) classic RCT showed 79% reduction in verified abuse. Best evidence for any maltreatment prevention.

Triple P (Positive Parenting Program)

Evidence-based parenting program with multiple tiers (universal media campaigns to intensive multi-week training). Population-level rollout in South Carolina county reduced substantiated maltreatment by 16% (Prinz et al., 2009). Works through teaching effective discipline, reducing parental stress, building parent confidence.

Head Start & preschool programs

Zhai et al. (2013): when children attend Head Start, parents become more committed to children's education, children succeed more in school, parental stress decreases, parents gain child-rearing confidence — all of which reduce abuse risk. The mechanism is indirect but real. Early childhood education is anti-maltreatment infrastructure.

Parent-Child Interaction Therapy (PCIT)

Evidence-based 14-20 session therapy for families where maltreatment has occurred or risk is elevated. Therapist coaches parent in real-time interaction with child. Teaches positive attention, ignoring minor misbehavior, effective limit-setting. Reduces re-abuse rates and improves child behavior. van IJzendoorn et al. (2020).

Home visiting (broader)

Healthy Families America, Parents as Teachers, Early Head Start home-based option. All deliver in-home support, parent education, and connection to resources for at-risk families. Varying evidence; NFP has the strongest. ALL beat the status quo of no support.

Cultural & policy change

Reducing acceptance of physical punishment, addressing poverty, paid parental leave, accessible mental health services, substance use treatment. Structural changes at population level prevent maltreatment at scales individual interventions can't match.

Two prevention principles

(1) Treat parents, not just children. Most parents who maltreat WANT to parent better — they often lack skills, supports, or capacity. Helping parents reduces re-abuse risk; punishment alone often produces worse outcomes for children if the alternative is foster care drift.

(2) Strengthen communities, not just families. Parents in thriving neighborhoods with strong networks and accessible services don't reach the breaking point at which maltreatment occurs. Community development IS child abuse prevention, even when it doesn't carry that label.

For nursing practice — what nurses can do

(1) Screening: ask about parenting stress, social support, financial stress at well-child visits. (2) Connect to resources: WIC, SNAP, home visiting, parenting classes, mental health services. (3) Recognize warning signs: unexplained injuries, developmental delays without medical cause, fearful demeanor around parent, parent who's dismissive or hostile to child. (4) Trauma-informed care: assume any patient may have a maltreatment history; build trust through predictability and respect. (5) Mandated reporting: when suspicion meets the threshold, report. Document objective observations. Report to CPS; document the report. (6) Don't moralize at parents: condemnation increases barriers to help. (7) Advocate systemically: better paid leave, accessible mental health care, evidence-based home visiting funding. (8) Self-care: working with child maltreatment is traumatic for clinicians; secondary traumatic stress is real. Get supervision and support.

7.2 · Peers

In middle childhood, peers move from "people I play with" to "people whose opinions of me matter". Friendships deepen, peer groups stratify into status categories, and bullying becomes a defining negative experience for some children.

Peers — friendships, status, bullying

Friendships deepen

Younger children's friendships are activity-based ("we play together"). By middle childhood they become person-based — friendships are defined by mutual liking, trust, and shared values. The ability to take a friend's perspective grows. Same-sex friendships dominate during this period; cross-sex friendships are rarer.

Having even one good friend buffers against many social stressors. The protective effect of a single best friend on mental health outcomes is consistently strong across studies.

Peer status — five categories

Researchers ask children to nominate peers they like most and like least. Combining these produces five categories:

Popular

Many positive nominations, few negative. Tend to be socially skilled, cooperative, helpful. Some "popular but mean" exist (a sub-type with high status but low actual liking).

Rejected

Many negative nominations, few positive. Highest-risk category. Sub-types: rejected-aggressive (impulsive, hostile) and rejected-withdrawn (anxious, isolated). Both at elevated risk for later mental-health and academic problems.

Controversial

Many of BOTH positive and negative nominations — liked by some, disliked by others. Often socially skilled but assertive or polarizing.

Neglected

Few of either positive or negative. Quiet, low visibility. Generally better outcomes than rejected children; many just prefer fewer relationships.

Average

Moderate numbers of both. The majority of children fall here.

A school counselor is identifying children most at-risk for future mental-health problems. Among the peer-status categories, which group warrants the most concern and why?
Rejected children — especially the rejected-aggressive and rejected-withdrawn sub-types. They receive many negative peer nominations, indicating sustained social exclusion. The exclusion is both a marker (it reflects existing problems) AND a contributor (it amplifies them through reduced practice, fewer prosocial models, and erosion of self-esteem). They are at elevated long-term risk for depression, anxiety, school dropout, and conduct issues. Neglected children, despite low visibility, generally do fine — many simply prefer fewer relationships. The size and persistence of the rejection signal is what matters.
A 12-year-old girl spends hours daily texting her best friend about how unfair things are, how anxious she feels, how someone said something hurtful. Mom is glad the daughter has a close friend. What does the research on co-rumination suggest?
Co-rumination = extensively, repeatedly discussing problems with a friend, focusing on negative feelings, speculating about causes, dwelling on consequences. Research shows it has a paradoxical effect: (1) Strengthens friendship quality and closeness — both partners feel deeply understood. (2) Increases risk for depression and anxiety in BOTH partners — rumination intensifies and prolongs negative affect. The friendship feels good while the mental health worsens. Co-rumination is more common in girls' friendships and predicts depression incidence longitudinally (Rose 2002, 2007). Nursing implications: (1) Don't dismiss close friendships in adolescence — they are developmentally important. (2) Notice the pattern: how much time is spent specifically rehashing negatives vs. enjoyable activities together? (3) Screen for depression and anxiety in adolescents with intense co-ruminative patterns, especially girls. (4) Therapeutic intervention: cognitive-behavioral therapy can teach alternatives — problem-solving framings, behavioral activation, distress tolerance, balanced thinking — without sacrificing the friendship. (5) Family education: parents may need to help their adolescent diversify activities and supports rather than depending on one friend for all emotional processing. (6) Boys also co-ruminate but less; gender differences in adolescent depression rates may partly reflect this pattern.

Bullying

Three required features

Bullying isn't just one-time aggression. The definition has three required features:

1. Intentional

The behavior is deliberate, not accidental. The bully means to cause harm.

2. Repeated

Happens over time, not a single incident. A one-time fight is aggression; sustained targeting is bullying.

3. Power imbalance

The bully has more power than the target — physical size, social status, numbers, or knowledge of vulnerabilities. The target can't easily defend themselves.

Forms

Physical, verbal, relational (exclusion, rumors), and cyberbullying. Relational bullying is more common in girls; physical more common in boys, though both occur across genders.

Effective anti-bullying programs are school-wide, change norms (so bystanders intervene rather than spectate), and address the underlying dynamics — not just punish bullies. Outcomes for both bullies and victims include long-term mental-health risks; the dynamic harms everyone involved.

Two children get into a heated fight at school. Is this bullying? What three criteria need to be checked?
Probably not bullying — at least not from one incident alone. The three criteria for bullying are: (1) intentional — was the harm deliberate? (2) repeated — has this happened to the same target over time? (3) power imbalance — is one child clearly more powerful (size, status, numbers) so the target can't defend themselves? A one-time fight between roughly equal kids is aggression, not bullying. Treating every conflict as bullying dilutes the term and misses the dynamics that actually need school-wide intervention.
Olweus research found that bullying continues when bystanders watch silently or laugh, and decreases dramatically when bystanders intervene. Why are most school anti-bullying programs now focusing on bystanders rather than just bullies and victims?
The shift to bystander-focused intervention reflects research showing that the social context — not just the bully-victim dyad — sustains bullying. Key findings: (1) Bullying happens with an audience — most incidents have peer witnesses. (2) Bystander silence is interpreted as approval by both bully and victim. (3) When peers intervene, bullying stops within 10 seconds in ~50% of cases. (4) Most bystanders feel bad watching but don't know what to do or fear becoming the next target. Why direct bully-victim interventions are limited: (1) Punishing the bully without changing peer culture allows new bullies to fill the role. (2) Removing victims (transferring schools, isolation) doesn't change the system. (3) Many bullies have low empathy or social processing differences that limit response to talking. Bystander-focused programs (Olweus, KiVa, others): (1) Train all students to recognize bullying, including subtler forms. (2) Teach specific intervention skills — speaking up safely, supporting the target, getting adult help. (3) Reframe identity — being a "silent bystander" becomes shameful, being an "upstander" becomes prestigious. (4) Change classroom and school norms so bullying loses social reward. (5) Adults model intervention. (6) Results: meta-analyses show ~20–50% reduction in bullying with well-implemented programs. For nursing: school nurses often partner with these programs and identify both targets and bystanders who need support.

7.3 · Electronic Media

Electronic media is now a developmental context as central as the playground once was. What matters isn't simply "how much screen time" but the interaction of content, context, and child.

Electronic media

What children use

School-age children's media use is dominated by video and games, with social media use rising rapidly in late middle childhood. Average daily time often exceeds 4–6 hours across screens. Like all averages, this masks huge variation by family.

Three factors that matter most

Content

Aggressive content produces modest but real increases in aggression (Bandura's Bobo doll legacy extended decades later). Prosocial content produces measurable prosocial gains. Educational content at appropriate developmental levels can support cognitive development.

Context

Co-viewing and discussing with adults turns passive consumption into learning. Background TV during dinner reduces family conversation. Screens in bedrooms predict worse sleep and outcomes.

Amount

High total screen time displaces sleep, physical activity, and face-to-face interaction. American Academy of Pediatrics guidelines emphasize quality over strict time limits, especially after early childhood.

The child

Same content affects children differently based on temperament, social context, and existing tendencies. Aggressive content has bigger effects on children already prone to aggression; isolated children's online connections can be protective.

A parent asks: "How much screen time is okay for my 8-year-old?" What's a more useful framing than a single time limit?
The research suggests quality matters more than a strict time number. Better questions to ask: (1) What content is the child consuming? (educational, prosocial, neutral, or aggressive?) (2) What context — is it co-viewed and discussed, or solo and background? (3) Is it displacing sleep, physical activity, face-to-face interaction, or homework? (4) How is the specific child responding — sleep, mood, behavior? A child watching educational programming with a parent for an hour can be net-positive; the same hour of background TV with aggressive content can be net-negative.

Spotlight on Research

Feedback on social media — when "likes" become validation

Lee, H. Y., et al. (2020) — Universities of Rochester & Texas

What was the aim?

Teenagers post personal content online and hope for peer approval in the form of "likes". When likes don't come, what happens emotionally — and are the consequences greater for teens who have already been victimized by peers?

How did they measure it?

Participants completed background questionnaires (including one measuring prior peer victimization). They were then told they would interact briefly online with 11 other teens. They selected an avatar, wrote a short self-description, and viewed avatars/descriptions from 11 fictitious peers, who they could "like". Participants then received either 11 likes or 2 likes from these fake peers. Afterward, they reported feelings of rejection, negative affect, and negative thoughts about themselves.

Who participated & what was the design?

579 ninth graders (average age 15.3), at an urban public high school. About 54% European American, 33% Hispanic/Latino. The design was experimental (random assignment to 11 vs. 2 likes). Ethical concerns were addressed: the "peers" were fictitious, the researchers debriefed participants, and they ensured no one remained distressed.

What were the results?

Feelings of rejection were significantly higher for teens who received 2 likes vs. 11 likes — and the effect was especially strong for adolescents who had previously been victimized by peers. The same pattern held for negative affect and negative thoughts about the self.

What did the investigators conclude?

In Lee and colleagues' words: "insufficient validation [receiving only 2 likes] was a brief yet powerful emotional event that threatened adolescents' social status and elicited emotional distress…" Being shunned — in the form of few likes — left adolescents feeling rejected, unhappy, and questioning their likeability. Previously-victimized teens were particularly vulnerable. The study provides experimental evidence that social-media metrics can produce real emotional harm, especially for already-at-risk adolescents.

What would strengthen the conclusions?

The study measured feelings immediately after a single brief interaction. It would be valuable to study persistence over time and effects of repeated unsupportive interactions — though that experimental design raises ethical concerns. Naturalistic studies of social-media use and emotional outcomes could provide converging evidence.

Lee et al. (2020) experimentally manipulated whether 9th graders received 11 likes or only 2 likes on a social-media-style task. What did they find about emotional response, and which adolescents were most vulnerable?
Adolescents who received only 2 likes (insufficient validation) reported significantly more feelings of rejection, negative affect, and negative thoughts about themselves than those who received 11 likes. The effect was a brief but powerful emotional event from a single 15-minute interaction. Most vulnerable: adolescents who had been previously victimized by peers showed substantially stronger negative responses than non-victimized peers. Clinical and parenting implications: social media isn't just a vehicle for content; the quantification of social approval itself produces measurable distress, particularly for already-at-risk adolescents. This isn't a "soft" or imagined effect — it shows up reliably in experimental designs with control over confounding variables. The strongest protective approach combines limiting metric-driven platforms, supporting offline social connections, and screening at-risk adolescents for cyberbullying or rejection-related symptoms.
A researcher claims that "children of divorce do worse than children with married parents". Another researcher counters that "family structure matters less than family processes". What does the family-processes-vs-structure literature actually show, and why does this matter for policy?
The evidence consistently shows that family processes — parental warmth, consistent discipline, low conflict, financial stability, supportive co-parenting — predict child outcomes more strongly than family structure (two married parents, divorced, single parent, blended, same-sex). When process variables are statistically controlled, structural differences shrink dramatically or disappear. Examples: children in low-conflict divorced families fare better than children in high-conflict intact families. Children raised by same-sex couples develop similarly to children raised by heterosexual couples (Farr et al., 2010; Golombok et al., 2014). Children in blended families fare comparably when warm parenting and consistent stepparenting are present. Policy implication: family policy that focuses on supporting process quality (parental leave, mental-health access, anti-poverty supports, parenting education) tends to produce better child outcomes than policy aimed at preserving any particular family structure. The "children need a mother and father" framing is empirically weaker than the "children need warm, consistent, supportive parenting" framing.
A school counselor is developing programming to reduce bullying's effects. Beyond stopping the bullying itself, what does the literature suggest about what protects bullied children from the worst long-term mental-health outcomes?
Stopping the bullying is the priority, but the literature identifies several protective factors for children who are or have been bullied: (1) At least one supportive friend — even one close peer relationship substantially buffers against the worst outcomes. (2) Adult support — at least one warm, trusted adult (parent, teacher, coach, religious leader) who validates the child's experience and supports them. (3) Skill-building — assertiveness and social-skills training reduce re-victimization. (4) Therapeutic support — for those showing depression, anxiety, or PTSD symptoms, evidence-based therapies (CBT) are effective. (5) School climate — anti-bullying policies that are actively enforced and that include bystander training reduce overall bullying rates. (6) Reducing victim-blaming framing — programs that frame bullied children as "needing to toughen up" increase rather than decrease distress. The Lee et al. (2020) finding — that previously-victimized teens are more vulnerable to subsequent rejection — underscores that bullying's effects compound, so the buffering work matters both during and after the bullying.

Test Yourself · Section 7.3

  1. What did Lee et al. (2020) find about the emotional impact of receiving only 2 vs. 11 likes on a social-media-style task?
  2. Which adolescents were most vulnerable to the negative effects in the Lee study, and why might that be?
  3. What framework should replace simple "screen time" measurement when evaluating media impact on children?
  4. Based on family-process research, do family structure (e.g., same-sex parents, blended families) and family process variables (warmth, conflict) have similar or different predictive power for child outcomes?
  5. What ethical considerations limit the kind of follow-up research that could establish the long-term effects of repeated social-media rejection?

7.4 · Understanding Others

How children describe other people, and themselves, transforms across middle childhood. From observable surface traits, they move to internal psychological characteristics — the foundation of mature social cognition.

Understanding others

Person perception develops

Ask a 5-year-old to describe a friend and you'll hear concrete, observable, behavioral features: "She has long hair. She lives next door. She's fast." Ask a 10-year-old and you'll hear psychological, trait-based features: "She's kind but she gets impatient. She's the kind of person who listens." This shift parallels broader cognitive growth — children are increasingly able to attribute behavior to enduring inner characteristics rather than just describing surface features.

A 5-year-old describes a friend as "she has red hair, she lives near me, she's good at running". The same friend at age 10 might be described as "she's loyal but sometimes really competitive; she gets frustrated easily". What developmental shift does this illustrate?
The shift from concrete behavioral / observable description to psychological / trait-based description of others. Young children describe what's on the surface; older children describe internal characteristics that explain patterns of behavior. This reflects deeper cognitive growth — the ability to infer enduring traits from observed behavior, to integrate contradictory data into a coherent person model, and to use traits to predict behavior. It's the foundation of mature social cognition.

Self-esteem becomes differentiated

Preschoolers tend to have global, often inflated self-esteem ("I'm great at everything"). In middle childhood, self-esteem becomes domain- specific: a child can feel competent academically but bad about athletics, or well-liked socially but unsure about their appearance. Susan Harter's research identifies several common domains: academic, social, athletic, physical appearance, behavioral conduct. Global self-worth emerges from these, weighted by which domains the child cares about.

The strongest predictor of overall self-esteem in middle childhood is perceived physical appearance — for both boys and girls, across cultures. This connects self-esteem closely to body image, especially as adolescence approaches.

Prejudice & stereotypes

Children pick up race, gender, and other social-category information very early — by preschool they can label and form preliminary preferences. In middle childhood, stereotypes can become more rigid OR more flexible depending on family, school, and peer environments. Direct contact with counter-stereotypical examples and explicit conversations about fairness are the most reliable interventions.

A school nurse partners with the school counselor to address rising peer tensions between racial/ethnic groups among 4th graders. Which approach has the strongest evidence base for reducing prejudice in middle childhood?
The most evidence-based approach combines multiple components targeting the cognitive, emotional, and behavioral roots of prejudice. Key principles: (1) Contact theory (Allport) — extensive research confirms that extended, equal-status, cooperative contact with members of other groups reduces prejudice. Brief or superficial contact doesn't work; sustained cooperative work toward shared goals does. (2) Counter-stereotypical exposure — children encountering competent, admirable members of other groups in media and curriculum revises rigid schemas. (3) Perspective-taking exercises — having children imagine the experience of someone from another group reduces implicit bias. (4) Explicit conversations about fairness — children who hear adults discuss inequality, fairness, and respect for differences show less prejudice than children whose parents avoid the topic ("colorblind" approaches actually leave children to absorb stereotypes from media). (5) Cooperative learning structures (Aronson's Jigsaw Classroom) — students depend on each other to succeed; reduces in-group/out-group salience. (6) Modeling — children copy adult behavior; teacher and parent diverse friendships matter. (7) Address adult attitudes — children pick up subtle adult biases; some interventions include parent and staff training. Approaches that DON'T work well: one-time diversity assemblies, brief lectures, didactic "be nice to everyone" messaging, calling out individual children. Critical window: middle childhood is when stereotypes can either become entrenched or remain flexible; intervention now is more effective than later in adolescence.

7.5 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Diana Baumrind — Parenting style typology (authoritative, authoritarian, permissive, uninvolved). Influential across decades of family research.
  • Eleanor Maccoby & John Martin — Refined Baumrind's framework into the two-dimensional model (warmth × demandingness) producing four styles.
  • Gerald Patterson — Documented negative reinforcement trap in coercive family processes; pioneered parent management training for aggressive children. Oregon Social Learning Center research.
  • Robert Selman — Stages of perspective-taking and recursive thinking; how children come to understand others' minds at increasing levels of complexity.
  • Dan Olweus — Pioneered modern bullying research; defined bullying as repeated, intentional, with power imbalance. Developed first widely-used anti-bullying programs.
  • E. Mavis Hetherington — Long-term studies of divorce and remarriage effects on children. Documented variability — divorce isn't uniformly devastating; child resilience and family process matter enormously.
  • Phillip & Carolyn Cowan — Couples and family-transition research; family process predicts outcomes better than family structure.
  • Albert Bandura — Social cognitive theory applied to media effects; observational learning from television and electronic media.

Specific studies cited

  • Lee et al. (2020) — Negative feedback on social media: Adolescents who received fewer "likes" on a simulated social media task showed measurable mood drops and increased self-criticism. Vulnerable adolescents (those with prior depression/anxiety) showed largest effects. Demonstrates social media as a real-world peer rejection context.
  • Olweus bullying prevention programs: Comprehensive whole-school programs reduce bullying ~20–50% in well-implemented sites. Components: clear policies, classroom rules, individual interventions, parent involvement.
  • Romanian orphanage studies (BEIP): Continued relevance for ch7 — children adopted from severely deprived environments show lasting peer relationship difficulties, demonstrating early caregiving's impact on social development.
  • Studies of children of same-sex parents: Meta-analyses find similar outcomes to children of heterosexual parents on virtually all measured dimensions — quality of parenting matters far more than parent gender combination.
  • NICHD Study of Early Child Care follow-up: Family process variables (warmth, structure, conflict) consistently predicted child outcomes more strongly than family structure variables (single vs married, working vs stay-at-home).

7.6 · Key Terms & Definitions

The 27 vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter.

Glossary by category

Family & parenting (12 terms)

  • Socialization — Teaching children the values, roles, and behaviors of their culture. The major task of all parenting across cultures.
  • Authoritarian parenting — High control + low warmth. Demands obedience, uses harsh discipline, low responsiveness. Produces obedient but anxious, less confident children.
  • Authoritative parenting — High control + high warmth. Sets clear standards, explains rules, listens to the child. Produces the most positive outcomes.
  • Permissive parenting — Low control + high warmth. Few rules, child-directed, indulgent. Produces impulsive, less self-regulated children.
  • Uninvolved parenting — Low control + low warmth. Detached, minimally responsive. Produces the worst outcomes across multiple domains.
  • Direct instruction — Telling a child what to do and why; one component of socialization.
  • Counterimitation — When a child sees the consequences of behavior happening to someone else and learns NOT to do that behavior.
  • Reinforcement — A consequence that increases the future likelihood of a behavior (positive: add something good; negative: remove something aversive).
  • Punishment — A consequence that decreases the future likelihood of a behavior (positive: add something aversive; negative: remove something good).
  • Time-out — A common punishment strategy: brief removal from reinforcement. Effective when used consistently for specific behaviors with young children.
  • Negative reinforcement trap — A common coercive pattern: parent makes a demand, child resists/cries, parent withdraws demand, child stops crying → parent learns to withdraw demands (negatively reinforced by quiet), child learns aversive behavior produces parental retreat. Patterson's classic framing.
  • Open adoption — Adoptive arrangements in which biological and adoptive parents have ongoing contact and information sharing. Increasingly common; research suggests no harm and possible benefits over closed adoption.

Family transitions & resilience (3 terms)

  • Joint custody — Post-divorce arrangement where both parents share legal and/or physical custody. Outcomes vary; what matters most is whether co-parenting is cooperative or conflictual.
  • Blended family — Family formed when one or both partners bring children from previous relationships. Stepparent-stepchild relationships take 2–5 years to stabilize; outcomes depend on warmth and patience.
  • Ego resilience — Ability to respond flexibly to changing demands; protective factor in children facing adversity (divorce, poverty, trauma).

Peers & status (9 terms)

  • Friendship — Voluntary mutual relationship characterized by positive feelings, shared activities, and (in middle childhood and beyond) emotional intimacy.
  • Co-rumination — Repeatedly discussing negative experiences with a friend; common in girls' friendships and linked to depression risk while also strengthening friendship bonds.
  • Clique — Small group (4–8) of children who spend time together and share activities and norms.
  • Crowd — Larger reputation-based grouping (jocks, nerds, etc.). Adolescents are classified by peers into crowds even when they don't see themselves that way.
  • Dominance hierarchy — Ordering of group members from most to least dominant. Observed in many species; reduces conflict by establishing predictable status. Children form dominance hierarchies in unstructured play.
  • Popular children — Liked by many peers, disliked by few. Tend to be cooperative, helpful, socially skilled.
  • Rejected children — Disliked by many peers, liked by few. Often aggressive (aggressive-rejected) or withdrawn (withdrawn-rejected). At high risk for academic, behavioral, and mental health problems.
  • Controversial children — Liked by many AND disliked by many. Often show both positive and negative behaviors.
  • Average children — Most children; modest numbers of friends, modest numbers of enemies. Largest peer-status category.

Aggression, social cognition (3 terms)

  • Neglected children — Neither liked nor disliked; low visibility. Often shy; outcomes generally adequate but may benefit from structured peer activities.
  • Instrumental aggression — Aggression with a goal (e.g., taking a toy). More common in younger children; usually decreases with age.
  • Hostile aggression — Aggression with the goal of harming someone. More serious; persistence into school years predicts later conduct problems.
  • Recursive thinking — Thinking about what others are thinking about what others are thinking ("she knows that I know that she knows"). Emerges around 7–8 years and matures through adolescence.

Clinical & supplementary terms (17 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • Bullying (Olweus definition) — Repeated aggressive behavior by one or more peers against a victim, characterized by an imbalance of power (physical, social, psychological). Three forms: physical, verbal, and relational (rumors, exclusion). Cyberbullying is the digital extension. Olweus pioneered the evidence-based whole-school intervention model.
  • Bystander effect (in bullying) — Phenomenon where bullying typically occurs with peers watching, and bystander silence is interpreted by bullies as approval. Evidence-based prevention programs (Olweus, KiVa) explicitly target bystander behavior — empowering peers to intervene appropriately reduces bullying substantially.
  • Self-concept — Organized, multi-dimensional understanding of who one is. In middle childhood, self-concept becomes more differentiated (across academic, social, athletic, physical appearance domains) and more comparative (comparing self to peers).
  • Self-esteem — Overall evaluation of one's worth or value as a person. Distinct from self-concept (which is descriptive). Domain-specific self-esteem (e.g., athletic self-esteem) and global self-esteem both matter. Can dip in adolescence, particularly for girls.
  • Coregulation (parent–child, middle childhood) — Maccoby's term for the shared parent-child responsibility for behavior characteristic of middle childhood. Parents transfer increasing decision-making to the child while still supervising; bridges complete adult control of early childhood and adolescent autonomy.
  • Peer rejection vs. peer neglect — Two distinct unfortunate sociometric statuses. Rejected children are actively disliked (high negative nominations from peers); often aggressive or disruptive. Neglected children receive few nominations either way; quieter, may not seek interaction. Outcomes differ — rejection predicts more negative trajectories than neglect.
  • Child neglect — Most common form of maltreatment (~75% of cases). Failure to provide adequate food, clothing, shelter, medical care, supervision, or education. May be due to poverty, parental impairment, or willful denial. Often invisible; signs include chronic understimulation, untreated medical conditions, hunger, hygiene neglect, school absences, abandonment.
  • Physical abuse (child) — Assault leading to injuries. Red flags: injuries inconsistent with developmental stage (a non-mobile baby with bruises), inconsistent explanations, pattern injuries (belt marks, cigarette burns, bite marks), delayed care-seeking, age-inappropriate fear of parent.
  • Sexual abuse (child) — Fondling, intercourse, exposure to sexual content, or other sexual behaviors imposed on a child. Most often intra-familial. Disclosure is typically delayed. Behavioral signs: regression, sexualized behavior inappropriate for age, withdrawal, sleep disturbance, somatic complaints, fear of specific person.
  • Psychological / emotional abuse — Ridicule, rejection, humiliation, terrorizing, isolating. Often co-occurs with other types. Hardest to identify and prosecute but among most damaging long-term. Signs: extreme low self-esteem, anxiety, suicidality, attachment disturbance, paradoxical over-compliance with abuser.
  • Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) — Original ACE study (Felitti et al., 1998) identified categories of childhood adversity (abuse, neglect, household dysfunction) that predict adult health outcomes. ACE score (count) is dose-dependent — more ACEs predict worse adult physical health, mental health, substance use, and life expectancy. Screening tool used in many clinical settings.
  • Nurse-Family Partnership (NFP) — Evidence-based home visiting program. RNs visit low-income first-time mothers from pregnancy through child's 2nd birthday. Olds et al. (1997) RCT showed 79% reduction in verified abuse. Strongest evidence base for any child maltreatment prevention program. Replicated and scaled internationally.
  • Triple P (Positive Parenting Program) — Evidence-based multi-tier parenting program. Universal media campaigns through intensive multi-week clinical training. Population-level South Carolina trial showed 16% reduction in substantiated maltreatment (Prinz et al., 2009).
  • Parent-Child Interaction Therapy (PCIT) — Evidence-based 14-20 session therapy for families with maltreatment history or elevated risk. Therapist coaches parent in real-time interaction. Teaches positive attention, ignoring minor misbehavior, effective limit-setting. Reduces re-abuse rates (van IJzendoorn et al., 2020).
  • Process vs. structure (family research) — Distinction between WHAT happens in a family (process variables like warmth, conflict, discipline) and WHO is in the family (structure variables like married/divorced, same-sex/opposite-sex parents). Decades of research show that process predicts child outcomes; structure usually doesn't.
  • Family Equality, GLSEN, PFLAG, Trevor Project — National LGBTQ+ family and youth advocacy organizations. Family Equality supports LGBTQ+ parents/families; GLSEN supports LGBTQ+ students in schools; PFLAG supports families of LGBTQ+ people; Trevor Project provides crisis support specifically for LGBTQ+ youth (1-866-488-7386, text START to 678-678).
  • Bidirectional family influence — Bronfenbrenner-rooted recognition that children shape parents as much as parents shape children. Difficult temperament elicits different parenting than easy temperament. Parental stress shapes child behavior, which shapes parent response. Modern family research treats family as a system, not a one-way influence chain.

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — a longitudinal portrait of how one child's descriptions of her friend change as she grows.

Tell me about a girl you like a lot — person perception case

The case: "Tell me about a girl you like a lot"

Every few years, Riley was asked to describe a girl she liked a lot. Her descriptions of her friend Madelyn changed strikingly across time:

At age 5

"Madelyn lives in my apartment building. She is a very good reader. She's good at running. She has long hair."

At age 8

"Madelyn is my best friend. We do everything together. She's smart. She helps me when I get stuck on homework. She's nice to everyone."

At age 11

"Madelyn is loyal but she can be moody. She's the kind of friend who'll give a helping hand. But in private, she can be really sarcastic — she can say some hurtful things and not realize it. She's complicated."

The question

What does the developmental sequence in Riley's descriptions reveal about social cognition? Connect to concepts in 7.4.

Worked analysis

Age 5 — concrete & observable

Everything Riley says is something you could verify by looking — location, abilities, physical appearance. No internal psychological content. This matches the preoperational/early-concrete pattern of describing people by surface features.

Age 8 — relational, partly evaluative

"Best friend... nice to everyone" — Riley now uses relationship language and starts attributing general dispositions ("smart", "nice"). But these are still relatively simple and one-dimensional.

Age 11 — complex psychological portrait

Riley now juggles contradictory traits — loyal AND moody, helpful AND sarcastic — without dismissing either. She can integrate inconsistency into a single coherent person model and use the word "complicated", recognizing humans aren't simple.

What changed cognitively

Riley moved from describing what's on the surface to inferring enduring traits and the ability to hold contradictions. This is genuinely cognitive development — better working memory, better trait reasoning, more nuanced theory of mind — happening in service of social understanding.

Why this matters

Person perception isn't a single skill — it's a window into how a child thinks. Riley at 11 has the cognitive equipment to understand that a friend can be loyal AND sarcastic at the same time. Same person, multiple qualities. This is the same shift that lets older children tolerate ambiguity, handle nuanced friendships, and avoid splitting people into all- good or all-bad. It's a quiet developmental triumph.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 7 core content.

Q1

A school administrator is concerned about a 9-year-old who is consistently named "least liked" by classmates and rarely named "most liked". She's quiet, withdrawn, and avoids group activities. Which peer status category fits, and what's the most appropriate level of concern?

  1. A. Neglected — relatively low concern, many such children just prefer fewer friends
  2. B. Rejected-withdrawn — significant concern, elevated risk for depression and anxiety
  3. C. Controversial — moderate concern, generally socially capable
  4. D. Popular but mean — high concern about her behavior toward others
Show rationale

A. Neglected children get FEW of both positive and negative nominations — they're low-visibility. This child gets many negative nominations.

B. CORRECT. Many negative + few positive nominations = rejected. The quiet, withdrawn, anxious profile is the rejected-withdrawn sub-type, the highest-risk peer status category. Linked to elevated long-term risk for depression, anxiety, school avoidance. Both rejected sub-types warrant active intervention.

C. Controversial children get MANY of BOTH — strongly polarizing. This child isn't liked by many.

D. Popular-but-mean is a sub-type of popular kids with social skill but low actual liking. This child is the opposite — rejected, not popular.

Q2

An 8-year-old reports that the same student calls him names, takes his lunch money several times a week, and threatens him if he tells anyone. The student is bigger and has friends who back him up. Which three criteria for bullying are met?

  1. A. Intentional and repeated, but no power imbalance
  2. B. Repeated and power imbalance, but unclear if intentional
  3. C. Intentional, repeated, and power imbalance — all three met
  4. D. None of the three are clearly met
Show rationale

A. Bigger + has backing friends = clear power imbalance.

B. Taking lunch money and threatening to silence the target are very deliberate behaviors — intent is clear.

C. CORRECT. All three criteria are clearly satisfied: intentional (taking money and threatening silence are deliberate), repeated (several times a week over time), and power imbalance (bigger size and social backing). This meets the textbook definition of bullying and warrants a school-level intervention.

D. All three are met. School intervention is warranted.

Q3

A clinician evaluates a 6-year-old whose parents recently divorced. The child shows mild behavioral regression and some sadness. The clinician should be MOST concerned about long-term outcomes if which factor is present?

  1. A. The child sees both parents on a regular schedule
  2. B. The parents speak about each other respectfully in front of the child
  3. C. The parents continue to fight openly and the child experiences multiple home transitions
  4. D. The child has supportive grandparents nearby
Show rationale

A. Continued involvement of both parents predicts BETTER outcomes — protective factor, not risk.

B. Low-conflict co-parenting is protective.

C. CORRECT. The biggest predictor of long-term harm isn't the divorce itself — it's continued conflict and multiple transitions. Children in chronically high-conflict situations (with or without divorce) fare worse than children in stable low-conflict situations. Repeated transitions also accumulate stress. Both are present here.

D. Supportive extended family is protective.

Q4

A 10-year-old says of a classmate: "She gets jealous easily and can be mean, but she's loyal to her friends and she'll stand up for you when things get tough." What does this description illustrate about middle-childhood social cognition?

  1. A. Trait-based, integrated person perception — can hold contradictory traits in one coherent model
  2. B. Concrete observable description typical of preschoolers
  3. C. The "imaginary audience" cognitive distortion
  4. D. A theory-of-mind delay
Show rationale

A. CORRECT. The 10-year-old is attributing internal psychological traits (jealous, mean, loyal, brave) and — critically — integrating contradictory traits into one coherent description. This is the classic middle-childhood shift from observable surface features to trait-based, multidimensional person perception.

B. A preschooler would describe physical, behavioral features ("she has brown hair, she runs fast"). This is well past that.

C. Imaginary audience is an adolescent concept (Ch 8) about feeling watched. Unrelated.

D. Theory of mind delay would prevent the child from attributing internal mental states at all. This child does so effectively.

Q5

A parent worries about their child's screen time. Based on the research, which question is MOST useful for evaluating whether the screen use is helpful or harmful?

  1. A. "How many total minutes per day?" (single metric)
  2. B. "What content is being watched, is it co-viewed, and what is being displaced?" (multi-factor)
  3. C. "Does the child enjoy it?" (preference-based)
  4. D. "Is the screen larger than 15 inches?" (technical specification)
Show rationale

A. Time alone is a poor predictor. The same time can be net-positive or net-negative depending on content and context.

B. CORRECT. Modern guidance from the AAP and developmental research emphasizes quality + context + displacement. Educational content co-viewed with discussion is very different from background TV with aggressive content; both can occupy the same 60 minutes. What's being displaced (sleep, physical activity, face-to-face interaction) matters as much as the screen itself.

C. Enjoyment doesn't tell us about effects — children can enjoy things that harm them.

D. Hardware specifications aren't a meaningful developmental variable.

Q6

In Lee et al.'s (2020) study, 579 ninth-graders were randomly assigned to receive either 11 likes or 2 likes on a fake social-media task. Which conclusion is best supported by the findings?

  1. A. Social-media use has no measurable effect on adolescent emotion
  2. B. Receiving few likes produced a brief but real emotional event — increased feelings of rejection, negative affect, and negative self-thoughts — with effects most pronounced for adolescents who had previously been victimized by peers
  3. C. Only adolescents who are already depressed are affected by social-media metrics
  4. D. The findings can't be generalized to real-world social media
Show rationale

A. The experimental design directly contradicts this claim — random assignment + significant effects = causal evidence that social-media-style feedback shifts emotion.

B. CORRECT. Random assignment to 11 vs. 2 likes produced measurable emotional differences in a single brief interaction. The effect was robust on rejection feelings, negative affect, and negative self-thoughts. The strongest moderator: prior victimization — already-bullied adolescents were more vulnerable to the negative feedback. This illustrates how social-media metrics can compound existing peer-related distress in real and measurable ways.

C. The study didn't measure prior depression as a moderator; previous victimization is what matters in this finding.

D. Experimental findings always require generalization caveats, but the convergence with naturalistic studies of social media + adolescent mental health gives the effect external validity.

Q7

A school administrator is debating whether to focus their family-engagement programming on supporting single parents and same-sex parents as much as married heterosexual parents. What does the family-processes-vs-structure literature suggest?

  1. A. Focus only on married heterosexual couples — they're the gold standard
  2. B. Family processes (warmth, consistency, low conflict, financial stability) predict child outcomes more strongly than family structure; programming should target the universal process factors and equally serve families across structures
  3. C. Same-sex parents and single parents need entirely different programming
  4. D. There's no research on this topic
Show rationale

A. The 'gold standard' framing is empirically weaker than once thought. Process variables matter more than structure.

B. CORRECT. Multiple studies (Farr et al. 2010, Golombok et al. 2014, Farr et al. 2020) demonstrate that family process variables — warmth, consistency, low conflict, financial stability — predict child outcomes more strongly than family structure. Children of same-sex parents, single parents, and blended families fare comparably to children of married heterosexual parents when process variables are accounted for. Effective family-engagement programs should target the universal process variables (responsive parenting, conflict management, stability) and serve families across structures equally.

C. Different family structures may face different external stressors (workplace policies, social stigma, financial strain), but the core developmental needs are shared.

D. There's a substantial and growing literature on this question.

Q8

A 10-year-old who's been bullied for several months is showing signs of anxiety. Beyond stopping the bullying, what protective factors does the research highlight as most effective?

  1. A. Tell the child to "toughen up" and stop being sensitive
  2. B. Protective factors include: at least one supportive friend, at least one warm trusted adult who validates the experience, assertiveness/social-skills training, evidence-based therapy if anxiety/depression are present, and active school anti-bullying enforcement including bystander training
  3. C. Switch schools immediately as the only solution
  4. D. Wait it out; children naturally recover from bullying
Show rationale

A. Victim-blaming framing has been shown to increase distress and prolong recovery.

B. CORRECT. Research consistently identifies these protective factors: (1) at least one supportive peer friend substantially buffers the worst outcomes; (2) at least one warm trusted adult who validates the experience; (3) assertiveness and social-skills training reduces re-victimization without victim-blaming; (4) evidence-based therapy (CBT) for anxiety or depression that emerges; (5) school climate work — including active enforcement and bystander training. Lee et al.'s (2020) finding that prior victimization makes adolescents more vulnerable to subsequent rejection underscores why this buffering work matters beyond just stopping the bullying.

C. Switching schools may be necessary in some cases but doesn't address the protective factors that prevent future re-victimization.

D. Many bullied children do NOT naturally recover; the long-term mental-health consequences are well-documented.

Q9

A school nurse evaluates a 10-year-old whose parents are divorcing. The child has been irritable, has frequent stomachaches with no medical findings, and is making more academic mistakes. What's the most evidence-based framing for the parents and most appropriate response?

  1. A. Divorce inevitably damages children and there's nothing to do
  2. B. Children's outcomes after divorce depend heavily on co-parenting quality (low conflict, predictable routines, both parents staying engaged) rather than divorce per se; somatic symptoms in school-age children commonly reflect distress; recommend support including school counselor, ensuring consistent caregiving and communication between homes, monitoring for depression/anxiety, and avoiding placing the child in the middle of conflict
  3. C. Tell parents to hide the conflict from the child completely
  4. D. Recommend immediate psychiatric medication
Show rationale

A. Hetherington's research shows substantial variability in child outcomes; divorce isn't uniformly damaging when handled well.

B. CORRECT. Hetherington's decades of research show that family process matters more than family structure. Children of divorce who maintain warm relationships with both parents, experience low parental conflict, and have predictable routines often do well; children exposed to chronic high conflict, inconsistent parenting, or being weaponized in custody disputes do poorly. Nursing response: (1) Validate child's distress; somatic symptoms commonly externalize emotional pain in school-age children. (2) Screen for depression/anxiety. (3) Refer to school counselor; family therapy can help establish co-parenting structure. (4) Educate parents specifically: avoid putting child between, minimize visible conflict, maintain consistent routines, communicate logistics through scheduled adult check-ins (not via child). (5) Maintain academic supports through school. (6) Monitor; many children adjust in 1–2 years with adequate support.

C. Some conflict will be visible; the goal is to manage it appropriately, not pretend it doesn't exist.

D. Medication is premature; the response should match the cause (life stress) with appropriate non-pharmacological interventions first.

Q10

An 8-year-old comes home from school crying because she was "left out" of a group chat. Parents dismiss it: "it's just a chat." School nurse weighs in. Which framing is best supported by current evidence?

  1. A. The parents are right; online stuff doesn't really matter
  2. B. Social media rejection produces measurable distress; for adolescents and increasingly preteens, online social experiences feel as real as offline; Lee et al. (2020) and similar research show even minor rejection cues affect mood, especially in already-vulnerable children; appropriate response includes validating the experience, evaluating for ongoing exclusion patterns, helping family develop reasonable digital boundaries, and screening for mental health symptoms if persistent
  3. C. Take the phone away forever
  4. D. Tell her to confront the other girls
Show rationale

A. Substantial research shows online rejection produces real psychological harm.

B. CORRECT. Lee et al. (2020) demonstrated that even minor online rejection cues produce measurable mood drops, especially in vulnerable adolescents. For preteens and adolescents, online social experience IS social experience — the digital/physical distinction isn't meaningful to them. Nursing role: (1) Validate — minimize the temptation to dismiss as "drama." (2) Assess pattern — was this one-time, or ongoing exclusion (= relational aggression / cyberbullying). (3) Family digital guidance — age-appropriate access, family agreements about device use, modeling. (4) Mental health screening if persistent. (5) Coordinate with school if it's part of broader peer dynamics. (6) Build resilience — friendships outside the chat, positive identity outside social media metrics.

C. Removing all access typically backfires (drives behavior underground, removes ability to learn digital citizenship); structured limits work better.

D. Confrontation is rarely effective with relational aggression; adult coordination is more effective.

Q11

A 9-year-old patient has been described by his teacher as "rejected by peers — aggressive, gets into fights, can't seem to read social cues." Which intervention approach has the strongest evidence base?

  1. A. Discipline alone (suspensions, detentions)
  2. B. Multi-component intervention: parent management training (e.g., addressing coercive cycles), social-skills training (perspective-taking, reading social cues, conflict resolution), academic supports if needed, screening for ADHD or other contributors, and structured peer activities with adult guidance; aggressive-rejected pattern is highest risk and benefits most from comprehensive intervention
  3. C. Wait until adolescence; he'll grow out of it
  4. D. Move him to a different school
Show rationale

A. Discipline alone doesn't teach skills; often worsens outcomes by isolating the child.

B. CORRECT. Aggressive-rejected children are at the highest risk for later conduct problems, substance use, academic failure, and antisocial outcomes — but intervention works. Evidence base: (1) Parent management training (Patterson, others) — disrupts coercive family cycles, teaches positive discipline. (2) Social-skills training in small groups — perspective-taking, conflict resolution, emotion regulation. (3) Academic support — many aggressive children are also struggling academically, which compounds problems. (4) Screening for ADHD, learning disabilities, language disorders, exposure to violence, trauma history. (5) Coordinated school approach — Olweus or similar whole-school programs. (6) Family therapy as appropriate. Earlier is better: aggressive patterns become more stable and harder to change after age 8–10.

C. Untreated aggressive-rejected status tends to consolidate, not resolve.

D. Moving schools alone doesn't address the underlying skill deficits; the same pattern often emerges.

Q12

A 7-year-old patient says her friend Sarah is "mean and nice." When asked how someone can be both, she says "I don't know — maybe sometimes she's mean and sometimes she's nice on different days." Compare to a 10-year-old who says "She's nice to her friends but mean to people who annoy her — she has standards." What developmental shift is illustrated?

  1. A. No real shift; both are similar
  2. B. Younger child uses concrete, temporal descriptors (different days = different behaviors) but can't yet integrate contradictory traits; older child uses dispositional and contextual reasoning (one underlying disposition that varies systematically with situation) — this is mature person perception emerging with concrete and early formal operational thinking, also reflecting development of recursive thinking
  3. C. The younger child is more honest
  4. D. The older child is being snobby
Show rationale

A. The shift is significant — from concrete sequential descriptions to integrated dispositional accounts.

B. CORRECT. A classic shift in middle childhood: concrete temporal/behavioral person descriptions ("sometimes mean, sometimes nice") → integrated dispositional descriptions ("nice to friends, less nice to others, has standards"). The younger child can't yet hold contradictory traits in one person; they explain inconsistency by appealing to time/situation as if they were independent. The older child constructs a coherent underlying disposition that systematically varies. This shift requires: (1) Higher working memory capacity. (2) Concrete operational reasoning applied to social information. (3) Theory of mind that includes context-dependent beliefs. (4) Recursive thinking — understanding others' minds shape behavior. Clinical relevance: with younger children, expect concrete person descriptions; don't expect them to integrate or understand mixed feelings in others.

C. Both children are being equally honest; what differs is cognitive capacity.

D. The older child's description shows nuance, not snobbery.

Unit 2 · School-Age & Adolescence · Chapter 8

Rites of Passage: Physical & Cognitive Development in Adolescence

TL;DR · One-glance summary

Adolescence is bookended by two transformations — physical (puberty) and cognitive (formal operational thinking). Puberty brings rapid growth, sexual maturation, and hormonal changes; timing matters more than the event. Formal operations enables abstract, hypothetical, and systematic reasoning — though not everyone uses it consistently. Adolescent health is mostly about behavior (sleep, sex, substance use, eating). Adolescents also build sophisticated moral reasoning capacities, captured by Kohlberg's three-level model.

Puberty

  • Primary (reproductive organs) + secondary (visible changes)
  • Menarche (~12.5) and spermarche (~13)
  • Hormones: estrogen, testosterone, growth hormones
  • Timing matters: early girls / late boys face most risks

Health

  • Sleep need: ~9 hours; most teens get ~7
  • Sexual behavior & STI risk
  • Substance use: alcohol most prevalent
  • Eating disorders: peak onset in adolescence

Cognitive change

  • Formal operational (~11+) — abstract, hypothetical
  • Imaginary audience — feeling watched
  • Personal fable — feeling uniquely special
  • Continued executive function development into 20s

Moral reasoning

  • Kohlberg: 3 levels, 6 stages
  • Preconventional — punishment / reward
  • Conventional — social conformity / law
  • Postconventional — abstract principles

8.1 · Pubertal Changes

Puberty is the biological transition from childhood to adulthood — rapid growth, hormonal cascade, and the development of reproductive capacity. The average timing matters less than the variation: early and late maturers face different risks.

Pubertal changes

Primary vs. secondary sex characteristics

Primary

Organs directly involved in reproduction: ovaries, uterus, vagina; testes, penis, scrotum. Mature during puberty but largely invisible externally.

Secondary

Visible signs of sexual maturation not directly tied to reproduction: breast development, facial and body hair, voice deepening, body-shape changes, increased musculature.

Menarche

First menstrual period in girls. Average ~12.5 years in industrialized countries, but range is wide (10–15+). Signifies reproductive capability.

Spermarche

First ejaculation in boys. Average ~13 years, often during sleep (nocturnal emission). Like menarche, occurs after much of the visible body change is already underway.

Hormonal & growth changes

Puberty is triggered by the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis — the hypothalamus releases hormones that signal the pituitary, which signals the gonads (testes / ovaries) to produce estrogen and testosterone. These drive the visible changes plus the growth spurt — rapid increases in height and weight that peak around age 12 for girls and 14 for boys. Growth in puberty is uneven: hands and feet often grow first, then limbs, then the trunk — producing the temporarily-awkward proportions of early adolescence.

Timing of puberty matters

Early vs late maturers

Two children entering puberty in the same school year can be in radically different developmental places — and timing has psychological consequences:

Early-maturing girls

Face the most adjustment risk. They look older but aren't yet emotionally mature; attract older social partners; more likely to face depression, eating issues, risky behavior. Effects fade by adulthood but are real during adolescence.

Early-maturing boys

Generally fare well socially (advantages in sports, popularity), but face slightly elevated risk for substance use and risk-taking.

Late-maturing girls

Relatively few negative effects — sometimes a more buffered adjustment to adolescence.

Late-maturing boys

Face the most adjustment difficulty among boys — physically smaller than peers for longer, lower social status during a key social period.

Two pairs of adolescents enter puberty at very different ages. Which two groups tend to face the most adjustment difficulty, and why?
Early-maturing girls and late-maturing boys. Early girls look older than they are emotionally and attract older social partners; elevated risk for depression, eating issues, and risky behavior. Late boys are physically smaller than peers during a high-stakes social period (middle school) when size and athletic ability strongly affect status. Both groups face mismatches between their apparent and actual developmental level during a key socialization window. Effects typically fade by adulthood but the adolescent years are harder.

8.2 · Health

Adolescents are mostly physically healthy. The big risks are behavioral — sleep, sexual behavior, substance use, eating patterns — and they accumulate consequences that show up later.

Adolescent health

Sleep

Teens need about 9 hours of sleep but average closer to 7. The biological clock shifts later during puberty — adolescents are wired to fall asleep later and wake later, just as schools shift earlier. Chronic sleep loss is linked to lower academic performance, worse mood regulation, higher accident risk, and increased risk for substance use. School districts that have shifted high school start times later show measurable academic and mental-health improvements.
