Alzheimer's disease — the most common dementia (60–70% of cases). Characteristic features all present: insidious onset, gradual steady decline over years, memory problems prominent (especially recent memory), multiple domains affected over time. Distinguishes from other dementias: not stepwise (vascular), no early personality/behavior changes (frontotemporal), no fluctuating cognition with hallucinations (Lewy body). Underlying pathology: amyloid plaques (beta-amyloid protein deposits between neurons) and tau tangles (twisted tau protein inside neurons), which together produce neuronal death. Current treatments slow progression somewhat; none reverse it.

14.4 · Mental Health & Intervention

Mental-health issues in older adults are common but often under-recognized. Two clinical distinctions matter most: late-life depression (often missed) and delirium (an acute medical emergency).

Mental health & intervention

Late-life depression

Depression in older adults is common but often missed. Reasons:

  • Presentation differs from younger adults — more somatic complaints (pain, fatigue, GI symptoms), more cognitive complaints (memory, concentration), sometimes less reported sad mood
  • Symptoms get attributed to "normal aging" or to chronic medical conditions
  • Older adults may be less likely to spontaneously report mental-health symptoms
  • Healthcare providers may screen less consistently in older patients

Risk factors: chronic illness, pain, recent bereavement, social isolation, lower SES, prior depression, dementia (depression often precedes or accompanies it). Treatment is highly effective — older adults respond well to therapy, medication, or both, but require attention to drug interactions and dosing in the context of polypharmacy.

Suicide rates are highest in older adults, particularly older white men. Untreated depression is the leading risk factor. Screening for depression should be routine in older-adult clinical care.

A 76-year-old patient with chronic pain and recent widowhood reports being "just tired all the time" and "losing interest in things". He denies feeling sad. His daughter mentions he hasn't eaten well or slept well in months. What should the clinician consider, and why is recognition particularly important?
Late-life depression — this is a textbook presentation. Key features: somatic symptoms prominent (fatigue, poor appetite, poor sleep), anhedonia (losing interest), in the context of chronic pain and recent loss. Older adults often under-report sad mood while showing other depressive symptoms, so denying sadness doesn't rule it out. Recognition is particularly important because: (1) late-life depression is commonly missed — symptoms get attributed to "aging" or chronic conditions; (2) suicide rates are highest in older adults, especially older men; (3) depression is highly treatable — therapy, medication, or both, with strong response rates. Screening tools like the Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) help. Treatment with attention to drug interactions and polypharmacy is essential.

Delirium — the critical distinction

Acute medical emergency

Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness, typically caused by an underlying medical condition (infection, medication, dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, post-surgical, etc.). It looks like dementia but it's fundamentally different:

Onset

Delirium: hours to days (acute). Dementia: months to years (insidious).

Course

Delirium: fluctuating throughout the day, often worse at night. Dementia: relatively stable from day to day.

Attention

Delirium: prominently disturbed — can't sustain or shift attention. Dementia: usually preserved until late stages.

Cause & treatment

Delirium: caused by underlying medical issue — reversible if cause is treated. Dementia: progressive neurodegenerative process — not generally reversible.

Getting this distinction right is clinically critical. Delirium is treatable — find and treat the underlying cause (infection? medication side effect? dehydration?) and the cognitive disturbance resolves. Mistaking delirium for dementia means the underlying medical emergency goes untreated, with potentially fatal consequences.

A nursing-home resident with mild memory issues at baseline suddenly becomes severely confused, can't focus, fluctuates from alert to drowsy, and is having hallucinations — all over the past 36 hours. What's the most likely cause, and why is the distinction from dementia critical?
This is delirium — not a worsening of dementia. Key indicators: acute onset (hours to days, not months to years), fluctuating course (alert to drowsy), severely impaired attention (can't focus). Likely causes: infection (UTI is extremely common in nursing-home settings), medication change, dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, pain, or other acute medical issue. The distinction from dementia is critical because delirium is a medical emergency requiring identification and treatment of the underlying cause — and it's reversible when treated. Mistaking delirium for "just dementia progression" means missing a potentially fatal underlying condition. Clinically: workup includes vital signs, UA, CBC, BMP, medication review, hydration status. Always think delirium first when an older adult shows acute cognitive change.
A home health nurse visits an 84-year-old patient who lives with her adult son. The nurse notices the patient has lost weight, has untreated pressure ulcers, seems fearful when her son enters the room, and her bank statements show unexplained large withdrawals. What types of elder abuse should the nurse consider, and what's the appropriate response?
Elder abuse is common, under-recognized, and a public health crisis. Up to 1 in 10 community-dwelling older adults experiences abuse annually; rates higher in those with cognitive impairment. Only 1 in 14 cases is reported. Forms of elder abuse: (1) Physical abuse — hitting, restraining, inappropriate use of medications. (2) Sexual abuse — non-consensual sexual contact. (3) Emotional/psychological abuse — threats, humiliation, isolation. (4) Financial exploitation — most common form; theft, coercion regarding assets, scams, exploitation by family. (5) Neglect — failure of caregiver to provide adequate food, hydration, hygiene, medical care. (6) Self-neglect — older adult unable/unwilling to care for self. (7) Abandonment — deserting at-risk older adult. This patient's signs: (1) Weight loss + neglected pressure ulcers = possible neglect. (2) Fear of caregiver = signal of emotional or physical abuse. (3) Unexplained withdrawals = financial exploitation. Risk factors for victims: (1) Cognitive impairment. (2) Functional dependence. (3) Social isolation. (4) Female sex (more prevalent victimization, though men also abused). (5) Living with the abuser. (6) Mental illness. (7) Previous trauma history. Risk factors for abusers: (1) Caregiver stress and burnout. (2) Substance use. (3) Mental illness. (4) Financial dependence on victim. (5) History of abuse perpetration. (6) Family dysfunction. Screening tools: (1) Elder Assessment Instrument (EAI). (2) Brief Abuse Screen for the Elderly (BASE). (3) Hwalek-Sengstock Elder Abuse Screening Test (H-S/EAST). (4) Caregiver Abuse Screen (CASE) for assessing perpetrator risk. Nursing response: (1) Interview the patient ALONE — abusers often answer for victim; insist on private interview. (2) Direct, non-judgmental questions: "Has anyone hurt you? Has anyone taken your money without permission? Are you afraid of anyone in your home?" (3) Document carefully — objective findings, patient's exact words, photos of injuries with consent. (4) Assess immediate safety. (5) Mandatory reporting — most US states mandate reporting of elder abuse for healthcare workers; check state-specific requirements. Report to Adult Protective Services (APS). (6) Don't confront the abuser directly — can escalate danger. (7) Crisis intervention if imminent risk — hospitalization or alternative placement. (8) Connect with resources — APS, area agency on aging, domestic violence services (some serve elder victims), legal aid. (9) Address caregiver burden if neglect rather than intentional abuse — sometimes interventions reduce abuse without removing victim. (10) Follow up. Critical: elder abuse is often hidden behind caregiving relationships; routine screening saves lives.

Spotlight on Research

Family-delivered Montessori intervention for nursing-home residents with dementia

Mbakile-Mahlanza, L., et al. (2020) — Monash University, Australia

What was the aim?

One-on-one structured Montessori-based activities are known to be effective for people with dementia — improving agitation and engagement. But long-term care staff often don't have time to implement them. The researchers reasoned that family members visiting their loved ones could be an untapped resource. The study tested whether families can be trained to deliver Montessori activities, and whether this improves outcomes for people with dementia.

How did they measure it?

Originally developed for children, Maria Montessori's philosophy is adapted for adults with dementia by leveraging preserved cognitive functions (Skrajner & Camp, 2007): self-paced learning and developmentally appropriate activities. Many techniques overlap with rehabilitation (task breakdown, guided repetition, simple-to-complex and concrete-to-abstract progression) and dementia care (external visual/auditory/tactile cues, engaging implicit/procedural memory that is often preserved when explicit memory fails). Sample activities: sorting tasks (silverware, fabrics, photos), matching, simple food preparation (washing produce, mixing batter), reading aloud, music, simple crafts — and the design is person-centered, connecting to the individual's history (a former gardener sorts seeds; a former tailor folds fabric), since personal meaning matters more than novelty. Outcomes measured: positive engagement and emotions in persons with dementia, and family caregiver satisfaction.

Who participated & what was the design?

20 persons with dementia living in a nursing home, and 20 family caregivers. The design was experimental with a crossover structure: one group received the Montessori intervention first while the other served as control; halfway through, the groups switched roles. The protocol was approved by Monash University and Monash Health ethics committees.

What ethical considerations were addressed?

Having persons with dementia as research participants raised important consent issues. Family caregivers gave written informed consent on their own behalf AND on behalf of the person with dementia. People with dementia were only included if they ALSO assented to participate. Researchers monitored participants' emotions throughout; if anyone became agitated or frustrated, the activity was stopped. The researchers took all these precautions seriously.

What were the results?

Compared to control conditions, the family-delivered Montessori intervention produced: (1) Significantly more positive engagement in persons with dementia. (2) More positive emotions in the persons with dementia. (3) Higher satisfaction from family caregivers themselves.

What did the investigators conclude?

The study added evidence for Montessori-based intervention effectiveness in dementia care. Most importantly, it demonstrated that family caregivers can successfully learn and deliver these techniques. This is significant because it expands the resource base for high-quality dementia care — families become active participants rather than helpless visitors, and trained family delivery can complement (rather than depend on) often-stretched professional staff. What would strengthen it: testing whether relationship quality moderates outcomes (do estranged family members do as well as close ones?) and whether the intervention delays the need for higher-acuity placement.

Why this matters — coaching family visits

Many families feel helpless or distressed visiting a loved one with dementia; brief training (15–30 minutes) on structured activities can transform visits from passive presence into active engagement. Provide activity menus matched to the resident's remaining abilities, take a person-centered approach by asking families about the resident's history, interests, and former occupation, and validate the difficulty — visiting is emotionally hard and family carers often feel grief and frustration. Recognize that family members know the resident in ways professional staff don't, so their participation often improves both care quality and family wellbeing. For Filipino families with elders in care: the strong tradition of family-as-caregiver can make nursing-home placement especially painful, and active engagement during visits helps families fulfill that cultural role within the constraints of institutional care.

What Do You Think? · The Cultural Context of Wisdom

"The concept of wisdom is ancient and deeply embedded in the cultural traditions, history of humanity" (Grossman & Kung, 2019, 2020). Definitions of wisdom differ across cultures. In Chinese, wisdom (智慧) concerns the integration of knowledge and intelligence, wit and brightness. In Arabic, wisdom (حكمة) implies prudence, insight, or a maxim. Western traditions emphasize intellectual humility, with two types: sophia (ultimate or divine knowledge about true nature) and phronesis (practical knowledge and rational thinking for good character). Non-Western traditions like India and China give more emphasis to intuitive and personal experience, non-interference with the natural order, and benevolence/prosocial behavior. Buchtel and colleagues (2015) found that Chinese adults were more likely than Americans, Australians, and Canadians to label behaviors they perceived as harmless but uncivilized (littering, swearing in public) as immoral. A follow-up (Buchtel et al., 2017) found Beijing Chinese were more likely to see wisdom as virtuous character — a pattern not observed in Canadians.

  • How do people across cultures learn what wisdom and "wise people" look like?
  • How do people across cultures actually become wise — through what processes?
  • With the decline in formal religious affiliation in many societies, what effect might this have on how wisdom develops?
  • What might Western clinical and developmental frameworks miss by emphasizing intellectual humility while underweighting prosocial behavior and proper conduct as wisdom components?
A nurse working in a memory-care unit notices that family visitors often look helpless or distressed during visits — not knowing how to engage with their relative who has dementia. Based on Mbakile-Mahlanza et al.'s (2020) research, what evidence-based approach could help, and why does this matter for nursing practice?
The research evidence supports training family members to deliver Montessori-based dementia activities during visits. Mbakile-Mahlanza et al. (2020) demonstrated that family caregivers can successfully learn and deliver structured Montessori techniques (self-paced activities, task breakdown, external cues, simple-to-complex progression). The intervention produced: (1) significantly more positive engagement and emotions in residents with dementia; (2) higher satisfaction in the family caregivers themselves. Why this matters for nursing practice: (1) Family visits often produce more anxiety than connection because relatives don't know how to interact with a loved one whose cognitive abilities have changed. (2) Trained family caregivers become an additional resource for high-quality dementia care, complementing rather than depending on stretched professional staff. (3) Family satisfaction matters — engaged, capable visitors are more likely to keep visiting, reducing the social isolation that compounds dementia decline. (4) Nursing can take a teaching role: brief, practical training on dementia-friendly activities (familiar music, sorting tasks, photo albums, simple gardening) substantially increases engagement without requiring specialized therapeutic credentials. Clinical translation: when families ask "what should I do during visits?", the right answer isn't "just be present" — it's giving them concrete, evidence-based activities they can do together.
A researcher wants to enroll a nursing-home resident with moderate Alzheimer's disease in a study of a new intervention. What ethical safeguards are required to protect this potentially vulnerable participant, and what does "assent" mean in this context?
Research with cognitively impaired participants requires layered safeguards because the participant may not fully understand procedures or freely consent. Required protections (illustrated by Mbakile-Mahlanza et al., 2020): (1) IRB / ethics committee approval with explicit attention to vulnerable-population protections. (2) Surrogate informed consent — a legally authorized representative (usually a family member with healthcare power of attorney) gives written informed consent on the participant's behalf. (3) Participant assent — the person with dementia must also agree to participate, in language they can understand. Assent isn't the same as informed consent (which requires understanding and decisional capacity); it's an affirmative indication that the person wants to participate or doesn't object. (4) Ongoing monitoring — researchers continually watch for signs of agitation, frustration, or distress. (5) Right to withdraw at any time, including by behavioral signs, not just verbal request. (6) Minimal risk — interventions should pose no more than minimal risk, or the risk-benefit ratio must be highly favorable. (7) Confidentiality safeguards with secure data storage. What "assent" means specifically: a willingness or absence of objection from someone who can't give full informed consent. If a person with dementia indicates they don't want to participate (verbally OR behaviorally — withdrawing, becoming distressed, refusing to engage), the researcher must stop, regardless of surrogate consent.
A Western researcher studying late-life wisdom administers a standardized wisdom inventory across US and Chinese samples. The Chinese sample scores particularly high on items related to social conduct and benevolence; the US sample scores higher on items related to intellectual humility and questioning. What does this pattern suggest about measuring wisdom cross-culturally?
The pattern reflects real cultural variation in what constitutes wisdom — not bias or measurement error in one direction. Western traditions emphasize intellectual humility, with the Greek concepts of sophia (ultimate knowledge about true nature) and phronesis (practical wisdom for good character). Non-Western traditions like Chinese Confucian thought place greater emphasis on civility, proper social conduct, benevolence, and non-interference with natural order. Buchtel and colleagues (2015, 2017) demonstrated this empirically: Chinese adults were more likely than Americans/Australians/Canadians to view "uncivilized but harmless" behaviors (littering, public swearing) as immoral, and Beijing Chinese were more likely to see wisdom as virtuous character. Implications for cross-cultural research and clinical work: (1) Standardized "wisdom" inventories developed in one cultural context may systematically under-measure wisdom in other contexts by missing constructs that don't appear on the scale. (2) Culturally-appropriate elder care must recognize different framings of wisdom — including what counts as "wise advice" from an elder. (3) The Western emphasis on individual cognitive sophistication may underweight relational and prosocial dimensions that other cultures consider central. (4) For nursing practice with diverse older adults, asking "what does it mean to live well at this stage of life?" opens space for culturally-grounded answers rather than assuming a Western template.

Test Yourself · Section 14.4

  1. What four common types of dementia are distinguished by clinical presentation, and what's the distinctive feature of each?
  2. What is the difference between informed consent and assent in research with cognitively impaired participants?
  3. Based on Mbakile-Mahlanza et al. (2020), can family members be successfully trained to deliver structured therapeutic activities to people with dementia? What were the documented benefits?
  4. Name three evidence-based approaches to engaging a nursing-home resident with moderate dementia during a family visit.
  5. How do Western and Chinese cultural traditions differ in what they consider central to wisdom, and what are the implications for cross-cultural elder care?

Linking Research to Life — assessing adults with diminished capacity

The clinical-legal challenge

One consequence of dementia or repeated brain trauma is impairment of judgment and decision-making. In some cases, impairment becomes severe enough to raise questions about a person's capacity to make informed decisions that reflect full understanding and are in the person's best interests.

Typical triggers: family members become concerned when a parent begins spending large amounts of money in ways that differ from past patterns, makes uncharacteristic legal decisions, or is at risk of exploitation. These situations may require evaluation of the person's capacity.

Determining diminished capacity is emotionally and legally challenging for the individual, family, and professionals. Done well, it protects autonomy AND prevents harm. Done poorly, it can either strip needed autonomy or leave vulnerable people unprotected.

ABA-APA joint guidelines

The American Bar Association Commission on Law and Aging and the American Psychological Association collaborated (2005, 2008) to develop guidelines. Solidly grounded in decades of research on:

  • Patterns of typical aging
  • Effects of physical and mental health disorders causing cognitive impairment
  • Whether impairment is permanent or temporary

The collaboration produced two handbooks — one for psychologists, one for lawyers — providing a comprehensive guide for determining diminished capacity. The dual-professional approach ensures BOTH:

  • The person in question's rights are better protected (legal advocacy)
  • Family members have a fair and equitable process to follow

The nine areas of clinical assessment

The APA assessment approach covers nine major areas:

1. Medical condition

Current physical health status, medications, recent changes. Some causes of cognitive impairment are reversible.

2. Cognitive functioning

Memory, attention, executive function, language, visuospatial abilities. Standardized testing (MMSE, MoCA, neuropsychological battery).

3. Everyday functioning

ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting) and IADLs (medications, finances, transportation). Functional capacity matters more than cognitive scores for many decisions.

4. Values and preferences

What does the person value? What are their preferences? Critical: appreciation of the individual's culture and values — capacity assessment must account for cultural context, not just imposed dominant-culture assumptions.

5. Risk profile

What are the specific risks of allowing the person to make this decision? Of preventing it? Capacity is decision-specific, not global.

6. Steps to enhance capacity

What accommodations might support the person's autonomy? Simpler language, visual aids, decision-making at better times of day, involvement of trusted advisors.

7. Psychiatric/emotional factors

Depression, anxiety, psychosis, substance use can all impair decision-making — and may be treatable. Treating depression sometimes restores capacity.

8. Judgment about the specific decision

Can the person understand the relevant information, appreciate consequences, reason through alternatives, and communicate a stable choice?

9. Clinical judgment

The clinician integrates all evidence into an overall assessment — with appropriate documentation and consultation when uncertain.

The legal framework — 16 lawyer questions

From the legal perspective, ABA produced a parallel framework: 16 questions lawyers pose about the case. These cover the legal standards for the specific decision in question (capacity to contract, to make a will, to consent to medical care, to vote, to drive, etc.). Capacity is decision-specific — a person may lack capacity to manage finances but retain capacity to make healthcare decisions, and vice versa.

Common legal mechanisms when diminished capacity is established:

  • Durable power of attorney for healthcare — pre-executed when capacity exists; activates when needed
  • Financial power of attorney — similar pre-execution
  • Guardianship / conservatorship — court-ordered when no advance planning exists or it's insufficient. Most restrictive; should be last resort
  • Limited guardianship — court order limited to specific decision areas
  • Supported decision-making agreements — emerging model preserving more autonomy by providing structured support

For nursing practice — capacity in everyday care

  • Capacity is decision-specific and dynamic: a patient may have capacity to consent to a routine procedure but not to a complex surgery; capacity may vary throughout the day or improve with treatment of underlying conditions.
  • Threshold for capacity ≠ "right" decision: a patient with capacity can refuse life-saving treatment. The question is whether they understand and appreciate, not whether they choose what we'd choose.
  • Treat reversible causes first: delirium (often acute, often reversible), depression, medication effects, electrolyte imbalances, infections, pain. Don't make permanent determinations during reversible states.
  • Document the assessment: not just "patient lacks capacity" but specifically what was assessed, what the patient could and couldn't articulate, what alternatives were tried.
  • Use available tools: MoCA, MMSE for cognitive screening; MacCAT-T for treatment consent capacity; clinical interview using the four-part standard (understanding, appreciation, reasoning, expression of choice).
  • Engage early planning: encourage advance directives, healthcare proxies, financial POAs while patients have capacity. Prevention beats crisis management.
  • Cultural humility: in some cultures, family-centered decision-making is normative; in others, individual autonomy is paramount. Assess what the patient values; don't impose Western individualism on collectivist families.
  • Filipino-specific considerations: family decision-making and deference to elders or eldest sibling may be culturally expected. Ask the patient directly how they want decisions made — most patients can articulate preferences for decision-making process even when they can't make complex decisions themselves.
  • Watch for exploitation: financial abuse of older adults is increasingly common. New "friends" or family members who suddenly insist on major financial or medical decision-making changes warrant scrutiny. Adult Protective Services exists.
  • For Mark's nursing practice: capacity assessment is a daily issue in many nursing roles — admissions, consent, code status, refusing treatment, leaving AMA. Knowing the framework prevents both over-treatment of those who can decide and under-protection of those who can't.

14.5 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Robert Butler — Coined the term "ageism" (1969); founding director of the National Institute on Aging. Documented discriminatory attitudes toward older adults and their consequences.
  • Bernice Neugarten — Distinguished "young-old" (active, healthy) from "old-old" (frail, dependent); foundational in gerontology. Documented psychological dimensions of aging.
  • Paul Baltes — Selective Optimization with Compensation (SOC) model; older adults adapt by selecting fewer domains, optimizing performance in those, and compensating for losses with alternative strategies.
  • Laura Carstensen — Socioemotional Selectivity Theory; as time horizon shortens with age, motivation shifts toward emotionally meaningful goals and relationships, away from knowledge acquisition.
  • Roger Bachman & geneticists — Telomere shortening research; physical aging marker linked to cellular senescence.
  • Alois Alzheimer (1906) — First described the disease that bears his name; identified neurofibrillary tangles and beta-amyloid plaques on autopsy.
  • Roy Goodacre, Charles Hirshey, others — Free radical / oxidative stress theory of aging.
  • Modern Alzheimer's research community — Mapping genetic risk (APOE-ε4 allele), amyloid hypothesis, anti-amyloid antibody trials (aducanumab, lecanemab).

Specific studies cited

  • MacArthur Studies of Successful Aging: Multi-decade research showing successful aging involves low disease/disability, high cognitive/physical function, AND engagement with life. Lifestyle factors more important than genetics for most.
  • Telomere research: Telomeres shorten with each cell division; chronic stress accelerates shortening; meditation, exercise associated with longer telomeres. Mechanism connecting lifestyle to cellular aging.
  • FINGER study (Finnish Geriatric Intervention): Multidomain lifestyle intervention (diet, exercise, cognitive training, vascular monitoring) showed cognitive benefits in at-risk older adults — strongest evidence for prevention.
  • Mbakile-Mahlanza et al. (2020): Family members can be successfully trained to deliver structured therapeutic activities (Tailored Activity Program — TAP) to people with dementia. Reduced behavioral symptoms, increased engagement, improved caregiver outcomes.
  • Anti-amyloid trial era: Recent FDA approvals (aducanumab 2021, lecanemab 2023, donanemab 2024) target beta-amyloid; modest cognitive benefit; cost and brain-bleed risks limit adoption; subject of ongoing debate about clinical significance.
  • Cross-cultural wisdom research: Western traditions emphasize accumulated knowledge and life experience; Chinese traditions integrate compassion, moral discernment, and capacity to maintain equanimity. Implications for cross-cultural elder care.

14.6 · Key Terms & Definitions

The 49 vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter.

Glossary by category

Demographics & longevity (6 terms)

  • Demographers — Researchers who study population characteristics, distribution, and trends.
  • Population pyramid — Graphical representation of age distribution by sex; reveals demographic shifts (aging populations show "rectangular" rather than pyramidal shapes).
  • Average longevity — Average length of time someone is expected to live, calculated from birth or current age.
  • Maximum longevity (life expectancy) — The theoretical upper limit of human lifespan; current estimates 120-125 years.
  • Active life expectancy — Number of years expected to live in good health, without significant chronic disease or disability.
  • Dependent life expectancy — Number of years expected to live with chronic disease or disability requiring assistance.

Cellular aging (6 terms)

  • Telomeres — Protective caps at chromosome ends; shorten with each cell division; eventual exhaustion limits cellular replication.
  • Telomerase — Enzyme that lengthens telomeres; active in some cells (stem cells, cancer cells); reduced activity in most adult cells.
  • Oxidative stress — Cellular damage from reactive oxygen species exceeding antioxidant defenses.
  • Free radicals — Unstable molecules with unpaired electrons; damage cellular components including DNA, proteins, lipids.
  • Antioxidants — Molecules that neutralize free radicals; include vitamins C, E; supplements not proven to extend life despite biological rationale.
  • Neurofibrillary tangles — Abnormal accumulations of tau protein inside neurons; hallmark of Alzheimer's disease.
  • Beta amyloid — Protein fragments that accumulate in plaques between neurons; major Alzheimer's hallmark; current treatment target.

Brain & cardiovascular (7 terms)

  • Neurotransmitters — Chemical messengers between neurons; many decline with age, contributing to cognitive and mood changes.
  • Structural neuroimaging — MRI, CT showing brain anatomy; reveals atrophy, infarcts, structural lesions.
  • Functional neuroimaging — fMRI, PET showing brain activity; reveals which regions are active during tasks.
  • Atherosclerosis — Plaque buildup in arteries; major cause of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular disease.
  • Strokes (cerebral vascular accidents, CVAs) — Sudden interruption of blood flow to brain; ischemic (most common) or hemorrhagic. Major cause of disability and death in late life.
  • Transient ischemic attacks (TIAs) — "Mini-strokes" with temporary symptoms; serious warning signs for major stroke.
  • Vascular dementia — Cognitive decline from cumulative small strokes; second most common dementia after Alzheimer's.

Sensory & metabolic (9 terms)

  • Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) — Progressive lung disease causing airflow limitation; usually from smoking; major cause of late-life disability and death.
  • Presbyopia — Age-related near-focus difficulty due to lens stiffening. Universal; correctable with reading glasses.
  • Cataracts — Clouding of the eye lens; surgically correctable with lens replacement. Most common cause of treatable vision loss in older adults.
  • Glaucoma — Damage to optic nerve, often from elevated intraocular pressure; treatable with drops or surgery; can cause blindness if untreated.
  • Age-related macular degeneration — Damage to central retina; affects reading and detailed vision; limited treatments for advanced disease.
  • Diabetic retinopathy — Damage to retinal blood vessels from diabetes; leading cause of preventable blindness in working-age adults.
  • Presbycusis — Age-related hearing loss; high-frequency sounds first; treatable with hearing aids though under-utilized.
  • Diabetes mellitus — Disorder of glucose metabolism; type 1 (autoimmune) or type 2 (insulin resistance).
  • Type 1 diabetes — Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing beta cells; typically childhood onset; requires insulin.
  • Type 2 diabetes — Insulin resistance with relative insulin deficiency; typically adult onset; lifestyle-related; rising prevalence with age and obesity.
  • Circadian rhythm — Internal 24-hour cycle regulating sleep, hormones, body temperature; tends to advance with age (earlier bedtime, earlier waking).
  • Metabolism — Body's processes for converting food to energy; slows with age, contributing to weight changes.

Memory systems (7 terms)

  • Working memory — Temporary holding and manipulation of information; central to many cognitive tasks; declines modestly with age.
  • Implicit memory — Unconscious recall (procedures, skills); largely preserved with aging.
  • Explicit memory — Conscious recall (facts, events); some decline with age, particularly for recent events.
  • Episodic memory — Memory for specific personal events tied to time and place; declines with age, especially recent events.
  • Semantic memory — Memory for facts, concepts, vocabulary; largely preserved; some retrieval slowing.
  • Autobiographical memory — Memories of one's life experiences; shows "reminiscence bump" — peak retention for events in adolescence/young adulthood.
  • External memory aids — Calendars, lists, notes, phone reminders; effective compensatory strategies.
  • Internal memory aids — Mental strategies (mnemonics, visualization, association) to improve memory.

Mental health & neurological (14 terms)

  • Dysphoria — Sad, irritable, anxious mood state; sometimes used to describe late-life depression presentation that differs from typical adult.
  • Internal belief systems — Personal frameworks that shape emotional responses; targets of cognitive therapy.
  • Behavior therapy — Treatment focused on changing observable behaviors and their environmental triggers.
  • Cognitive therapy — Treatment focused on identifying and changing dysfunctional thought patterns; evidence-based for late-life depression.
  • Anxiety disorders — Group of conditions characterized by excessive fear, worry, or avoidance; often co-occur with depression in older adults.
  • Dementia — Acquired cognitive impairment affecting daily functioning; multiple types with different causes.
  • Alzheimer's disease — Most common dementia; gradual onset, progressive memory and cognitive decline; characterized by amyloid plaques and tau tangles.
  • Incontinence — Loss of bladder or bowel control; common in advanced dementia and other conditions.
  • Parkinson's disease — Neurodegenerative movement disorder; tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability; dementia can develop in advanced cases.
  • Chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) — Neurodegenerative disease in people with repeated head trauma (football, boxing, military); progressive cognitive and behavioral decline; diagnosed at autopsy.

Clinical & supplementary terms (22 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • Beers Criteria — American Geriatrics Society's list of potentially inappropriate medications for older adults — drugs with high risk-benefit ratios in this population. Categories: (1) avoid in older adults generally; (2) avoid with certain conditions; (3) use with caution; (4) drug-drug interactions to avoid; (5) renal-dose adjustments. Updated regularly (latest 2023).
  • STOPP/START criteria — European-developed prescribing tool complementary to Beers. STOPP: Screening Tool of Older Person's Prescriptions — identifies potentially inappropriate medications to stop. START: Screening Tool to Alert to Right Treatment — identifies under-prescribed medications older adults should be on but often aren't.
  • Anticholinergic burden — Cumulative effect of medications with anticholinergic (acetylcholine-blocking) properties. Common anticholinergics: diphenhydramine, tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline), oxybutynin, certain antipsychotics. In older adults, anticholinergic burden produces confusion, falls, urinary retention, dry mouth, constipation, and is associated with elevated dementia risk.
  • Cholinesterase inhibitors — Class of medications for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease that increase acetylcholine availability by blocking its breakdown. Approved drugs: donepezil (Aricept), rivastigmine (Exelon), galantamine (Razadyne). Modest cognitive benefit; main side effects gastrointestinal (nausea, diarrhea).
  • Memantine (Namenda) — NMDA receptor antagonist used in moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's disease, often combined with a cholinesterase inhibitor. Different mechanism than the cholinesterase inhibitors. Modest cognitive and functional benefit; generally well-tolerated.
  • Anti-amyloid antibody therapy — Newer class of Alzheimer's treatments targeting beta-amyloid plaques. FDA approvals: aducanumab (2021, controversial), lecanemab (2023), donanemab (2024). Modest cognitive slowing in mild AD; risks include amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA — brain swelling/microbleeds); cost and safety limit broad adoption; subject of ongoing debate about clinical significance.
  • ACHIEVE trial (hearing aids) — Aging and Cognitive Health Evaluation in Elders trial (2023). Showed hearing aids slowed cognitive decline by 48% over 3 years in at-risk older adults — providing strong evidence that hearing loss is a modifiable dementia risk factor.
  • Lancet Commission on Dementia Prevention — Influential expert reports (2017, 2020, 2024) identifying modifiable risk factors that collectively could potentially reduce dementia incidence by ~40%. Factors include education, hearing loss, hypertension, smoking, obesity, depression, physical inactivity, diabetes, social isolation, alcohol, air pollution, traumatic brain injury, vision loss, high cholesterol (added 2024).
  • Anhedonia — Diminished ability to feel pleasure or interest in activities that were previously enjoyable. A core symptom of major depression — often more prominent than reported sadness in late-life depression. Persistent anhedonia warrants depression evaluation regardless of whether the patient endorses sadness.
  • GDS (Geriatric Depression Scale) — Yesavage's validated depression screening instrument designed for older adults. Avoids somatic symptoms that overlap with physical illness. Short form (15 items) commonly used; score ≥5 suggests depression warranting evaluation. Free, easily administered, widely used in primary care and geriatric settings.
  • CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) — Validated bedside tool for delirium identification developed by Inouye et al. Four features: (1) acute onset/fluctuating course; (2) inattention; (3) disorganized thinking; (4) altered consciousness. Delirium diagnosis requires features 1 AND 2 plus 3 OR 4. Specialized versions (CAM-ICU, 3D-CAM, etc.) exist for different settings.
  • APOE-ε4 allele — Genetic variant of the apolipoprotein E gene strongly associated with elevated Alzheimer's disease risk. Having one copy ~2-3x risk; two copies ~10-12x risk. Not deterministic — many ε4 carriers never develop AD, and many AD patients lack ε4. Testing in clinical practice is controversial without effective preventive interventions.
  • Hayflick limit — Cellular phenomenon discovered by Leonard Hayflick: cells grown in culture undergo a fixed number of divisions before dying. Number of possible divisions decreases with donor age. Foundational discovery linking cellular biology to aging — cells have finite replicative capacity.
  • Telomeres — Protective tips of chromosomes that shorten with each cell division. Eventually become too short, chromosomes become unstable, cell dies. Telomere length is a biomarker of cellular aging. Chronic stress accelerates shortening; exercise may slow it. Cancer cells often acquire telomerase activity, escaping the Hayflick limit.
  • Telomerase — Enzyme that rebuilds telomeres during DNA replication. Normally absent in somatic cells (body cells), present in stem cells and germline. Cancer cells frequently express telomerase, contributing to their unlimited replicative capacity. Telomerase activation as longevity intervention remains experimental and may carry cancer risk.
  • Free radicals and oxidative stress — Free radicals are highly reactive molecules produced randomly in normal metabolism. Oxidative stress is the imbalance between free radicals and antioxidants. Cumulative damage to proteins, lipids, and DNA contributes to age-related disease. Antioxidant supplements have shown less benefit than dietary patterns rich in antioxidants (Mediterranean diet, plant-forward eating).
  • Apoptosis (programmed cell death) — Genetically regulated process by which cells die in an orderly, controlled way. Different from necrosis (uncontrolled cell death from injury). Essential for normal development (e.g., shaping fingers), tissue maintenance, and cancer prevention. Dysregulation of apoptosis contributes to both cancer (insufficient apoptosis) and neurodegenerative disease (excessive apoptosis).
  • Mbakile-Mahlanza et al. (2020) Montessori-dementia study — Australian study demonstrating that family members can be successfully trained to deliver Montessori-based activities to nursing home residents with dementia. Crossover experimental design with 20 residents and 20 family carers. Found increased positive engagement and emotion in residents, increased satisfaction in family carers. Expands access to evidence-based dementia intervention.
  • Montessori method for dementia — Adaptation of Maria Montessori's educational philosophy for dementia care. Uses preserved cognitive functions (implicit memory, procedural skills), structured activities matched to remaining abilities, person-centered approach connecting to life history. Reduces agitation, improves engagement, can be delivered by trained family members.
  • ABA-APA Diminished Capacity Guidelines — Joint guidelines (2005, 2008) from the American Bar Association and American Psychological Association for assessing diminished capacity in older adults. Two handbooks (one for psychologists, one for lawyers); 9-area clinical assessment framework; 16-question legal framework. Ensures both rights protection and family/clinical accountability.
  • Decision-making capacity (four-part standard) — Clinical-legal framework for assessing capacity to make a specific decision. Four elements: (1) Understanding the relevant information; (2) Appreciation of how it applies to one's own situation; (3) Reasoning through alternatives; (4) Expression of a stable choice. Decision-specific and dynamic — varies by decision complexity and over time.
  • MacCAT-T (MacArthur Competence Assessment Tool — Treatment) — Validated semi-structured interview for assessing decision-making capacity regarding medical treatment. Systematically evaluates the four-part standard. Takes 15-20 minutes. Useful when capacity is contested or documentation is critical. Has parallel versions for research consent (MacCAT-CR) and clinical research (MacCAT-T-Plus).

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — a public-health phenomenon named after a celebrity health decision, illustrating how visible role models shape population behavior.

The Angelina Jolie effect case

The case: "The Angelina Jolie Effect"

In May 2013, actress Angelina Jolie published an op-ed announcing that she had tested positive for the BRCA1 gene mutation — a variant that substantially elevates lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer. Considering her family history (her mother had died of ovarian cancer at 56), Jolie chose to undergo a prophylactic double mastectomy. She wrote about her decision publicly to encourage other women to consider their family history and genetic risk.

Subsequent research documented a striking "Jolie effect":

  • UK referrals for genetic breast-cancer screening more than doubled in the weeks after her announcement
  • Increases were sustained over months, not just a short spike
  • Prophylactic mastectomy rates rose
  • Public awareness of BRCA mutations and their implications increased significantly

Why does a celebrity disclosure produce these effects? What does this case reveal about how health behaviors are shaped beyond individual decision- making, and what are the implications for public-health communication?

Worked analysis

Bandura's social cognitive theory revisited (Ch 1)

Health behaviors aren't just shaped by information — they're shaped by observed models. A respected, visible person making a particular health choice provides others with information about (a) what's possible, (b) what's normal, and (c) how to think about a difficult decision. Jolie's disclosure functioned as a high-profile model of "this is something you can consider".

Removing stigma

Many women with BRCA family history had never discussed it openly, viewing it as private or shameful. A celebrity disclosure normalizes the conversation — patients suddenly feel they can ask their doctors without embarrassment.

The information-access effect

Many women who later sought screening were not directly influenced by Jolie's choice — they had been unaware that BRCA testing existed, or that prophylactic surgery was an option. Public visibility created an information cascade that reached people who needed it.

Equity caveats

The Jolie effect was strongest among educated, higher-income women — those with access to genetic counseling, insurance coverage, and follow-up care. The effect was much smaller in populations without these resources. A celebrity disclosure alone doesn't fix structural access gaps; without supporting infrastructure, increased demand only helps those already advantaged.

The textbook's larger point

Health behaviors — particularly in older adulthood, when chronic disease prevention matters most — are shaped by far more than individual decisions. Cultural framing, social modeling, information access, and structural support all interact. The Jolie effect is a natural experiment showing how visible decisions by trusted figures can shift population behavior — for better, when paired with adequate resources; for worse, when not. Public-health communication that ignores these social dynamics misses much of what actually drives behavior change.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 14 core content.

Q1

A 78-year-old patient mentions they sometimes need to write things down to remember appointments, occasionally lose their keys, and have to ask others to repeat things in noisy restaurants. They live independently, manage their own finances and medications, and their family hasn't noticed any concerning changes. What's the most likely category?

  1. A. Normal aging
  2. B. Mild cognitive impairment
  3. C. Early Alzheimer's disease
  4. D. Delirium
Show rationale

A. CORRECT. The features described — using lists to compensate, occasional lost objects, hearing difficulty in noise — are textbook normal aging. The key indicators that this is NOT MCI or dementia: independent function (finances, medications), no family-noticed changes, no functional impairment. Normal aging includes gradual fluid-intelligence change with effective compensation strategies.

B. MCI would require cognitive change beyond normal that patient/family notice and that shows on testing — without the daily function impairment.

C. Alzheimer's involves cognitive change that interferes with daily function. Not described here.

D. Delirium has acute onset over hours/days with fluctuating attention. Not the pattern described.

Q2

A nursing-home resident who normally has stable mild dementia suddenly becomes severely confused over 24 hours — extreme difficulty focusing, alternating between agitation and drowsiness, seeing things that aren't there. What should you suspect FIRST, and what's the most appropriate immediate response?

  1. A. Worsening dementia — increase environmental supports and reassure family
  2. B. Delirium — investigate underlying medical causes (infection, medication, dehydration) urgently; this is an acute change requiring medical workup
  3. C. A normal response to stress — wait and see
  4. D. Routine sundowning — give a sedative
Show rationale

A. Dementia changes occur over months/years, not 24 hours. Acute change is delirium until proven otherwise.

B. CORRECT. Acute onset + fluctuating attention + hallucinations = delirium, not dementia. This is a medical emergency. Common reversible causes: infection (UTI is extremely common in nursing-home residents and often presents this way), medication side effect or interaction, dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, pain, hypoxia, post-anesthesia. Workup: vital signs, urinalysis, blood work, medication review, hydration assessment. Treat the underlying cause and the delirium typically resolves.

C. "Wait and see" with this presentation can be fatal. Delirium has substantial mortality if the underlying cause goes untreated.

D. Sedatives can worsen delirium and mask the underlying problem. Sundowning is a specific dementia pattern that doesn't fit this acute presentation.

Q3

An 80-year-old patient developed slowly progressive memory loss over 3 years — first forgetting recent conversations, then names, now sometimes getting lost in familiar neighborhoods and unable to manage medications without help. There's no history of strokes, no personality changes, no hallucinations. Which dementia type fits best?

  1. A. Alzheimer's disease
  2. B. Vascular dementia
  3. C. Lewy body dementia
  4. D. Frontotemporal dementia
Show rationale

A. CORRECT. The pattern fits Alzheimer's disease: slow insidious onset, gradual steady decline over years, memory problems prominent (especially recent memory), eventually interfering with daily function. Alzheimer's is the most common dementia (60–70% of cases). The absence of features pointing to other types (stepwise decline, hallucinations, personality changes) supports the Alzheimer's diagnosis.

B. Vascular dementia typically has stepwise decline (each stroke or vascular event drops function) and history of cardiovascular disease. Neither described.

C. Lewy body dementia features fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, Parkinsonian motor symptoms. Not described.

D. Frontotemporal dementia typically starts earlier (50s–60s) with prominent personality / behavioral changes before memory issues. Not described.

Q4

A 75-year-old man with chronic pain and recent loss of his wife reports being "tired all the time," having lost his appetite and interest in his hobbies, and sleeping poorly. He denies feeling sad. Which is the most appropriate clinical response?

  1. A. Reassure that this is normal grief and aging; recommend waiting
  2. B. Screen for depression — late-life depression often presents with somatic symptoms and anhedonia, with patients underreporting sad mood; recognition matters because untreated late-life depression carries high suicide risk and is highly treatable
  3. C. Recommend more exercise and resolve to revisit in 6 months
  4. D. Increase pain medication
Show rationale

A. "Normal aging / normal grief" framing is exactly how late-life depression gets missed. Symptoms beyond 2–3 months and across multiple domains warrant screening.

B. CORRECT. The pattern fits late-life depression: somatic symptoms prominent, anhedonia, poor sleep and appetite, in context of chronic pain and recent loss. Older adults often underreport sad mood, so denying it doesn't rule out depression. Late-life depression is often missed (attributed to "aging" or chronic conditions). Recognition matters: older adults have the highest suicide rates (especially older men), and depression is highly treatable. Use a validated screening tool (Geriatric Depression Scale), consider therapy and/or medication with attention to drug interactions.

C. "Wait and see" with an older man at high suicide risk is inadequate.

D. Pain medication alone doesn't address the depression and may worsen it.

Q5

A geriatrician is designing exercise recommendations for a healthy 72-year-old patient. Which framing is most consistent with research on aging?

  1. A. Older adults shouldn't exercise to avoid injury
  2. B. Regular physical activity, including aerobic and resistance training, is the single most powerful intervention available — supporting cardiovascular function, muscle mass, balance, cognition, and mood, with substantial mortality benefit
  3. C. Light walking only — anything more is too risky after 70
  4. D. Exercise has no significant benefit after age 70
Show rationale

A. The opposite of what evidence supports. Avoiding exercise itself causes greater harm (sarcopenia, frailty, deconditioning, fall risk).

B. CORRECT. Regular physical activity is the single most powerful intervention in older adults — better than most pharmaceuticals across a wide range of outcomes. Aerobic exercise supports cardiovascular function and cognition; resistance training preserves muscle mass (preventing sarcopenia) and bone density; balance work prevents falls. Mortality benefit is substantial across studies. Recommendations: roughly 150 min/week moderate aerobic + 2x/week strength + balance work, adjusted for individual capacity. Healthy 72-year-olds tolerate this very well.

C. Resistance and aerobic exercise are both safe and beneficial for healthy 70-somethings.

D. Strong evidence shows exercise benefits continue across the lifespan, including in the oldest-old.

Q6

A nursing home administrator is concerned that family visitors often seem uncertain how to engage with their relatives who have dementia. Based on Mbakile-Mahlanza et al.'s (2020) research, what approach would be evidence-based?

  1. A. Discourage family visits since they cause stress
  2. B. Train family caregivers to deliver structured Montessori-based activities during visits — research shows family members can successfully learn and implement these techniques, producing more positive engagement and emotions in residents with dementia AND higher family-caregiver satisfaction
  3. C. Have professionals do all engagement; families should just observe
  4. D. Limit visit length to reduce confusion
Show rationale

A. Family visits provide essential social connection; reducing isolation matters for dementia outcomes.

B. CORRECT. Mbakile-Mahlanza et al. (2020) demonstrated that family caregivers can successfully learn and deliver Montessori-based dementia interventions, with documented benefits: more positive engagement and emotions in residents, and higher satisfaction in family caregivers. This expands the resource pool for high-quality dementia care without depending solely on stretched professional staff. Practical translation: brief training sessions on dementia-friendly activities (familiar music, sorting tasks, photo albums, simple gardening, task breakdown) substantially increase the quality of visits.

C. Family visits are most beneficial when family members are active participants in engagement, not just observers.

D. Shorter visits aren't the solution; better-quality engagement is.

Q7

A researcher wants to study a new intervention with nursing-home residents who have moderate dementia. Multiple participants have indicated they don't fully understand the study procedures. What ethical safeguards are required, and what is "assent" in this context?

  1. A. Get informed consent only from the resident; their participation is fine since they're adults
  2. B. Require: (1) IRB approval with vulnerable-population protections, (2) surrogate informed consent from a legally authorized representative, (3) participant assent (an affirmative indication they want to participate or don't object, distinct from informed consent), (4) ongoing emotional monitoring with stopping if distress emerges, (5) right to withdraw including by behavioral signs
  3. C. Don't include people with dementia in research
  4. D. Get verbal consent only
Show rationale

A. If the resident doesn't fully understand procedures, they can't give legally valid informed consent. Surrogate consent is needed.

B. CORRECT. Research with cognitively impaired participants requires layered safeguards: (1) IRB / ethics approval with vulnerable-population attention. (2) Surrogate informed consent from a legally authorized representative. (3) Participant assent — affirmative agreement or absence of objection from the person themselves; assent isn't full informed consent but is essential. (4) Ongoing monitoring for distress with stopping when needed. (5) Right to withdraw by verbal OR behavioral signs. (6) Minimal risk threshold. The Mbakile-Mahlanza study (2020) provides a worked example of all these safeguards in practice.

C. Excluding people with dementia from research would deny their condition appropriate study. Layered safeguards make ethical participation possible.

D. Written documentation is required for research; verbal alone is insufficient and provides no accountability.

Q8

A nurse practitioner is working with an immigrant Chinese family caring for an elderly grandmother. The family describes their grandmother as "wise" — and emphasize that this means she has lived a life of proper conduct and benevolence toward family, more than that she possesses unusual knowledge. How should the nurse understand this framing of wisdom?

  1. A. The family doesn't understand what wisdom really means
  2. B. Western and Chinese traditions emphasize different aspects of wisdom: Western traditions emphasize intellectual humility (sophia, phronesis); Chinese Confucian traditions emphasize civility, proper conduct, benevolence, and prosocial behavior as central to wisdom (Buchtel et al., 2015, 2017); both are legitimate framings, and culturally-grounded elder care recognizes the family's framework
  3. C. The family is describing virtuous behavior, not wisdom
  4. D. Cultural framings of wisdom don't matter clinically
Show rationale

A. The framing is empirically supported by cross-cultural research, not a misunderstanding.

B. CORRECT. Cross-cultural research consistently shows that different cultures emphasize different aspects of wisdom. Western traditions emphasize intellectual humility (sophia = ultimate knowledge, phronesis = practical reasoning for good character). Non-Western traditions like Chinese Confucian thought emphasize civility, proper social conduct, benevolence, and prosocial behavior. Buchtel et al. (2015, 2017) demonstrated that Chinese adults are more likely than Americans, Australians, and Canadians to view wisdom as virtuous character. Both framings are legitimate and culturally-grounded. Clinical practice implication: culturally-responsive elder care recognizes the family's framework rather than imposing a Western template. When the family describes their grandmother as wise, they're describing real cultural wisdom — not misusing the term.

C. In Confucian thought, virtuous behavior IS wisdom; the two aren't separable. The Western separation isn't universal.

D. Cultural framings affect what older adults value, how they seek care, how families make decisions, and what "living well" means in late life — all clinically relevant.

Q9

An 82-year-old patient is admitted to the hospital for pneumonia. On day 2 of hospitalization, he becomes agitated, can't recognize his daughter, has fluctuating attention, and sees imaginary bugs on the wall. His family says he was at his cognitive baseline last week. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what's the priority intervention?

  1. A. Sudden-onset Alzheimer's disease
  2. B. Delirium — an acute confusional state caused by underlying medical illness; characterized by acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganized thinking, and altered consciousness; common precipitants include infection (UTI, pneumonia), medications (especially anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids), electrolyte imbalances, dehydration, sleep deprivation, sensory deprivation, and unfamiliar environment; priority interventions include identifying and treating underlying cause, medication review and deprescribing, reorientation (clock, calendar, familiar objects), keeping family present, ensuring sleep and adequate hydration, avoiding restraints, minimizing sedating medications, and managing pain; delirium has substantial mortality and morbidity if untreated
  3. C. Just normal aging
  4. D. Personality change
Show rationale

A. Alzheimer's disease has gradual onset over months to years, not sudden onset over hours; the presentation is inconsistent with Alzheimer's.

B. CORRECT. This is classic delirium — one of the most important conditions for nurses to recognize. Diagnostic features (DSM-5): (1) Acute onset (hours to days) and fluctuating course. (2) Disturbance in attention and awareness. (3) Additional cognitive disturbance (memory, orientation, language, perception). (4) Change is from baseline. (5) Direct physiological consequence of medical condition. Three subtypes: (1) Hyperactive: agitated, restless, hallucinations (most recognizable). (2) Hypoactive: withdrawn, drowsy, decreased activity (often missed; worse prognosis). (3) Mixed: alternates. Hospital prevalence: 20–30% of older medical inpatients; 50%+ in ICU and postoperative. Common precipitants (often multiple): (1) Infection — UTI, pneumonia, sepsis. (2) Medications — anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids, sedatives, polypharmacy. (3) Metabolic — dehydration, electrolytes, glucose, hypoxia, uremia. (4) Pain (under- or over-treated). (5) Sleep deprivation. (6) Sensory issues (missing hearing aids, glasses). (7) Constipation, urinary retention. (8) Unfamiliar environment, frequent room changes. (9) Withdrawal (alcohol, benzodiazepines). Priority interventions: (1) Identify and treat underlying cause — labs (CBC, BMP, UA, CXR), targeted workup. (2) Medication review — deprescribe culprits (Beers Criteria). (3) Reorientation — clocks, calendars, familiar objects, frequent reminders. (4) Sensory aids — glasses, hearing aids on. (5) Family presence — familiar faces reduce confusion. (6) Sleep hygiene — minimize nighttime disruption, light/dark cues. (7) Mobility — out of bed when possible. (8) Hydration and nutrition. (9) Pain management — adequate but not over-sedating. (10) Avoid restraints — worsen agitation. (11) Avoid antipsychotics unless absolutely necessary — black box warning in dementia; reserve for severe agitation threatening safety; low-dose haloperidol or quetiapine if used. Outcomes: delirium is associated with prolonged hospitalization, functional decline, new institutionalization, persistent cognitive impairment, and elevated mortality (one-year mortality ~35-40%). Critical to prevent and treat.

C. Normal aging doesn't cause sudden confusion and hallucinations; this is an emergency.

D. Acute confusion with fluctuating attention isn't personality change; it's a medical emergency.

Q10

A 78-year-old man tells his nurse he's been feeling "just tired" and doesn't enjoy things anymore. His wife died 6 months ago. He denies feeling sad. He's lost weight, isn't sleeping well, and has stopped attending his church group. Which framework best explains his presentation, and what should the nurse do?

  1. A. This is normal grief; do nothing
  2. B. Late-life depression often presents differently than depression in younger adults — somatic complaints (fatigue, weight loss, sleep), anhedonia, and functional decline may be more prominent than reported sadness; bereavement increases depression risk substantially; screen with Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) which is validated for older adults; assess suicide risk (older men have highest US suicide rate); refer for treatment combining psychotherapy (CBT, IPT, problem-solving therapy validated in older adults) and consider antidepressants (SSRIs first-line; start low, go slow); evaluate for grief vs depression vs both; medical workup for treatable contributors (thyroid, B12, medications); connect with bereavement support; depression is treatable in older adults
  3. C. He should "snap out of it"
  4. D. Prescribe a long benzodiazepine course
Show rationale

A. While grief is real, the duration and functional impact suggest possible major depression requiring evaluation.

B. CORRECT. Late-life depression is commonly missed because it presents differently than typical adult depression. Distinctive features: (1) Somatic complaints predominate — fatigue, weight loss, sleep disturbance, pain, GI complaints — rather than reported sadness. (2) Anhedonia often most prominent — loss of interest/pleasure. (3) Cognitive complaints — concentration problems, indecision, memory concerns ("pseudodementia"). (4) Functional decline — withdrawal from activities, decreased self-care. (5) Irritability and anxiety. (6) Often denies sadness — older adults may not endorse "feeling depressed." Why missed: (1) Symptoms attributed to medical illness or "normal aging." (2) Older adults less likely to verbalize emotional distress. (3) Provider bias — depression treated as expected after losses. (4) Time constraints in primary care. Bereavement and depression: complicated/persistent grief and depression overlap; about 15–25% of bereaved older adults develop major depression within first year. Suicide risk: older white men have the highest US suicide rate — far higher than other groups; often through firearm; bereavement is a risk factor. Screen with explicit questions; means restriction crucial. Assessment: (1) Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) — 15-item version validated; scores ≥5 suggestive. (2) PHQ-9 also usable. (3) Medical workup for treatable contributors — thyroid, B12, electrolytes, medication review (beta-blockers, steroids, etc.). (4) Cognitive screening to differentiate depression from dementia. (5) Substance use assessment. Treatment: (1) Psychotherapy — CBT, IPT (Interpersonal Therapy), problem-solving therapy all evidence-based for older adults. (2) Antidepressants — SSRIs first-line; sertraline, citalopram (with QTc caution at higher doses) commonly used; start low, titrate slow; avoid TCAs in most cases (anticholinergic burden). (3) Combined treatment for moderate-severe. (4) ECT for severe, treatment-resistant. (5) Address modifiable factors — pain, sleep, isolation. (6) Bereavement-specific support. (7) Reduce suicide risk — means restriction, mental health follow-up. Treatment is effective — older adults respond to depression treatment comparably to younger adults; ageist assumption that depression is "natural" produces under-treatment.

C. "Snap out of it" reflects ageist dismissal; depression requires treatment.

D. Benzodiazepines are inappropriate first-line for late-life depression; high risk of falls, cognitive impairment, dependence (Beers Criteria).

Q11

An 80-year-old patient lives alone, has had three falls in the past 6 months, and has hypertension, mild kidney disease, osteoporosis, and depression. She takes 9 medications. Her daughter is worried about her safety. What comprehensive geriatric assessment framework should guide care?

  1. A. Just adjust the hypertension medication
  2. B. Comprehensive geriatric assessment (CGA) — multidimensional evaluation across medical (multimorbidity, polypharmacy, syndromes), functional (ADLs/IADLs, falls, mobility), cognitive (screening), psychosocial (depression, social support, abuse), and environmental (home safety, finances) domains; fall workup includes medication review (Beers Criteria, STOPP/START), vision check, orthostatic vitals, gait/balance assessment (Timed Up and Go), home safety evaluation; falls predict future falls, fractures, decline, and mortality; multidisciplinary approach typically includes geriatrician, pharmacist (deprescribing), physical therapy (balance training), occupational therapy (home modifications), social work (support resources), and continued nursing engagement; goal is preserving function and quality of life while managing risks
  3. C. Place her in a nursing home immediately
  4. D. Add more medications
Show rationale

A. Single-issue focus misses the interconnected nature of geriatric problems.

B. CORRECT. Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) is the gold-standard framework for complex older adults with multiple problems. Domains: (1) Medical — chronic diseases, multimorbidity, geriatric syndromes (falls, frailty, incontinence, delirium), medication review. (2) Functional — ADLs (basic self-care: bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, transferring, continence), IADLs (instrumental: shopping, cooking, medications, finances, transportation, housework, phone), mobility, falls. (3) Cognitive — screening (MoCA, Mini-Cog, MMSE), assessment of decision-making capacity. (4) Psychosocial — depression (GDS), anxiety, social support, isolation, elder abuse screen. (5) Environmental — home safety, finances, access to services, advance directives. Polypharmacy: 9 medications increases risk substantially. (1) Beers Criteria identifies potentially inappropriate medications in older adults. (2) STOPP/START criteria for stopping inappropriate and starting indicated medications. (3) Deprescribing is appropriate clinical practice — not just "more is better." (4) Medication reconciliation at each visit. Falls workup: (1) History — circumstances, prodrome, witnesses. (2) Medications — culprits: BP meds (orthostasis), sedatives, anticholinergics, hypoglycemics. (3) Orthostatic vitals. (4) Vision assessment. (5) Gait and balance — Timed Up and Go (>12 seconds concerning), 30-second chair stand, Berg Balance Scale. (6) Cognitive screening. (7) Foot exam. (8) Home safety — lighting, rugs, stairs, bathroom safety, grab bars. (9) Vitamin D status. (10) Bone density. Falls have major consequences: 1 in 4 older adults falls annually; 1 in 5 falls causes serious injury; hip fractures have 20-25% one-year mortality. Multidisciplinary team: geriatrician, pharmacist (deprescribing), PT (balance training, strength), OT (home modifications, ADL training), social work (resources), continuing primary care/nursing. Goals: preserve function, maintain quality of life, prevent decline, support patient preferences, advance care planning. Not just adding medications or rushing to placement.

C. Premature; many interventions can keep her safely at home if explored.

D. Adding medications is the opposite of what's needed; deprescribing is often more therapeutic.

Q12

A 75-year-old patient has been diagnosed with mild Alzheimer's disease. Her daughter asks the nurse what the family can do to slow progression and support her mother. Which is the most evidence-based response?

  1. A. "Nothing helps; just wait"
  2. B. Multi-pronged approach with evidence base: (1) medical care including cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) for mild-moderate AD with modest cognitive benefit, memantine for moderate-severe, evaluate for anti-amyloid antibody therapy if appropriate (eligibility includes mild disease, biomarker confirmation, no contraindications); (2) cardiovascular risk factor management (BP, diabetes, lipids, smoking cessation) which slows progression; (3) physical activity with strong evidence for cognitive and functional benefit; (4) cognitive engagement and social activity; (5) Mediterranean/MIND diet; (6) sleep optimization; (7) hearing aid use if hearing loss; (8) caregiver support — Alzheimer's Association resources, support groups, respite care, structured activity programs (TAP); (9) safety planning — driving evaluation, medications, wandering; (10) advance care planning while decision-making capacity intact; (11) regular monitoring for depression which is common; reframe: while AD progresses, quality of life, independence, and family relationships can be substantially supported
  3. C. "Just take supplements"
  4. D. "Place her in memory care now"
Show rationale

A. "Nothing helps" is incorrect and damaging; substantial evidence-based interventions support quality of life and slow functional decline.

B. CORRECT. Alzheimer's disease management has multiple evidence-based components. Medications: (1) Cholinesterase inhibitors — donepezil (Aricept), rivastigmine (Exelon), galantamine (Razadyne); modest cognitive benefit for mild-moderate AD; side effects mainly GI; some patients show meaningful function maintenance. (2) Memantine (Namenda) — NMDA antagonist for moderate-severe AD; often combined with cholinesterase inhibitor. (3) Anti-amyloid antibody therapy — aducanumab (2021, controversial), lecanemab (2023), donanemab (2024); for mild AD with biomarker confirmation; modest cognitive slowing; risks include brain bleeding/swelling (ARIA-E, ARIA-H); high cost. Risk factor modification: (1) Vascular risk factors — hypertension, diabetes, lipid management; substantial impact on progression. (2) Smoking cessation. (3) Limit alcohol. Lifestyle interventions with evidence: (1) Physical activity — aerobic and resistance; strongest non-pharmacologic evidence; FINGER trial. (2) Cognitive engagement — social activities, mentally challenging tasks; less robust evidence but generally recommended. (3) Social engagement — protective against decline; isolation accelerates. (4) Mediterranean/MIND diet — observational evidence. (5) Sleep optimization — sleep apnea treatment if present. (6) Hearing aids — recent research links hearing loss to dementia risk and progression. Caregiver support: (1) Alzheimer's Association — comprehensive resources, hotline, support groups. (2) Support groups for caregivers. (3) Respite care — critical for caregiver wellbeing. (4) Tailored Activity Program (TAP) — Mbakile-Mahlanza et al. showed family-delivered structured activities reduce behaviors and improve outcomes. (5) Education about dementia trajectory and management. Safety planning: (1) Driving evaluation — eventually needs to stop; assess proactively. (2) Medication management — eventually needs supervision. (3) Wandering precautions — ID bracelet, GPS, locks if needed. (4) Home safety — stove safety, falls prevention. (5) Financial safety — vulnerable to exploitation. Advance care planning: (1) While capacity intact — healthcare proxy, advance directive, financial POA, MOLST/POLST. (2) Discuss preferences — life-sustaining treatment, hospitalization, feeding tube preferences. (3) Palliative care introduction in later stages. Monitor for: depression (common, treatable), anxiety, behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia, caregiver burnout. Reframe: while AD progresses inexorably, quality of life, independence, dignity, family connection, and meaningful engagement can be substantially supported throughout the course.

C. Supplements lack evidence; many are marketed misleadingly to families desperate for hope.

D. Many people with mild AD live at home with appropriate support for years; premature placement isn't appropriate.

Unit 4 · Later Life & Dying · Chapter 15

Social Aspects of Later Life

TL;DR · One-glance summary

Late-life social development is more about quality and selectivity than quantity. Carstensen's socioemotional selectivity theory explains why older adults' social networks shrink while satisfaction rises — they prune toward what matters most. Erikson's final stage is integrity vs. despair — looking back at life and finding meaning vs. regret. Retirement is now a multi-phase transition, not a single event. Older adults rely heavily on friendship and family; widowhood is a major life transition. Elder abuse is real, often hidden, and clinically important to recognize.

Theories

  • Erikson: integrity vs. despair → wisdom
  • Carstensen SST: prioritize emotional ties
  • Baltes SOC: select / optimize / compensate
  • Lawton: competence × environmental press

Personality & self

  • Big Five remain stable
  • Life review — common, often productive
  • Self-evaluations remain positive for most
  • Wisdom develops with experience plus reflection

Retirement & relationships

  • Retirement: now a transition, not a cliff
  • Adjustment depends on health, finances, social ties
  • Friendships become more selective & deeper
  • Widowhood: real grief, varied resilience

Social issues

  • Long-term care options: home → assisted → nursing
  • Caregiver burden & respite
  • Elder abuse: physical, emotional, financial, neglect
  • Ageism in policy & culture

15.1 · Theories of Psychosocial Aging

Several frameworks help explain why older adults often report higher wellbeing than middle-aged adults — despite physical decline. The answer: they organize their lives differently.

Theories of psychosocial aging

Erikson — integrity vs. despair

8th psychosocial stage

The final stage in Erikson's framework. The central task: looking back at one's life and integrating it into a coherent, accepted whole. Integrity = the sense that one's life was meaningful, lived with purpose, and is at peace with what came. Despair = the opposite — regret about choices not made, time wasted, and an inability to make peace with mortality. The strength acquired through integrity is wisdom.

Integrity isn't about having had a "perfect" life. People with hard, marked, even tragic lives can develop integrity through honest reckoning with what happened and what it meant. Conversely, people with externally successful lives can end in despair if they cannot integrate it.
