(3) Honor cultural practices: when in-person isn't possible, ask family what rituals they'd want to perform and how staff can facilitate (e.g., positioning the body for prayer, allowing specific words to be spoken, accommodating timing requests). (4) Train all staff in supportive scripting: bedside nurses, physicians, chaplains, social workers — all may need to facilitate these conversations. (5) Document the goodbye: many family members later wish they had a record. Recording a goodbye conversation (with consent) provides this. (6) For nurses providing this care: facilitating mediated goodbyes is emotionally costly. It requires deliberate support and debriefing. (7) Address the racial disparities directly: track outcomes by patient race and family experience. Don't accept "we tried our best" if outcomes are worse for some populations.

What the textbook asks

  • What could have improved people's ability to say goodbye during the pandemic — what systems were missing?
  • What specific healthcare disparities affected the families and friends of those who died from COVID-19?
  • How can we address racial and ethnic disparities so they aren't barriers in the next pandemic?
  • How might religious and cultural death rituals — which often involve specific prayers, presence, or physical actions — be supported in future public-health emergencies that limit physical access?

Filipino bereavement traditions — death, mourning, and family

A blended cultural-religious framework

Filipino approaches to death and bereavement blend Catholicism with pre-colonial indigenous traditions. The result is a rich, structured approach to mourning that prioritizes:

  • Communal participation — death is a family and community event, never individual
  • Continuity — the deceased remains part of the family across the boundary of death
  • Structured ritual — specific observances at specific times
  • Extended timeline — mourning extends substantially beyond the funeral

Understanding these practices is essential for clinicians caring for Filipino patients, family members, and colleagues — and for healthcare institutions serving Filipino communities.

Lamay — the wake

Multi-day visitation

The lamay (wake) typically lasts several days to a week — often 3-7 nights of family and community visitation with the deceased. Far longer than typical American funeral home visitations. Family takes turns staying awake through the night with the body.

Communal food and games

Throughout the lamay, the home is open to visitors. Food is provided continuously; visitors typically bring contributions. Card games and conversation continue through the night — keeping company with the deceased and with the family. The atmosphere blends grief and connection.

Spiritual observances

Catholic prayers and rosaries are recited throughout — often led by family elders. Priests may be invited for blessings. Photographs, candles, religious icons surround the casket.

Cultural protocols

Various beliefs and observances may attend: not sweeping the floor where someone died (until after burial); not bathing during certain hours; certain birds or insects in the home considered messengers; specific protocols for transporting the body.

Pasiyam — nine days of prayer

After the burial, pasiyam begins — a 9-day cycle of evening prayer. The word comes from "siyam" (nine in Tagalog) — paralleling the Catholic novena tradition. Each evening:

  • Family and friends gather (often at the deceased's home)
  • The rosary is prayed
  • Food is served
  • Stories about the deceased are shared
  • Community presence supports the grieving family

The pasiyam ends with a larger gathering on the 9th day — often including a special Mass. Spiritually, the 9 days are understood as the soul's journey transitioning to the afterlife; the prayers support that journey.

For Filipino families, expectations of attendance can be substantial — extended family, neighbors, godparents (ninong / ninang), and family friends participating. Missing pasiyam can carry social weight.

Forty-day prayer and babang-luksa

40th day observance

Some Filipino families observe a 40th day remembrance — paralleling the period the soul is understood to spend before final passage. A special prayer or Mass marks this milestone.

Babang-luksa — end of mourning

Babang-luksa (literally "lowering of mourning" or "descent from mourning") typically occurs one year after the death. This is the formal end of the mourning period. A Mass is held; family and close friends gather. Black mourning clothes (worn for the year) are formally set aside or replaced with white as a symbol of resurrection.

Death anniversaries

Following babang-luksa, the deceased continues to be remembered on death anniversaries — typically with a Mass and family meal. Undás (November 1-2, All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day) is a major national observance where Filipino families visit cemeteries and clean graves; entire communities gather at cemeteries for prayers, candles, and meals.

Continuing connection

Throughout these observances, the deceased is understood to remain part of the family. Photos, candles, and altars maintain their presence. Decisions may be made "considering what they would have wanted" — not as memory but as ongoing relationship.

The intersection with Filipino American immigration

For Filipino Americans, traditional practices encounter US healthcare and funeral systems that may not accommodate them:

  • Hospital wakes: US hospitals often don't allow extended viewing or vigil presence. Quick body transport to mortuary is the default.
  • Multi-day visitations: many US funeral homes structure brief, scheduled viewings rather than multi-day open presence.
  • Repatriation: many Filipino families wish to bring the body or ashes back to the Philippines for burial. Expensive, paperwork-intensive, time-pressured.
  • Distance and travel: extended family in the Philippines may not be able to attend; older siblings or parents abroad may experience compounded grief from inability to participate.
  • Pasiyam in immigrant communities: often adapted — sometimes condensed, sometimes maintained, sometimes done with virtual participation by family abroad.
  • Funeral costs: Filipino funeral expectations (community feeding, extended ceremony) can create financial strain for immigrant families. Many begin death savings early.

For nursing practice with Filipino patients and families

Specific recommendations

(1) Ask, don't assume: Filipino practices vary by region, generation, religious tradition (Catholic majority but also Protestant, Iglesia ni Cristo, INC, others), and acculturation. Open questions about what the family needs. (2) Honor pre-death preparations: many Filipino families want to bring religious items (rosaries, scapulars, prayer cards, candles), have a priest present for anointing of the sick, perform last visits — accommodate within hospital policy. (3) Body care after death: ask about specific traditions for handling the body — washing, dressing, cosmetic preparation. Some families want to participate. (4) Extend family identification: in Filipino culture, "family" often includes godparents (ninong/ninang), elder cousins, family friends called "auntie/uncle." Don't restrict information sharing or decision-making rights to nuclear family alone. (5) Anticipate community presence: when a Filipino patient is dying, expect many visitors. Help families navigate visitor policies; advocate for flexibility when possible. (6) Recognize anticipatory and complicated grief: family caregivers may have provided intense care for years; the death of an elder may bring both relief and devastating loss. Both are normal. (7) Spiritual care integration: hospital chaplains, community priests, Catholic religious orders, INC ministers — connect families to appropriate clergy. (8) Death certificate and repatriation: hospitals can help families understand the steps needed for body repatriation. Connect early to consular services if requested. (9) Follow-up after death: a brief follow-up call from the nurse who cared for the patient is deeply appreciated. Family may share photos of the pasiyam or babang-luksa — accept these gestures of connection. (10) Self-knowledge for Filipino healthcare workers: Mark, as you encounter Filipino patient deaths in your nursing career, your own family's practices will give you both insight and emotional resonance. Recognize when you may need support yourself.

16.5 · Dying & Bereavement Across the Life Span

Children, adolescents, and adults all process death — but they do so very differently, and supporting them requires understanding what their developmental stage allows them to comprehend.

Dying & bereavement across the lifespan

Children's understanding of death develops in stages

Preschool (~3–6)

Death is understood as reversible, temporary, like sleep. The child may ask repeatedly when the deceased is "coming back". They may believe their own thoughts or actions caused the death (magical thinking).

Early school age (~6–9)

Death is understood as final but external — something that happens to other people or in unlikely circumstances. The child grasps irreversibility but may not yet accept universality (that everyone, including parents and themselves, will eventually die).

Later school age (~9–12)

Mature concept emerges: death is universal, irreversible, and biological. Children at this stage can grasp the abstract reality of mortality and may show anxiety about their own and loved ones' eventual deaths.

Adolescence onward

Formal-operational reasoning adds existential reflection — meaning of life, philosophical and religious questions about death, sometimes a sense of personal invulnerability (the "personal fable" of Ch 8) that can paradoxically reduce death anxiety.

A 4-year-old whose grandmother died last week asks every morning, "When is Grandma coming back?" How should the family respond, given children's developmental understanding of death?
At age 4, the child understands death as reversible, temporary, like sleep. The repeated question reflects their stage, not denial or pathology — they genuinely don't yet grasp that Grandma is permanently gone. Appropriate response: age-appropriate honesty without euphemism. Say clearly that Grandma died (avoid "went to sleep" or "we lost her" — these confuse a young child who may then fear sleeping or being lost themselves). Explain in concrete terms: "Her body stopped working, and she can't come back. We won't see her again, but we can remember her and talk about her." Repeat as the child re-asks (they will). Allow questions. Watch for magical thinking ("did I make her die?") — children this age sometimes believe their thoughts caused the death and need direct reassurance. As the child gets older, their understanding will mature; the early gentle truth-telling lays groundwork for that.

Adult bereavement variations

Different losses affect adults differently. Some patterns:

  • Loss of parent is the most common adult bereavement. Often expected, sometimes long-anticipated; still profoundly affecting, particularly the second parent's death (which makes the adult an "orphan").
  • Loss of partner in older adulthood — discussed in Ch 15. Major life transition; elevated mental and physical health risks in the first year.
  • Loss of child — at any age — is consistently rated as the most difficult bereavement. Higher rates of complicated grief, marital strain, and long-term distress. Specialized bereavement support is particularly important.
  • Sudden / traumatic / violent loss — accidents, suicide, homicide, overdose. Higher rates of complicated grief, PTSD, and prolonged adjustment difficulty than expected losses.

Cultural context shapes grief expression enormously — appropriate mourning practices, expected duration, public vs. private expression, and meaning- making frameworks all vary across cultures. Clinical care should be culturally-informed; what looks like "abnormal" grief in one cultural frame may be entirely normal in another.

A hospice nurse cares for a Filipino-American family whose elder is dying. They request specific rituals: novena prayers, gathering of extended family, traditional foods near the patient, and after death they request the body be kept home for viewing rather than immediately to funeral home. How should the nurse support culturally-grounded bereavement, and what general principles apply across cultures?
Cultural variation in death and mourning practices is enormous; culturally responsive care is foundational to good end-of-life nursing. For this Filipino family — practices reflect the deep integration of Catholic faith, family-centered values (familism), and ancestral traditions: (1) Novena (nine days of prayer after death) — central practice for many Filipino Catholics; community gathers, prayers said for the soul. (2) Pasiyam (9-day commemoration) and babang-luksa (1-year end-of-mourning). (3) Extended family gathering — multi-generational presence at death is expected; family supports the dying and each other. (4) Traditional foods — meaningful comfort and connection. (5) Body kept home for viewing — common in many Filipino families; allows family time, prayers, photographs, paying respects before funeral. How nursing supports: (1) Ask, don't assume — even within cultural groups, individual family practices vary. (2) Provide private space for prayer and rituals. (3) Don't rush body removal — coordinate with funeral homes that accommodate family preferences. (4) Welcome extended family — don't enforce limited visitor rules at end of life. (5) Coordinate with clergy — Catholic priest for last rites/anointing of the sick; this is medically significant for the family. (6) Allow food from home when feasible. (7) Respect family hierarchy — often elders or eldest child make decisions in consultation. (8) Filipino concept of utang na loob (debt of gratitude) — family may insist on personally caring for the dying as expression of love and obligation; support this. (9) Don't pathologize emotional expression — loud crying, visible grief is appropriate in this and many cultures; restraint isn't required. (10) Be aware of stigma around mental health — some Filipino families resist "therapy" framing; can connect to religious community support instead. General principles across cultures: (1) Religious traditions: Catholic anointing/last rites; Jewish chevra kadisha (preparation of body), shiva (7-day mourning); Muslim ghusl (ritual washing), burial within 24 hours when possible; Hindu cremation traditions and rituals; Buddhist meditation and chanting; Indigenous and Native American smudging, songs, family presence. (2) Body care varies — some traditions require specific positioning, washing, dressing; some prohibit autopsy. (3) Disclosure norms vary — some cultures prefer direct disclosure to patient; others see this as harmful. (4) Decision-making — individualistic (Western patient autonomy) vs collectivistic (family-centered). (5) Mourning duration varies — from days to a year or more. (6) Expression — public/loud to private/restrained. Nursing competence: (1) Don't memorize cultures — ask patients and families about their specific practices. (2) Provide flexibility in institutional rules. (3) Coordinate with chaplaincy services. (4) Recognize culture-bound expressions of grief — somatic complaints in some cultures; possession states or visions; ancestor communication. (5) Watch for clinical concerns within cultural frame — culturally appropriate intense grief is different from pathological grief; understanding the cultural baseline helps differentiate. (6) Respect even when unfamiliar — practices that seem strange may be deeply meaningful.

16.6 · Key Studies & Researchers

Key contributors and studies cited in this chapter.

Key contributors & studies

Foundational figures

  • Elisabeth Kübler-Ross — Five stages of dying (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance). Pioneered open conversation about death; framework widely misused as linear sequence; actually meant as common emotional responses, not stages everyone follows.
  • Cicely Saunders — Founded the modern hospice movement (St. Christopher's Hospice, London, 1967); pioneered modern palliative care; framework of "total pain" (physical, emotional, social, spiritual).
  • Margaret Stroebe & Henk Schut — Dual Process Model of bereavement; healthy grieving involves oscillation between loss orientation (confronting grief) and restoration orientation (building new life).
  • Sheldon Solomon, Jeff Greenberg, Tom Pyszczynski — Terror Management Theory; awareness of death is the human condition; defense mechanisms (cultural worldviews, self-esteem, close relationships) manage existential anxiety.
  • George Bonanno — Resilience after loss research; documented that most bereaved people show resilience patterns rather than prolonged distress.
  • Pauline Boss — Concept of ambiguous loss; losses without resolution (missing persons, dementia caregiving, military families).
  • Kenneth Doka — Disenfranchised grief; losses not socially recognized (ex-spouse, gay partner before marriage equality, pet, miscarriage, addiction-related).
  • Atul Gawande — Modern thought leader on end-of-life care; Being Mortal book; advocate for shifting medical culture toward patient-centered death.
  • Brittany Maynard — 29-year-old whose 2014 public choice to use Oregon's Death with Dignity Act for terminal glioblastoma catalyzed medical aid-in-dying legislation expansion.

Specific studies cited

  • Bonanno resilience studies: ~50-60% of bereaved show resilient trajectory (mild brief distress, returning to baseline); ~30% recovery (more distress but eventual recovery); ~10-15% chronic grief or pre-existing depression. Challenges the assumption that all loss produces prolonged distress.
  • Temel et al. (2010) early palliative care study: Patients with metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer randomized to early palliative care alongside oncology vs. standard oncology alone. Early palliative care group had better quality of life, less depression, AND lived longer despite less aggressive treatment. Foundational evidence for early palliative care integration.
  • Coping with Loss research (Stroebe/Schut): Documented oscillation pattern in healthy grief; rigid loss-focused OR restoration-focused approaches associated with worse outcomes than flexible oscillation.
  • Hospice outcome studies: Patients in hospice have less pain, less aggressive interventions, more time with family, AND some studies show longer survival than matched patients receiving aggressive curative treatment to the end.
  • Cross-cultural bereavement research: Documented enormous variation in mourning practices, expression, duration, and meaning across cultures; what looks like pathological grief in one culture is normal in another.
  • COVID-19 end-of-life impact: Documented harm from visitor restrictions, isolated dying, deferred grief rituals, mass loss; lessons for healthcare crisis planning.

16.7 · Key Terms & Definitions

The 33 vocabulary terms the textbook flags as essential for this chapter.

Glossary by category

Definitions of death (4 terms)

  • Thanatology — The scientific study of death, dying, and bereavement.
  • Clinical death — Cessation of heartbeat and breathing; the historical definition of death; now reversible in the early minutes through CPR.
  • Whole-brain death — Irreversible cessation of all brain activity, including brainstem function. The legal standard for death in most US jurisdictions. Distinguishable from persistent vegetative state.
  • Persistent vegetative state — Wakeful unconscious state with sleep-wake cycles but no awareness; the brainstem functions; the person is NOT brain-dead. Can persist for years.

Ethics & advance care (10 terms)

  • Bioethics — Field examining ethical issues in healthcare, biology, and biomedical research.
  • Euthanasia — Acts intended to ease the suffering of a dying person; literally "good death" in Greek.
  • Active euthanasia — Intentional action to end life (e.g., lethal injection); illegal in most US jurisdictions.
  • Passive euthanasia — Withholding or withdrawing life-sustaining treatment; legal and ethically accepted in most contexts when patient or proxy chooses.
  • Physician-assisted suicide — Provider provides means (typically lethal medication prescription) that patient self-administers. Legal in some US states under "Death with Dignity" or "End of Life Options" laws.
  • End-of-life issues — Decisions and care during the final phase of life.
  • Final scenario — How a person wishes to die; preferences for circumstances, place, people present, interventions.
  • Living will — Advance directive specifying treatment preferences in the event of inability to communicate.
  • Healthcare power of attorney — Designation of a person to make healthcare decisions when patient cannot. Also called healthcare proxy.
  • Do not resuscitate (DNR) order — Medical order specifying that CPR should not be performed in the event of cardiac arrest.

Death anxiety & end-of-life care (5 terms)

  • Death anxiety — Fear and dread of one's own death; varies across the lifespan, typically peaks in midlife.
  • Terror management theory — Solomon, Greenberg, Pyszczynski: existential awareness of death drives much human behavior; cultural worldviews and self-esteem function as defense mechanisms.
  • Palliative care — Specialized care focused on symptom relief, quality of life, and emotional/spiritual support. Available at ANY stage of serious illness, NOT only end-of-life. Often combined with curative treatment.
  • Hospice — End-of-life care for those with terminal illness (typically prognosis ≤6 months); focuses on comfort, dignity, family support; can be home-based, free-standing facility, or hospital-based.
  • Death doula — Trained non-medical professional who provides emotional, spiritual, and practical support to dying people and families; growing field paralleling birth doulas.

Grief & bereavement (14 terms)

  • Bereavement — The objective state of having lost someone significant.
  • Grief — The internal emotional response to loss.
  • Mourning — The culturally-prescribed expressions and rituals associated with loss.
  • Anticipatory grief — Grief that occurs before the actual death; common with long terminal illnesses.
  • Grief work — The process of working through loss; Freudian concept later refined.
  • Anniversary reaction — Resurgence of grief on dates significant to the loss (date of death, birthday, holidays).
  • Four-component model — Bonanno's framework: context (situation, person, social), continuing relationship (how the bond is maintained), coping resources, meaning-making.
  • Grief work as rumination hypothesis — Stroebe & colleagues' challenge to obligatory grief work; for some bereaved, prolonged focus on loss prolongs distress.
  • Dual process model (DPM) — Stroebe & Schut: healthy grieving involves oscillation between loss orientation (confronting grief, sadness) and restoration orientation (building new life, new identity, new activities).
  • Model of adaptive grieving dynamics (MAGD) — Newer model emphasizing flexibility in grieving rather than fixed sequences.
  • Ambiguous loss — Pauline Boss: losses without resolution; e.g., missing persons, severe dementia (physically present but psychologically absent), addiction, refugees.
  • Prolonged grief — Persistent intense grief lasting beyond cultural norms; DSM-5-TR Prolonged Grief Disorder.
  • Separation distress — Painful yearning for the deceased; one dimension of prolonged grief.
  • Traumatic distress — Intrusion symptoms, avoidance, hyperarousal related to the death; another dimension of complicated grief.
  • Disenfranchised grief — Doka: grief not socially recognized or supported (ex-spouse, gay partner pre-marriage equality, pet loss, miscarriage, suicide loss, addiction death). Lack of social support compounds distress.

Clinical & supplementary terms (26 terms)

Terms beyond the textbook's original glossary — clinical concepts, frameworks, and cross-disciplinary vocabulary that appear in the mid-reads and exercises in this chapter.

  • REMAP framework (VitalTalk) — Goals-of-care conversation framework: Reframe the situation when status changes; Expect emotion and respond; Map what's important to the patient; Align with patient values; Plan medical recommendations. Evidence-based protocol for structured serious-illness conversations.
  • Ariadne Labs Serious Illness Conversation Guide — Structured 7-question framework for serious illness conversations. Key questions explore: patient understanding, information preferences, goals, fears, function thresholds, willingness to undergo treatment for time, and family knowledge of priorities.
  • Dignity Therapy (Chochinov) — Brief structured psychotherapy for people near end of life. Interview-based; trained therapist asks about life experiences, accomplishments, values, things to be remembered for, messages for loved ones. Results in an edited document the patient can give to family. Evidence base for dignity, sense of meaning, family bereavement.
  • POLST / MOLST — Physician/Medical Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment. Portable medical orders translating patient wishes into orders that follow the patient across settings (hospital, nursing home, home, EMS). Typically covers CPR, mechanical ventilation, artificial nutrition/hydration, antibiotics. Different from advance directive — POLST/MOLST is a medical order, immediately actionable.
  • Five Wishes — Widely-used advance directive form designed to be accessible to non-lawyers. Addresses medical preferences AND personal, emotional, spiritual wishes — who can make decisions, kinds of medical treatment wanted, comfort preferences, how one wants to be treated, what one wants loved ones to know.
  • Doctrine of double effect — Ethical and legal principle: an action with potentially harmful side effects is morally acceptable if (1) the action itself is good or neutral, (2) the intent is the good effect, (3) the bad effect is not the means of achieving the good, (4) the good outweighs the bad. Applied to end-of-life pain management: appropriate opioid dosing is acceptable even if it theoretically could hasten death, because intent is symptom relief.
  • Prolonged Grief Disorder (DSM-5-TR) — Added to DSM-5-TR in 2022 as a formal diagnosis. Criteria: persistent intense yearning/longing OR preoccupation with the deceased ≥12 months after death (≥6 months children), plus 3+ associated symptoms (identity disruption, disbelief, avoidance, emotional pain, difficulty reintegrating, numbness, meaninglessness, loneliness), with clinically significant impairment exceeding cultural norms.
  • Dual Process Model of grieving (Stroebe & Schut) — Healthy grieving involves oscillation between two orientations: loss orientation (confronting grief, sadness, yearning, ruminating) and restoration orientation (building new life, new identity, new activities, attending to daily demands). Flexibility in oscillation predicts better outcomes than rigid focus on either.
  • Terror Management Theory (TMT) — Solomon, Greenberg, Pyszczynski's social psychology framework: humans manage existential awareness of mortality through cultural worldviews, self-esteem, and close relationships. Mortality salience (reminders of death) increases worldview defense, prejudice, attachment-seeking, and self-esteem striving. Relevant to healthcare workers' chronic mortality exposure.
  • Disenfranchised grief (Doka) — Kenneth Doka's term for grief over losses not socially recognized, validated, or supported. Categories: relationship not recognized (ex-partner, gay relationship pre-marriage equality, affair, in-law after divorce); loss not recognized (miscarriage, pet, dementia caregiving, addiction-related); griever excluded (children, intellectually disabled); circumstances stigmatized (suicide, overdose, AIDS); grief expression doesn't fit norms.
  • Ambiguous loss (Boss) — Pauline Boss's concept of losses without resolution. Physical presence with psychological absence: dementia, addiction, severe brain injury. Physical absence with psychological presence: missing persons, military deployment, immigration separation, foster care. Ambiguous losses are particularly difficult to grieve because the loss isn't complete and rituals may not apply.
  • Novena (Catholic / Filipino-Catholic tradition) — Nine days of prayer following death, central to many Filipino Catholic families. Community gathers in the home of the deceased, prays for the soul, shares food and remembrance. Part of the formal mourning structure that supports bereavement.
  • Pasiyam (Filipino tradition) — Nine-day commemoration after death in Filipino tradition, often overlapping with novena prayers. Marks an important transition in the mourning process. Family and community presence is central.
  • Babang-luksa (Filipino tradition) — Filipino end-of-mourning observance, typically one year after death, marking the transition out of formal mourning. Often involves a Mass, family gathering, removal of mourning attire. Provides a structured endpoint to the mourning period.
  • Utang na loob (Filipino) — Filipino cultural concept often translated as "debt of gratitude" or "inner debt" — a deep, often lifelong sense of obligation, especially toward parents and others who have given much. Shapes end-of-life caregiving decisions and family presence.
  • GRASP (Grief Recovery After a Substance Passing) — Peer support organization specifically for those bereaved by overdose or substance-use-related death. Addresses the disenfranchised grief and complex emotions (anger, guilt, stigma) common in these losses. Online and in-person meetings.
  • Hospice eligibility (Medicare) — Medicare hospice benefit requires: (1) terminal illness with prognosis ≤6 months if disease runs natural course; (2) certified by two physicians; (3) patient elects hospice and forgoes curative treatment of the terminal condition. Re-certification continues as long as patient remains eligible. Patients sometimes stabilize and "graduate" from hospice.
  • Lamay (Filipino wake) — Multi-day Filipino wake (often 3-7 nights) with continuous family and community presence around the deceased. Includes communal food, card games, rosaries, conversation. Far longer than typical American funeral home visitations. Reflects communal nature of Filipino grief and the principle of accompanying the deceased through transition.
  • Undás (Filipino All Souls' Day) — November 1-2 Filipino national observance of All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day. Filipino families visit cemeteries, clean graves, bring candles, food, flowers, and spend the day at the cemetery praying and remembering. One of the most important Filipino family observances of the year. Maintains connection with deceased across years and generations.
  • Das et al. (2021) Northern India parental grief study — Qualitative study of 47 bereaved parents documenting four major themes in stillbirth/perinatal/child loss: grief anticipation and expression, impact of bereavement, gendered coping mechanisms, sociocultural norms. Found ~60% of mothers with perinatal/child death and ~80% with stillbirth report severe grief at 6-9 months — far exceeding general grief patterns. Demonstrates need for long-term, family-level, spiritually-integrated support.
  • Perinatal Grief Scale (PGS) — Validated 33-item self-report measure (also short forms) of grief severity following perinatal loss (miscarriage, stillbirth, neonatal death). Three subscales: active grief, difficulty coping, despair. Used internationally for research and clinical assessment.
  • VitalTalk — Nonprofit organization providing evidence-based communication training for healthcare workers in serious illness conversations. Offers in-person courses, online resources, and tools (including the REMAP framework). Widely adopted in palliative care fellowships, oncology training, and primary care continuing education.
  • Center to Advance Palliative Care (CAPC) — National organization based at Mount Sinai's Icahn School of Medicine. Develops and disseminates resources for palliative care delivery. During COVID-19, developed scripts for healthcare workers facilitating virtual end-of-life conversations. Major resource for institutional palliative care development.
  • SPIKES protocol — Six-step framework for delivering bad news: Setting up the interview, assessing patient Perception, obtaining Invitation to disclose, giving Knowledge, addressing patient Emotions, Strategy and summary. Developed for oncology but applicable broadly. Reduces patient distress and improves understanding.
  • NURSE statements (verbal de-escalation in emotional moments) — Mnemonic for responding to patient/family emotion: Name the emotion, Understand it, Respect it, Support the person, Explore what's underneath. Brief framework for trauma-informed responses during difficult conversations.
  • Anticipatory grief — Grief experienced BEFORE a death, when a terminal diagnosis or chronic decline makes loss expected. Common in dementia, cancer, ALS caregiving. Sometimes considered protective (allows preparation); other times intensifies post-death grief through chronic mourning. Recognition matters for both patient and family support.

Real People · Case Study

From the textbook — a 29-year-old's public end-of-life decision and its influence on the medical aid-in-dying movement.

Brittany Maynard — death with dignity case

The case: Brittany Maynard

In January 2014, Brittany Maynard, a 29-year-old California schoolteacher and newlywed, was diagnosed with a glioblastoma — an aggressive brain tumor. Surgeons performed a partial resection of her temporal lobe to slow the tumor's growth. By April, it was clear the surgery had not worked. Brittany was given a prognosis of about 6 months.

After researching her options, Brittany determined that the available treatment paths would extend her life only modestly while causing significant suffering and progressive loss of capacity. She decided that, when the disease's progression made her life intolerable, she would want to end it on her own terms. California did not at that time permit medical aid in dying; Oregon did. Brittany and her family moved to Oregon, established residency, and obtained the certifications required by Oregon's Death with Dignity Act.

Brittany spent much of her remaining time advocating for the death-with- dignity movement, working with Compassion & Choices. She gave interviews, wrote about her decision, and made the case publicly. She wanted other families facing terminal illness to have the option she had needed to move states to access.

On November 2, 2014, Brittany took the prescribed lethal medication and died, surrounded by her husband, mother, and closest family, in her Oregon home. She was 29.

Use this chapter's frameworks. What does Brittany Maynard's case illustrate about end-of-life decision-making, palliative care, autonomy, and the changing landscape of dying? What ethical questions does her case raise on both sides?

Worked analysis

The autonomy framework

Brittany's case foregrounds patient autonomy — the right to determine the conditions of one's own death within a system of medical safeguards. By the time she chose to use the Oregon law, she had: terminal diagnosis verified by multiple physicians, ~6-month prognosis, full mental capacity, multiple voluntary requests, the required waiting period, and the ability to self-administer the medication. The safeguards built into death-with-dignity laws are precisely designed to support genuine autonomy.

What palliative care does and doesn't reach

One major debate in this area: whether high-quality palliative care addresses what drives patients toward medical aid in dying, or whether some terminal experiences remain intolerable even with the best care. Brittany received good care; her concerns were not primarily about pain. They were about loss of cognitive capacity, of agency, of personhood — concerns that palliative medicine can mitigate but cannot fully resolve for some patients.

The ethical disagreement

Reasonable people disagree about medical aid in dying on principled grounds. Proponents emphasize autonomy, suffering reduction, and the dignity of choosing one's death. Opponents raise concerns about coercion of vulnerable populations, the slippery slope to non-voluntary euthanasia, the impact on the doctor-patient relationship, and religious or philosophical objections to medically-assisted death. Empirical evidence from jurisdictions with such laws has not generally supported the slippery-slope concerns, but the ethical debates continue beyond the empirics.

The public-advocacy dimension

Brittany's most lasting impact was probably as advocate, not patient. Her public discussion of her diagnosis and decision contributed to changes in several state laws. California passed its End of Life Option Act in 2015, the year after her death. Other states followed. Her case became a catalyst in the broader national conversation about end-of-life options.

The textbook's broader point

Dying is no longer a simple biological event handled by professionals out of patients' sight. Modern medicine has made dying more complicated — extending lives that previously would have ended naturally, providing options that didn't previously exist, and raising ethical questions that don't have settled answers. End-of-life decision- making now requires patients, families, and clinicians to engage with these complications thoughtfully and individually. Brittany's case is a reminder that what counts as a "good death" is contested, that autonomy matters but doesn't resolve every question, and that the conversation about how we die continues to evolve.

Practice · Application Exercises

Five questions covering Ch 16 core content.

Q1

A patient with metastatic cancer has approximately 18-month expected survival. The family asks whether hospice is appropriate. What's the most accurate framing of options?

  1. A. Hospice now or aggressive treatment forever — these are the only two choices
  2. B. Hospice generally requires ~6-month prognosis and a focus on comfort over cure; the patient is an excellent candidate for palliative care now, which can run alongside curative treatment and improve both quality of life and survival
  3. C. Hospice should never be considered until the patient is actively dying
  4. D. Palliative care and hospice are identical
Show rationale

A. False dichotomy that misses palliative care as an option appropriate now.

B. CORRECT. The patient may not yet qualify for hospice (requires ~6-month prognosis + comfort-focused care decision). But they're an excellent candidate for palliative care, which can be provided at any stage of serious illness, alongside curative treatment. Early palliative-care involvement is associated with better quality of life AND modestly longer survival than aggressive treatment alone. The transition to hospice can happen later as prognosis tightens and goals shift.

C. Hospice is most effective with weeks-to-months of involvement, not last-days only. Late-referral hospice is a known gap in US end-of-life care.

D. Related but distinct — see (B).

Q2

A patient is on a ventilator with no spontaneous breathing, no neurological response, and no brainstem reflexes. Brain death has been certified by two physicians. The heart still beats with ventilator and pressor support. The family asks if the patient is "still alive." What's the appropriate response?

  1. A. Yes, because the heart is beating
  2. B. Under the current US legal standard, the patient has died — brain death is the legally accepted definition of death; the continued heartbeat is mechanical support of an already-dead person; clarifying this allows appropriate decisions about withdrawal of support and potential organ donation
  3. C. The patient is "maybe" alive — wait several months
  4. D. The patient is in a persistent vegetative state
Show rationale

A. The heartbeat is being maintained by the ventilator and medications, not by an alive person. Under brain-death criteria, this patient has died.

B. CORRECT. Brain death (irreversible cessation of all brain function including brainstem) is the current US legal definition of death. The criteria have been met and certified. Clarifying this clearly to the family allows: (1) acceptance and grieving, (2) appropriate decision about withdrawal of mechanical support, and (3) discussion of potential organ donation (which often occurs from brain-dead donors with circulation maintained by ventilator). Compassionate but clear communication is essential.

C. Brain death is irreversible by definition — there's no "wait and see."

D. Persistent vegetative state is preserved brainstem function with loss of higher cortical function — different from brain death. PVS patients ARE alive; brain-dead patients are not.

Q3

A nurse documents that a terminally ill patient is "in the bargaining stage" and adds that the patient "needs to progress to acceptance." What's the issue with this framing of Kübler-Ross's work?

  1. A. There's no issue — the framing is accurate
  2. B. Kübler-Ross's five responses were observed as common emotional experiences, not a required sequence; treating them as a checklist patients must progress through is empirically wrong and clinically harmful — it can dismiss the patient's actual experience and impose a script over genuine listening
  3. C. The bargaining stage doesn't exist
  4. D. Only depression matters; the other stages are irrelevant
Show rationale

A. Treating Kübler-Ross's framework as a required developmental track is one of the most common misuses of her work.

B. CORRECT. Kübler-Ross's framework has been substantially misunderstood in clinical practice. Her original observation was that dying patients commonly experience these five emotional responses — not that they progress through them in sequence, not that everyone reaches "acceptance", and not that this is a target clinicians should push patients toward. The right clinical stance is to meet patients where they are, listen to what they're actually experiencing, and provide support without imposing a track. Pressuring patients to "move through stages" or reach acceptance on a timetable is harmful.

C. Bargaining is one of the responses Kübler-Ross described — it does exist as an observation; what's problematic is treating it as a required stage.

D. Misreading the framework — none of the responses is "the important one."

Q4

A 5-year-old whose father recently died is told that "Daddy went to sleep and isn't coming back." The child becomes terrified of going to bed at night. What developmental factor explains this, and what would have been better language?

  1. A. The "sleep" euphemism is misleading for young children, who at this age understand death as reversible, like sleep — the language merges the two concepts; better: direct, age-appropriate honesty ("Daddy died — his body stopped working, and he can't come back; we'll miss him and remember him")
  2. B. Children that age never grieve
  3. C. The child has a sleep disorder unrelated to the death
  4. D. Direct talk about death is always traumatic for children
Show rationale

A. CORRECT. Children at 5 understand death as reversible / sleep-like. The "went to sleep" euphemism merges sleep and death in their mind, producing literal fear of sleeping. Best practice: age-appropriate honesty using accurate words. "Daddy died" — not "passed away," "went to sleep," "we lost him," or "is with the angels" (all of which confuse young children). Explain in concrete terms: "His body stopped working. He can't come back. We can remember him and talk about him." Repeat as the child re-asks (they will). Watch for magical thinking and provide reassurance the child didn't cause it.

B. Children of all ages can grieve; the form varies developmentally.

C. The temporal connection makes the death-related explanation overwhelmingly likely.

D. The opposite — gentle, accurate language supports children better than euphemisms.

Q5

A 60-year-old man whose adult daughter died 2 years ago in an accident still experiences daily intense longing, intrusive thoughts about his daughter, has been unable to return to work or social activities, cannot accept that she is gone, and avoids any reminders while being unable to put away her belongings. Which is the most accurate framing?

  1. A. Normal grief — leave alone; time will heal
  2. B. Complicated / prolonged grief — a recognized condition (DSM-5-TR prolonged grief disorder) characterized by persistent intense grief with functional impairment beyond 12 months; affects ~10% of bereaved adults; specific therapies are effective; warrants referral
  3. C. Major depressive disorder — no other diagnosis needed
  4. D. PTSD only
Show rationale

A. Time alone, after 2 years of this pattern, has not been integrating the grief — that's the marker of complicated grief, not normal grief.

B. CORRECT. The pattern fits complicated / prolonged grief disorder — now in DSM-5-TR. Key features: persistent intense grief >12 months, inability to accept the death, intense longing and intrusive thoughts, major functional impairment, oscillation stuck between avoidance and immersion. Affects roughly 10% of bereaved adults; higher rates after sudden/violent loss or loss of a child. Important: specific therapies are effective (Complicated Grief Therapy, Prolonged Grief Disorder Therapy, modified CBT). Distinguish from depression (overlapping but focus differs). Warrants referral to a clinician trained in this specific condition rather than just "more time."

C. Complicated grief and depression overlap but differ in focus and treatment. The longing-and-loss focus, with specific therapies developed for this, makes complicated grief the better primary frame; depression should also be screened for.

D. PTSD criteria require specific trauma-related symptoms (re-experiencing trauma, hyperarousal). Some bereavement, especially after sudden violent loss, includes PTSD features. But the primary picture described is grief-focused.

Q6

Das et al. (2021) studied 47 bereaved parents in Northern India after stillbirth, neonatal death, and child death. Which finding about gendered coping is BEST supported by their results?

  1. A. Only mothers experience severe grief; fathers don't grieve
  2. B. Fathers and mothers both experience severe grief but express it differently — mothers coped through household work, caring for other children, and spiritual activities, while fathers coped through avoidance of grief discussion and through work/professional engagement; fathers resumed work after 5–20 days, mothers took 2–6 weeks to resume household chores; fathers' quicker return to work is NOT denial but a culturally-shaped coping style
  3. C. Indian parents grieve completely differently than Western parents
  4. D. Grief always resolves within weeks
Show rationale

A. Das et al. explicitly documented severe grief in fathers — they expressed it less openly but felt it deeply.

B. CORRECT. Das and colleagues (2021) found that both mothers and fathers experienced severe grief, but the expression and coping mechanisms differed. Mothers: household work, caring for other children, spiritual activities; resumed chores in 2–6 weeks. Fathers: avoiding grief discussion, work and professional engagement; resumed work in 5–20 days. Critical clinical implication: a father's quick return to work is NOT denial or absence of grief — it's a gendered coping style. Pathologizing it pushes fathers away from support. Bereavement programs should ask fathers about their experience directly and offer support designed for action-oriented coping styles, not just talk-based interventions.

C. The patterns Das et al. found resemble gendered coping patterns documented across many cultures; they're not unique to Indian parents.

D. Das et al. found that 60–80% of mothers still reported severe grief at 6–9 months — far from "weeks."

Q7

A mother who experienced a stillbirth 7 months ago tells her primary care provider she's still grieving intensely and worries something is wrong with her for not "moving on." What's the most appropriate response based on the research literature?

  1. A. Tell her she should have moved on by now and prescribe an SSRI
  2. B. Validate that ongoing severe grief at 7 months is statistically typical (Das et al. found 60% of mothers with perinatal/child death and 80% of mothers with stillbirth report severe grief at 6–9 months); screen for treatable comorbidities (postpartum depression, anxiety, PTSD); connect to perinatal-loss-specific support; provide explicit permission to continue grieving; plan long-term follow-up; distinguish ongoing grief from prolonged grief disorder (typically 12+ months with significant functional impairment)
  3. C. Refer immediately for psychiatric hospitalization
  4. D. Tell her to stop talking about it because it's making things worse
Show rationale

A. Pathologizing typical perinatal grief at 7 months adds harm. The mother needs validation, not the message that her grief is abnormal.

B. CORRECT. Das et al. (2021) found that ~60% of mothers with perinatal/child death and 80% with stillbirth still reported severe grief at 6–9 months. The mother's persistent grief is statistically typical, not pathological. Evidence-based response: (1) Validate the pattern. (2) Distinguish ongoing grief from complicated/prolonged grief disorder (12+ months with significant functional impairment). (3) Screen for treatable comorbidities (postpartum depression, anxiety, PTSD — especially after traumatic loss). (4) Connect to perinatal-loss support (peer groups, specialized counseling, faith communities). (5) Permission to grieve — many bereaved parents feel social pressure to "be over it." Explicit clinician permission to continue grieving is therapeutic. (6) Long-term follow-up, not just immediate post-loss period.

C. Psychiatric hospitalization is for acute crises (active suicidality, severe psychotic symptoms). Typical perinatal grief at 7 months doesn't meet criteria.

D. Discouraging grief expression is contraindicated and increases the risk of complicated grief.

Q8

A hospital is creating a protocol for future public-health emergencies that may restrict visitor access. Based on the COVID-19 experience, which approach to virtual goodbyes for dying patients is BEST aligned with equity considerations?

  1. A. Use video calls as the standard for all families
  2. B. Build equity-by-design into the protocol from the start — recognizing that COVID-19 deaths were 2.4× higher in Native American/Latino populations and 2× higher in non-Latino African American populations, and that the digital divide left many families unable to connect; the protocol should proactively address device access, broadband, language support, religious accommodations (pre-approved clergy protocols), multiple communication modalities (video, phone, SMS, recorded messages), trained and rotated staff using CAPC-style scripts, post-loss bereavement outreach, and trauma-informed staff support
  3. C. Only allow virtual goodbyes for families with insurance
  4. D. Don't bother with virtual goodbyes — they don't help
Show rationale

A. Video alone misses families without devices or broadband — exactly the same families with disproportionate COVID mortality.

B. CORRECT. The COVID-19 experience exposed how unequal access to end-of-life family connection compounded already-unequal death rates. CDC data showed 2.4× higher COVID death rates for Native American/Alaska Native and Latino/a populations, 2× higher for non-Latino/a African American populations. The digital divide meant that many families from these same communities couldn't even use virtual-goodbye systems. Equity-by-design means building access from the start: device lending, mobile broadband, language support, multiple modalities (not just video), pre-approved religious/cultural accommodations, trained staff with rotation to prevent moral injury, scripted support (CAPC), post-loss bereavement outreach (given elevated complicated-grief risk after curtailed goodbyes), and trauma-informed staff support.

C. Restricting end-of-life family connection by insurance status amplifies exactly the disparities the protocol should address.

D. Virtual goodbyes provided documented help during COVID-19 and reduced (though didn't eliminate) the burden on families. The lesson isn't to abandon them but to build them better.

Q9

A nurse is caring for a 70-year-old patient with end-stage heart failure. The family asks: "We've heard about hospice and palliative care — what's the difference, and is it time for us to consider one?" What's the most evidence-based explanation?

  1. A. "Hospice and palliative care are the same thing"
  2. B. Palliative care and hospice are related but distinct: PALLIATIVE CARE is specialized care focused on symptom relief, quality of life, and emotional/spiritual support, available at ANY stage of serious illness, often combined with curative or disease-modifying treatment; HOSPICE is end-of-life care for those with terminal illness (typically prognosis ≤6 months under Medicare hospice benefit), focuses on comfort over cure, can be home-based, facility-based, or inpatient; both are interdisciplinary (physicians, nurses, social workers, chaplains, aides, volunteers); Temel et al. (2010) showed early palliative care alongside oncology produced better quality of life, less depression, AND longer survival than oncology alone in advanced cancer; for this patient, palliative care consultation is appropriate now alongside heart failure management; hospice may become appropriate as disease progresses; early conversations about goals of care are beneficial; reframe: both extend rather than shorten meaningful life
  3. C. "Hospice means giving up"
  4. D. "Just continue aggressive treatment"
Show rationale

A. Distinguishing them matters for appropriate care timing and patient/family understanding.

B. CORRECT. The hospice/palliative care distinction is one of the most important concepts for nurses to convey. Palliative care: (1) Specialty focused on symptom relief, quality of life, and emotional/spiritual support. (2) Available at any stage of serious illness. (3) Often combined with curative or disease-modifying treatment. (4) Provided by interdisciplinary team — physicians (some with palliative care boards), nurses, social workers, chaplains, sometimes pharmacists. (5) Domains addressed: pain and symptoms, advance care planning, family support, psychological/spiritual concerns, transitions of care. (6) Settings: hospital consult, outpatient clinic, home-based. (7) Insurance: usually covered like other medical care. Hospice: (1) End-of-life care for those with terminal illness. (2) Medicare hospice benefit requires prognosis ≤6 months if illness runs natural course, certified by two physicians; recertification ongoing. (3) Focus shifts from cure to comfort; aggressive curative treatment generally discontinued. (4) Interdisciplinary team: physician medical director, RN case manager, social worker, chaplain, certified nursing assistant, volunteers, bereavement counselor for family. (5) Settings: home (most common), free-standing hospice facility, hospital inpatient hospice unit, nursing home, sometimes assisted living. (6) Insurance: Medicare hospice benefit covers nearly all costs related to terminal illness; Medicaid and private insurance generally similar; small co-pays. (7) Continues for as long as eligibility maintained — patients sometimes stabilize on hospice and graduate; can re-enroll if condition declines again. Evidence base: (1) Temel et al. (2010) randomized trial in metastatic non-small-cell lung cancer: early palliative care vs. standard oncology. Early palliative care patients had better quality of life, less depression, less aggressive end-of-life care, AND lived longer (median 11.6 vs 8.9 months). (2) Subsequent studies in heart failure, COPD, dementia: similar quality of life and family satisfaction benefits. (3) Hospice patients show less pain, less aggressive interventions, more family time, sometimes longer survival than matched patients receiving full aggressive treatment. For this patient: (1) Palliative care consultation now — alongside heart failure management; addresses symptom burden (dyspnea, fatigue, edema, anxiety), supports family, advance care planning. (2) Hospice eligibility — advanced heart failure with NYHA Class IV symptoms can qualify; assess prognosis. (3) Goals of care conversations — what matters most to patient? Hospital admissions vs. home? Hospitalizations vs. comfort? (4) Advance directives — review and update. (5) Family education and support. Reframe: neither palliative care nor hospice means "giving up" — both focus on what matters most to the patient, often including longer survival than aggressive treatment alone.

C. Hospice doesn't mean giving up — it means redirecting focus to comfort, quality, and meaning; many patients live longer on hospice than with aggressive end-stage treatment.

D. Aggressive treatment without palliative care often produces worse quality of life and sometimes shorter survival; integration is better.

Q10

An older patient in the hospital is dying. The family asks the nurse about advance directives and the difference between a living will, healthcare power of attorney, and DNR. They want to ensure the patient's wishes are honored. How should the nurse explain these tools?

  1. A. "These are all the same thing"
  2. B. Three distinct advance care planning tools: (1) Living will is a written document specifying treatment preferences in the event of inability to communicate (life-sustaining treatments, artificial nutrition/hydration, mechanical ventilation, antibiotics); (2) Healthcare power of attorney (also called healthcare proxy or durable power of attorney for healthcare) designates a person to make healthcare decisions when patient cannot — typically more flexible than living will because the agent can respond to specific situations; (3) DNR order is a specific medical order entered into the chart by a physician (often based on POLST/MOLST) specifying that CPR not be performed at cardiac arrest; some states use POLST/MOLST forms which translate patient wishes into portable medical orders that follow the patient across settings; best practice is to have all three components — living will for specific preferences, healthcare power of attorney for unexpected situations, and POLST/MOLST when medically appropriate; nursing role includes ensuring documents are accessible and honored, advocating for patient wishes, supporting family understanding of options
  3. C. "DNR means do nothing"
  4. D. "Documents don't matter — doctors decide"
Show rationale

A. Each serves a different function; clarity matters for honoring patient wishes.

B. CORRECT. Advance care planning ensures patient wishes guide care when they cannot speak for themselves — one of the most important interventions in adult medicine. Three core documents: (1) Living will — written document specifying treatment preferences for end-of-life or incapacity. Typically addresses: life-sustaining treatments (mechanical ventilation, CPR, dialysis), artificial nutrition and hydration, antibiotics, comfort measures only. Limitations: hard to anticipate every situation; some patients regret blanket choices made in advance. (2) Healthcare Power of Attorney (HCPOA) / Healthcare Proxy / Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare — legal designation of a person to make healthcare decisions when patient cannot. Often more flexible than living will because the agent can respond to specific evolving situations. Patient should discuss wishes with agent BEFORE need arises. (3) DNR (Do Not Resuscitate) order — medical order entered by physician in patient's chart specifying that CPR should not be performed at cardiac arrest. Different from "do not treat" — patient still receives all other care including pain management, antibiotics, comfort measures unless additional orders specify otherwise. POLST/MOLST (Physician/Medical Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) — increasingly used; translates patient wishes into portable medical orders that follow patient across settings (hospital, nursing home, home, EMS); typically covers CPR, mechanical ventilation, artificial nutrition/hydration, antibiotics. Five Wishes — widely-used advance directive form addressing medical, personal, emotional, spiritual wishes. Best practice: All three core components plus ongoing family conversation. Documents should be accessible (in chart, in wallet, with proxy, with primary care provider). Common problems: (1) Documents exist but aren't accessible at the moment of need. (2) Documents exist but family disagrees with patient's wishes. (3) Family hasn't discussed wishes with proxy. (4) Documents conflict with each other. (5) Provider unfamiliar with patient's documents. Nursing role: (1) Ensure documents are in chart and accessible. (2) Advocate for patient wishes. (3) Support family understanding. (4) Identify gaps in advance care planning. (5) Refer for palliative care or social work consultation. (6) Recognize cultural variation — some cultures emphasize family decision-making over individual autonomy; respectfully navigate. (7) Address conflicts when family disagrees with patient wishes — typically patient wishes prevail but social work and ethics consultation helpful. (8) Update documents as wishes evolve.

C. DNR means specifically no CPR; other treatments continue unless additional orders.

D. Advance directives are legally binding; nursing role includes ensuring they're honored.

Q11

A nurse cares for a 35-year-old patient with terminal cancer. The patient is in significant pain. The family is worried that increasing opioid doses will hasten death and asks the nurse about this concern. How should the nurse address this evidence-based?

  1. A. "You're right — we should keep doses low"
  2. B. Address opioid myths with evidence: appropriate dosing of opioids for terminal pain does NOT shorten life — patients with well-controlled pain often live AS LONG or LONGER than those with uncontrolled pain; the principle of double effect (ethically and legally accepted) means that even if a higher dose theoretically could hasten death, the intent is symptom relief and the action is acceptable; under-treatment of pain at end of life is a much bigger problem than over-treatment; pain itself is associated with worse outcomes — agitation, immune suppression, increased delirium; appropriate pain management includes around-the-clock dosing with breakthrough doses, dose escalation as tolerance develops, multimodal approaches (NSAIDs, acetaminophen, adjuvants like gabapentin, antiemetics, anxiolytics, non-pharmacologic supports), addressing side effects (constipation universally, nausea sometimes), and team approach (palliative care or pain management consultation); explore family fears and provide reassurance
  3. C. "We should never give opioids in terminal care"
  4. D. "There's nothing we can do for pain"
Show rationale

A. Keeping doses inadequately low to treat severe terminal pain causes preventable suffering.

B. CORRECT. Pain management at end of life is one of the most important and most ethically complex areas of palliative care. Key myths and the evidence: (1) "Opioids hasten death." Research consistently shows that appropriately titrated opioids in terminal pain do NOT shorten life. In fact, well-controlled patients often live AS LONG OR LONGER than those with uncontrolled pain. Pain itself produces measurable physiological harm — agitation, sleep deprivation, immune suppression, increased delirium, cardiovascular strain. (2) "Opioids cause addiction." Addiction in terminal pain context is rare and irrelevant for life-limiting illness; "dependence" (physiological adaptation) is expected and different from addiction. (3) "There's a ceiling dose." No ceiling for true opioids (oral, IV, transdermal); patients can require dramatically increasing doses as tolerance and disease progression occur; what matters is symptom control and side effect management. (4) "Sleeping = peaceful." Sometimes; but ensure patient isn't sleeping from inadequate care vs. genuine comfort. Doctrine of double effect: ethically and legally accepted principle that an action with potentially harmful side effect (e.g., respiratory depression) is acceptable if (1) the action itself is good or neutral, (2) the intent is the good effect (symptom relief), (3) the bad effect is not the means of achieving the good, (4) the good outweighs the bad. Applied to terminal pain: even if higher opioid dose theoretically could hasten death, the intent is symptom relief — this is acceptable. Appropriate pain management protocol: (1) Assess regularly — use validated scales (0-10 numeric, FLACC for non-verbal, PAINAD for dementia, behavioral cues for unconscious). (2) Around-the-clock dosing for persistent pain — not just PRN. (3) Breakthrough doses — typically 10-20% of 24-hour dose. (4) Titrate to comfort — increase dose by 25-50% if uncontrolled. (5) Multimodal — NSAIDs (if tolerated), acetaminophen, adjuvants (gabapentin for neuropathic, antidepressants, dexamethasone for nerve compression), antiemetics, anxiolytics. (6) Address side effects proactively — constipation prevention with EVERY opioid (stimulant laxatives, NOT just stool softeners), nausea, sedation tolerance, itching. (7) Route considerations — oral first; subcut, IV, transdermal, sublingual as appropriate; PCA for some. (8) Non-pharmacologic supports — positioning, massage, music, presence, environmental adjustments. (9) Address total pain (Saunders) — physical, emotional, social, spiritual. (10) Consultation — palliative care or pain specialist for complex cases. Family education and support: (1) Address fears explicitly. (2) Explain double effect. (3) Reassure that goal is comfort, not hastening death. (4) Acknowledge their love and concern. (5) Family role in symptom monitoring and reporting. Under-treatment is the bigger problem: studies consistently show that despite advances, many terminally ill patients still die in pain because of provider hesitation, family concerns, or inadequate access to palliative care. Nurses are central to recognizing and addressing this.

C. Opioids are central to terminal pain management; categorical avoidance produces preventable suffering.

D. Effective pain management is achievable in nearly all terminal cases with appropriate intervention.

Q12

A pediatric oncology nurse cares for a 7-year-old with terminal leukemia. Parents ask: "Should we tell her she's dying? Can a child her age understand?" What does developmental research say about children's understanding of death, and what's the evidence-based approach?

  1. A. "Don't tell her anything — she's too young to understand"
  2. B. Children's understanding of death develops in stages: under age 5 often see death as reversible or temporary; 5-9 years recognize death is final but may attribute it to specific factors (illness, accident); 9-10+ understand death as universal, irreversible, and biological; at 7, this patient likely understands death is permanent but may have magical thinking about cause; evidence STRONGLY supports honest, developmentally-appropriate communication — children who are not told often feel more isolated and frightened than those who are; pediatric palliative care principles include: (1) follow the child's lead — answer questions honestly, don't over-explain; (2) use concrete language ('dying,' 'won't get better,' not 'going to sleep'); (3) involve child in decisions appropriate to age; (4) ensure child has someone to talk to about fears; (5) maintain normal life elements as much as possible; (6) family support throughout; (7) sibling considerations; (8) bereavement planning that begins before death; resource: Together for Short Lives, ACT, hospice pediatric programs
  3. C. "Lie to her to protect her"
  4. D. "She'll figure it out herself"
Show rationale

A. Excluding children from honest communication causes more harm than the discomfort of conversation.

B. CORRECT. Pediatric palliative care is one of the most challenging and important areas of nursing. Children's developing understanding of death: (1) Under age 5: often see death as reversible, temporary, like sleep or travel. May understand factually but emotionally treat it as recoverable. (2) Ages 5-9: recognize death is final and irreversible but may attribute it to specific causes ("only sick people die" or "only old people die"). May have magical thinking — fear they caused it, fear they're next. (3) Ages 9-10+: more adult-like understanding — death is universal, inevitable, irreversible, biological. (4) Throughout: significant individual variation; cognitively advanced children may understand earlier; experience with illness or family death accelerates understanding. For this 7-year-old: likely understands death is permanent but may have magical thinking; can engage in age-appropriate honest conversation. Evidence on honest communication: (1) Strong evidence that children who are told honestly fare better emotionally than those kept in the dark. (2) Children often know anyway — they sense changes in adult behavior, hear conversations, recognize their own bodies declining. (3) Not being told isolates the child — can't talk to anyone about their fears, feelings of bewilderment. (4) Children who are told can: express wishes, say goodbye, address fears, participate in their care, feel respected. (5) Adult avoidance often more for adult comfort than child's benefit. Approach principles: (1) Follow the child's lead — answer questions honestly at the level asked; don't over-explain. (2) Use concrete language — "dying," "won't get better," not "going to sleep" (which can produce sleep phobia) or "going to a better place" (which can confuse). (3) Allow questions over time — understanding evolves; ongoing conversation. (4) Address fears directly: Will it hurt? (we'll manage pain) Will you be sad? (yes, deeply) What happens to my toys/pets? (concrete worries matter) Am I being punished? (no — emphasize). (5) Involve in appropriate decisions — choices about clothing, activities, who visits, what to eat. (6) Maintain normal life elements — play, friends, school when possible. (7) Address spiritual/religious questions per family's framework. (8) Family support — both parents and child need support; sometimes through different providers. (9) Siblings — they also need honest communication, opportunity to ask questions, support; risk of feeling forgotten or fearful they'll get sick too. (10) Bereavement planning — begin before death; legacy projects, memory boxes, letters from the child for family. (11) School integration — depending on age and family preferences. Resources: Together for Short Lives, ACT (Association for Children's Palliative Care), Children's Hospice International, hospital-based pediatric palliative care teams. For nursing: pediatric end-of-life care requires advanced skills and self-care; team support essential; recognize own grief and seek support. The clinical and human stakes are profound, and the work is profoundly meaningful.

C. Lying to dying children damages trust, isolates them, and produces worse psychological outcomes.

D. Leaving children alone with terror they can't articulate causes profound psychological harm.

Lifespan Companion · Human Development: A Life-Span View (Kail & Cavanaugh, 9th ed.)

Personal study tool — not affiliated with the publisher.
